## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**  
> **Define ion implantation. List any two advantages of ion implantation over thermal diffusion in semiconductor doping.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Ion implantation is a semiconductor doping process in which dopant ions are *accelerated* to high kinetic energy (typically 1 keV – 1 MeV) using an electric field and *physically driven* into a silicon substrate. The depth of penetration is controlled by the **beam energy**, and the number of dopant atoms introduced per unit area is controlled by the **dose (ions/cm²)**.

**Two Advantages (1 mark — 0.5 each):**
1. **Independent control** of junction depth and dopant concentration (energy vs. dose), unlike diffusion where both are coupled through time and temperature.
2. **Lower process temperature** (~800–1050 °C anneal vs. 1000–1200 °C diffusion), which prevents redistribution of previously doped regions and enables shallow, abrupt junctions.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample]**  
> **In a BJT, why is the emitter doped much more heavily than the base? What is the role of the buried layer in an integrated BJT?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Heavy emitter doping (2 marks):**
The **emitter injection efficiency** $\gamma = \dfrac{N_E D_n}{N_E D_n + N_B D_p}$ must be close to unity for high current gain $\beta$. This requires $N_E \gg N_B$. Practically, $N_E \approx 10^{20}$ cm⁻³ while $N_B \approx 10^{17}$–$10^{18}$ cm⁻³, giving $\gamma \approx 0.99$ and $\beta$ in the range 100–500.

**Role of buried layer (1 mark):**
The n⁺ buried layer, formed by *ion implantation* (As or Sb) **before** epitaxy, provides a low-resistance path for collector current. Without it, current would flow through the high-resistivity n-type epitaxial layer, drastically increasing the **collector series resistance $r_C$** and degrading transistor performance at high frequencies.

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Question A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified] (14 Marks)**  
> **(a)** Describe the **ion implantation process** with a neat block diagram of the implanter system. Explain the role of (i) the mass analyzer, (ii) the acceleration tube, and (iii) post-implant annealing. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** With the help of a step-by-step process flow, explain the **fabrication of an integrated NPN BJT** using a 6-mask process. Clearly indicate which steps use ion implantation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Ion Implantation Process — Description (7 marks)**

**1. Definition & Principle (2 marks):**
Ion implantation is a *physical* doping process where dopant ions (e.g., B⁺, P⁺, As⁺) are ionized in a source, mass-filtered, accelerated through a high potential $V_{acc}$, and injected into a Si wafer. The ions penetrate the surface and come to rest at a mean depth $R_p$ (the **projected range**) with a standard deviation $\Delta R_p$ (the **projected straggle**).

**2. Implanter Block Diagram (2 marks):**

The system consists of the following sub-systems in sequence:

```
[Ion Source] → [Extractor] → [Mass Analyzer] → [Acceleration Tube]
    → [Focusing Lens] → [Scanner] → [Wafer Platen]
```

**3. Role of Each Subsystem (2 marks):**

| Subsystem | Function | Why Important |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Mass Analyzer** | A magnetic sector that deflects ions; only ions of correct mass/charge (e.g., ¹¹B⁺) pass through the slit. | Removes isotopic and molecular contaminants; ensures only the desired dopant enters the wafer. |
| **(ii) Acceleration Tube** | Applies a high voltage $V_{acc}$ (10–200 kV) across a series of electrodes to accelerate the ions. | The kinetic energy $E = qV_{acc}$ directly sets the implant depth $R_p$. |
| **(iii) Post-Implant Anneal** | A thermal step (RTA at 800–1050 °C for 10–60 s) applied *after* implantation. | (a) Repairs lattice damage caused by ion collisions; (b) Places dopants on substitutional sites (**electrical activation**); (c) Allows limited diffusion to smooth the profile. |

**4. Gaussian Profile (1 mark):**
The final activated profile is approximately:

$$N(x) = \dfrac{\Phi}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\Delta R_p}\exp\!\left[-\dfrac{(x - R_p)^2}{2\Delta R_p^2}\right]$$

---

**(b) Fabrication of NPN BJT — 6-Mask Process Flow (7 marks)**

| Mask | Step | Process | Role of Implantation |
|---|---|---|---|
| — | 1 | p-type (100) Si wafer, $\rho \approx$ 10 Ω·cm | — |
| — | 2 | Grow thin SiO₂ (~5000 Å) | — |
| **M1** | 3 | Photolithography + etch to open buried-layer windows | — |
| — | 4 | **Implant As⁺ or Sb⁺ at 80 keV, $\Phi = 5\times10^{15}$ cm⁻²** | Forms n⁺ buried layer |
| — | 5 | Strip oxide; grow n-type epitaxial layer (5–10 μm, $N_D \approx 10^{15}$ cm⁻³) | — |
| **M2** | 6 | Pattern isolation regions; **Implant B⁺ at 100 keV, $\Phi = 10^{15}$ cm⁻²** | Forms p⁺ junction isolation walls |
| — | 7 | Drive-in anneal to deepen isolation | — |
| **M3** | 8 | Pattern base; **Implant B⁺ at 40 keV, $\Phi = 10^{14}$ cm⁻²** | Forms p-type base |
| — | 9 | Anneal to activate base dopant | — |
| **M4** | 10 | Pattern emitter; **Implant P⁺ at 50 keV, $\Phi = 5\times10^{15}$ cm⁻²** | Forms n⁺ emitter |
| — | 11 | Anneal (RTA) to activate emitter | — |
| **M5** | 12 | Pattern and etch contact holes | — |
| **M6** | 13 | Deposit Al (~1 μm); pattern and etch metal | — |
| — | 14 | Deposit PSG/Si₃N₄ passivation; sinter at 400 °C in H₂/N₂ | — |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The completed device has a vertical NPN structure (n⁺ emitter / p base / n collector / n⁺ buried layer / p substrate) with Al contacts to E, B, C. Ion implantation was used in **four critical steps** (buried layer, isolation, base, emitter), giving precise control of doping and junction depth.

---

> **Question B [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified] (14 Marks) [INTERNAL CHOICE]**  
> **(a)** Derive the **peak concentration** of an implanted Gaussian profile. A Boron implant has dose $\Phi = 2 \times 10^{14}$ cm⁻² and straggle $\Delta R_p = 40$ nm. Find $N_{peak}$ and comment on whether this is suitable for a BJT base. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** Compare **ion implantation and thermal diffusion** as doping techniques. Why is ion implantation *specifically preferred* for forming the base region of a high-performance NPN BJT? **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Peak Concentration Derivation (7 marks)**

**Derivation (4 marks):**
The implanted dopant distribution, after a brief anneal, follows a Gaussian profile:

$$N(x) = N_{peak}\exp\!\left[-\dfrac{(x-R_p)^2}{2\Delta R_p^2}\right]$$

The total dose $\Phi$ (ions/cm²) equals the area under this curve:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} N(x)\,dx = \Phi$$

Since $R_p \gg \Delta R_p$ in most practical cases, the lower limit can be extended to $-\infty$:

$$N_{peak}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\exp\!\left[-\dfrac{(x-R_p)^2}{2\Delta R_p^2}\right]dx = \Phi$$

Using the standard result $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-u^2}du = \sqrt{\pi}$:

$$N_{peak}\cdot\sqrt{2\pi}\,\Delta R_p = \Phi$$

$$\boxed{\,N_{peak} = \dfrac{\Phi}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\Delta R_p}\,}$$

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**
Given $\Phi = 2 \times 10^{14}$ cm⁻² and $\Delta R_p = 40$ nm $= 4 \times 10^{-6}$ cm:

$$N_{peak} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{14}}{\sqrt{2\pi} \times 4 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{14}}{1.005 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$N_{peak} \approx 1.99 \times 10^{19} \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

**Comment (1 mark):**
This peak concentration is **far too high** for a BJT base. A typical base needs $N_B \approx 10^{17}$–$10^{18}$ cm⁻³ to ensure $N_E \gg N_B$ for high $\gamma$. Such a heavy $1.99 \times 10^{19}$ cm⁻³ peak would *exceed* typical emitter doping and invert the transistor action. Therefore, this dose is more appropriate for an **n⁺ emitter or buried layer** rather than a base. To form a base, the dose should be reduced to $\Phi \approx 10^{13}$–$10^{14}$ cm⁻² with appropriate post-anneal diffusion.

---

**(b) Comparison: Ion Implantation vs. Thermal Diffusion (7 marks)**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Property | Ion Implantation | Thermal Diffusion |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Physical (ballistic) | Chemical (concentration gradient driven) |
| **Temperature** | Room-temp implant + 800–1050 °C anneal | 1000–1200 °C continuous |
| **Profile shape** | Gaussian, near-abrupt | Complementary error function (broad, graded) |
| **Junction depth control** | Direct (via beam energy) | Indirect (via time & temperature) |
| **Dose control** | Direct and accurate (±0.5%) | Indirect |
| **Lateral spread** | Minimal | Significant (isotropic) |
| **Masking** | Photoresist (low-temp) | Thick SiO₂ (high-temp stable) |
| **Lattice damage** | Yes — requires anneal | None |
| **Throughput** | Medium (one wafer at a time) | High (batch) |
| **Cost** | High (multi-M$ tool) | Low (furnace) |

**Why Implantation for BJT Base (3 marks):**

1. **Sharp, thin base profile:** A high-performance BJT needs a base width $W_B \approx 0.1$–$0.3$ μm to minimize base transit time $\tau_B = W_B^2/2D_n$ and maximize $f_T$. Implantation can place dopants at a precise depth with minimal lateral spread; diffusion would smear the profile and widen $W_B$.

2. **Precise dose for matched pairs:** Analog ICs (current mirrors, differential pairs) require transistors with *matched* $\beta$ and $V_{BE}$, which depend on $N_B$. Implantation's dose accuracy of ±0.5% (vs. ~±5% for diffusion) ensures excellent matching.

3. **Low thermal budget:** Once the base is implanted, subsequent high-temperature steps for the emitter must *not* rediffuse the base. Implantation produces a profile that is *more resistant* to additional thermal cycles compared to a deeply diffused base, giving the process engineer better control.

4. **Buried layer compatibility:** The buried layer is also implanted; using the same equipment for both base and buried layer simplifies the process flow and reduces cost.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Conflating dose and energy:** Students often write "implantation controls doping by temperature" — this is **wrong**. Dose controls *concentration*; energy controls *depth*. The examiner will deduct 1 mark for this confusion.
> 2. **Skipping the anneal step:** If you mention ion implantation in BJT fabrication but forget the post-implant anneal, you lose 1 mark. Anneal is *mandatory* to repair lattice damage and activate dopants electrically.
> 3. **Wrong process order:** Writing that the emitter is implanted *before* the base will cost full marks on process-flow questions. The base MUST come before the emitter; otherwise the emitter's high dose will counter-dope the base.
> 4. **Forgetting the buried layer:** The buried layer is the *first* implantation step and is *frequently tested*. Missing it in a process-flow diagram is a 2-mark penalty.
> 5. **Numerical mistakes in $N_{peak}$:** Watch the unit conversion: $\Delta R_p$ must be in **cm**, not nm. The examiner will specifically check this.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Ion Implantation** = a *physical, ballistic* doping method where accelerated dopant ions are shot into a Si wafer.
- **Beam Energy (keV)** controls **junction depth** ($R_p$).
- **Dose (ions/cm²)** controls **dopant concentration** ($N_{peak}$).
- **Mass analyzer** ensures only the correct isotope reaches the wafer.
- **Post-implant anneal** is *mandatory* to repair damage and electrically activate dopants.
- **BJT NPN fabrication** uses **4 ion implant steps** in a 6-mask process: **buried layer → isolation → base → emitter**.
- The **base is implanted before the emitter** (process order is critical).
- The **emitter is the most heavily doped region** ($N_E \approx 10^{20}$ cm⁻³) to ensure high injection efficiency $\gamma \approx 1$.
- The **buried layer** is implanted *before* epitaxy to reduce collector series resistance.
- **Gaussian profile** equation: $N(x) = N_{peak}\exp[-(x-R_p)^2/2\Delta R_p^2]$.
- **Peak concentration formula:** $N_{peak} = \Phi / (\sqrt{2\pi}\,\Delta R_p)$ — a *must-remember* KTU formula.
- **Implantation advantages over diffusion:** sharper profiles, lower temperature, independent dose/energy control, better transistor matching.
- **Implantation disadvantages:** equipment cost, lattice damage (needs anneal), lower throughput.
- **Sheet concentration** $N_{2D} = \Phi$ is critical for designing emitter resistance.
- For SiGe HBTs and modern high-speed BJTs, *ion implantation is irreplaceable* — diffusion cannot achieve sub-100 nm base widths.
- **Channeling prevention** is achieved by tilting the wafer by **7°** off the (100) axis during implant.
- **KTU favorite question types:** (1) Draw the implanter block diagram and explain each block, (2) Explain BJT process flow with 6 masks, (3) Derive $N_{peak}$ from given dose and straggle, (4) Compare implantation vs. diffusion.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MOSFETs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT402 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** MOSFETs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. MOSFETs: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MOSFET (Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor)** is a four-terminal, voltage-controlled, unipolar semiconductor device in which the current flowing between the **Source** and **Drain** terminals is modulated by the electric field generated by the voltage applied to the **Gate** terminal, which is insulated from the conducting channel by a thin layer of **silicon dioxide (SiO₂)**.

The four terminals of a MOSFET are:
- **Gate (G)** — Controls the channel conductivity via an applied electric field.
- **Drain (D)** — Terminal through which carriers (electrons for NMOS, holes for PMOS) leave the channel.
- **Source (S)** — Terminal through which carriers enter the channel.
- **Body / Substrate / Bulk (B)** — The underlying semiconductor wafer; usually tied to the source in discrete devices but accessible in ICs.

> [!NOTE]
> Two complementary variants exist:
> - **NMOS (n-channel MOSFET)** — Conduction by electrons; uses n⁺ source/drain in a p-substrate.
> - **PMOS (p-channel MOSFET)** — Conduction by holes; uses p⁺ source/drain in an n-substrate.

The symbol **"MOSFET"** in the KTU 2024 Linear Integrated Circuits syllabus is the foundational device upon which all modern CMOS analog and digital ICs (op-amps, timers, converters) are constructed.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Water-Tap & Pipe System**
>
> Imagine a horizontal pipe carrying water from a reservoir (Source) to a sink (Drain). Normally, the pipe is **pinched closed** by a mechanical clamp. The clamp is operated by a lever, but the lever is connected to the pipe handle only through a **rubber diaphragm** (the SiO₂ insulation) — so water can never leak back to the lever.
>
> - When you **push the lever harder** (apply more gate voltage, $V_{GS}$), the clamp loosens, the pipe opens wider, and more water flows.
> - When the lever is **released** (no $V_{GS}$), the clamp tightens, the pipe shuts, and water flow stops.
> - The **water pressure at the sink end** (drain voltage, $V_{DS}$) also influences the flow once the pipe is open.
>
> This is exactly how a MOSFET works: the **gate voltage** controls the channel conductivity, the **insulating oxide** prevents any DC gate current, and the **drain-to-source voltage** drives the carriers through the channel.

## 1.3 Physical Construction Summary

An **enhancement-type NMOS** is fabricated as follows:
- A **p-type silicon substrate** (the body) is taken as the starting wafer.
- Two heavily-doped **n⁺ regions** are diffused into the substrate to form the **Source** and **Drain**.
- A thin layer of **silicon dioxide (SiO₂)** is thermally grown over the region between source and drain.
- A **polysilicon or metal layer** is deposited over the oxide to form the **Gate**.
- **Metallic contacts** are made to the Source, Drain, Gate, and Body (substrate).

The threshold voltage $V_{th}$ (or $V_T$) is the **minimum gate-to-source voltage** required to create a conducting inversion layer (channel) at the oxide-semiconductor interface. The standard CMOS process threshold is approximately **V_th ≈ 0.5 V to 1.0 V** for modern 180 nm–65 nm nodes, with a typical value of **0.7 V** used in textbook problems.

## 1.4 Two Fundamental Operating Modes

| Mode | Description |
|---|---|
| **Enhancement-Mode (E-MOSFET)** | Channel is **physically absent** at $V_{GS} = 0$; must be **induced** by applying $\vert V_{GS} \vert > \vert V_{th} \vert$. Most common in ICs. |
| **Depletion-Mode (D-MOSFET)** | Channel is **physically present** at $V_{GS} = 0$ (due to doping); can be **enhanced or depleted** by gate bias. Used in special RF/analog applications. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the KTU PCEBT402 syllabus, the **enhancement-type NMOS** is the primary focus, as it forms the building block of every CMOS op-amp and analog cell.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

The MOSFET operates by **field effect** — the gate voltage creates an electric field that modulates the carrier concentration in the channel region.

**Step 1 — The OFF state (Cutoff Region):**
When $V_{GS} < V_{th}$, the electric field from the gate is insufficient to attract minority carriers (electrons in a p-substrate) to the surface. The channel region remains p-type, and no current flows between source and drain. The MOSFET behaves like an **open switch**.

**Step 2 — Channel formation (Threshold reached):**
When $V_{GS} = V_{th}$, the gate field becomes strong enough to **invert** the surface of the p-substrate into an n-type layer. This thin n-type layer is the **inversion layer** or **channel**, which physically connects the n⁺ source to the n⁺ drain.

**Step 3 — Conduction begins (Triode / Ohmic Region):**
For $V_{GS} > V_{th}$, the channel is well-formed, and applying a small $V_{DS} > 0$ drives electrons from source to drain. The device acts as a **voltage-controlled resistor** whose resistance is inversely proportional to $(V_{GS} - V_{th})$.

**Step 4 — Channel Pinch-off (Saturation Region):**
As $V_{DS}$ increases further, the voltage drop across the channel near the drain end **reduces the effective gate-to-channel voltage** below $V_{th}$. At the pinch-off point $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$, the channel disappears at the drain end, and the current becomes **saturated** — controlled almost entirely by $V_{GS}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical 'Why':** In saturation, carriers are injected from the pinch-off point into the depletion region near the drain and swept across by the high lateral field — analogous to a **velocity-saturated waterfall** where the flow rate is determined by what enters the fall, not by how steep it is.

## 2.2 Three Regions of Operation (NMOS Reference)

| Region | Condition | Physical Behavior | I-V Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cutoff** | $V_{GS} < V_{th}$ | No channel exists | $I_D = 0$ |
| **Triode (Ohmic)** | $V_{GS} > V_{th}$ AND $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{th}$ | Continuous channel; device acts as resistor | $I_D$ depends on both $V_{GS}$ and $V_{DS}$ |
| **Saturation (Active)** | $V_{GS} > V_{th}$ AND $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{th}$ | Channel pinched off at drain; current saturates | $I_D$ depends mainly on $V_{GS}$ |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all essential MOSFET equations** for KTU 2024 PCEBT402. Memorize these — they form the basis of every derivation and problem.

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_D = 0$ for $V_{GS} < V_{th}$ | **Cutoff** current | A |
| 2 | $I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{th}) V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^{2}}{2} \right]$ | **Triode region** current (long-channel) | A |
| 3 | $I_{D,sat} = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^{2}$ | **Saturation** current (ideal, no CLM) | A |
| 4 | $I_{D,sat} = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^{2} (1 + \lambda V_{DS})$ | Saturation with **Channel Length Modulation (CLM)** | A |
| 5 | $V_{ov} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$ | **Overdrive voltage** (also $V_{eff}$, $V_{DS,sat}$) | V |
| 6 | $k_n' = \mu_n C_{ox}$ | **Process transconductance parameter** | A/V² |
| 7 | $k_n = k_n' \dfrac{W}{L} = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}$ | **Device transconductance parameter** | A/V² |
| 8 | $I_{D,sat} = \dfrac{1}{2} k_n (V_{GS} - V_{th})^{2}$ | Compact saturation formula | A |
| 9 | $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th}) = k_n' \dfrac{W}{L} V_{ov}$ | **Transconductance** (from $I_D$ expression) | A/V (S) |
| 10 | $g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} I_D} = \sqrt{2 k_n' \dfrac{W}{L} I_D}$ | $g_m$ in terms of bias current $I_D$ | A/V (S) |
| 11 | $g_m = \dfrac{2 I_D}{V_{GS} - V_{th}} = \dfrac{2 I_D}{V_{ov}}$ | Alternative $g_m$ form | A/V (S) |
| 12 | $r_o = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D}$ | **Output resistance** due to CLM | Ω |
| 13 | $A_v = -g_m (r_o \,\vert\vert\, R_D)$ | Small-signal voltage gain (CS amp) | V/V (unitless) |
| 14 | $V_{th} = V_{th0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert} \right)$ | **Body effect** on threshold voltage | V |

Where the parameters are:
- $\mu_n$ = electron mobility in the channel ≈ **500–1350 cm²/(V·s)** (process dependent)
- $C_{ox}$ = gate oxide capacitance per unit area = $\varepsilon_{ox} / t_{ox}$ ≈ a few fF/µm²
- $W$ = channel width, $L$ = channel length
- $\lambda$ = channel-length modulation parameter ≈ **0.01 to 0.1 V⁻¹**
- $\gamma$ = body-effect coefficient ≈ **0.3 to 0.8 V^(1/2)**
- $2\phi_F$ = surface potential ≈ **0.6 to 0.9 V**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> MOSFETs are the **workhorse of modern electronics**:
> - In **digital ICs** (microprocessors, memory): billions of MOSFETs act as nanosecond switches.
> - In **analog ICs** (op-amps like µA741, 555 timers, ADCs): matched MOSFET pairs form input differential stages, current mirrors, and active loads.
> - In **power electronics**: power MOSFETs switch kilowatts at megahertz speeds.
> - In **RF design**: MOSFETs provide low-noise amplification up to tens of GHz.
> - The **µA741 op-amp** (a KTU textbook classic) contains 24 BJTs but its modern CMOS counterparts (e.g., LM358, TL082) use MOSFETs exclusively.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: I-V Relationship in the Triode Region

**Starting premise:** We model the channel as a voltage-controlled resistor. The inversion-layer charge per unit area at position $x$ along the channel is:

$$Q(x) = -C_{ox} \left[ V_{GS} - V_{th} - V(x) \right]$$

where $V(x)$ is the channel potential at point $x$ (with $V(0) = 0$ at the source, $V(L) = V_{DS}$ at the drain). The negative sign indicates electron charge.

**Step 1 — Express the incremental resistance:**
The channel has cross-sectional area $W \cdot t_{ch}$ (width × inversion-layer thickness), and length $dx$. The resistance of an infinitesimal slice is:

$$dR = \dfrac{dx}{\mu_n \cdot \vert Q(x) \vert \cdot W} = \dfrac{dx}{\mu_n C_{ox} W \left[ V_{GS} - V_{th} - V(x) \right]}$$

**Step 2 — Voltage drop across the slice:**
By Ohm's law, the voltage drop across the slice is $dV = I_D \cdot dR$. Therefore:

$$dV = I_D \cdot \dfrac{dx}{\mu_n C_{ox} W \left[ V_{GS} - V_{th} - V(x) \right]}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-multiply and integrate:**

$$I_D \, dx = \mu_n C_{ox} W \left[ V_{GS} - V_{th} - V(x) \right] dV$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from source ($x=0$, $V=0$) to drain ($x=L$, $V=V_{DS}$):**

$$\int_{0}^{L} I_D \, dx = \int_{0}^{V_{DS}} \mu_n C_{ox} W \left[ V_{GS} - V_{th} - V(x) \right] dV$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the left integral (constant current):**

$$I_D \cdot L = \mu_n C_{ox} W \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{th}) V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^{2}}{2} \right]$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $I_D$:**

$$\boxed{I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{th}) V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^{2}}{2} \right]}$$

This is the **complete triode-region I-V equation** of the long-channel NMOSFET.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Saturation Current (Pinch-off Condition)

**Starting premise:** The triode equation is valid only while the inversion charge $Q(x)$ remains positive everywhere along the channel. The channel disappears at the drain end when:

$$V_{GS} - V_{th} - V_{DS} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the pinch-off voltage into the triode equation:**

$$I_{D,sat} = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{th})(V_{GS} - V_{th}) - \dfrac{(V_{GS} - V_{th})^{2}}{2} \right]$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the bracket:**

$$I_{D,sat} = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{th})^{2} - \dfrac{(V_{GS} - V_{th})^{2}}{2} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Factor out $(V_{GS} - V_{th})^{2}$:**

$$I_{D,sat} = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} \cdot \dfrac{(V_{GS} - V_{th})^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Final compact form:**

$$\boxed{I_{D,sat} = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^{2} = \dfrac{1}{2} k_n (V_{GS} - V_{th})^{2}}$$

This is the **square-law** characteristic of the MOSFET in saturation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Transconductance $g_m$

**Starting premise:** Transconductance is the small-signal gain parameter, defined as the sensitivity of drain current to gate-source voltage at a fixed bias point:

$$g_m \equiv \left. \dfrac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} \right|_{V_{DS} = \text{const}}$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate the saturation current with respect to $V_{GS}$:**

$$g_m = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial V_{GS}} \left[ \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^{2} \right]$$

**Step 2 — Apply the power rule:**

$$g_m = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} \cdot 2 (V_{GS} - V_{th}) \cdot 1$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$\boxed{g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th}) = k_n' \dfrac{W}{L} \cdot V_{ov}}$$

**Step 4 — Alternative form using bias current:**
Substitute $V_{GS} - V_{th} = \sqrt{2 I_D / (k_n' W/L)}$ into the $g_m$ expression:

$$g_m = k_n' \dfrac{W}{L} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 I_D}{k_n' W/L}}$$

$$g_m = \sqrt{2 k_n' \dfrac{W}{L} \cdot I_D}$$

**Step 5 — Final form using $I_D$ directly:**

$$\boxed{g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} \cdot I_D}}$$

**Key insight:** $g_m$ is **proportional to $\sqrt{I_D}$** — doubling the bias current increases $g_m$ by only $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$. This is the fundamental trade-off in MOSFET amplifier design: more bias current → more gain, but also more power dissipation.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Channel Length Modulation and Output Resistance

**Starting premise:** In an ideal long-channel MOSFET, the saturation current is independent of $V_{DS}$. In a real (short-channel) device, increasing $V_{DS}$ shortens the effective channel length from $L$ to $L - \Delta L$, which slightly increases the current.

**Step 1 — Model the channel-length reduction:**
The empirical model is $\Delta L \propto V_{DS}$, leading to the modified saturation current:

$$I_{D,sat} = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L - \Delta L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the small-perturbation approximation** $\Delta L \ll L$:

$$\dfrac{1}{L - \Delta L} \approx \dfrac{1}{L} (1 + \lambda V_{DS})$$

where $\lambda$ is the channel-length modulation parameter (units V⁻¹).

**Step 3 — Substitute back:**

$$\boxed{I_{D,sat} = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^{2} (1 + \lambda V_{DS})}$$

**Step 4 — Derive the output resistance:**
By definition, $r_o$ is the inverse slope of the $I_D$–$V_{DS}$ curve in saturation:

$$r_o = \left. \dfrac{\partial V_{DS}}{\partial I_D} \right|_{V_{GS}} = \dfrac{1}{\partial I_D / \partial V_{DS}}$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate the CLM equation:**

$$\dfrac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{DS}} = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^{2} \cdot \lambda = \lambda \cdot I_{D,sat}^{\text{ideal}}$$

**Step 6 — Invert to get $r_o$:**

$$\boxed{r_o = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D}}$$

**Physical insight:** A larger $\lambda$ (shorter channel) means the saturation current rises more steeply with $V_{DS}$, giving a **lower $r_o$** and thus a **lower intrinsic gain** $g_m r_o$. This is the principal reason that analog IC design favors longer-channel transistors for high-gain stages.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Body Effect on Threshold Voltage

**Starting premise:** When the source-to-body voltage $V_{SB} > 0$ (source at higher potential than the body), the body-source junction becomes reverse-biased, widening the depletion region beneath the channel. This additional depletion charge must be compensated by the gate, effectively raising $V_{th}$.

**Step 1 — Total depletion charge per unit area:**

$$Q_B = \sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}$$

**Step 2 — Threshold voltage definition:**
$V_{th}$ is the gate voltage needed to make the surface potential equal $2\phi_F$ and to support the depletion charge:

$$V_{th} = V_{th0} + \dfrac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A}}{C_{ox}} \left[ \sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Define the body-effect coefficient:**

$$\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A}}{C_{ox}}$$

**Step 4 — Final compact form:**

$$\boxed{V_{th} = V_{th0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert} \right)}$$

where $V_{th0}$ is the threshold voltage at $V_{SB} = 0$.

**Engineering consequence:** In an IC, the body is usually tied to ground (or to the most negative supply), so different NMOS transistors can have different source voltages — leading to **different effective $V_{th}$ values** for otherwise identical devices. This is the **body effect**, and it is a major source of design complexity in analog ICs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MOSFET Physical Cross-Section (NMOS Enhancement)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **layered physical construction** of an n-channel enhancement MOSFET, mapping the spatial relationship of the four terminals and the inversion layer.

```mermaid
graph TD
    gateA["GATE TERMINAL - Metal or Polysilicon"] --- oxideA["SiO2 Gate Oxide - Insulating Layer"]
    oxideA --- channelA["Inversion Channel - n-type layer induced by VGS"]
    channelA --- sourceA["SOURCE n+ Region"]
    channelA --- drainA["DRAIN n+ Region"]
    sourceA --- bodyA["p-type Substrate BODY BULK"]
    drainA --- bodyA
    bodyA --- contactA["BODY CONTACT tied to ground or VSS"]
    sourceA --- srcA["SOURCE TERMINAL S"]
    drainA --- drnA["DRAIN TERMINAL D"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Top to bottom: Gate metal → thin SiO₂ → p-substrate.
- The **Source** and **Drain** are n⁺ diffusions on either side of the gate.
- The induced n-channel forms a thin sheet beneath the oxide, connecting S to D when $V_{GS} > V_{th}$.

## 4.2 MOSFET Operating Regions — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    inputA["VGS and VDS applied to MOSFET"] --> decideA{"Is VGS greater than Vth?"}
    decideA -- NO --> regionA["REGION 1 - CUTOFF - ID equals 0 A - Open Switch"]
    decideA -- YES --> decideB{"Is VDS less than VGS minus Vth?"}
    decideB -- YES --> regionB["REGION 2 - TRIODE - ID depends on VGS and VDS - Voltage Controlled Resistor"]
    decideB -- NO --> regionC["REGION 3 - SATURATION - ID depends mainly on VGS - Constant Current Source"]
    regionB --> appA["Used in - Analog Switches - Variable Resistors - Pass Transistors"]
    regionC --> appB["Used in - Amplifier Stages - Current Sources - Active Loads"]
    regionA --> appC["Used in - Digital Logic OFF State - Power Switching"]
```

## 4.3 Small-Signal Equivalent Circuit (Hybrid-π Model)

```mermaid
graph TD
    gndA["GROUND REFERENCE"] --- roA["ro - Output Resistance - 1 over lambda times ID"]
    roA --- nodeA["Drain Node D"]
    gsA["Gate Node G"] --- gmA["gm times vgs - Dependent Current Source"]
    gmA --- nodeA
    srcA["Source Node S"] --- gndA
    nodeA --- outA["Output Voltage vout"]
```

**Interpretation:**
- $g_m \cdot v_{gs}$ is a **voltage-controlled current source** between drain and source.
- $r_o$ is in **parallel** with the dependent source, modeling the finite output resistance due to channel-length modulation.
- $v_{gs}$ is the controlling small-signal gate-source voltage.
- The **gate draws no DC current** (infinite input resistance) — a key advantage over BJTs.

## 4.4 Common-Source Amplifier — Block-Level Signal Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    inA["Vin AC signal source"] --- cinA["Cin coupling capacitor blocks DC"]
    cinA --- gateB["Gate of NMOS - high input impedance"]
    gateB --- devA["NMOS Transistor in Saturation Region"]
    devA --- outNode["Drain Node - output taken here"]
    outNode --- routA["Rout drain resistor to VDD - converts ID to voltage"]
    outNode --- coutA["Cout coupling capacitor blocks DC"]
    coutA --- outB["Vout amplified and inverted AC signal"]
    devA --- srcB["Source tied to ground - common-source configuration"]
```

**Function:** The input AC signal modulates $V_{GS}$, which modulates $I_D$, which develops an amplified, **inverted** output voltage across $R_{out}$ (or $R_D$).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

> **Q: With the help of a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of an n-channel enhancement-type MOSFET. Mention the role of the SiO₂ layer.**

**Model Answer:**

An n-channel enhancement MOSFET is a four-terminal device constructed on a **p-type silicon substrate**. Two heavily doped **n⁺ regions** are formed in the substrate to serve as the **Source (S)** and **Drain (D)** terminals. A thin layer of **silicon dioxide (SiO₂)** is thermally grown between the source and drain regions, and a **metal or polysilicon Gate (G)** electrode is deposited on top of this oxide. The fourth terminal, the **Body (B)**, is the p-type substrate contact.

**Working Principle:**
When the gate-to-source voltage $V_{GS} = 0$, no conducting channel exists between source and drain, and the device is in **cutoff**. When $V_{GS}$ is increased and exceeds the **threshold voltage $V_{th}$**, the vertical electric field from the gate repels holes from the surface and attracts minority-carrier electrons, forming a thin **inversion layer** (n-channel) just beneath the oxide. This channel connects the n⁺ source to the n⁺ drain. A drain-to-source voltage $V_{DS}$ then drives electrons from source to drain, producing drain current $I_D$.

**Role of SiO₂:**
The SiO₂ layer serves two critical functions:
1. It **insulates the gate from the channel**, so the gate draws essentially **zero DC current** (input resistance > 10¹² Ω). This makes MOSFETs ideal for high-input-impedance amplifiers.
2. The thin oxide (typically **5–20 nm** in modern processes) allows the gate field to penetrate and **modulate the channel charge** efficiently.

The magnitude of $V_{th}$ for an enhancement NMOS typically lies in the range **0.3 V to 1.5 V**, depending on doping, oxide thickness, and body bias.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **Q: Define the threshold voltage of a MOSFET. Explain the body effect.**

**Model Answer:**

The **threshold voltage $V_{th}$** of a MOSFET is defined as the minimum gate-to-source voltage $V_{GS}$ required to create (or strongly invert) a conducting channel at the surface of the semiconductor beneath the gate oxide. For an n-channel enhancement device, the condition for channel formation is:

$$V_{GS} \geq V_{th}$$

When $V_{GS} = V_{th}$, the surface of the p-type substrate becomes inverted, forming a thin n-type layer that electrically connects the source and drain. Typical values for a standard CMOS process lie between **0.5 V and 1.0 V**.

**Body Effect:**
The body effect (also called the **back-gate effect** or **substrate-bias effect**) describes the dependence of $V_{th}$ on the **source-to-body voltage $V_{SB}$**. In a discrete MOSFET, the body is shorted to the source ($V_{SB} = 0$), so $V_{th}$ is fixed. In an integrated circuit, however, the body of every NMOS is typically tied to the most negative supply (ground), while the sources of different transistors may sit at different potentials. A reverse-biased source-body junction widens the depletion region beneath the channel, requiring a larger gate voltage to form the inversion layer. The mathematical model is:

$$V_{th} = V_{th0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert} \right)$$

where $\gamma$ is the **body-effect coefficient** (typically **0.3 to 0.8 V^(1/2)**) and $2\phi_F \approx$ **0.6 to 0.9 V** is the surface potential. The body effect is undesirable in analog design because it makes the threshold voltage (and hence the bias current) depend on the signal swing at the source.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Note:** Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, every Part B question has internal choice. **Attempt ANY ONE** of the two alternatives.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

> **Q: (a)** Derive the I–V characteristics of an enhancement-type NMOS in the **triode region** and the **saturation region**. Clearly state the boundary condition between the two regions. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** An NMOS transistor has $\mu_n C_{ox} = 100 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2$, $W/L = 10$, and $V_{th} = 0.8\,\text{V}$. It is biased at $V_{GS} = 2.0\,\text{V}$ and $V_{DS} = 1.5\,\text{V}$. Calculate:
> - (i) The overdrive voltage $V_{ov}$,
> - (ii) The drain current $I_D$ (check the region of operation),
> - (iii) The transconductance $g_m$,
> - (iv) The output resistance $r_o$ assuming $\lambda = 0.02\,\text{V}^{-1}$. (7 marks)

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

The complete derivation is presented in **Section 3.1 and 3.2** of these notes. The key results are:

**Triode region** ($V_{GS} > V_{th}$ and $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{th}$):

$$I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{th}) V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^{2}}{2} \right]$$

**Boundary condition** between triode and saturation:

$$V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$$

**Saturation region** ($V_{GS} > V_{th}$ and $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{th}$):

$$I_{D,sat} = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^{2}$$

The derivation involves:
- Writing the channel charge per unit area: $Q(x) = -C_{ox}[V_{GS} - V_{th} - V(x)]$
- Expressing the incremental resistance of an elemental slice of the channel
- Integrating from source ($x=0$, $V=0$) to drain ($x=L$, $V=V_{DS}$)
- Substituting the pinch-off condition $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$ to obtain the saturation equation

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write the saturation formula as $I_D = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})$ (a **linear** dependence) instead of the correct **square-law** form. This loses 2–3 marks instantly. **Always square the overdrive voltage.**

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Given data:**
- $\mu_n C_{ox} = 100 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2 = 100 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{A/V}^2$
- $W/L = 10$
- $V_{th} = 0.8\,\text{V}$
- $V_{GS} = 2.0\,\text{V}$
- $V_{DS} = 1.5\,\text{V}$
- $\lambda = 0.02\,\text{V}^{-1}$

**Step 1 — Compute overdrive voltage:**

$$V_{ov} = V_{GS} - V_{th} = 2.0 - 0.8 = 1.2\,\text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Determine region of operation:**

$$V_{DS,sat} = V_{ov} = 1.2\,\text{V}$$

Since $V_{DS} = 1.5\,\text{V} > V_{DS,sat} = 1.2\,\text{V}$, the device operates in the **saturation region**.

**Step 3 — Compute drain current (using saturation formula):**

$$I_D = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^{2}$$

$$I_D = \dfrac{1}{2} \times (100 \times 10^{-6}) \times 10 \times (1.2)^{2}$$

$$I_D = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 10^{-3} \times 1.44 = 0.72 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{A}$$

$$\boxed{I_D = 0.72\,\text{mA} = 720\,\mu\text{A}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute transconductance:**

$$g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})$$

$$g_m = (100 \times 10^{-6}) \times 10 \times 1.2$$

$$g_m = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{S}$$

$$\boxed{g_m = 1.2\,\text{mA/V} = 1.2\,\text{mS}}$$

**Verification using the alternative form:**

$$g_m = \dfrac{2 I_D}{V_{ov}} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.72\,\text{mA}}{1.2\,\text{V}} = 1.2\,\text{mS} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Compute output resistance:**

$$r_o = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \dfrac{1}{0.02 \times 0.72 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$r_o = \dfrac{1}{1.44 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\boxed{r_o \approx 69.44\,\text{k}\Omega}$$

**Step 6 — Intrinsic gain (bonus insight):**

$$A_{v0} = g_m \cdot r_o = (1.2 \times 10^{-3}) \times (69.44 \times 10^{3}) = 83.33\,\text{V/V} \approx 38.4\,\text{dB}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **Q: (a)** Draw the **small-signal hybrid-π equivalent circuit** of a MOSFET in saturation. Derive expressions for the **transconductance $g_m$** and **output resistance $r_o$**, and explain how the **intrinsic voltage gain** $A_{v0} = g_m r_o$ depends on the bias current $I_D$ and channel length $L$. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** For a common-source NMOS amplifier with $R_D = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ as the drain load, the transistor has $\mu_n C_{ox} = 50\,\mu\text{A/V}^2$, $W/L = 20$, $V_{th} = 1.0\,\text{V}$, and $\lambda = 0.01\,\text{V}^{-1}$. The DC bias sets $I_D = 0.5\,\text{mA}$. Determine:
> - (i) The overdrive voltage $V_{ov}$,
> - (ii) The transconductance $g_m$,
> - (iii) The output resistance $r_o$,
> - (iv) The small-signal voltage gain $A_v = -g_m (r_o \,\vert\vert\, R_D)$. (7 marks)

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Small-signal hybrid-π model:**
The MOSFET in saturation, when driven by a small AC signal $v_{gs}$ superimposed on the DC bias, is modeled as follows (also shown in **Section 4.3** of these notes):
- A **voltage-controlled current source** $g_m v_{gs}$ between drain and source.
- An **output resistance** $r_o$ in parallel with the current source, representing the finite slope of the $I_D$–$V_{DS}$ curve in saturation.
- An **open circuit** at the gate (infinite input resistance) and an **open circuit** at the body (if body effect is neglected).

**Derivation of $g_m$** (from Section 3.3):

$$g_m = \left. \dfrac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} \right|_{V_{DS}} = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th}) = k_n' \dfrac{W}{L} V_{ov}$$

Equivalently:

$$g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} I_D} = \sqrt{2 k_n' \dfrac{W}{L} I_D}$$

**Derivation of $r_o$** (from Section 3.4):

$$r_o = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D}$$

**Intrinsic gain:**

$$A_{v0} = g_m \cdot r_o = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} I_D} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D}$$

$$A_{v0} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \mu_n C_{ox}}{\text{W/L}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{I_D}}$$

$$A_{v0} = \dfrac{1}{I_D} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \mu_n C_{ox} W/L}{\lambda^{2}}}$$

**Dependencies:**
- $A_{v0}$ is **inversely proportional to $\sqrt{I_D}$** — increasing bias current *reduces* the intrinsic gain. This is a key reason analog stages are biased at low currents.
- $A_{v0}$ is **inversely proportional to $\sqrt{W/L}$** — narrower, longer devices give higher gain.
- $A_{v0}$ is **inversely proportional to $\lambda$**, and $\lambda$ itself is **inversely proportional to $L$**. Therefore, **doubling the channel length $L$ quadruples the intrinsic gain** ($A_{v0} \propto L$ for fixed $I_D$ and $W/L$).
- This is the fundamental reason that **analog IC transistors are deliberately made long** (large $L$) to maximize gain, even at the cost of silicon area and speed.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write $A_v = g_m r_o \cdot g_m R_D$ or compute $A_v$ as a product of independent gains. The correct expression is the **parallel combination** of $r_o$ and $R_D$ (or $R_L$) as the effective load, multiplied by $g_m$. Memorize $A_v = -g_m (r_o \parallel R_D)$ exactly.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Given data:**
- $R_D = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$
- $\mu_n C_{ox} = 50 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2 = 50 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{A/V}^2$
- $W/L = 20$
- $V_{th} = 1.0\,\text{V}$
- $\lambda = 0.01\,\text{V}^{-1}$
- $I_D = 0.5\,\text{mA} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{A}$

**Step 1 — Overdrive voltage from the saturation current formula:**

$$I_D = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} V_{ov}^{2}$$

$$0.5 \times 10^{-3} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 20 \times V_{ov}^{2}$$

$$0.5 \times 10^{-3} = 500 \times 10^{-6} \times V_{ov}^{2} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times V_{ov}^{2}$$

$$V_{ov}^{2} = 1.0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad V_{ov} = 1.0\,\text{V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{ov} = 1.0\,\text{V}}$$

**Step 2 — Transconductance** (use the form tied to $I_D$ for higher reliability):

$$g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} I_D}$$

$$g_m = \sqrt{2 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 20 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$g_m = \sqrt{2 \times 1000 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$g_m = \sqrt{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{S}$$

$$\boxed{g_m = 1.0\,\text{mA/V} = 1.0\,\text{mS}}$$

**Verification using the overdrive form:**

$$g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} V_{ov} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 20 \times 1.0 = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{S} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Output resistance:**

$$r_o = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \dfrac{1}{0.01 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{1}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 200\,\text{k}\Omega$$

$$\boxed{r_o = 200\,\text{k}\Omega}$$

**Step 4 — Effective load and voltage gain:**

First compute the parallel combination $r_o \parallel R_D$:

$$r_o \parallel R_D = \dfrac{200 \times 10^{3} \times 10 \times 10^{3}}{200 \times 10^{3} + 10 \times 10^{3}} = \dfrac{2000 \times 10^{6}}{210 \times 10^{3}}$$

$$r_o \parallel R_D = 9.524\,\text{k}\Omega$$

Now compute the small-signal voltage gain:

$$A_v = -g_m \cdot (r_o \parallel R_D) = -(1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times (9.524 \times 10^{3})$$

$$\boxed{A_v \approx -9.52\,\text{V/V}}$$

The negative sign indicates a **180° phase inversion** between input and output, characteristic of a common-source amplifier.

**Step 5 — Convert to decibels (bonus, for engineering practice):**

$$A_v \text{ in dB} = 20 \log_{10}(9.52) \approx 19.6\,\text{dB}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A common mistake is forgetting to **include $r_o$ in parallel with $R_D$** when computing the gain. The total effective load is $R_D \parallel r_o$, not $R_D$ alone. Forgetting $r_o$ gives $A_v = -g_m R_D = -10\,\text{V/V}$ — close, but **inaccurate**, and a 1-mark deduction is typical in the KTU valuation key.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for MOSFETs (Module 1, PCEBT402)**

- **Device type:** MOSFET = **voltage-controlled, unipolar** device with **insulated gate** (SiO₂). Gate current is essentially zero (input resistance > 10¹² Ω).
- **Two main types:** **NMOS** (n-channel on p-substrate, electrons as carriers) and **PMOS** (p-channel on n-substrate, holes as carriers). KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes **enhancement-mode NMOS**.
- **Three operating regions:**
  1. **Cutoff:** $V_{GS} < V_{th}$ → $I_D = 0$ (open switch).
  2. **Triode (Ohmic):** $V_{GS} > V_{th}$ and $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{th}$ → device acts as a **voltage-controlled resistor**.
  3. **Saturation (Active):** $V_{GS} > V_{th}$ and $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{th}$ → device acts as a **voltage-controlled current source**.
- **Boundary condition (pinch-off):** $V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{th} = V_{ov}$.
- **Square-law in saturation:** $I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^{2}$. **Always square** the overdrive voltage — never linearize.
- **Transconductance key formulas:**
  - $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th}) = k_n' \frac{W}{L} V_{ov}$
  - $g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} I_D}$
  - $g_m = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{ov}}$
- **Channel Length Modulation (CLM):** real MOSFETs have a finite output resistance $r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D}$, where $\lambda \propto 1/L$.
- **Body effect:** $V_{th}$ increases with $V_{SB}$ per $V_{th} = V_{th0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert} \right)$.
- **Common-source voltage gain:** $A_v = -g_m (r_o \parallel R_D)$ — note the **parallel combination**, not just $R_D$.
- **Intrinsic gain $g_m r_o$:** inversely proportional to $\sqrt{I_D}$ and directly proportional to $L$ — analog stages prefer **long, low-current** transistors.
- **Symbols and units:** $\mu_n C_{ox}$ in A/V², $C_{ox} = \varepsilon_{ox}/t_{ox}$ in F/m², $W/L$ dimensionless, $\lambda$ in V⁻¹, $g_m$ in A/V (S), $r_o$ in Ω, $I_D$ in A.
- **Key KTU mistakes to avoid:**
  1. Writing $I_D$ as linear (not squared) in $V_{ov}$ — **always square**.
  2. Forgetting to check **region of operation** before applying a formula.
  3. Ignoring $r_o$ in the gain calculation.
  4. Confusing $V_{th}$ (DC threshold) with $V_{ov}$ (overdrive).
  5. Treating the gate as drawing current — **it does not** (that's the whole point of MOSFETs).

> **End of Module 1 — MOSFETs Notes | PCEBT402 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operational Amplifiers: Introduction to Operational Amplifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT402 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Operational Amplifiers: Introduction to Operational Amplifiers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)** is a direct-coupled, high-gain electronic voltage amplifier with differential inputs and a single-ended output, designed to be used with external feedback components such as resistors and capacitors. The name "operational" originates from its original use in analog computers to perform mathematical operations such as addition, subtraction, integration, and differentiation.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:**
> An op-amp is a multi-stage, directly coupled amplifier available as a monolithic integrated circuit (IC). It accepts two input voltages (inverting $V^-$ and non-inverting $V^+$) and produces a single output voltage $V_{out}$ that is an amplified version of the difference between them:
> $$V_{out} = A_{OL}(V^+ - V^-)$$
> where $A_{OL}$ is the open-loop voltage gain.

## 1.2 Standard Circuit Symbol

The op-amp is represented by a **triangular symbol** with two inputs and one output:

- **Inverting input ($V^-$ or $V_1$):** Marked with a minus sign ($-$); output is inverted relative to this input.
- **Non-inverting input ($V^+$ or $V_2$):** Marked with a plus sign ($+$); output is in phase with this input.
- **Output ($V_{out}$):** Located at the apex of the triangle.
- **Power supply pins ($+V_{CC}$ and $-V_{EE}$):** Usually not drawn on the symbol but are mandatory for operation.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lever of Electronics"

Imagine an **op-amp as a mechanical lever of the electronics world**:

- A tiny push on either side (a few microvolts at the input) can lift a massive weight (a few volts at the output).
- Just as a lever amplifies **force**, the op-amp amplifies **voltage**.
- However, like a stiff lever that needs anchors (fulcrum), the op-amp needs **feedback networks** (resistors/capacitors) to control how much it amplifies and to keep it stable.

This is why op-amps are rarely used without feedback — the open-loop gain is so enormous (typically **200,000** or $10^5$ to $10^6$) that without feedback, even the tiniest noise would saturate the output to the supply rails ($\pm V_{sat}$).

## 1.4 Key Conceptual Block Diagram

Conceptually, an op-amp is a **three-port device**:

| Port | Symbol | Function |
|------|--------|----------|
| Inverting Input | $V^-$ | Produces $180^\circ$ phase-shifted output |
| Non-inverting Input | $V^+$ | Produces in-phase output |
| Output | $V_{out}$ | Delivers amplified difference signal |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Differential"?**
> The op-amp amplifies the **difference** $(V^+ - V^-)$, not either input alone. This is the foundation of its ability to reject common-mode noise — a property quantified by the **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Transfer Equation

The single most important equation governing op-amp behavior is:

$$V_{out} = A_{OL}\,(V^+ - V^-)$$

where:
- $A_{OL}$ = **Open-loop differential voltage gain** (dimensionless, typically $10^5$ to $10^6$)
- $(V^+ - V^-)$ = **Differential input voltage** $V_d$ (in volts)
- $V_{out}$ = **Output voltage** (in volts), bounded by $\pm V_{sat}$

## 2.2 Ideal vs. Practical Op-Amp Characteristics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently tests the difference between ideal and practical op-amp parameters.** Memorize this table — it appears in nearly every semester exam.

| Parameter | Ideal Value | Practical (e.g., $\mu$A741) | Units |
|-----------|-------------|------------------------------|-------|
| Open-loop gain ($A_{OL}$) | $\infty$ | $2 \times 10^5$ | V/V |
| Input impedance ($Z_{in}$) | $\infty$ | $2 \times 10^6$ | $\Omega$ |
| Output impedance ($Z_{out}$) | $0$ | $75$ | $\Omega$ |
| Input bias current ($I_B$) | $0$ | $80$ | nA |
| Input offset voltage ($V_{io}$) | $0$ | $2$ | mV |
| Bandwidth ($BW$) | $\infty$ | $1$ (with unity gain) | MHz |
| CMRR | $\infty$ | $90$ | dB |
| Slew Rate ($SR$) | $\infty$ | $0.5$ | V/$\mu$s |
| Power Supply Rejection Ratio | $\infty$ | $76$ | dB |

## 2.3 DC Parameters — Detailed Explanation

### (a) Input Bias Current ($I_B$)
The **average** of the DC currents flowing into both input terminals of the op-amp when the output is at zero volts.

$$I_B = \frac{I_{B1} + I_{B2}}{2}$$

- **Cause:** Biasing of internal transistor base terminals in the input differential stage requires small currents.
- **Typical value:** $80\,\text{nA}$ for $\mu$A741.

### (b) Input Offset Current ($I_{io}$)
The **difference** between the two input bias currents:

$$I_{io} = \vert I_{B1} - I_{B2} \vert$$

- **Typical value:** $20\,\text{nA}$ for $\mu$A741.
- **Significance:** Causes output offset voltage even when both inputs are grounded.

### (c) Input Offset Voltage ($V_{io}$)
The small DC voltage that must be applied between the input terminals to make the output exactly **zero**.

$$V_{io} = \frac{V_{out(offset)}}{A_{OL}}$$

- **Typical value:** $2\,\text{mV}$ for $\mu$A741.
- **Significance:** $V_{io}$ is the most practical DC error parameter — it is multiplied by the closed-loop gain.

## 2.4 AC Parameters — Detailed Explanation

### (a) Open-Loop Voltage Gain ($A_{OL}$)
The gain of the op-amp with **no feedback** applied. Expressed in dB:

$$A_{OL(dB)} = 20\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in(differential)}}\right)$$

For $\mu$A741: $A_{OL(dB)} = 20\log_{10}(2 \times 10^5) \approx \mathbf{106\,\text{dB}}$.

### (b) Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)
Measures the ability to reject signals common to **both** inputs:

$$\text{CMRR} = \frac{A_d}{A_c} \quad\text{or in dB:}\quad \text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$$

where $A_d$ = differential gain and $A_c$ = common-mode gain.

### (c) Slew Rate (SR)
The maximum rate of change of output voltage with respect to time:

$$SR = \left.\frac{dV_{out}}{dt}\right|_{max}$$

- **Typical value:** $0.5\,\text{V/}\mu\text{s}$ for $\mu$A741.
- **Significance:** Limits the maximum usable frequency for large-signal applications.

### (d) Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP)
A constant for a given op-amp, equal to the frequency at which the gain drops to unity:

$$\text{GBP} = A_{CL} \times f_c = \text{constant}$$

where $f_c$ is the closed-loop $-3\,\text{dB}$ bandwidth.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Op-amps are the **universal building blocks** of analog electronics. They are used in:

- **Signal conditioning** in instrumentation (sensors, medical equipment like ECG/EKG)
- **Active filters** (low-pass, high-pass, band-pass, band-stop)
- **Comparators and Schmitt triggers** in digital interface circuits
- **Oscillators and waveform generators** (function generators, audio oscillators)
- **Voltage regulators and precision rectifiers** in power electronics
- **ADC/DAC buffer amplifiers** in data acquisition systems
- **Audio pre-amplifiers and mixers** in entertainment systems

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU focuses on this topic:** Every analog IC design — from simple amplifiers to complex filters — starts with an understanding of op-amp fundamentals. Mastery here directly enables the remaining modules (filters, oscillators, comparators, timers).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Closed-Loop Gain from the Open-Loop Equation

Consider an op-amp with **negative feedback** through a resistor network. Let the feedback fraction be $\beta$ (a dimensionless ratio determined by external resistors). We will derive the closed-loop gain.

### Step 1: Define the input signal

Let the differential input be the difference between the two inputs. For a non-inverting amplifier, $V_{in}$ is applied at $V^+$ and the feedback voltage appears at $V^-$:

$$V_d = V^+ - V^- = V_{in} - \beta V_{out}$$

### Step 2: Apply the open-loop equation

Substitute into the fundamental op-amp equation:

$$V_{out} = A_{OL}\,(V_{in} - \beta V_{out})$$

### Step 3: Rearrange algebraically

Expand and collect $V_{out}$ terms on one side:

$$V_{out} = A_{OL}V_{in} - A_{OL}\beta V_{out}$$

$$V_{out} + A_{OL}\beta V_{out} = A_{OL}V_{in}$$

$$V_{out}\,(1 + A_{OL}\beta) = A_{OL}V_{in}$$

### Step 4: Solve for closed-loop gain

$$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = A_{CL} = \frac{A_{OL}}{1 + A_{OL}\beta}$$

### Step 5: Apply the ideal assumption $A_{OL}\beta \gg 1$

For an ideal op-amp with infinite open-loop gain:

$$A_{CL} \approx \frac{A_{OL}}{A_{OL}\beta} = \frac{1}{\beta}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Result:** When $A_{OL} \to \infty$, the closed-loop gain becomes **independent of the op-amp's internal parameters** and depends **only on the external feedback network**. This is the cornerstone of all op-amp circuit design.

## 3.2 Derivation: Output Offset Voltage Due to $V_{io}$

Given an input offset voltage $V_{io}$ at the inputs, the resulting output offset in a non-inverting amplifier with closed-loop gain $A_{CL}$ is:

### Step 1: Effective input is $V_{io}$

$$V_d^{eff} = V_{io}$$

### Step 2: Apply closed-loop gain

$$V_{out(offset)} = A_{CL} \times V_{io}$$

### Step 3: Substitute $A_{CL} = 1/\beta$

$$V_{out(offset)} = \frac{V_{io}}{\beta}$$

**Numerical example (KTU-style):** If $V_{io} = 2\,\text{mV}$ and feedback fraction $\beta = 0.01$ (gain = 100):

$$V_{out(offset)} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{0.01} = 0.2\,\text{V}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Slew Rate Limitation on Maximum Frequency

For a sinusoid $V_{out}(t) = V_p \sin(2\pi f t)$, the maximum rate of change occurs at zero crossings:

$$\left.\frac{dV_{out}}{dt}\right|_{max} = 2\pi f V_p$$

Setting this equal to the slew rate:

$$2\pi f_{max} V_p = SR$$

Solving for the maximum undistorted frequency:

$$f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_p}$$

**Numerical example:** For $SR = 0.5\,\text{V/}\mu\text{s}$ and peak output $V_p = 5\,\text{V}$:

$$f_{max} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6}{2\pi \times 5} \approx 15.9\,\text{kHz}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Summary Table of Key Equations

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $V_{out} = A_{OL}(V^+ - V^-)$ | Open-loop output | V |
| 2 | $A_{CL} = \dfrac{A_{OL}}{1 + A_{OL}\beta}$ | Closed-loop gain | V/V |
| 3 | $A_{CL} \approx \dfrac{1}{\beta}$ | Ideal closed-loop gain | V/V |
| 4 | $I_B = \dfrac{I_{B1} + I_{B2}}{2}$ | Input bias current | A |
| 5 | $I_{io} = \vert I_{B1} - I_{B2} \vert$ | Input offset current | A |
| 6 | $V_{out(offset)} = \dfrac{V_{io}}{\beta}$ | Output offset voltage | V |
| 7 | $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$ | Common-mode rejection | dB |
| 8 | $f_{max} = \dfrac{SR}{2\pi V_p}$ | Slew-rate limited frequency | Hz |
| 9 | $\text{GBP} = A_{CL} \cdot f_c$ | Gain-bandwidth product | Hz |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Inside an Op-Amp IC

The following Mermaid diagram maps the internal stage-by-stage functional architecture of a typical monolithic op-amp (e.g., $\mu$A741):

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Differential Amplifier Stage] --> B[High Gain Intermediate Stage]
    B --> C[Level Shifter and Bias Circuit]
    C --> D[Output Driver Push Pull Stage]
    D --> E[Vout Single Ended Output]
    F[V Plus Non Inverting Input] --> A
    G[V Minus Inverting Input] --> A
    H[Plus VCC Positive Supply Rail] --> D
    I[Minus VEE Negative Supply Rail] --> D
    J[External Feedback Network Beta] -.-> G
    J -.-> E
```

## 4.2 Equivalent Circuit Model of a Non-Ideal Op-Amp

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[Input V Plus] --> RIN1[Input Resistance Rin]
    RIN1 --> G[Voltage Controlled Voltage Source A OL times Vd]
    IN2[Input V Minus] --> RIN2[Input Resistance Rin]
    RIN2 --> G
    G --> RO[Output Resistance Rout]
    RO --> OUT[Output Vout]
    VB[Input Offset Voltage Source Vio] --> IN2
    IB1[Input Bias Current IB1] --> IN1
    IB2[Input Bias Current IB2] --> IN2
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Flowchart — Solving an Op-Amp Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start Identify Given Parameters] --> S2{Is Feedback Path Present}
    S2 -- Yes Closed Loop --> S3[Determine Feedback Fraction Beta from Resistor Network]
    S3 --> S4[Apply A CL equals 1 over Beta for Ideal Op Amp]
    S4 --> S5[Compute Output Voltage]
    S2 -- No Open Loop --> S6[Check Input Difference and Saturation Limits]
    S6 --> S7[Apply V out equals A OL times Vd then Clamp to plus minus V sat]
    S5 --> END[Final Answer with Units]
    S7 --> END
```

## 4.4 Standard IC Pinout Reference (DIP-8 Package for $\mu$A741)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pin 1 Offset Null] --> P2[Pin 2 Inverting Input V Minus]
    P2 --> P3[Pin 3 Non Inverting Input V Plus]
    P3 --> P4[Pin 4 VEE Negative Supply]
    P4 --> P5[Pin 5 Offset Null]
    P5 --> P6[Pin 6 Output Vout]
    P6 --> P7[Pin 7 VCC Positive Supply]
    P7 --> P8[Pin 8 NC No Connection]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Learning Aid:** Memorize the DIP-8 pinout. In KTU lab exams, students are often asked to identify pin functions on a physical IC. The $\mu$A741 is the most commonly tested device.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define an ideal operational amplifier. List any four ideal characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**

An ideal operational amplifier is a hypothetical amplifier with infinite parameters that perfectly amplifies the difference between its two input voltages.

The four ideal characteristics are:

1. **Infinite open-loop voltage gain** ($A_{OL} = \infty$)
2. **Infinite input impedance** ($Z_{in} = \infty$) — draws no current from the source
3. **Zero output impedance** ($Z_{out} = 0$) — delivers any current without voltage drop
4. **Infinite bandwidth** — amplifies signals from DC to infinite frequency

Additionally, ideal op-amps have zero output when inputs are equal, zero input offset voltage, zero input bias current, and infinite CMRR.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is meant by slew rate? State its unit and significance.**

**Model Answer:**

Slew rate is defined as the maximum rate of change of output voltage with respect to time.

$$SR = \left.\frac{dV_{out}}{dt}\right|_{max}$$

- **Unit:** Volts per microsecond (V/$\mu$s)
- **Significance:** It limits the maximum operating frequency at which the op-amp can deliver large-amplitude signals without distortion. If the input signal demands a faster rate of change than the slew rate permits, the output becomes triangular instead of sinusoidal (slew-induced distortion).
- **Typical value for $\mu$A741:** $0.5\,\text{V/}\mu\text{s}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the block diagram of an operational amplifier and describe the function of each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the ideal and practical characteristics of the $\mu$A741 op-amp. Define CMRR and Slew Rate. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution for Q-A(a):

The internal block diagram of a typical op-amp consists of **four cascaded stages**:

1. **Input Differential Amplifier Stage:** Provides differential gain, high input impedance, and good common-mode rejection. Uses an emitter-coupled or source-coupled pair of transistors.

2. **High-Gain Intermediate Stage:** A common-emitter or common-source amplifier that provides most of the voltage gain (typically several thousand). Often includes a constant-current source for active loading.

3. **Level Shifter and Bias Circuit:** Translates the DC level of the signal so that the output can swing both positive and negative around 0 V with zero input. Provides stable biasing for all stages.

4. **Output Stage:** A complementary push-pull (Class-AB) emitter follower providing low output impedance and current-driving capability. This is the only stage that sources/sinks significant output current.

#### Solution for Q-A(b):

| Parameter | Ideal | $\mu$A741 Practical |
|-----------|-------|---------------------|
| Open-loop gain $A_{OL}$ | $\infty$ | $2 \times 10^5$ |
| Input impedance $Z_{in}$ | $\infty$ | $2\,\text{M}\Omega$ |
| Output impedance $Z_{out}$ | $0$ | $75\,\Omega$ |
| Bandwidth $BW$ | $\infty$ | $1\,\text{MHz}$ |
| CMRR | $\infty$ | $90\,\text{dB}$ |
| Slew Rate $SR$ | $\infty$ | $0.5\,\text{V/}\mu\text{s}$ |

**CMRR (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio):** The ratio of differential gain to common-mode gain, expressed in dB. It measures the ability of the op-amp to reject signals common to both inputs.

$$\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$$

**Slew Rate:** The maximum rate of change of output voltage per unit time, expressed in V/$\mu$s.

$$SR = \left.\frac{dV_{out}}{dt}\right|_{max}$$

For $\mu$A741: $A_{OL} = 2 \times 10^5 \Rightarrow A_{OL(dB)} = 20\log_{10}(2 \times 10^5) \approx 106\,\text{dB}$.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a circuit diagram, derive the closed-loop voltage gain of a non-inverting amplifier using an op-amp. State the assumption made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an op-amp, the input bias currents are $I_{B1} = 100\,\text{nA}$ and $I_{B2} = 80\,\text{nA}$. Calculate the input bias current $I_B$ and input offset current $I_{io}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution for Q-B(a):

Consider a non-inverting amplifier with input $V_{in}$ applied to $V^+$, and feedback through $R_f$ from output to $V^-$, with $R_1$ from $V^-$ to ground.

**Step 1:** Apply voltage divider rule at the inverting node:

$$V^- = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$$

**Step 2:** Identify feedback fraction $\beta$:

$$\beta = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$$

**Step 3:** Using the virtual short concept ($V^+ = V^- = V_{in}$):

$$V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f} = V_{in}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for closed-loop gain:

$$A_{CL} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

**Assumption made:** Ideal op-amp with infinite open-loop gain ($A_{OL} \to \infty$), infinite input impedance ($i^- = i^+ = 0$), and virtual short ($V^+ = V^-$).

#### Solution for Q-B(b):

Given: $I_{B1} = 100\,\text{nA}$, $I_{B2} = 80\,\text{nA}$

**Step 1 — Input bias current** (average):

$$I_B = \frac{I_{B1} + I_{B2}}{2} = \frac{100 + 80}{2} = 90\,\text{nA}$$

**Step 2 — Input offset current** (absolute difference):

$$I_{io} = \vert I_{B1} - I_{B2} \vert = \vert 100 - 80 \vert = 20\,\text{nA}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Confusing $I_B$ with $I_{io}$:** $I_B$ is the *average*, $I_{io}$ is the *absolute difference*. Marks are deducted if the wrong formula is used.
> 2. **Forgetting absolute value:** $I_{io}$ is always non-negative by convention — students often forget the modulus.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency:** Always express currents in A (or nA/$\mu$A) consistently. Mixing units leads to wrong numerical answers.
> 4. **Open-loop vs. Closed-loop confusion:** In derivation problems, students often jump directly to $A_{CL} = 1/\beta$ without showing the $A_{OL} \to \infty$ assumption — KTU awards partial marks for the assumption statement.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must Memorize:**

- ☐ Op-amp transfer equation: $V_{out} = A_{OL}(V^+ - V^-)$
- ☐ Ideal op-amp has: $A_{OL} = \infty$, $Z_{in} = \infty$, $Z_{out} = 0$, $BW = \infty$, $I_B = 0$
- ☐ Practical $\mu$A741 values: $A_{OL} = 2 \times 10^5$, $SR = 0.5\,\text{V/}\mu\text{s}$, $BW = 1\,\text{MHz}$, $\text{CMRR} = 90\,\text{dB}$
- ☐ Closed-loop gain: $A_{CL} = \dfrac{A_{OL}}{1 + A_{OL}\beta} \approx \dfrac{1}{\beta}$ for ideal case
- ☐ Non-inverting amplifier: $A_{CL} = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$
- ☐ Inverting amplifier: $A_{CL} = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$
- ☐ Input bias current: $I_B = \dfrac{I_{B1} + I_{B2}}{2}$
- ☐ Input offset current: $I_{io} = \vert I_{B1} - I_{B2} \vert$
- ☐ Output offset voltage: $V_{out(offset)} = \dfrac{V_{io}}{\beta}$
- ☐ Slew-rate limited frequency: $f_{max} = \dfrac{SR}{2\pi V_p}$
- ☐ CMRR in dB: $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$
- ☐ Internal stages: Diff Amp → High Gain Stage → Level Shifter → Output Driver
- ☐ DIP-8 pinout for $\mu$A741: Pin 2 = $V^-$, Pin 3 = $V^+$, Pin 6 = Output, Pin 7 = $+V_{CC}$, Pin 4 = $-V_{EE}$
- ☐ Virtual short concept valid only in **closed-loop** with negative feedback
- ☐ Op-amp saturates at $\pm V_{sat}$ which is slightly less than $\pm V_{CC}$

**End of Module 1 — Introduction to Operational Amplifiers**
