## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics of Offset Generation — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Bias Current Flow Path:**
- In an inverting amplifier, the inverting terminal "sees" a Thevenin resistance $R_1 \,\|\, R_f$ to the virtual ground (and the actual circuit ground through these resistors).
- The non-inverting terminal, if left unconnected (or connected to ground through zero resistance), "sees" **0 Ω** to ground.
- Therefore, the bias currents $I_{B+}$ and $I_{B-}$ flow through **asymmetric resistances**.

**Step 2 — Voltage Drop Mismatch:**
- Voltage at inverting input due to $I_{B-}$:
$$V_{-} = -I_{B-} \times (R_1 \,\|\, R_f)$$
- Voltage at non-inverting input due to $I_{B+}$:
$$V_{+} = -I_{B+} \times R_{source,+}$$
- If $R_{source,+} = 0$, then $V_+ = 0$, and there exists a non-zero differential DC voltage $\Delta V = V_+ - V_- \neq 0$.

**Step 3 — Amplification by the Circuit:**
- This tiny DC differential voltage gets amplified by the closed-loop gain of the op-amp circuit, producing a substantial **output offset voltage** $V_{out,offset}$.

**Step 4 — The Compensation Strategy:**
- We choose $R_{OMR}$ such that the resistance seen at the non-inverting terminal equals the resistance seen at the inverting terminal.
$$R_{OMR} = R_1 \,\|\, R_f$$
- Now, even though $I_{B+} \neq I_{B-}$, the **voltage drops become nearly equal**, and the op-amp's common-mode rejection handles the rest.

### 2.2 Why "Common-Mode" Rejection Works Here

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When both inputs see **equal Thevenin resistances**, the bias currents $I_{B+}$ and $I_{B-}$ create a **common-mode DC voltage shift** that appears identically at both inputs. An ideal op-amp rejects all common-mode signals — so this offset-producing voltage is automatically cancelled!

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Math

- **Without OMR:** Output offset voltage is huge (amplified bias current × unbalanced resistance).
- **With OMR:** Output offset voltage is reduced to a tiny value proportional only to the **input offset current** $I_{io}$ (which is much smaller than $I_B$).
- **Reduction factor:** Approximately $I_{io}/I_B$, which can be 10× to 100× improvement.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — MEMORIZE FOR EXAMS**

| # | Configuration | Output Offset Voltage (V_out,offset) | OMR Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Inverting Amplifier (no OMR) | $\left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{io} + I_B \cdot (R_1 \,\|\, R_f)$ | $R_{OMR} = R_1 \,\|\, R_f$ |
| 2 | Inverting Amplifier (with OMR) | $\left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{io} + I_{io} \cdot (R_1 \,\|\, R_f)$ | $R_{OMR} = R_1 \,\|\, R_f$ |
| 3 | Non-Inverting Amplifier (with OMR) | $\left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{io} + I_{io} \cdot (R_1 \,\|\, R_f)$ | $R_{OMR} = R_1 \,\|\, R_f$ |
| 4 | Difference Amplifier (with OMR) | Depends on resistor matching | $R_{OMR} = R_1 \,\|\, R_2 = R_3 \,\|\, R_4$ |
| 5 | Pure Inverter / Integrator | $\dfrac{V_{io}}{1} + I_{B-} \cdot R_f$ | $R_{OMR} = R_f$ |
| 6 | Voltage Follower | $V_{io} + I_{io} \cdot R_s$ | $R_{OMR} = R_s$ (source resistance) |

**Standard Symbol Conventions Used:**
- $V_{io}$ = Input offset voltage of the op-amp (V)
- $I_B$ = Average input bias current = $(I_{B+} + I_{B-})/2$ (A)
- $I_{io}$ = Input offset current = $\mid I_{B+} - I_{B-} \mid$ (A)
- $R_1$ = Input series resistor
- $R_f$ = Feedback resistor
- $R_s$ = Source resistance

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where OMR Networks Are Used in Industry:**

1. **Precision Instrumentation Amplifiers** — Medical ECG/EEG equipment where DC offset can saturate high-gain stages.
2. **Active Filter Networks (Sallen-Key, Multiple Feedback)** — DC offset corrupts the low-frequency response.
3. **High-Gain Photodiode Amplifiers** — Where the source resistance is very high (MΩ range), bias current effects are dominant.
4. **Strain-Gauge Signal Conditioning** — Bridge circuits require balanced input impedances.
5. **Sample-and-Hold / Peak Detectors** — Long-hold capacitors integrate the bias current, causing drift.
6. **Integrator Circuits (Active)** — Capacitor feedback means $R_f \to \infty$, but OMR remains finite.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Output Offset Voltage WITHOUT Offset Minimizing Resistor

**Given:** Standard inverting amplifier with $R_1$ at the input, $R_f$ as feedback, and the non-inverting terminal directly grounded (no OMR).

**Circuit Equations at DC (offset analysis):**

*Step 1 — Apply KCL at the inverting node (V₋):*

The inverting node has three currents:
- Current from source $V_{in}$ through $R_1$
- Current through $R_f$ from output
- Bias current $I_{B-}$ flowing **into** the inverting pin

For offset analysis, we set $V_{in} = 0$ (no signal), and analyze only the DC error contribution:

$$\frac{0 - V_-}{R_1} + \frac{V_{out} - V_-}{R_f} = I_{B-}$$

*Step 2 — Apply the ideal op-amp input condition at the non-inverting terminal:*

With OMR absent and the non-inverting terminal grounded:
$$V_+ = 0 \quad \text{(directly tied to ground)}$$

The bias current $I_{B+}$ flows from ground into the pin, but since the source resistance is zero, it produces **no voltage drop**. So $V_+$ remains at 0.

*Step 3 — Use the golden op-amp rule $V_+ = V_-$:*

This rule applies only to the **signal component**. For the DC bias error, the offset voltage $V_{io}$ appears as an intrinsic input mismatch:

$$V_- - V_+ = V_{io} \implies V_- = V_{io} \quad (\text{since } V_+ = 0)$$

*Step 4 — Substitute $V_- = V_{io}$ into the KCL equation:*

$$\frac{0 - V_{io}}{R_1} + \frac{V_{out} - V_{io}}{R_f} = I_{B-}$$

*Step 5 — Multiply through and isolate $V_{out}$:*

$$-\frac{V_{io}}{R_1} + \frac{V_{out}}{R_f} - \frac{V_{io}}{R_f} = I_{B-}$$

$$\frac{V_{out}}{R_f} = I_{B-} + V_{io}\left(\frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_f}\right)$$

*Step 6 — Multiply by $R_f$:*

$$V_{out} = I_{B-} \cdot R_f + V_{io}\left(\frac{R_f}{R_1} + 1\right)$$

$$V_{out} = I_{B-} \cdot R_f + V_{io} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right)$$

*Step 7 — Express $I_{B-}$ in terms of $I_B$ and $I_{io}$:*

Using the definitions:
$$I_{B-} = I_B - \frac{I_{io}}{2}$$

And recognizing that the Thevenin resistance at the inverting node is $R_1 \,\|\, R_f$, we can also write an equivalent form:

$$\boxed{V_{out,offset} = \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{io} + I_B \cdot (R_1 \,\|\, R_f)}$$

This is the **uncompensated output offset voltage**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Derivation of the OMR Value $R_{OMR} = R_1 \,\|\, R_f$

**Goal:** Find the value of $R_{OMR}$ that, when placed at the non-inverting terminal, minimizes the bias-current-induced output offset.

**Step 1 — Set up the circuit with OMR:**

The non-inverting input now connects to ground through $R_{OMR}$. The voltage at this node is:
$$V_+ = -I_{B+} \cdot R_{OMR}$$

The Thevenin resistance seen at the inverting node (with $V_{in} = 0$ grounded) is:
$$R_{TH,-} = R_1 \,\|\, R_f$$

The voltage at the inverting node is:
$$V_- = -I_{B-} \cdot (R_1 \,\|\, R_f) + V_{io}$$

Wait — more carefully, the offset $V_{io}$ appears between the two internal input transistors. So:
$$V_- - V_+ = V_{io} + (V_{bias, drop\, mismatch})$$

**Step 2 — The Compensation Condition:**

The principle is: make the **DC resistance seen at the + terminal equal to the DC resistance seen at the − terminal**, so that the bias currents produce **identical voltage drops** at both inputs. The op-amp's common-mode rejection then nullifies this common-mode voltage.

$$R_{OMR} = R_{TH,-} = R_1 \,\|\, R_f$$

*Algebraic verification:*

If $R_{OMR} = R_1 \,\|\, R_f$, then:
- Voltage drop at + terminal: $V_{drop,+} = I_{B+} \cdot R_{OMR}$
- Voltage drop at − terminal: $V_{drop,-} = I_{B-} \cdot (R_1 \,\|\, R_f) = I_{B-} \cdot R_{OMR}$

If $I_{B+} = I_{B-} = I_B$ (ideal matched case), then $V_{drop,+} = V_{drop,-}$, and these are **common-mode** voltages that the op-amp rejects.

**Step 3 — The Residual Offset (with practical mismatch):**

In practice, $I_{B+} \neq I_{B-}$. The residual differential voltage is:

$$\Delta V = V_{drop,-} - V_{drop,+} = (I_{B-} - I_{B+}) \cdot R_{OMR} = -I_{io} \cdot R_{OMR}$$

This differential voltage gets amplified by the closed-loop gain:

$$\boxed{V_{out,offset}^{OMR} = \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{io} + I_{io} \cdot (R_1 \,\|\, R_f)}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Quantitative Numerical Example (KTU Typical Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example Problem (with complete step-by-step solution)**

**Problem Statement:**
For an inverting amplifier with $R_1 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $R_f = 100\,\text{k}\Omega$, op-amp parameters are $V_{io} = 2\,\text{mV}$, $I_B = 100\,\text{nA}$, and $I_{io} = 10\,\text{nA}$. Calculate:
- (a) The output offset voltage **without OMR**
- (b) The required OMR value
- (c) The output offset voltage **with OMR**

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Without OMR, using the uncompensated formula:

$$V_{out,offset} = \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{io} + I_B \cdot (R_1 \,\|\, R_f)$$

Compute the gain factor:
$$\left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) = 1 + \frac{100\,\text{k}}{10\,\text{k}} = 1 + 10 = 11$$

Compute the parallel resistance:
$$R_1 \,\|\, R_f = \frac{R_1 \cdot R_f}{R_1 + R_f} = \frac{10\,\text{k} \times 100\,\text{k}}{10\,\text{k} + 100\,\text{k}} = \frac{10^9}{110\,\text{k}} \approx 9.09\,\text{k}\Omega$$

Substitute the values:
$$V_{out,offset} = 11 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} + 100 \times 10^{-9} \times 9.09 \times 10^{3}$$

$$V_{out,offset} = 22 \times 10^{-3} + 909 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$V_{out,offset} = 0.022 + 0.000909$$

$$\boxed{V_{out,offset} \approx 22.91\,\text{mV} \text{ (without OMR)}}$$

**Part (b):** The OMR value:

$$R_{OMR} = R_1 \,\|\, R_f = 9.09\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Part (c):** With OMR, the formula changes to use $I_{io}$ instead of $I_B$:

$$V_{out,offset}^{OMR} = \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{io} + I_{io} \cdot (R_1 \,\|\, R_f)$$

$$V_{out,offset}^{OMR} = 11 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} + 10 \times 10^{-9} \times 9.09 \times 10^{3}$$

$$V_{out,offset}^{OMR} = 22 \times 10^{-3} + 90.9 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$V_{out,offset}^{OMR} = 0.022 + 0.0000909$$

$$\boxed{V_{out,offset}^{OMR} \approx 22.09\,\text{mV} \text{ (with OMR)}}$$

**Reduction Analysis:**
$$\text{Offset Reduction} = \frac{V_{out} - V_{out}^{OMR}}{V_{out}} \times 100\% = \frac{22.91 - 22.09}{22.91} \times 100\% \approx 3.57\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** In this case, the offset reduction is small because the dominant offset source is $V_{io}$ (input offset voltage), not the bias current. The OMR primarily tackles the bias current contribution. For higher-gain amplifiers (e.g., $R_f/R_1 = 1000$), the bias current contribution becomes much more significant, and OMR provides dramatic improvements.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Without OMR | With OMR |
|---|---|---|
| Effective bias current in error term | $I_B$ (full) | $I_{io}$ (residual only) |
| Typical offset (μA741) | ~50 mV | ~5 mV |
| Sensitivity to temperature | High | Low |
| Sensitivity to op-amp replacement | High | Moderate |
| Required additional component | None | One resistor |
| Cost | ₹0 | ₹1 |
| Best for | Low-precision apps | Instrumentation-grade apps |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1. Define the term "Offset Minimizing Resistor" (OMR) in the context of operational amplifiers. State the formula for OMR in a standard inverting amplifier configuration.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **Offset Minimizing Resistor (OMR)** is a resistor connected in series with the non-inverting input terminal of an op-amp to balance the DC resistance seen at both input terminals, thereby minimizing the output offset voltage caused by input bias currents.

**Formula:** For an inverting amplifier with input resistor $R_1$ and feedback resistor $R_f$:

$$R_{OMR} = R_1 \,\|\, R_f = \frac{R_1 \cdot R_f}{R_1 + R_f}$$

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for explanation, 1 mark for formula — Total: 3 marks)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2. Differentiate between Input Bias Current (I_B) and Input Offset Current (I_io). Why is the use of OMR preferred over direct current-cancellation techniques?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Input Bias Current (I_B):** The average of the DC currents flowing into the inverting and non-inverting input terminals of the op-amp, mathematically defined as $I_B = (I_{B+} + I_{B-})/2$. Typical range: 10 nA to 1 μA.

- **Input Offset Current (I_io):** The algebraic difference between the bias currents at the two input terminals, defined as $I_{io} = \mid I_{B+} - I_{B-} \mid$. Typical range: 1 nA to 100 nA.

**Why OMR is Preferred:**
- OMR is a simple, single passive resistor (low cost, no active components).
- It is a passive, reliable solution with no power consumption.
- It is easy to design and integrate into existing circuits.
- It reduces the offset from being proportional to $I_B$ to being proportional to $I_{io}$, which is typically 10–100× smaller.

*(1 mark each for the two definitions, 1 mark for advantages — Total: 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper Style]**

---

#### **Question A: Inverting Amplifier Offset Analysis with OMR (14 Marks)**

**Question A(a):** For an inverting amplifier using op-amp μA741, the following parameters are given: $R_1 = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_f = 100\,\text{k}\Omega$, $V_{io} = 5\,\text{mV}$, $I_B = 80\,\text{nA}$, and $I_{io} = 8\,\text{nA}$.

**Calculate:**
- (i) The output offset voltage **without** the Offset Minimizing Resistor.
- (ii) The required value of OMR.
- (iii) The output offset voltage **with** the OMR connected.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i): Output offset voltage without OMR:**

The formula is:
$$V_{out,offset} = \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{io} + I_B \cdot (R_1 \,\|\, R_f)$$

Calculate the gain factor:
$$\left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) = 1 + \frac{100}{1} = 101$$

Calculate the parallel resistance:
$$R_1 \,\|\, R_f = \frac{1\,\text{k} \times 100\,\text{k}}{1\,\text{k} + 100\,\text{k}} = \frac{100 \times 10^6}{101 \times 10^3} \approx 990.1\,\Omega$$

Substitute the values:
$$V_{out,offset} = 101 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} + 80 \times 10^{-9} \times 990.1$$

$$V_{out,offset} = 0.505 + 79.21 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out,offset} \approx 505.08\,\text{mV}}$$

**Part (ii): Required OMR value:**

$$R_{OMR} = R_1 \,\|\, R_f = 990.1\,\Omega \approx 1\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Part (iii): Output offset voltage with OMR:**

$$V_{out,offset}^{OMR} = \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{io} + I_{io} \cdot (R_1 \,\|\, R_f)$$

$$V_{out,offset}^{OMR} = 101 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} + 8 \times 10^{-9} \times 990.1$$

$$V_{out,offset}^{OMR} = 0.505 + 7.92 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out,offset}^{OMR} \approx 505.01\,\text{mV}}$$

**Observation:** The dramatic bias-current contribution dropped from $79.21\,\mu\text{V}$ to $7.92\,\mu\text{V}$ (a 10× reduction, since $I_B / I_{io} = 10$ in this case). However, the dominant $V_{io}$ term ($505\,\text{mV}$) remains unchanged because OMR does not address offset voltage — only offset current.

---

**Question A(b):** Explain the **principle of operation** of the Offset Minimizing Resistor. Why is it called a "common-mode compensation" technique? Discuss the limitations of OMR. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle of Operation:**
The OMR works on the principle of **input resistance balancing**. The input bias currents $I_{B+}$ and $I_{B-}$ of the op-amp flow continuously through whatever DC resistance is present at each input terminal. If the resistances are unequal, the bias currents create **unequal DC voltage drops**, which appear as a differential DC error at the op-amp inputs. This differential error is amplified by the closed-loop gain, producing a significant output offset.

By inserting an OMR at the non-inverting terminal such that $R_{OMR}$ equals the Thevenin resistance at the inverting terminal ($R_1 \,\|\, R_f$), the **voltage drops at both inputs become equal in magnitude and sign** (i.e., a common-mode voltage). The op-amp's internal common-mode rejection mechanism (high CMRR) then suppresses this common-mode signal, leaving only the residual difference due to $I_{io} = \mid I_{B+} - I_{B-} \mid$.

**Why "Common-Mode Compensation":**
The technique is called common-mode compensation because the bias-current-induced voltage drop, after OMR insertion, becomes a **common-mode signal** — appearing identically at both input terminals. Op-amps are specifically designed to reject common-mode signals via their differential input stage and high CMRR. Thus, the OMR converts a problematic **differential-mode error** into a benign **common-mode signal** that the op-amp naturally rejects.

**Limitations of OMR:**
1. **Cannot compensate for input offset voltage (V_io):** The OMR only addresses bias current effects, not the intrinsic V_io of the op-amp. V_io requires separate nulling circuits.
2. **Effectiveness depends on resistor matching:** The OMR must exactly equal $R_1 \,\|\, R_f$. Any tolerance mismatch reduces effectiveness.
3. **Adds thermal noise:** The OMR contributes Johnson-Nyquist noise proportional to $\sqrt{4 k_B T R_{OMR}}$, which can be problematic in low-noise designs.
4. **Affects input impedance:** The OMR reduces the input impedance of the non-inverting terminal, which may load high-impedance sources.
5. **Limited improvement factor:** Even with perfect OMR, the offset is reduced only by the ratio $I_{io}/I_B$, which is finite (typically 10–100).
6. **Frequency-dependent behavior:** At high frequencies, parasitic capacitances can upset the resistance matching and degrade OMR effectiveness.
7. **Not a complete solution:** For ultra-precision applications, additional techniques like chopper stabilization, auto-zeroing, or nulling potentiometers are required.

---

#### **Question B: Difference Amplifier and Non-Inverting OMR (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**Question B(a):** Derive the expression for the output offset voltage of a **difference amplifier** with an Offset Minimizing Resistor. Show that for optimum offset compensation in a difference amplifier, the condition $R_1/R_2 = R_3/R_4$ must be satisfied, where $R_3$ and $R_4$ are the OMR-split network values. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Difference Amplifier Topology:**
- Input $V_1$ connects to the inverting terminal through $R_1$.
- Feedback resistor $R_f = R_2$ connects $V_{out}$ to the inverting terminal.
- Input $V_2$ connects to the non-inverting terminal through $R_3$.
- Resistor $R_4$ connects the non-inverting terminal to ground (this is the OMR-related resistor).

**Ideal Output Equation (no offset):**
$$V_{out} = \frac{R_2}{R_1} (V_2 - V_1) \quad \text{when} \quad \frac{R_2}{R_1} = \frac{R_4}{R_3}$$

**Including Offset Voltages and Bias Currents:**

Apply KCL at the inverting node (assuming $V_1 = V_2 = 0$ for offset analysis):
$$\frac{0 - V_-}{R_1} + \frac{V_{out} - V_-}{R_2} = I_{B-}$$

At the non-inverting node:
$$V_+ = 0 - I_{B+} \cdot R_4 = -I_{B+} R_4$$

(assuming $R_3$ connects to a grounded source, so no current flows through $R_3$ from $V_2$ when $V_2 = 0$)

The offset equation $V_- - V_+ = V_{io}$ gives:
$$V_- = V_{io} - I_{B+} R_4$$

**Substituting into the KCL equation:**
$$\frac{-V_{io} + I_{B+} R_4}{R_1} + \frac{V_{out} - V_{io} + I_{B+} R_4}{R_2} = I_{B-}$$

Multiply through by $R_1 R_2$ and isolate $V_{out}$:

$$V_{out} R_1 = I_{B-} R_1 R_2 + R_2 (V_{io} - I_{B+} R_4) - R_1 (V_{io} - I_{B+} R_4)$$

$$V_{out} R_1 = I_{B-} R_1 R_2 + (R_2 - R_1) V_{io} - (R_2 - R_1) I_{B+} R_4$$

$$V_{out} = \frac{I_{B-} R_1 R_2}{R_1} + \frac{(R_2 - R_1) V_{io}}{R_1} - \frac{(R_2 - R_1) I_{B+} R_4}{R_1}$$

For optimal offset cancellation, we need the **bias current terms to cancel out**. Setting $I_{B+} = I_{B-} = I_B$ (matched ideal case), the requirement is:

$$I_B R_2 - I_B R_4 \cdot \frac{(R_2 - R_1)}{R_1} = 0$$

Simplifying this, the matching condition becomes:
$$\frac{R_2}{R_1} = \frac{R_4}{R_3} \quad \text{and} \quad R_3 = R_1, R_4 = R_2$$

This means $R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$, equivalently $\dfrac{R_1}{R_3} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_4}$.

**Conclusion:** The OMR-related network $R_3$–$R_4$ in a difference amplifier must satisfy:

$$\boxed{\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{R_3}{R_4}}$$

When this is satisfied, the **Thevenin resistance at the non-inverting terminal** ($R_3 \,\|\, R_4$) equals the **Thevenin resistance at the inverting terminal** ($R_1 \,\|\, R_2$), ensuring bias-current compensation.

---

**Question B(b):** For a non-inverting amplifier with $R_1 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $R_f = 90\,\text{k}\Omega$, calculate the OMR required and explain its placement. Given op-amp data: $V_{io} = 3\,\text{mV}$, $I_{B+} = 110\,\text{nA}$, $I_{B-} = 100\,\text{nA}$, determine the output offset voltage. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — OMR Placement:**
In a non-inverting amplifier, the input signal $V_{in}$ is applied directly to the non-inverting terminal. The OMR is placed between this terminal and ground (or between the terminal and the source, depending on the application). For ideal compensation, the OMR should be chosen to balance the inverting-terminal Thevenin resistance.

**Step 2 — OMR Calculation:**
The Thevenin resistance at the inverting terminal is $R_1 \,\|\, R_f$:
$$R_{OMR} = R_1 \,\|\, R_f = \frac{10\,\text{k} \times 90\,\text{k}}{10\,\text{k} + 90\,\text{k}} = \frac{900 \times 10^6}{100 \times 10^3} = 9\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Bias Current Calculations:**
$$I_B = \frac{I_{B+} + I_{B-}}{2} = \frac{110 + 100}{2} = 105\,\text{nA}$$

$$I_{io} = \mid I_{B+} - I_{B-} \mid = \mid 110 - 100 \mid = 10\,\text{nA}$$

**Step 4 — Output Offset Voltage with OMR:**

Using the formula:
$$V_{out,offset} = \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{io} + I_{io} \cdot (R_1 \,\|\, R_f)$$

Calculate the gain:
$$1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 1 + 9 = 10$$

Substitute:
$$V_{out,offset} = 10 \times 3 \times 10^{-3} + 10 \times 10^{-9} \times 9 \times 10^3$$

$$V_{out,offset} = 30 \times 10^{-3} + 90 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out,offset} = 30.09\,\text{mV}}$$

**Verification — Without OMR:**
$$V_{out,offset}^{No\,OMR} = 10 \times 3 \times 10^{-3} + 105 \times 10^{-9} \times 9 \times 10^3$$

$$V_{out,offset}^{No\,OMR} = 0.030 + 0.000945 = 30.945\,\text{mV}$$

**Improvement:** $\Delta V_{out} = 0.855\,\text{mV}$ reduction (~2.76% improvement), demonstrating OMR effectiveness.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (–1 to –2 marks):** Students frequently mix up $k\Omega$ and $\Omega$, or forget to convert nA to A. Always double-check: $1\,\text{nA} = 10^{-9}\,\text{A}$.
>
> 2. **Confusing $I_B$ with $I_{io}$ in Formula Selection (–2 marks):** When OMR is used, the formula must use $I_{io}$ (input offset current), NOT $I_B$ (input bias current). This is the single most common error.
>
> 3. **Incorrect OMR Formula for Difference Amplifiers (–3 marks):** Students often write $R_{OMR} = R_1 \,\|\, R_f$ for a difference amplifier, forgetting that the OMR network is $R_3$–$R_4$ and must satisfy $\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_4}$.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the $V_{io}$ Contribution (–2 marks):** OMR does NOT eliminate the $V_{io}$ term. The output offset still contains $\left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{io}$. Many students wrongly think OMR makes the output offset zero.
>
> 5. **Sign Convention Errors:** When applying $V_- - V_+ = V_{io}$, be consistent with the sign convention defined by your textbook.
>
> 6. **Not Mentioning Practical Tolerances (–1 mark):** Real OMR resistors have ±1% or ±5% tolerance, which limits the perfect matching. Mention this in design discussions for full marks.
>
> 7. **Skipping the Thevenin Equivalent Step (–1 mark):** Always explicitly compute $R_1 \,\|\, R_f$ before substituting into formulas.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION SUMMARY**

✅ **Core Concept:** OMR is a passive resistor added at the non-inverting input to balance the DC resistances seen at both inputs of an op-amp, thereby minimizing bias-current-induced output offset.

✅ **Master Formula:** $R_{OMR} = R_1 \,\|\, R_f$ for inverting and non-inverting amplifiers.

✅ **Difference Amplifier OMR Condition:** $R_1 / R_2 = R_3 / R_4$ (resistor matching for both gain and offset compensation).

✅ **Output Offset Equation WITHOUT OMR:**
$V_{out} = (1 + R_f/R_1) \cdot V_{io} + I_B \cdot (R_1 \,\|\, R_f)$

✅ **Output Offset Equation WITH OMR:**
$V_{out} = (1 + R_f/R_1) \cdot V_{io} + I_{io} \cdot (R_1 \,\|\, R_f)$

✅ **Key Definitions to Memorize:**
- $I_B = (I_{B+} + I_{B-})/2$ — Input Bias Current
- $I_{io} = \mid I_{B+} - I_{B-} \mid$ — Input Offset Current
- $V_{io}$ — Input Offset Voltage (NOT compensated by OMR)

✅ **OMR Reduces the Offset Proportional to $I_B$** to one proportional to $I_{io}$, which is typically **10× to 100× smaller**.

✅ **Common-Mode Rejection:** OMR converts a differential-mode error into a common-mode signal that the op-amp's high CMRR naturally rejects.

✅ **Limitations of OMR:**
- Does not compensate for $V_{io}$
- Sensitive to resistor tolerance and temperature
- Adds Johnson noise
- Limited by $I_{io}/I_B$ ratio
- Effectiveness decreases at high frequencies due to parasitics

✅ **Practical Rule:** Always use OMR in **high-gain DC amplifiers**, **instrumentation amplifiers**, **integrators**, and **active filters** where DC precision matters.

✅ **Exam Tip:** When asked to design for minimum offset, always state the OMR formula explicitly and verify by computing both $V_{out}$ (with and without OMR) to show the improvement.

✅ **Quick Test for OMR Placement:** If the inverting node has $R_1$ and $R_f$ as its Thevenin resistance, the OMR **must equal** that parallel combination — this is the golden rule.

✅ **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Never confuse the **feedback resistor** $R_f$ with the **source resistance** $R_s$ in OMR calculations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feedback Topologies - Voltage Series
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEBT402 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Feedback Topologies - Voltage Series # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Voltage-Series Feedback (also called Series-Shunt Feedback)** is a negative feedback topology in which the **output voltage** is sampled (shunt sampling) and a proportional **feedback voltage** is fed back in **series** with the input source (series mixing). It is ideally suited to function as a **Voltage Amplifier**, where the goal is to produce a stable, low-impedance output voltage that faithfully follows the input. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this configuration belongs to the broader class of **four canonical feedback topologies**, classified based on two binary decisions: 1. **Output Sampling** — Voltage (shunt / parallel) vs. Current (series) 2. **Input Mixing** — Voltage (series) vs. Current (shunt) | Input ↓ \ Output → | **Voltage (Shunt)** | **Current (Series)** | |---|---|---| | **Voltage (Series)** | ✅ **Voltage-Series** | Current-Series | | **Current (Shunt)** | Voltage-Shunt | Current-Shunt | The Voltage-Series topology is therefore the **"Series-Shunt"** configuration: **Series** at the input port and **Shunt** at the output port. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Cruise Control 🚗 Imagine driving a car on a highway. You set your **desired speed** of 80 km/h on the cruise control panel — this is your **input signal $V_i$**. The car's **actual speed** — the **output signal $V_o$** — is continuously measured by a speed sensor (this is **output voltage sampling**, i.e., *shunt sampling*). The cruise controller **subtracts** the actual speed from your setpoint in series (this is **series mixing** at the input). The error signal — the difference between desired and actual speed — is amplified and used to adjust the throttle. If the car climbs a hill and slows down, the system automatically opens the throttle to restore the 80 km/h. The whole system is a **Voltage-Series Feedback** loop: - **Sampling** the output (speed) → Voltage feedback - **Mixing** in series (subtracting from input) → Series mixing - The result: stable, accurate, and reproducible **output voltage** ## 1.3 Key Identifier — The "Sentinel" Rules > [!IMPORTANT] > **How to identify any feedback topology in 2 seconds:** > - **Output side:** If you "tap" the output with a wire (in parallel with the load) → **Voltage (Shunt) sampling**. If you break the output loop and insert a meter → **Current (Series) sampling**. > - **Input side:** If the feedback signal is **subtracted as a voltage** (KVL) → **Series mixing**. If the feedback signal is **injected as a current** (KCL) → **Shunt mixing**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Voltage-Series = Series-Shunt = Voltage Amplifier.** The amplifier type, sampling, and mixing are **all linked** by Miller's theorem and the requirement to maximize $R_{in}$ and minimize $R_{out}$ for an ideal voltage source. # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Conceptual Building Blocks To analyze Voltage-Series feedback, we replace the messy real circuit with a clean **block-diagram model** containing three idealized blocks: 1. **Basic Amplifier (A-block):** Has input resistance $R_i$, output resistance $R_o$, and open-loop gain $A$. 2. **Feedback Network (β-block):** A passive two-port network (usually resistors) that samples the output and returns a fraction $\beta$ as a voltage to the input. 3. **Summing Junction (Σ):** Performs $V_a = V_i - V_f$, where $V_f = \beta V_o$ is the feedback voltage. > [!NOTE] > **Fundamental Assumptions of Block-Diagram Reduction (Bode's Method):** > 1. **Unilateral Source → Feedback Network:** The feedback network does not load the basic amplifier's input or output. (In practice, we account for loading separately.) > 2. **Unilateral Feedback Network → Source:** The feedback network does not feed forward to the output. > 3. The **input signal is transmitted forward only through A**, and the **output signal is transmitted backward only through β**. ## 2.2 The Closed-Loop Gain (The "Master Equation") Starting with the basic feedback equation: $$V_o = A \cdot V_a = A \cdot (V_i - V_f) = A \cdot (V_i - \beta V_o)$$ Solving for the closed-loop transfer function $A_f = V_o / V_i$: $$V_o + A \beta V_o = A V_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_o (1 + A\beta) = A V_i$$ $$\boxed{A_f = \dfrac{A}{1 + A \beta}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The term **$1 + A\beta$** is called the **Desensitivity Factor** (or **Return Difference**), denoted **$D$**. It governs *every* property of the closed-loop amplifier. $A\beta$ itself is called the **Loop Gain**. ## 2.3 Effect on Input Resistance (Series Mixing) For series mixing, the feedback voltage $V_f$ appears in **series** with the source, so by KVL the same input current $I_i$ must traverse both $R_i$ and the feedback network. The closed-loop input resistance is: $$\boxed{R_{if} = R_i \, (1 + A\beta)}$$ **Physical meaning:** Series mixing forces the amplifier to demand a much larger voltage to push the same current through — i.e., it presents a **larger input impedance** to the source. This is essential for voltage amplifiers, which should not load down the preceding stage. ## 2.4 Effect on Output Resistance (Shunt Sampling) For shunt sampling, the feedback network is in parallel with the output. Applying a test voltage $V_t$ at the output with $V_i = 0$, the feedback creates an "internal" short-circuit current that reduces the effective output impedance: $$\boxed{R_{of} = \dfrac{R_o}{1 + A\beta}}$$ **Physical meaning:** The amplifier behaves more like an **ideal voltage source** — its output impedance is reduced by the desensitivity factor. This minimizes **load regulation** errors. ## 2.5 Effect on Bandwidth, Noise, and Nonlinear Distortion > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Gain-Bandwidth Trade-off (constant GBP):** > The gain is reduced by a factor of $D = (1 + A\beta)$, but the upper cutoff frequency $f_H$ is **increased** by the same factor: > $$f_{Hf} = f_H \cdot (1 + A\beta)$$ > The **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP)** remains **invariant**. The same logic applies to the lower cutoff: $f_{Lf} = f_L / (1 + A\beta)$. Other improvements (all scaled by $D$): - **Nonlinear distortion** $\to$ reduced by $D$ - **Noise voltage** $\to$ reduced by $D$ (if noise is injected in the loop) - **Supply sensitivity (PSRR)** $\to$ improved by $D$ - **Gain sensitivity to temperature / device parameters** $\to$ reduced by $D$ (quantified by $\frac{dA_f/A_f}{dA/A} = \frac{1}{1 + A\beta}$) ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Voltage-Series Feedback — Master Formula Table** | Quantity | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop | Effect of Feedback | |---|---|---|---| | Voltage Gain | $A$ | $A_f = A / (1+A\beta)$ | Reduced by $D$ | | Input Resistance | $R_i$ | $R_{if} = R_i(1+A\beta)$ | **Increased** by $D$ | | Output Resistance | $R_o$ | $R_{of} = R_o / (1+A\beta)$ | **Decreased** by $D$ | | Upper Cutoff $f_H$ | $f_H$ | $f_{Hf} = f_H(1+A\beta)$ | Increased by $D$ | | Lower Cutoff $f_L$ | $f_L$ | $f_{Lf} = f_L / (1+A\beta)$ | Decreased by $D$ | | Bandwidth $BW$ | $BW$ | $BW_f = BW \cdot (1+A\beta)$ | Increased by $D$ | | Loop Gain | — | $T = A\beta$ | — | | Desensitivity $D$ | — | $D = 1 + A\beta$ | — | | Feedback in dB | — | $20\log_{10} D$ dB | — | | Amplifier Type | — | **Voltage Amplifier** | $R_{in}\to\infty$, $R_{out}\to 0$ | | Sampling (Output) | — | **Shunt (Voltage)** | — | | Mixing (Input) | — | **Series (Voltage)** | — | > Here $D \equiv (1 + A\beta)$ and $T \equiv A\beta$ (loop gain). # 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Closed-Loop Voltage Gain $A_f$ We begin with the fundamental block diagram equations. Let: - $V_i$ = source input voltage - $V_f$ = feedback voltage produced by the β-network - $V_a$ = net voltage appearing at the input of the basic amplifier - $V_o$ = output voltage of the basic amplifier - $A$ = open-loop voltage gain of the basic amplifier ($V_o / V_a$) - $\beta$ = reverse transmission of the feedback network ($V_f / V_o$, dimensionless for voltage-series) **Step 1:** Write the open-loop relation of the basic amplifier. $$V_o = A \cdot V_a$$ **Step 2:** Express the input of the basic amplifier in terms of source and feedback (series mixing = subtraction). $$V_a = V_i - V_f$$ **Step 3:** Express the feedback voltage in terms of output (shunt sampling). $$V_f = \beta \cdot V_o$$ **Step 4:** Substitute Step 3 into Step 2. $$V_a = V_i - \beta V_o$$ **Step 5:** Substitute Step 4 into Step 1. $$V_o = A(V_i - \beta V_o)$$ **Step 6:** Distribute the multiplier $A$. $$V_o = A V_i - A\beta V_o$$ **Step 7:** Collect $V_o$ terms on the left-hand side. $$V_o + A\beta V_o = A V_i$$ **Step 8:** Factor $V_o$ on the left. $$V_o (1 + A\beta) = A V_i$$ **Step 9:** Solve for the ratio $V_o / V_i$. $$A_f = \frac{V_o}{V_i} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **golden equation** of negative feedback. Note that for $|A\beta| \gg 1$ (deep feedback), $A_f \approx 1/\beta$ — the closed-loop gain depends **only on the passive feedback network** and not on the (drifting) active device parameters. ## 3.2 Derivation of Closed-Loop Input Resistance $R_{if}$ We need the resistance "seen" by the source $V_i$. In series-mixing topologies, the feedback voltage appears in series with $R_i$, so we apply **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** at the input port. **Step 1:** Write the source equation using KVL. $$V_i = I_i R_i + V_f$$ **Step 2:** Express $V_f$ using the feedback and gain relations. From earlier: $V_f = \beta V_o$ and $V_o = A V_a = A(V_i - V_f)$. **Step 3:** Substitute the expression for $V_o$ in terms of $V_i$ and $V_f$. $$V_f = \beta \cdot A (V_i - V_f)$$ **Step 4:** Expand. $$V_f = A\beta V_i - A\beta V_f$$ **Step 5:** Collect $V_f$ terms. $$V_f + A\beta V_f = A\beta V_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_f(1 + A\beta) = A\beta V_i$$ **Step 6:** Solve for $V_f$ in terms of $V_i$. $$V_f = \frac{A\beta}{1 + A\beta} V_i$$ **Step 7:** Substitute back into the KVL equation from Step 1. $$V_i = I_i R_i + \frac{A\beta}{1 + A\beta} V_i$$ **Step 8:** Move the feedback-voltage term to the left. $$V_i - \frac{A\beta}{1 + A\beta} V_i = I_i R_i$$ **Step 9:** Combine the $V_i$ terms with a common denominator. $$V_i \left( \frac{1 + A\beta - A\beta}{1 + A\beta} \right) = I_i R_i$$ **Step 10:** Simplify the numerator. $$V_i \left( \frac{1}{1 + A\beta} \right) = I_i R_i$$ **Step 11:** Solve for the ratio $V_i / I_i$ to obtain $R_{if}$. $$R_{if} = \frac{V_i}{I_i} = R_i (1 + A\beta)$$ > [!NOTE] > This result is intuitive: in series mixing, the source has to "fight" against the feedback voltage, so the amplifier draws **less current** for the same $V_i$ — equivalent to a **larger** input resistance. ## 3.3 Derivation of Closed-Loop Output Resistance $R_{of}$ For voltage (shunt) sampling, we apply a **test voltage $V_t$** at the output port, with the independent source **deactivated** ($V_i = 0$). We then measure the resulting current $I_t$ and compute $R_{of} = V_t / I_t$. **Step 1:** Deactivate the source and apply a test voltage at the output. $$V_i = 0, \quad V_o = V_t$$ **Step 2:** Compute the feedback voltage. Since $V_f = \beta V_o = \beta V_t$: $$V_f = \beta V_t$$ **Step 3:** Compute the input to the basic amplifier. With $V_i = 0$ and series mixing $V_a = V_i - V_f$: $$V_a = 0 - \beta V_t = -\beta V_t$$ **Step 4:** Compute the open-circuit voltage that the basic amplifier would generate. The basic amplifier with output resistance $R_o$ acts as a dependent source: $$V_{oc,basic} = A V_a = -A\beta V_t$$ **Step 5:** Now apply KCL at the output node. The total current $I_t$ delivered by the test source splits into: - A current through the output resistance $R_o$ of the basic amplifier (from the dependent source): $\dfrac{V_t - (-A\beta V_t)}{R_o} = \dfrac{V_t(1 + A\beta)}{R_o}$ - A current $I_{fb}$ injected by the feedback network (which we are neglecting to first order, by the unilateral assumption). **Step 6:** Write KCL at the output node. $$I_t = \frac{V_t - A V_a}{R_o} = \frac{V_t - A(-\beta V_t)}{R_o} = \frac{V_t + A\beta V_t}{R_o}$$ **Step 7:** Factor. $$I_t = \frac{V_t (1 + A\beta)}{R_o}$$ **Step 8:** Solve for $R_{of} = V_t / I_t$. $$R_{of} = \frac{V_t}{I_t} = \frac{R_o}{1 + A\beta}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering insight:** A small output resistance is the signature of a good **voltage source**. Voltage-series feedback actively creates a "virtual low-impedance" at the output, which is why this topology is the natural choice for driving low-impedance loads (e.g., loudspeakers, cables). ## 3.4 Derivation of Bandwidth Enhancement The gain of a typical single-stage amplifier falls off above $f_H$ at $-20$ dB/decade. We model this with a single-pole transfer function: $$A(s) = \frac{A_0}{1 + s / \omega_H}$$ With feedback, applying the master equation $A_f(s) = A(s) / [1 + A(s)\beta]$: **Step 1:** Substitute $A(s)$ into the loop gain $A(s)\beta$. $$A(s)\beta = \frac{A_0 \beta}{1 + s / \omega_H}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute into the closed-loop gain. $$A_f(s) = \frac{\dfrac{A_0}{1 + s/\omega_H}}{1 + \dfrac{A_0 \beta}{1 + s/\omega_H}}$$ **Step 3:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1 + s/\omega_H)$. $$A_f(s) = \frac{A_0}{1 + s/\omega_H + A_0 \beta} = \frac{A_0}{(1 + A_0 \beta)\left(1 + \dfrac{s}{\omega_H(1 + A_0 \beta)}\right)}$$ **Step 4:** Identify the new DC gain and new pole frequency. $$A_{f,0} = \frac{A_0}{1 + A_0 \beta} \quad , \quad \omega_{Hf} = \omega_H (1 + A_0 \beta)$$ **Step 5:** Conclude: the new bandwidth is $f_{Hf} = f_H (1 + A_0 \beta)$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP) is conserved:** > $$A_0 \cdot f_H = A_{f,0} \cdot f_{Hf}$$ > This is the fundamental trade-off — you "spend" the gain you gave up to **buy** bandwidth. # 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Voltage-Series Feedback) ```mermaid graph LR Src["Source Vi"] SJ["Summing Junction
Sigma"] Ablock["Basic Amplifier
Gain A, Ri, Ro"] OutNode["Output Node Vo"] Load["Load RL"] Beta["Feedback Network
Beta block"] Gnd["Ground"] Src --> SJ SJ -->|"Va = Vi minus Vf"| Ablock Ablock -->|"Vo = A times Va"| OutNode OutNode --> Load OutNode --> Beta Beta -->|"Vf = Beta times Vo"| SJ SJ --- Gnd ``` ## 4.2 Equivalent Circuit — Two-Port Representation ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Input Port - Series Mixing" IP1["Input Terminal +"] IP2["Input Terminal -"] Vsrc["Vi Source"] end subgraph "Basic Amplifier Block" Rin["Ri"] Av["Dependent Source A times Va"] Rout["Ro"] end subgraph "Feedback Beta-Network" Betablock["Beta"] end subgraph "Output Port - Shunt Sampling" OP1["Output Terminal +"] OP2["Output Terminal -"] RLnode["Load RL"] end IP1 --> Rin Rin --> SJnode["Node Va"] SJnode --> Av Av --> Rout Rout --> OP1 OP1 --> RLnode RLnode --> OP2 OP2 --> IP2 OP1 --> Betablock Betablock --> SJnode ``` ## 4.3 Signal-Flow Graph (Mason's Gain Formula View) ```mermaid graph LR Vin["Vi"] -->|"Forward 1"| SumJ["Summing Node"] SumJ -->|"Forward A"| Vout["Vo"] Vout -->|"Feedback minus Beta"| SumJ SumJ --> Va["Va"] Va -->|"Times 1/Ri"| Iin["Ii"] Vout -->|"Times 1/Ro plus RL"| Iout["Io"] ``` ## 4.4 Sampling & Mixing Identification Guide ```mermaid graph TB Q1{"Output is sampled how?"} Q2{"Input is mixed how?"} A1["Voltage-Series
Shunt sampling + Series mixing
= Voltage Amplifier"] A2["Voltage-Shunt
Shunt sampling + Shunt mixing
= Transresistance Amplifier"] A3["Current-Series
Series sampling + Series mixing
= Transconductance Amplifier"] A4["Current-Shunt
Series sampling + Shunt mixing
= Current Amplifier"] Q1 -->|"In parallel with load"| Q2 Q1 -->|"By breaking the loop"| Q2 Q2 -->|"KVL subtract"| A1 Q2 -->|"KCL inject"| A2 Q2 -->|"KVL subtract"| A3 Q2 -->|"KCL inject"| A4 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Compilation Notes used in the above diagrams:** > - All node IDs are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `Vin`, `SumJ`, `Ablock`). > - All double-quoted labels contain only plain uppercase/lowercase text and standard punctuation — **no bold tags, no italics, no HTML tables** inside labels. > - The `end` and `subgraph` keywords are used **only** as Mermaid directives, never as node names. # 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. Define voltage-series feedback amplifier. Mention its effect on input and output resistance.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** Voltage-series feedback is a negative feedback topology in which a fraction **$\beta$** of the **output voltage** is sampled (shunt sampling) and fed back in **series** with the input source (series mixing), so that the feedback voltage opposes the source voltage (KVL subtraction). - **Input resistance is increased:** $R_{if} = R_i(1 + A\beta)$ — because the feedback voltage reduces the net voltage across $R_i$ for the same input current. - **Output resistance is decreased:** $R_{of} = R_o / (1 + A\beta)$ — because the shunt-sampled feedback actively cancels the output-voltage variation under load changes. This makes it behave as an ideal **voltage amplifier** (high $R_{in}$, low $R_{out}$). --- ### **Q2. With a neat sketch, identify the four feedback topologies and their amplifier classifications.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Topology | Output Sampling | Input Mixing | Amplifier Type | |---|---|---|---| | Voltage-Series | Shunt (Voltage) | Series (Voltage) | Voltage Amplifier | | Voltage-Shunt | Shunt (Voltage) | Shunt (Current) | Transresistance Amplifier | | Current-Series | Series (Current) | Series (Voltage) | Transconductance Amplifier | | Current-Shunt | Series (Current) | Shunt (Current) | Current Amplifier | (Each entry carries $1/2$ mark, and the table itself counts as 1 mark for the sketch; the explanation of the *Series–Shunt* Voltage-Series case earns the remaining 1 mark.) --- ## 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > 1. **Forgetting to specify the "type"** (Voltage / Current / Transconductance / Transresistance amplifier) costs **1 mark** — examiners expect this classification explicitly. > 2. **Mixing up sampling and mixing terminology** is the most common error: *Series sampling* means breaking the output, *Shunt sampling* means tapping in parallel. Confusing these gives the *opposite* $R_{if}$/$R_{of}$ expressions and **all subsequent marks are lost**. > 3. **Not stating the assumption of ideal unilateral feedback** during derivations loses **1 mark** in the derivation section. --- ### **❓ Question A (14 Marks)** #### **Part (a) — Derive the closed-loop gain, input resistance, and output resistance of a voltage-series feedback amplifier. Show the block diagram. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Draw and label the block diagram (1 mark)** — show Source $V_i$, summing junction, $A$-block, output $V_o$, and $\beta$-block returning to the input. **Step 2: Write the master equations (1 mark).** $$V_o = A V_a \quad , \quad V_a = V_i - V_f \quad , \quad V_f = \beta V_o$$ **Step 3: Derive $A_f$ (1.5 marks).** Combine and solve: $$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$ **Step 4: Derive $R_{if}$ (1.5 marks).** Apply KVL, substitute $V_f = \frac{A\beta}{1 + A\beta} V_i$: $$R_{if} = R_i (1 + A\beta)$$ **Step 5: Derive $R_{of}$ (2 marks).** Apply test voltage $V_t$ with $V_i = 0$, use KCL at output: $$R_{of} = \frac{R_o}{1 + A\beta}$$ **Final tabulation (extra credit):** State the amplifier type and identify it as a **Voltage Amplifier**. --- #### **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)** A voltage-series feedback amplifier has the following open-loop parameters: - Open-loop voltage gain: $A = 2000$ - Input resistance: $R_i = 4 \text{ k}\Omega$ - Output resistance: $R_o = 80 \text{ }\Omega$ - Feedback factor: $\beta = 0.02$ - Upper cutoff frequency: $f_H = 50 \text{ kHz}$ **Calculate:** (i) Loop gain $A\beta$, (ii) Desensitivity factor $D$, (iii) Closed-loop gain $A_f$, (iv) Closed-loop input resistance $R_{if}$, (v) Closed-loop output resistance $R_{of}$, (vi) Closed-loop bandwidth $BW_f$, (vii) Amount of feedback in dB. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]` **Model Solution:** **(i) Loop gain:** $$A\beta = 2000 \times 0.02 = 40$$ **(ii) Desensitivity factor:** $$D = 1 + A\beta = 1 + 40 = 41$$ **(iii) Closed-loop gain:** $$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta} = \frac{2000}{41} \approx 48.78$$ **(iv) Closed-loop input resistance:** $$R_{if} = R_i (1 + A\beta) = 4000 \times 41 = 164{,}000 \text{ }\Omega = 164 \text{ k}\Omega$$ **(v) Closed-loop output resistance:** $$R_{of} = \frac{R_o}{1 + A\beta} = \frac{80}{41} \approx 1.95 \text{ }\Omega$$ **(vi) Closed-loop bandwidth:** $$f_{Hf} = f_H (1 + A\beta) = 50 \text{ kHz} \times 41 = 2050 \text{ kHz} = 2.05 \text{ MHz}$$ **(vii) Amount of feedback in dB:** $$\text{Fb (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(1 + A\beta) = 20 \log_{10}(41) \approx 32.26 \text{ dB}$$ **Verification of GBP conservation (1 mark):** - Open-loop GBP: $A \cdot f_H = 2000 \times 50\text{ kHz} = 10^8 \text{ Hz}$ - Closed-loop GBP: $A_f \cdot f_{Hf} \approx 48.78 \times 2.05 \text{ MHz} \approx 10^8 \text{ Hz}$ ✓ --- ### **❓ Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)** #### **Part (a) — Explain the concept of voltage-series feedback with the help of a practical emitter-follower circuit. Discuss its advantages and disadvantages. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **Model Solution:** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Define voltage-series feedback — output voltage sampled, fed back in series with the input. **Step 2 (2 marks):** Discuss the **emitter follower (common-collector BJT)** as a practical example. - The emitter voltage $V_E$ follows the base voltage $V_B$ (with a $\sim 0.7$ V offset). - Output is taken at the emitter (low $R_{out}$, voltage sampling). - The full output voltage is fed back in series with the base-emitter junction. - The feedback fraction is essentially $\beta = 1$ (100 % voltage-series feedback). - Closed-loop gain: $A_f = A/(1+A) \approx 1$ (unity follower). **Step 3 (2 marks):** **Advantages.** - Extremely high input impedance — does not load the source. - Extremely low output impedance — good voltage driver. - Wide bandwidth (since the GBP is large and gain is small). - Excellent linearity (deep feedback, $A\beta \gg 1$). **Step 4 (2 marks):** **Disadvantages.** - Gain is slightly less than 1 (cannot amplify voltage). - No current isolation between base and emitter (DC biasing issues). - Limited slew rate dictated by the transistor. - The $V_{BE}$ drop introduces a small DC offset at the output. --- #### **Part (b) — Derive the expressions for the four key feedback equations: (i) $A_f$, (ii) $R_{if}$, (iii) $R_{of}$, and (iv) $f_{Hf}$, and indicate clearly which one is the **master equation**. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **Model Solution:** **Master equation (1 mark):** $$\boxed{A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}}$$ This is the **master equation** — every other closed-loop parameter is derived from it by elementary two-port reasoning. **(i) Closed-loop gain $A_f$ (1.5 marks)** — derived in Section 3.1 of these notes. Combine $V_o = A V_a$ and $V_a = V_i - \beta V_o$. **(ii) Closed-loop input resistance $R_{if}$ (1.5 marks)** — derived in Section 3.2. KVL at input: $$R_{if} = R_i (1 + A\beta)$$ **(iii) Closed-loop output resistance $R_{of}$ (1.5 marks)** — derived in Section 3.3. Test voltage at output with $V_i = 0$: $$R_{of} = \frac{R_o}{1 + A\beta}$$ **(iv) Closed-loop bandwidth $f_{Hf}$ (1 mark)** — derived in Section 3.4. Pole shifts to higher frequency: $$f_{Hf} = f_H (1 + A\beta)$$ **Final summary (0.5 mark):** All four quantities are governed by the single **desensitivity factor $D = 1 + A\beta$**, demonstrating the **unifying power** of the master equation. --- ## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Voltage-Series Feedback — Rapid Revision Checklist** - **Topology name:** Voltage-Series = Series-Shunt = Voltage Amplifier. - **Sampling:** Output **voltage** is sampled (shunt, parallel with load). - **Mixing:** Feedback is mixed as a **voltage** in **series** (KVL subtraction) with the source. - **Ideal port characteristics:** $R_{in} \to \infty$ and $R_{out} \to 0$ (perfect voltage source behavior). - **Master equation:** $A_f = A / (1 + A\beta)$. - **Desensitivity factor:** $D = 1 + A\beta$. Every closed-loop quantity is governed by $D$. - **$R_{if}$ formula:** $R_{if} = R_i(1 + A\beta)$ — **increased** by $D$. - **$R_{of}$ formula:** $R_{of} = R_o / (1 + A\beta)$ — **decreased** by $D$. - **Bandwidth:** $f_{Hf} = f_H(1 + A\beta)$ — **increased** by $D$. - **GBP invariance:** $A \cdot f_H = A_f \cdot f_{Hf}$ — gain traded for bandwidth. - **Deep-feedback limit:** $A\beta \gg 1 \Rightarrow A_f \approx 1/\beta$ — gain set by the **passive** feedback network (stable, accurate). - **Distortion, noise, supply sensitivity:** all reduced by a factor of $D$. - **Practical example:** **Emitter follower** (BJT) and **Source follower** (FET) are the textbook voltage-series feedback amplifiers with $\beta \approx 1$. - **Common KTU pitfall:** Confusing *series sampling* with *series mixing* — remember that the **first word** of the topology name describes the **input** (mixing) and the **second word** describes the **output** (sampling). Voltage-Series = Voltage-mixing, Series-sampling would be wrong; it is **Series-mixing, Voltage-sampling** (often written "Series-Shunt" in international texts). - **Quick identification trick:** If the question mentions **sampling output voltage** and **subtracting a feedback voltage from input** → it is **Voltage-Series**.

topics.title: Voltage Shunt
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT402 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Voltage Shunt

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of Linear Integrated Circuits, **Voltage-Shunt Feedback** (also widely called **Shunt-Shunt Feedback** or **Voltage-Sampling, Current-Mixing Feedback**) is one of the four fundamental negative feedback topologies used to modify the characteristics of an amplifier. The nomenclature follows the **connection convention at the input and output ports**:

- **"Voltage" (first word):** Refers to the **output sampling method** — the output **voltage** $V_o$ is sampled (shunt/parallel connection at the output node).
- **"Shunt" (second word):** Refers to the **input mixing method** — the feedback signal is a **current** that is mixed (subtracted) in parallel (shunt) with the input current source $I_s$.

The feedback network samples the output voltage and converts it into a feedback current $I_f$, which is subtracted from the source current $I_s$ to produce the net error current $I_i$ that drives the basic amplifier.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Voltage-Shunt feedback is synonymous with a **Transresistance Amplifier** (also called Transimpedance Amplifier, TIA). The closed-loop transfer function is a **transresistance** with units of **Volts per Ampere (Ω)**, not a dimensionless voltage gain.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Current-Sensing Water Tank"

Imagine a large overhead water tank (the **output voltage $V_o$**) connected to a tap. We want to keep the water level (output) stable regardless of how many people open the tap (load changes).

- A small **pressure gauge** (the **feedback network**) is connected in **parallel** to the tank's outlet. It senses the water pressure (the output voltage).
- The gauge doesn't just measure — it pushes back a small **counter-flow of water** (the **feedback current $I_f$**) into the incoming supply pipe.
- This counter-flow **subtracts** from the main water inflow $I_s$, creating a **net inflow** $I_i = I_s - I_f$ that actually enters the tank.
- The pump (basic amplifier) reacts only to this **net inflow**, automatically adjusting the level whenever the gauge senses an error.

> This "sensing voltage, returning current" is the precise essence of **Voltage-Shunt Feedback**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Conventions

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Open-loop transresistance gain | $A$ (or $R_M$) | $\sim 10^5$ to $10^6\ \Omega$ | $V_o / I_i$ of basic amplifier |
| Feedback factor | $\beta$ | Siemens (S) or A/V | $I_f / V_o$ of feedback network |
| Loop gain | $A\beta$ | Dimensionless | $> 10^3$ typical |
| Source resistance | $R_s$ | Ω | Seen by the source current $I_s$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The **"shunt"** word at the output refers to the fact that voltage is sampled by placing the feedback network in **parallel (shunt)** with the load — directly across the output terminals, not in series. This distinction is crucial for KTU numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Decomposition — The Four Functional Blocks

A Voltage-Shunt feedback amplifier is universally decomposed into four logical blocks for analysis:

1. **Source / Input Port:** A current source $I_s$ with internal source resistance $R_s$ feeds the summing junction. (Voltage source $V_s$ may be used with series $R_s$.)
2. **Summing (Mixing) Junction:** A **shunt (parallel) node** where $I_s$, feedback current $I_f$, and the error current $I_i$ add algebraically: $I_i = I_s - I_f$.
3. **Basic Amplifier (A-block):** A transresistance amplifier that converts the input error current $I_i$ into the output voltage $V_o$ via its open-loop transresistance $A = V_o / I_i$.
4. **Feedback Network (β-block):** A **shunt-connected passive network** placed in **parallel** with the output that samples $V_o$ and produces a proportional current $I_f = \beta V_o$. The parameter $\beta$ has units of **Siemens (℧ = A/V)**.

### 2.2 The Core Negative-Feedback Equation

For any negative feedback topology, the closed-loop transfer function follows the universal form:

$$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$

For voltage-shunt, this $A_f$ is the **closed-loop transresistance**:

$$R_{Mf} = \frac{V_o}{I_s} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the feedback *stabilizes* gain:** Even if $A$ drifts by ±20% due to temperature or device variations, if $A\beta \gg 1$, then $A_f \approx 1/\beta$. Since $\beta$ is set by stable passive resistors, the closed-loop gain becomes nearly **independent of the active device**. This is the **single most important reason** feedback is taught in KTU Module 2.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula (Exact) | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Open-loop transresistance | $A = V_o / I_i$ | Basic amplifier gain |
| 2 | Feedback factor | $\beta = I_f / V_o$ (S) | Set by external resistors |
| 3 | Closed-loop transresistance | $R_{Mf} = A / (1 + A\beta)$ | Ideal: $\approx 1/\beta$ |
| 4 | Closed-loop current gain | $A_{If} = I_o / I_s$ | Reduced from open-loop value |
| 5 | Input Resistance with feedback | $R_{if} = R_i / (1 + A\beta)$ | **Decreased** by loop gain |
| 6 | Output Resistance with feedback | $R_{of} = R_o / (1 + A\beta)$ | **Decreased** by loop gain |
| 7 | Closed-loop voltage gain | $A_{vf} = V_o / V_s = (R_{Mf} \cdot R_s) / (R_s + R_{if})$ | Depends on $R_s$ |
| 8 | Bandwidth product | $(BW)_f = (BW)(1 + A\beta)$ | **Increased** by loop gain |
| 9 | Feedback current | $I_f = \beta \cdot V_o$ | Output-sampled voltage |
| 10 | Error current | $I_i = I_s - I_f$ | Drives the A-block |

> **Critical Note on Units:** The closed-loop transresistance has units of **Ω** (Volts per Ampere). A common KTU pitfall is treating this as a dimensionless voltage gain — **do not** confuse it with a standard voltage amplifier!

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Voltage-shunt feedback (transimpedance amplifiers) is the **backbone of modern instrumentation**:

- **Photodiode Preamplifiers:** Photodiodes are essentially current sources. A TIA converts the tiny photocurrent (pA to µA) into a usable voltage with high bandwidth and low noise.
- **Oscilloscope Input Stages:** Used for current-mode sensing probes.
- **Optical Communication Receivers:** Front-end of fiber-optic receivers.
- **Charge Amplifiers in Piezoelectric Sensors:** With an integrating capacitor, the TIA becomes a charge-to-voltage converter.
- **ADC Front-Ends:** Driving current-output DACs and current-mode sensors into an ADC.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Closed-Loop Transresistance

**Given:** Basic amplifier with open-loop transresistance $A = V_o / I_i$, feedback network with $\beta = I_f / V_o$.

**Step 1 — KCL at the Shunt Summing Junction:**

Since the input is a **shunt node**, currents sum algebraically. The feedback current $I_f$ is *subtracted* from the source current $I_s$:

$$I_i = I_s - I_f$$

**Step 2 — Output of the Basic Amplifier:**

The basic amplifier produces the output voltage $V_o$ proportional to $I_i$:

$$V_o = A \cdot I_i$$

**Step 3 — Feedback Network Output:**

The feedback network produces a current proportional to the sampled output voltage:

$$I_f = \beta \cdot V_o$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $I_i$ into the Amplifier Equation:**

$$V_o = A(I_s - I_f)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $I_f$ in terms of $V_o$:**

$$V_o = A(I_s - \beta V_o)$$

**Step 6 — Expand the Right-Hand Side:**

$$V_o = A \cdot I_s - A\beta \cdot V_o$$

**Step 7 — Collect $V_o$ Terms on the Left:**

$$V_o + A\beta \cdot V_o = A \cdot I_s$$

**Step 8 — Factor Out $V_o$:**

$$V_o(1 + A\beta) = A \cdot I_s$$

**Step 9 — Solve for the Closed-Loop Transresistance $R_{Mf}$:**

$$\boxed{\,R_{Mf} = \frac{V_o}{I_s} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}\,}$$

This is the **exact general negative feedback equation** evaluated for voltage-shunt topology.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Input Resistance with Feedback ($R_{if}$)

**Step 1 — Definition of Open-Loop Input Resistance:**

$$R_i = \frac{V_i}{I_i}$$

**Step 2 — Define Input Resistance with Feedback:**

$$R_{if} = \frac{V_i}{I_s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply KCL at the Input Node (Shunt Mixing):**

$$I_s = I_i + I_f$$

**Step 4 — Express Both $I_i$ and $I_f$ in Terms of $V_i$ and $V_o$:**

$$I_i = \frac{V_i}{R_i}, \qquad I_f = \beta V_o$$

**Step 5 — Use $V_o = A \cdot I_i$ to Eliminate $V_o$:**

$$I_f = \beta A I_i = \beta A \left(\frac{V_i}{R_i}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Substitute into the KCL Equation:**

$$I_s = \frac{V_i}{R_i} + \frac{\beta A V_i}{R_i} = \frac{V_i}{R_i}(1 + A\beta)$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $R_{if}$:**

$$\frac{V_i}{I_s} = \frac{R_i}{1 + A\beta}$$

$$\boxed{\,R_{if} = \frac{R_i}{1 + A\beta}\,}$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** Voltage-shunt feedback **reduces the input resistance** by the factor $(1+A\beta)$. This makes the amplifier behave like an ideal **current-sensing** device (low input impedance is desirable when the source is a current source).

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Output Resistance with Feedback ($R_{of}$)

**Step 1 — Definition:**

$$R_{of} = \left.\frac{V_o}{I_o}\right|_{I_s = 0}$$

**Step 2 — Kill the Source:** Setting $I_s = 0$ implies $I_i = -I_f$.

**Step 3 — Apply Test Source:** Apply a test voltage $V_t$ at the output and measure $I_t$.

**Step 4 — Apply KCL at the Output (Shunt Sampling):**

$$I_t = I_L + I_f$$

where $I_L$ is the current through the open-loop output resistance $R_o$.

**Step 5 — Express $I_f$ via $\beta$ and the Error Current:**

Since $I_i = -I_f = -I_t \cdot (\text{feedback loading factor})$ and after standard loading analysis:

$$R_{of} = \frac{R_o}{1 + A\beta}$$

> The exact derivation involves **loading of the feedback network on the output** (a key nuance in KTU 14-mark questions — see Section 5).

### 3.4 Summary of Topology Effects (The Master Table)

| Property | Voltage-Series | Voltage-Shunt | Current-Series | Current-Shunt |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Samples | Voltage | Voltage | Current | Current |
| Mixes | Voltage | **Current** | Voltage | Current |
| $R_{if}$ | Increases | **Decreases** | Increases | Decreases |
| $R_{of}$ | Decreases | **Decreases** | Increases | Increases |
| Stabilizes | $A_v$ | $R_M$ ($V_o/I_s$) | $G_M$ ($I_o/V_s$) | $A_i$ |
| $A_f$ formula | $A/(1+A\beta)$ | $A/(1+A\beta)$ | $A/(1+A\beta)$ | $A/(1+A\beta)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the **universal form** $A_f = A/(1+A\beta)$ applies to *all four* topologies. The only difference is **what the gain $A$ physically represents** (voltage gain, transresistance, transconductance, or current gain) and **how $R_{if}$ and $R_{of}$ change**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of Voltage-Shunt Feedback Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Current Source Is with source resistance Rs] -->|Is| sumA{Shunt Summing Junction}
    fbA[Feedback Network with factor beta] -->|If| sumA
    sumA -->|Ii error current| ampA[Basic Amplifier open loop transresistance A]
    ampA -->|Vo output voltage| outA[Output Node across load RL]
    outA -->|voltage sampling shunt connection| fbA
    outA --> voutA[Vo to external load]
    style srcA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style ampA fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style fbA fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style sumA fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
```

### 4.2 Detailed Functional Architecture — Loading and Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT_PORT[Input Port Shunt Mixing]
        Vsrc[Source Vs] --> Rsrc[Source resistance Rs]
        Rsrc --> Node1((Summing node V1))
        Node1 --> R11[Input resistance Ri of A block]
    end
    subgraph A_BLOCK[Basic Amplifier transresistance A]
        R11 --> AmpCore[Opamp core with gain A]
        AmpCore --> RoNode[Output resistance Ro]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT_PORT[Output Port Shunt Sampling]
        RoNode --> Node2((Output node Vo))
        Node2 --> RL[Load RL]
        Rf1[Feedback resistor Rf] --> Node2
        Rf1 --> Node1
        Rg1[Resistor Rg to ground] --> Node1
    end
    subgraph FB_NET[Feedback Network beta = If divided by Vo]
        Rf1
        Rg1
    end
    style INPUT_PORT fill:#e7f3ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style A_BLOCK fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style OUTPUT_PORT fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style FB_NET fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f
```

### 4.3 Signal-Flow Graph (Closed-Loop Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Is source current] --> B1((Summing node minus))
    B1 --> C1[Ii]
    C1 --> D1[Block A open loop transresistance]
    D1 --> E1[Vo]
    E1 --> F1[Block beta feedback factor]
    F1 --> B1
    E1 --> G1[Vo to load]
    style B1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000
    style D1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style F1 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
```

### 4.4 Physical Op-Amp Realization (Inverting TIA Configuration)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inpA[Current source Is] --> nodeA((Inverting node Vn))
    nodeA -->|input current Ii| opaA[Opamp with open loop gain Aol]
    opaA --> voutA[Output Vo]
    rfA[Feedback resistor Rf] --> nodeA
    rfA --> voutA
    noninvA[Non inverting input] --> gndA[Ground]
    style opaA fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style rfA fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style nodeA fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** In all four schematics, trace the loop **Is → Ii → A → Vo → β → If → back to summing node**. The "shunt" nature is visible at the **output** (feedback network sits in parallel with load) and at the **input** (currents sum at a node, not voltages around a loop).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
**Define Voltage-Shunt feedback. Why is it called a transresistance amplifier?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Voltage-Shunt feedback is a negative feedback topology in which the output voltage is sampled (shunt connection at output) and the feedback signal is a current that is mixed (subtracted) in parallel with the input source current. It is called a transresistance amplifier because its closed-loop transfer function is the ratio $V_o / I_s$, which has units of resistance (ohms). The ideal closed-loop transresistance is $R_{Mf} \approx 1/\beta$, stabilized against device variations. *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for sampling/mixing, 1 for transresistance justification.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**
**What is the effect of Voltage-Shunt feedback on the input and output resistance of an amplifier?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Voltage-Shunt feedback **decreases** both the input resistance and the output resistance of the amplifier by a factor of $(1 + A\beta)$. Mathematically: $R_{if} = R_i / (1 + A\beta)$ and $R_{of} = R_o / (1 + A\beta)$. The reduction in input resistance occurs because the input is a current-mixing (shunt) node, and the reduction in output resistance occurs because the output is voltage-sampled (shunt). *(3 marks — formula for $R_{if}$ 1.5 marks, formula for $R_{of}$ 1.5 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]**

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a Voltage-Shunt feedback amplifier and explain the signal flow. Identify which quantity is sampled at the output and which is mixed at the input. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For a Voltage-Shunt feedback amplifier, the open-loop transresistance is $A = 10^5\ \Omega$, the feedback factor is $\beta = 10^{-3}\ \text{S}$ (Siemens), the source resistance is $R_s = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$, the basic amplifier input resistance is $R_i = 50\ \text{k}\Omega$, and the basic amplifier output resistance is $R_o = 100\ \Omega$. Calculate the closed-loop transresistance, input resistance with feedback, output resistance with feedback, and the closed-loop voltage gain $A_{vf} = V_o / V_s$. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a):

**Step 1 — Block Diagram (3 marks):**
Draw the four blocks: Current source $I_s$ → Shunt summing junction (with $-$ for $I_f$) → Basic amplifier ($A = V_o/I_i$) → Output node $V_o$ → Feedback network ($\beta = I_f/V_o$) → back to summing junction. The **output** is connected in **parallel (shunt)** to the feedback network — this is the "voltage" sampling. The **input** is a **node** where currents add — this is the "shunt" mixing.

**Step 2 — Mathematical Relations (2 marks):**
- At the summing node: $I_i = I_s - I_f$ (shunt KCL).
- Amplifier output: $V_o = A \cdot I_i$.
- Feedback output: $I_f = \beta \cdot V_o$.

**Step 3 — Identification (2 marks):**
- **Sampled quantity (output):** Output voltage $V_o$.
- **Mixed quantity (input):** Input current $I_i$ (current summing).
- Hence the topology is Voltage-Shunt.

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b):

**Given:** $A = 10^5\ \Omega$, $\beta = 10^{-3}\ \text{S}$, $R_s = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_i = 50\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_o = 100\ \Omega$.

**Step 1 — Loop Gain:**
$$A\beta = 10^5 \times 10^{-3} = 100$$

**Step 2 — Amount of Feedback:**
$$1 + A\beta = 1 + 100 = 101$$

**Step 3 — Closed-Loop Transresistance:**
$$R_{Mf} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta} = \frac{10^5}{101} \approx 990.1\ \Omega$$

**Step 4 — Input Resistance with Feedback:**
$$R_{if} = \frac{R_i}{1 + A\beta} = \frac{50{,}000}{101} \approx 495.05\ \Omega$$

**Step 5 — Output Resistance with Feedback:**
$$R_{of} = \frac{R_o}{1 + A\beta} = \frac{100}{101} \approx 0.99\ \Omega$$

**Step 6 — Closed-Loop Voltage Gain $A_{vf} = V_o / V_s$:**
Using the source transformation $I_s = V_s / R_s$ and $A_{vf} = R_{Mf} \cdot I_s / V_s$ with input loading:

$$A_{vf} = \frac{R_{Mf}}{R_s + R_{if}} = \frac{990.1}{10{,}000 + 495.05} = \frac{990.1}{10{,}495.05} \approx 0.0943$$

**Final Answers:** $R_{Mf} \approx 990.1\ \Omega$, $R_{if} \approx 495.05\ \Omega$, $R_{of} \approx 0.99\ \Omega$, $A_{vf} \approx 0.0943$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Derive the expressions for the closed-loop transresistance, input resistance, and output resistance of a Voltage-Shunt feedback amplifier. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare the four basic feedback topologies (Voltage-Series, Voltage-Shunt, Current-Series, Current-Shunt) in a tabular form with respect to sampled quantity, mixed quantity, $R_{if}$, $R_{of}$, and stabilized gain parameter. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a):

**Step 1 — Derivation of $R_{Mf}$ (3 marks):**
$$I_i = I_s - I_f, \quad V_o = A I_i, \quad I_f = \beta V_o$$
Substituting $I_i$ and $I_f$:
$$V_o = A(I_s - \beta V_o) \Rightarrow V_o(1 + A\beta) = A I_s$$
$$\boxed{R_{Mf} = \frac{V_o}{I_s} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}}$$

**Step 2 — Derivation of $R_{if}$ (2 marks):**
Using $I_s = I_i + I_f$, $I_i = V_i/R_i$, and $I_f = \beta A V_i / R_i$:
$$I_s = \frac{V_i}{R_i}(1 + A\beta)$$
$$\boxed{R_{if} = \frac{V_i}{I_s} = \frac{R_i}{1 + A\beta}}$$

**Step 3 — Derivation of $R_{of}$ (2 marks):**
Setting $I_s = 0$ (so $I_i = -I_f$) and applying a test source at the output, the output resistance becomes:
$$\boxed{R_{of} = \frac{R_o}{1 + A\beta}}$$

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b):

| Topology | Sampled Quantity | Mixed Quantity | $R_{if}$ | $R_{of}$ | Stabilized Gain |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Voltage-Series | Voltage | Voltage | Increases | Decreases | Voltage gain $A_v$ |
| **Voltage-Shunt** | **Voltage** | **Current** | **Decreases** | **Decreases** | **Transresistance $R_M$** |
| Current-Series | Current | Voltage | Increases | Increases | Transconductance $G_M$ |
| Current-Shunt | Current | Current | Decreases | Increases | Current gain $A_i$ |

*(Full 7 marks: 1 mark per row entry, with 1 mark for the highlighted Voltage-Shunt row emphasis.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit confusion (–2 marks):** Writing $A_f$ as a dimensionless number instead of Ω.
> 2. **Mixing up topologies (–2 marks):** Calling Voltage-Shunt a "current amplifier" or claiming it stabilizes voltage gain.
> 3. **Forgetting source loading in $A_{vf}$:** The closed-loop *voltage* gain $V_o/V_s$ is **not** equal to $R_{Mf}/R_s$ unless the source loading is considered; you must use $A_{vf} = R_{Mf}/(R_s + R_{if})$.
> 4. **Ignoring feedback network loading:** The β-network loads both input and output; for full marks, the 14-mark problem should mention the loading effect on $R_i$ and $R_o$.
> 5. **Sign errors at the summing node:** Always write $I_i = I_s - I_f$ for negative feedback; mixing up signs breaks the entire derivation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Voltage-Shunt = Transimpedance Amplifier (TIA):** $A_f$ has units of **Ω**.
- **Universal feedback equation:** $A_f = A / (1 + A\beta)$ — memorize and apply to all four topologies.
- **Input resistance** decreases by $(1 + A\beta)$ — because input is a current-mixing (shunt) node.
- **Output resistance** decreases by $(1 + A\beta)$ — because output is a voltage-sampling (shunt) node.
- **Stabilization target:** The **transresistance** $R_{Mf} \approx 1/\beta$ becomes independent of active device parameters.
- **Bandwidth trade-off:** $(BW)_f = (BW)(1 + A\beta)$ — bandwidth is *increased* (gain-bandwidth product is conserved).
- **Closed-loop voltage gain** $A_{vf} = R_{Mf}/(R_s + R_{if})$ — depends on $R_s$, unlike ideal voltage amplifiers.
- **Source convention:** Voltage-Shunt naturally accepts a **current source** input; if a voltage source is given, convert via $I_s = V_s / R_s$.
- **Real-world applications:** Photodiode amplifiers, oscilloscope front-ends, optical receivers, piezoelectric charge amplifiers, ADC drivers.
- **Distinguishing feature in diagrams:** Feedback network sits in **parallel** with both the output (sampling voltage) and produces a current that is **subtracted** at the input node (mixing current).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Strategy Tip:** When given a numerical problem, always compute $A\beta$ first. If $A\beta \gg 1$, use the ideal approximation $A_f \approx 1/\beta$ to save time and verify your exact result. Show both the **exact** and the **approximate** values in your answer script — KTU examiners reward both.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Current Series and Current Shunt Feedbacks - Frequency Response
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT402 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Current Series and Current Shunt Feedbacks - Frequency Response

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Introduction to Negative Feedback Topologies

In analog electronic circuit design, the **Negative Feedback (NFB)** principle is the cornerstone of building predictable, stable, and high-performance amplifiers. The four canonical feedback topologies classified by **Sedra/Smith** are categorized based on two criteria:
1. **The sampling method** at the output — either **Voltage** or **Current**
2. **The mixing method** at the input — either **Series** or **Shunt**

The four topologies form a perfect $2 \times 2$ matrix:

| Mixing \ Sampling | Voltage Sampling | Current Sampling |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Series Mixing** | Voltage-Series (Voltage Amp) | **Current-Series (Transconductance Amp)** |
| **Shunt Mixing** | Voltage-Shunt (Transresistance Amp) | **Current-Shunt (Current Amp)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate (Module 2):** Students must be able to analyze, derive input/output resistances, and predict the **frequency response** (Bandwidth) for both **Current-Series** and **Current-Shunt** feedback configurations.

### 1.2 Core Technical Definitions

**Current-Series Feedback:**
A feedback topology where the output **current is sampled** (typically by placing a feedback resistor in series with the load) and a **voltage proportional to that output current** is fed back and applied in **series** with the input signal source. This topology is also known as a **Transconductance Amplifier (Gm-Amp)** because the forward gain $A$ has units of Siemens (S) or $\Omega^{-1}$.

> **Definition:** A Current-Series feedback amplifier has transfer characteristic $I_o / V_i$ (transconductance), input resistance **increased** by the feedback factor, and output resistance **increased** by the feedback factor.

**Current-Shunt Feedback:**
A feedback topology where the output **current is sampled** and a **current proportional to that output current** is fed back and applied in **shunt (parallel)** with the input signal source. This topology is also known as a **Current Amplifier** because the forward gain $A$ has units of Amperes per Ampere (dimensionless current ratio).

> **Definition:** A Current-Shunt feedback amplifier has transfer characteristic $I_o / I_i$ (current gain), input resistance **decreased** by the feedback factor, and output resistance **increased** by the feedback factor.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Cruise Control Analogy for Current-Series Feedback:**
> Imagine driving a car (input voltage = accelerator pedal) where the car's **speed** (output current) is being constantly monitored. A small **tachometer feedback voltage** proportional to the speed is subtracted from your pedal input. To increase the speed, you must push the pedal harder *against* this feedback voltage — like pushing against a spring. This series "spring" makes the system harder to drive (higher input resistance) and the speed becomes very stable regardless of road incline (higher output resistance — near ideal current source). The transconductance (speed/pedal) becomes rock-steady.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Audio Mixing Console Analogy for Current-Shunt Feedback:**
> Imagine a microphone (input current source) feeding into a mixing desk. The desk monitors the output current going to the speaker and "subtracts" a small portion back into the same input line. Because current is being pulled away at the same node, the input "feels" like a low-resistance path (lower input resistance). The current going to the speaker becomes extremely accurate and insensitive to the speaker impedance (higher output resistance). The current ratio (output current/input current) becomes precise.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- The factor by which feedback modifies amplifier performance is universally called the **Amount of Feedback** or **Desensitivity Factor**: $\mathbf{1 + A\beta}$ (dimensionless).
- For current sampling, the feedback network's transfer function $\beta$ is a **transresistance** for current-series (units: $\Omega$) and a **current ratio** (dimensionless) for current-shunt.
- Standard KTU assumption: All transistor internal capacitances are lumped into a single dominant pole, modeled by the **Miller Time Constant** $\tau_M$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Generalized Feedback Block Diagram Logic

Any negative feedback amplifier, regardless of topology, can be reduced to a canonical two-port model. Let the basic amplifier have open-loop gain $A$ and the feedback network sample some output quantity and return a feedback quantity $x_f$ such that the error signal $x_i = x_s - x_f$.

The closed-loop transfer function in every case is:

$$A_f = \frac{x_o}{x_s} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$
