The term $1 + A\beta$ is the **loop gain** + 1, and is the universal **desensitivity, distortion reduction, and bandwidth extension factor**.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Analysis

#### For Current-Series Feedback (Transconductance Amplifier):

**Step 1: Identify the sampling.**
A small resistor $R_E$ (emitter resistor in BJT, source resistor in FET) is placed in series with the output current path. The voltage across $R_E$ is $V_f = I_o R_E$, which serves as the feedback signal.

**Step 2: Identify the mixing.**
Since $V_f$ is a voltage, it must be mixed in **series** with the input voltage source $V_s$. The input loop becomes: $V_s - V_f \rightarrow V_i$ (the error voltage that actually drives the basic amplifier).

**Step 3: Define open-loop transconductance.**
$$A = \frac{I_o}{V_i} \quad \text{(units: Siemens, S or A/V)}$$

**Step 4: Define feedback transresistance.**
$$\beta = \frac{V_f}{I_o} = R_E \quad \text{(units: }\Omega\text{)}$$

**Step 5: Closed-loop transconductance.**
$$A_f = \frac{I_o}{V_s} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta} = \frac{A}{1 + A R_E}$$

**Step 6: Input resistance modification (Series mixing → increases).**
$$R_{if} = R_i(1 + A\beta)$$

**Step 7: Output resistance modification (Current sampling → increases).**
$$R_{of} = R_o(1 + A\beta)$$

This is the **ideal transconductance amplifier** behavior: infinite input resistance and infinite output resistance (ideal current source behavior at the output).

#### For Current-Shunt Feedback (Current Amplifier):

**Step 1: Identify the sampling.**
A feedback resistor $R_f$ connects the output node back to the input node. The output current $I_o$ flows partially through $R_f$, and a feedback current $I_f$ is sampled.

**Step 2: Identify the mixing.**
Since $I_f$ is a current, it is mixed in **shunt (parallel)** with the input current source $I_s$. By KCL at the input node: $I_i = I_s - I_f$.

**Step 3: Define open-loop current gain.**
$$A = \frac{I_o}{I_i} \quad \text{(dimensionless)}$$

**Step 4: Define feedback current ratio.**
$$\beta = \frac{I_f}{I_o} \quad \text{(dimensionless)}$$

**Step 5: Closed-loop current gain.**
$$A_f = \frac{I_o}{I_s} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$

**Step 6: Input resistance modification (Shunt mixing → decreases).**
$$R_{if} = \frac{R_i}{1 + A\beta}$$

**Step 7: Output resistance modification (Current sampling → increases).**
$$R_{of} = R_o(1 + A\beta)$$

This approximates the **ideal current amplifier**: zero input resistance and infinite output resistance.

### 2.3 Frequency Response Analysis — The Heart of This Topic

When a feedback amplifier is analyzed in the frequency domain, two crucial phenomena occur that are heavily tested in KTU exams:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pole-Splitting Phenomenon (Miller Effect Compensation):**
> The dominant pole of the amplifier **shifts to a lower frequency**, and a new **higher-frequency pole** appears. The result is that the **closed-loop bandwidth is wider** than the open-loop bandwidth by exactly the desensitivity factor $1 + A\beta$.

**Open-Loop Frequency Response (single-pole assumption):**
$$A(j\omega) = \frac{A_o}{1 + j(f/f_H)}$$

where $A_o$ is the midband open-loop gain and $f_H$ is the open-loop upper $-3\text{ dB}$ cutoff frequency.

**Closed-Loop Frequency Response (single-pole assumption):**
$$A_f(j\omega) = \frac{A_{fo}}{1 + j(f/f_{Hf})}$$

where:
- $A_{fo} = \dfrac{A_o}{1 + A_o \beta}$ is the midband closed-loop gain
- $f_{Hf} = f_H(1 + A_o \beta)$ is the closed-loop upper cutoff frequency

> **Critical Insight:** The **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP)** is conserved:
> $$GBP = A_o \cdot f_H = A_{fo} \cdot f_{Hf} = \text{constant}$$

**Pole-Splitting Mathematical Origin (Miller's Theorem):**
For a common-emitter amplifier, the Miller capacitance $C_M = C_{\mu}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ dominates. When feedback is applied:
- The effective Miller capacitance reduces to: $C_{Mf} = \dfrac{C_M}{1 + A\beta}$
- The input pole frequency becomes: $f_{p1f} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{in} C_{Mf}} = \dfrac{1 + A\beta}{2\pi R_{in} C_M}$
- A new high-frequency pole $f_{p2f}$ appears at the output node

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this table — every formula below is a guaranteed KTU question element.**

| # | Parameter | Current-Series Feedback | Current-Shunt Feedback |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Open-loop gain $A$ | Transconductance $I_o/V_i$ (S) | Current gain $I_o/I_i$ |
| 2 | Feedback $\beta$ | Transresistance $V_f/I_o = R_E$ ($\Omega$) | Current ratio $I_f/I_o$ |
| 3 | Closed-loop gain $A_f$ | $A/(1+A\beta)$ (S) | $A/(1+A\beta)$ |
| 4 | Input resistance $R_{if}$ | $R_i(1 + A\beta)$ — **increases** | $R_i/(1 + A\beta)$ — **decreases** |
| 5 | Output resistance $R_{of}$ | $R_o(1 + A\beta)$ — **increases** | $R_o(1 + A\beta)$ — **increases** |
| 6 | Open-loop bandwidth $f_H$ | $1/(2\pi R_{in}C_{in})$ | $1/(2\pi R_{in}C_{in})$ |
| 7 | Closed-loop bandwidth $f_{Hf}$ | $f_H(1 + A\beta)$ | $f_H(1 + A\beta)$ |
| 8 | Gain-Bandwidth Product | $A_o f_H = A_{fo} f_{Hf}$ | $A_o f_H = A_{fo} f_{Hf}$ |
| 9 | Effect on noise/distortion | Reduced by $1/(1 + A\beta)$ | Reduced by $1/(1 + A\beta)$ |
| 10 | Ideal amplifier type | Transconductance Amp (Gm) | Current Amplifier |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Current-Series Feedback** is the workhorse inside op-amp internal stages, operational transconductance amplifiers (OTAs), and differential pair gain stages. It stabilizes the transconductance and makes it predictable.
- **Current-Shunt Feedback** is the foundation of current-mode signal processing, current-feedback operational amplifiers (CFOAs / CFB op-amps), translinear circuits, and bias-current generators in integrated circuits.
- The **bandwidth extension** property is the single most important reason feedback is used in wideband video amplifiers, RF front-ends, and high-speed data converters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Complete Derivation: Closed-Loop Transfer Function

Starting from the canonical two-port feedback model with arbitrary signals $x_s$ (source), $x_i$ (error), $x_o$ (output), and $x_f$ (feedback):

The basic amplifier equation gives:
$$x_o = A \cdot x_i$$

The feedback network equation gives:
$$x_f = \beta \cdot x_o$$

The mixing equation gives (subtractive feedback):
$$x_i = x_s - x_f$$

Substituting $x_f$ into the mixing equation:
$$x_i = x_s - \beta \cdot x_o = x_s - \beta \cdot A \cdot x_i$$

Collecting $x_i$ terms:
$$x_i (1 + A\beta) = x_s$$

Therefore:
$$x_i = \frac{x_s}{1 + A\beta}$$

Substituting back into the basic amplifier equation:
$$x_o = A \cdot \frac{x_s}{1 + A\beta}$$

Hence, the universal closed-loop transfer function is:
$$\boxed{A_f = \frac{x_o}{x_s} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}}$$

### 3.2 Complete Derivation: Input Resistance Modification (Current-Series)

Consider a series-mixed input port. The open-loop input resistance $R_i$ relates the error voltage to the source current:
$$v_i = i_s \cdot R_i$$

The voltage across the input terminals of the feedback amplifier (from the source's perspective) is $v_s$. Because the feedback voltage $v_f$ is in series:
$$v_s = v_i + v_f$$

But $v_f = \beta \cdot x_o = \beta \cdot A \cdot v_i$, so:
$$v_s = v_i + A\beta \cdot v_i = v_i(1 + A\beta)$$

The current entering the amplifier is still $i_s = v_i / R_i$. The input resistance as seen by the source is:
$$R_{if} = \frac{v_s}{i_s} = \frac{v_i(1 + A\beta)}{v_i / R_i}$$

$$\boxed{R_{if} = R_i(1 + A\beta) \quad \text{[Current-Series, increases]}}$$

### 3.3 Complete Derivation: Input Resistance Modification (Current-Shunt)

For a shunt-mixed input port, KCL at the input node gives:
$$i_s = i_i + i_f$$

where $i_f$ is the feedback current. Since $i_f = \beta \cdot i_o = \beta \cdot A \cdot i_i$:
$$i_s = i_i + A\beta \cdot i_i = i_i(1 + A\beta)$$

The voltage across the input terminals is $v_s = i_i \cdot R_i$. The input resistance is:
$$R_{if} = \frac{v_s}{i_s} = \frac{i_i \cdot R_i}{i_i(1 + A\beta)}$$

$$\boxed{R_{if} = \frac{R_i}{1 + A\beta} \quad \text{[Current-Shunt, decreases]}}$$

### 3.4 Complete Derivation: Output Resistance Modification (Current Sampling)

To find $R_{of}$, the output is excited by an independent test current $i_t$ with the input source deactivated ($x_s = 0$). The error signal becomes $x_i = -x_f = -\beta \cdot i_t$.

The amplifier produces an output current: $i_{o,amp} = A \cdot x_i = -A\beta \cdot i_t$

Applying KCL at the output node (the test current $i_t$ must supply the amplifier's output current plus flow through $R_o$):
$$i_t = i_{o,amp} + \frac{v_t}{R_o} = -A\beta \cdot i_t + \frac{v_t}{R_o}$$

Rearranging:
$$i_t(1 + A\beta) = \frac{v_t}{R_o}$$

$$v_t = i_t \cdot R_o(1 + A\beta)$$

$$\boxed{R_{of} = \frac{v_t}{i_t} = R_o(1 + A\beta) \quad \text{[Both Current-Series and Current-Shunt, increases]}}$$

### 3.5 Complete Derivation: Frequency Response and Bandwidth Extension

Assume the open-loop amplifier has a single dominant pole. Its transfer function is:
$$A(s) = \frac{A_o}{1 + s/\omega_H}$$

where $\omega_H = 2\pi f_H$ is the open-loop radian bandwidth. The closed-loop transfer function is:
$$A_f(s) = \frac{A(s)}{1 + A(s)\beta}$$

Substituting:
$$A_f(s) = \frac{\dfrac{A_o}{1 + s/\omega_H}}{1 + \dfrac{A_o \beta}{1 + s/\omega_H}}$$

$$A_f(s) = \frac{A_o}{1 + s/\omega_H + A_o \beta}$$

$$A_f(s) = \frac{A_o}{(1 + A_o \beta)\left[1 + \dfrac{s}{\omega_H(1 + A_o \beta)}\right]}$$

Defining the closed-loop midband gain $A_{fo} = \dfrac{A_o}{1 + A_o \beta}$:

$$A_f(s) = \frac{A_{fo}}{1 + s/\omega_{Hf}}$$

where the closed-loop bandwidth is:
$$\omega_{Hf} = \omega_H(1 + A_o \beta) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{f_{Hf} = f_H(1 + A_o \beta)}$$

**Verification of GBP conservation:**
$$GBP = A_o \cdot f_H = \frac{A_o}{1 + A_o\beta} \cdot f_H(1 + A_o\beta) = A_{fo} \cdot f_{Hf} = \text{constant} \quad \blacksquare$$

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A current-series feedback amplifier has an open-loop transconductance $A = 100$ mA/V, open-loop input resistance $R_i = 2$ k$\Omega$, open-loop output resistance $R_o = 50$ k$\Omega$, and feedback resistor $R_E = 100$ $\Omega$. The dominant open-loop pole is at $f_H = 1$ MHz. Calculate: (a) closed-loop transconductance, (b) $R_{if}$, (c) $R_{of}$, (d) closed-loop bandwidth $f_{Hf}$.

**Solution:**

**Step (a):** Loop gain calculation.
$$A\beta = (100 \times 10^{-3}) \times 100 = 10$$
$$1 + A\beta = 11$$
$$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta} = \frac{100 \text{ mA/V}}{11} = 9.09 \text{ mA/V}$$

**Step (b):** Input resistance.
$$R_{if} = R_i(1 + A\beta) = 2000 \times 11 = 22 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step (c):** Output resistance.
$$R_{of} = R_o(1 + A\beta) = 50000 \times 11 = 550 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step (d):** Closed-loop bandwidth.
$$f_{Hf} = f_H(1 + A\beta) = 1 \text{ MHz} \times 11 = 11 \text{ MHz}$$

**Verification:**
$$GBP_{\text{open}} = 100 \text{ mA/V} \times 1 \text{ MHz} = 100 \text{ (mA/V)·MHz}$$
$$GBP_{\text{closed}} = 9.09 \text{ mA/V} \times 11 \text{ MHz} \approx 100 \text{ (mA/V)·MHz} \quad \checkmark$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Canonical Block Diagram of a Negative Feedback Amplifier

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeXS[Source Signal Xs] --> nodeMixer[Subtractor Mixer Node]
    nodeMixer --> nodeXI[Error Signal Xi]
    nodeXI --> nodeAmpA[Basic Amplifier Gain A]
    nodeAmpA --> nodeXO[Output Signal Xo]
    nodeXO --> nodeLoad[Connected Load]
    nodeXO --> nodeFB[Feedback Network Beta]
    nodeFB --> nodeXF[Feedback Signal Xf]
    nodeXF --> nodeMixer
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Source $X_s$ enters the mixer, where feedback $X_f$ is subtracted to produce the error $X_i$. The basic amplifier $A$ amplifies $X_i$ to produce $X_o$, which simultaneously drives the load and the feedback network $\beta$. The feedback signal closes the loop.

### 4.2 Current-Series Feedback Topology — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeVs[Input Voltage Source Vs] --> nodeRif[Input Port Rif INCREASED]
    nodeRif --> nodeA1[Basic Amplifier A transconductance]
    nodeA1 --> nodeIo[Output Current Io]
    nodeIo --> nodeLoadRL[Load RL]
    nodeIo --> nodeRE[Sampling Resistor RE]
    nodeRE --> nodeVf[Feedback Voltage Vf]
    nodeVf --> nodeRif
    nodeIo --> nodeRo[Output Port Rof INCREASED]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The current $I_o$ flowing through the load also flows through $R_E$, producing $V_f = I_o R_E$. This voltage is fed back in series with the source. The input resistance $R_{if}$ rises because the source must now supply $V_i$ plus overcome $V_f$. The output resistance $R_{of}$ rises because the amplifier behaves more like an ideal current source.

### 4.3 Current-Shunt Feedback Topology — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeIs[Input Current Source Is] --> nodeRif2[Input Port Rif DECREASED]
    nodeRif2 --> nodeA2[Basic Amplifier A current gain]
    nodeA2 --> nodeIo2[Output Current Io]
    nodeIo2 --> nodeLoadRL2[Load RL]
    nodeIo2 --> nodeRf[Sampling Resistor Rf]
    nodeRf --> nodeIf[Feedback Current If]
    nodeIf --> nodeRif2
    nodeIo2 --> nodeRo2[Output Port Rof INCREASED]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The output current $I_o$ flowing through the load also passes partially through $R_f$, producing a feedback current $I_f$ that is reinjected into the input node. By KCL, $I_i = I_s - I_f$. The input node "sees" a low-resistance path. The output looks more like an ideal current source.

### 4.4 Frequency Response Comparison: Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph OpenLoop[Open Loop Response]
        nodeA1[Midband Gain Ao] --> nodeA2[Bandwidth fH]
    end
    subgraph ClosedLoop[Closed Loop Response]
        nodeB1[Midband Gain Afo = Ao divided by 1+AoBeta] --> nodeB2[Bandwidth fHf = fH times 1+AoBeta]
    end
    OpenLoop --> ClosedLoop
    nodeNote1[GBP Constant: Ao times fH equals Afo times fHf] --> ClosedLoop
```

> **Reading the diagram:** When feedback is applied, the gain drops by $1/(1+A\beta)$ but the bandwidth expands by the *same* factor $1+A\beta$. The product (Gain × Bandwidth) is invariant — this is the **Gain-Bandwidth Product conservation principle**.

### 4.5 Pole-Splitting Phenomenon Visualization

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeOpenPole[Open Loop Single Pole at fH] --> nodeAction[Apply Negative Feedback]
    nodeAction --> nodePole1[New Dominant Pole fH divided by 1+AoBeta LOWER frequency]
    nodeAction --> nodePole2[New Non Dominant Pole at HIGHER frequency]
    nodePole1 --> nodeResult[Effective Bandwidth widens to fHf]
    nodePole2 --> nodeResult
```

> **Reading the diagram:** A single open-loop pole splits into a low-frequency dominant pole and a high-frequency non-dominant pole when feedback is engaged. This is **pole-splitting** (or pole-separation). The net effect is bandwidth enhancement.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*What is meant by current-series feedback? State two of its effects on the amplifier performance.*

**Model Answer:**
Current-series feedback is a negative feedback topology in which the output **current is sampled** and a **voltage proportional to that current is fed back in series** with the input signal. It is also called a transconductance amplifier.

**Two effects:**
1. The input resistance is **increased** by the factor $(1 + A\beta)$.
2. The output resistance is **increased** by the factor $(1 + A\beta)$, making the output approach an ideal current source.

*(Acceptable alternatives: closed-loop gain becomes A/(1+Aβ); bandwidth is extended by the same factor; distortion is reduced.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Compare current-shunt and current-series feedback topologies in terms of input resistance and output resistance modifications.*

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Current-Series | Current-Shunt |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Input Resistance $R_{if}$ | $R_i(1+A\beta)$ — **Increases** | $R_i/(1+A\beta)$ — **Decreases** |
| Output Resistance $R_{of}$ | $R_o(1+A\beta)$ — **Increases** | $R_o(1+A\beta)$ — **Increases** |
| Sampling | Output current | Output current |
| Mixing | Series (voltage) | Shunt (current) |

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Current-Series Focus

**[KTU University Exam - July 2024]**
**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a current-series feedback amplifier and derive expressions for closed-loop transconductance gain, input resistance, and output resistance. **7 marks**

**(b)** An amplifier with open-loop transconductance $A = 50$ mA/V, $R_i = 1$ k$\Omega$, $R_o = 40$ k$\Omega$, and dominant pole at $f_H = 2$ MHz uses current-series feedback with $R_E = 200$ $\Omega$. Calculate the closed-loop gain, $R_{if}$, $R_{of}$, and the new upper $-3$ dB frequency. **7 marks**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — Block Diagram Description:** *(1 mark)*
The block diagram consists of an input voltage source $V_s$ connected to a series-mixing node, a basic transconductance amplifier with gain $A = I_o/V_i$, an output current $I_o$ driving the load, and a feedback resistor $R_E$ in the output current path generating $V_f = I_o R_E$, which is fed back in series with $V_s$.

**Step 2 — Closed-Loop Gain Derivation:** *(2 marks)*

The mixing equation gives:
$$V_i = V_s - V_f = V_s - I_o R_E$$

The amplifier equation gives:
$$I_o = A \cdot V_i$$

Substituting:
$$I_o = A(V_s - I_o R_E)$$
$$I_o + A R_E I_o = A V_s$$
$$I_o(1 + A R_E) = A V_s$$

$$\boxed{A_f = \frac{I_o}{V_s} = \frac{A}{1 + A R_E}}$$

**Step 3 — Input Resistance Derivation:** *(2 marks)*

$$R_{if} = \frac{V_s}{I_s} = \frac{V_i(1 + A\beta)}{I_s} = \frac{I_s R_i (1 + A\beta)}{I_s}$$
$$\boxed{R_{if} = R_i(1 + A R_E)}$$

**Step 4 — Output Resistance Derivation:** *(2 marks)*
Apply a test current $I_t$ at the output with $V_s = 0$, then by KCL and amplifier action, $R_{of} = R_o(1 + A R_E)$.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given:** $A = 50$ mA/V $= 0.05$ A/V, $R_E = 200$ $\Omega$, $R_i = 1$ k$\Omega$, $R_o = 40$ k$\Omega$, $f_H = 2$ MHz.

**Loop gain:** $A\beta = A R_E = 0.05 \times 200 = 10$
**Desensitivity factor:** $1 + A\beta = 11$

**Closed-loop transconductance:** $A_f = 50 \text{ mA/V} / 11 = 4.545$ mA/V
**Input resistance:** $R_{if} = 1000 \times 11 = 11$ k$\Omega$
**Output resistance:** $R_{of} = 40000 \times 11 = 440$ k$\Omega$
**Closed-loop bandwidth:** $f_{Hf} = 2 \text{ MHz} \times 11 = 22$ MHz

**GBP check:** Open-loop $GBP = 50 \times 2 = 100$ (mA/V)·MHz. Closed-loop $GBP = 4.545 \times 22 \approx 100$ ✓

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Current-Shunt Focus

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain current-shunt feedback. Derive the expression for closed-loop current gain and show that both input and output resistance modifications are governed by the same loop-gain factor. **7 marks**

**(b)** A current-shunt feedback amplifier has $A = 200$, $\beta = 0.04$, $R_i = 5$ k$\Omega$, $R_o = 20$ k$\Omega$, and open-loop bandwidth $f_H = 500$ kHz. Determine the closed-loop current gain, $R_{if}$, $R_{of}$, and the new $-3$ dB bandwidth. Also verify the GBP is conserved. **7 marks**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Block diagram explanation:** *(1 mark)*
A current source $I_s$ drives the input node. The output current $I_o$ is sampled via a feedback resistor $R_f$, producing a feedback current $I_f$ that is reinjected at the input node (shunt mixing). The error current $I_i = I_s - I_f$ drives the basic current amplifier of gain $A$.

**Closed-loop current gain derivation:** *(3 marks)*

$$I_i = I_s - I_f, \quad I_f = \beta I_o, \quad I_o = A I_i$$
$$I_o = A(I_s - \beta I_o) = A I_s - A\beta I_o$$
$$I_o(1 + A\beta) = A I_s$$
$$\boxed{A_f = \frac{I_o}{I_s} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}}$$

**Input resistance:** $R_{if} = R_i / (1 + A\beta)$ — **decreases** *(1 mark)*
**Output resistance:** $R_{of} = R_o(1 + A\beta)$ — **increases** *(1 mark)*
**Same factor:** Both modifications are governed by the same loop-gain factor $(1 + A\beta)$ — one in the denominator (shunt input) and one in the numerator (current-sampled output). *(1 mark)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given:** $A = 200$, $\beta = 0.04$, $R_i = 5$ k$\Omega$, $R_o = 20$ k$\Omega$, $f_H = 500$ kHz.

**Loop gain:** $A\beta = 200 \times 0.04 = 8$
**Desensitivity factor:** $1 + A\beta = 9$

**Closed-loop current gain:** $A_f = 200 / 9 \approx 22.22$
**Input resistance:** $R_{if} = 5000 / 9 \approx 555.6$ $\Omega$
**Output resistance:** $R_{of} = 20000 \times 9 = 180$ k$\Omega$
**Closed-loop bandwidth:** $f_{Hf} = 500 \text{ kHz} \times 9 = 4.5$ MHz

**GBP conservation check:**
- Open-loop: $GBP = 200 \times 500 \text{ kHz} = 100,000 \text{ kHz} = 10^5 \cdot A \cdot \text{Hz}$
- Closed-loop: $GBP = 22.22 \times 4500 \text{ kHz} = 99,990 \text{ kHz} \approx 10^5 \cdot A \cdot \text{Hz}$ ✓

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Mixing up the four topologies.** Students often confuse series mixing (voltage subtraction) with shunt mixing (current subtraction). Always identify the feedback signal type FIRST, then the mixing method.
> 2. **Wrong units of $\beta$.** For current-series, $\beta$ is in $\Omega$ (transresistance), not dimensionless. For current-shunt, $\beta$ is dimensionless.
> 3. **Forgetting the $(1 + A\beta)$ factor applies to BANDWIDTH too.** Many students correctly compute the new gain but forget that bandwidth increases by the SAME factor.
> 4. **Writing $R_{if} = R_i(1 + A\beta)$ for current-shunt.** This is the most common error. Shunt mixing DECREASES input resistance — it should be $R_{if} = R_i/(1 + A\beta)$.
> 5. **Not verifying GBP.** A two-mark verification step is often asked explicitly; not showing it loses easy marks.
> 6. **Ignoring the dominant pole assumption.** KTU questions often state "single-pole" — never derive a two-pole transfer function unless explicitly told.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam**

- ✅ **Current-Series** = Current sampling (via series resistor like $R_E$) + Series mixing at input → **Transconductance Amplifier**.
- ✅ **Current-Shunt** = Current sampling (via feedback resistor $R_f$) + Shunt mixing at input → **Current Amplifier**.
- ✅ The universal closed-loop gain: $A_f = A / (1 + A\beta)$.
- ✅ The desensitivity factor is $1 + A\beta$ — it controls **everything**: gain reduction, bandwidth increase, resistance modifications, distortion reduction, and noise reduction.
- ✅ **Current-Series input resistance INCREASES** by $1 + A\beta$ (because series mixing).
- ✅ **Current-Shunt input resistance DECREASES** by $1 + A\beta$ (because shunt mixing).
- ✅ **Both topologies' output resistance INCREASES** by $1 + A\beta$ (because current sampling).
- ✅ **Bandwidth extends** by $1 + A\beta$: $f_{Hf} = f_H(1 + A\beta)$.
- ✅ **Gain-Bandwidth Product is conserved** — this is the most-tested KTU fact.
- ✅ **Pole-splitting** is the physical origin of bandwidth extension: a single open-loop pole splits into a lower dominant pole and a higher non-dominant pole.
- ✅ **Ideal targets:** Current-Series → $R_{if} \to \infty$, $R_{of} \to \infty$ (ideal transconductance amplifier). Current-Shunt → $R_{if} \to 0$, $R_{of} \to \infty$ (ideal current amplifier).
- ✅ For KTU derivations, always show the loop-gain calculation, then the four key outputs: $A_f$, $R_{if}$, $R_{of}$, and $f_{Hf}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Virtual Ground - Closed Loop Gain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT402 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Concept of Virtual Ground - Closed Loop Gain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of operational amplifiers (op-amps) operating under **negative feedback**, the **Virtual Ground** is the conceptual node at the **inverting input terminal ($V^{-}$)** which *appears* to be at the same electric potential as the **non-inverting input terminal ($V^{+}$)**, even though it is **not physically shorted** to the reference potential. When the non-inverting terminal is tied to actual ground ($V^{+} = 0\text{ V}$), the inverting terminal $V^{-}$ is forced to track this $0\text{ V}$ potential — hence the term *virtual* (imaginary) ground.

The **Closed Loop Gain ($A_{CL}$)** of an op-amp is the gain of the amplifier when a feedback network is connected between the output and one of the input terminals, forming a closed loop. It is a deterministic, externally controlled parameter, in sharp contrast to the huge, uncontrolled **Open Loop Gain ($A_{OL}$)** which typically exceeds **$10^{5}$** for a standard 741-type op-amp.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The concept of *Virtual Ground* is the single most important assumption used to solve **every** op-amp circuit problem in KTU examinations. Mastering it is mandatory for Module 2, 3, and 4 problem solving.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two water tanks connected at their bases by a wide, flexible hose (the feedback path). Tank A (the $V^{+}$ input) is held exactly at sea level by a fixed support. Tank B (the $V^{-}$ input) is not physically bolted down, but because of the wide hose, the water level in Tank B is forced to remain exactly equal to the water level in Tank A. So Tank B *behaves* as if it were grounded to sea level, even though no physical ground wire is attached to it.

- The **op-amp** is the *active controller* that pumps water between the tanks to maintain this level equality.
- The **hose** is the **negative feedback** mechanism.
- **Sea level** is the **ground reference (0 V)**.

This "forced equality" is what we call **Virtual Short** between the two input terminals. When the non-inverting input is genuinely at ground, the inverting input is **virtually grounded**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** *Virtual Ground* applies only to the **inverting terminal** in standard inverting configurations. *Virtual Short* ($V^{-} = V^{+}$) is the more general concept applicable to all negative-feedback op-amp circuits.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Open Loop Gain ($A_{OL}$):** $2 \times 10^{5}$ for $\mu$A741
- **Ideal Op-Amp Input Impedance ($Z_{in}$):** $\infty$ ($\Omega$)
- **Ideal Op-Amp Output Impedance ($Z_{out}$):** $0$ ($\Omega$)
- **Input Bias Current:** $0$ A (ideal)
- **Input Offset Voltage:** $0$ V (ideal)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Golden Rules of Ideal Op-Amp Analysis

These two rules are the *foundation* of every closed-loop op-amp problem in KTU exams.

1. **Rule 1 — Infinite Input Impedance (No Current Rule):**
   Since an ideal op-amp has infinite input resistance, **no current flows into either the inverting or non-inverting input terminals**.
   $$i^{+} = i^{-} = 0 \text{ A}$$

2. **Rule 2 — Virtual Short (Zero Differential Voltage Rule):**
   Under negative feedback, the differential input voltage is driven to zero because $A_{OL} \to \infty$ while the output is finite. This means the two input terminals sit at the *same potential*.
   $$V^{-} = V^{+}$$

### 2.2 Why Does Virtual Ground Exist? (The "Why" Behind the Concept)

Consider the open-loop transfer equation of an op-amp:
$$V_{out} = A_{OL} \cdot (V^{+} - V^{-})$$

- If $V^{+} - V^{-} = 1 \text{ \mu V}$ and $A_{OL} = 2 \times 10^{5}$, then $V_{out} = 0.2 \text{ V}$ — completely reasonable.
- For the output to be a *finite* value (say 5 V), the input differential must be:
  $$V^{+} - V^{-} = \frac{V_{out}}{A_{OL}} = \frac{5}{2 \times 10^{5}} = 25 \text{ \mu V} \approx 0 \text{ V}$$

So when $V^{+} = 0\text{ V}$ (grounded), the inverting terminal $V^{-}$ sits at **approximately $0 \text{ V}$** — virtually grounded.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Point:** The virtual ground is *not* an exact $0 \text{ V}$. It is offset by a few microvolts called the **input offset voltage**. For KTU problem-solving, we treat it as **exactly $0 \text{ V}$** because the deviation is negligible compared to circuit signal levels.

### 2.3 Closed Loop Gain — The Concept

When a feedback resistor $R_f$ is connected from output to the inverting input, the enormous open-loop gain is "sacrificed" in exchange for **predictability, linearity, and stability**. The closed-loop gain $A_{CL}$ is determined *entirely by external passive components* (resistors and capacitors), **not by the op-amp itself**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Configuration | Closed-Loop Gain $A_{CL}$ | Input Impedance | Output Impedance | Phase |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Inverting Amplifier | $A_{CL} = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}}$ | $R_{in}$ (Low) | Very Low | $180^{\circ}$ Inverted |
| Non-Inverting Amplifier | $A_{CL} = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}}$ | Very High (ideally $\infty$) | Very Low | $0^{\circ}$ In-Phase |
| Voltage Follower (Unity Buffer) | $A_{CL} = 1$ | Very High (ideally $\infty$) | Very Low | $0^{\circ}$ In-Phase |
| Summing Amplifier (n inputs) | $V_{out} = -\left(\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}V_1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_2}V_2 + \cdots + \dfrac{R_f}{R_n}V_n\right)$ | Depends on $R_i$ | Very Low | $180^{\circ}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The closed-loop gain principle is the foundation of every commercial amplifier IC. Audio amplifiers, ECG machines, signal conditioners in industrial automation, and active filters in communication systems all rely on this *external-component-defined gain* concept for precise, repeatable performance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Inverting Amplifier Closed-Loop Gain (Using Virtual Ground)

**Circuit Description:** Input voltage $V_{in}$ is applied through $R_{in}$ to the inverting input. Feedback resistor $R_f$ connects output to the inverting input. The non-inverting input is grounded.

**Step 1 — Apply Virtual Ground Concept:**
Since $V^{+}$ is grounded, $V^{+} = 0$. By Rule 2, $V^{-} = V^{+} = 0$. Therefore, the inverting input is at virtual ground.

$$V^{-} = 0 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at the Inverting Node ($V^{-}$):**
The currents entering the node must equal currents leaving. The current from $V_{in}$ through $R_{in}$ plus the current from $V_{out}$ through $R_f$ must equal the current entering the op-amp input. By Rule 1, no current enters the op-amp.

$$I_{in} + I_f = 0$$

**Step 3 — Express Each Current Using Ohm's Law:**
Since the inverting node is at $0 \text{ V}$:

$$I_{in} = \frac{V_{in} - V^{-}}{R_{in}} = \frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_{in}} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_{in}}$$

$$I_f = \frac{V_{out} - V^{-}}{R_f} = \frac{V_{out} - 0}{R_f} = \frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into KCL Equation:**

$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_{in}} + \frac{V_{out}}{R_f} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the Closed-Loop Gain ($V_{out}/V_{in}$):**

$$\frac{V_{out}}{R_f} = -\frac{V_{in}}{R_{in}}$$

$$V_{out} = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in}} \cdot V_{in}$$

$$\boxed{A_{CL} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in}}}$$

The **negative sign** indicates a $180^{\circ}$ phase inversion between input and output — a defining characteristic of the inverting configuration.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Non-Inverting Amplifier Closed-Loop Gain

**Circuit Description:** Input $V_{in}$ is applied directly to the non-inverting terminal. $R_{in}$ connects from the inverting terminal to ground, and $R_f$ provides feedback from output to the inverting terminal.

**Step 1 — Apply Virtual Short:**
By Rule 2, $V^{-} = V^{+} = V_{in}$. The inverting terminal is *not* at ground in this configuration — it sits at $V_{in}$.

$$V^{-} = V_{in}$$

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at the Inverting Node:**
The current through $R_{in}$ (from $V^{-}$ to ground) equals the current through $R_f$ (from $V_{out}$ to $V^{-}$), since no current enters the op-amp.

$$\frac{V^{-} - 0}{R_{in}} = \frac{V_{out} - V^{-}}{R_f}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $V^{-} = V_{in}$:**

$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_{in}} = \frac{V_{out} - V_{in}}{R_f}$$

**Step 4 — Cross-Multiply:**

$$V_{in} \cdot R_f = R_{in} \cdot (V_{out} - V_{in})$$

$$V_{in} \cdot R_f = R_{in} \cdot V_{out} - R_{in} \cdot V_{in}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $V_{out}$:**

$$R_{in} \cdot V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot R_f + R_{in} \cdot V_{in}$$

$$R_{in} \cdot V_{out} = V_{in} (R_f + R_{in})$$

$$\boxed{A_{CL} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}}$$

The gain is always **positive and greater than 1**, meaning the output is in-phase with the input and amplified. Setting $R_f = 0$ or $R_{in} = \infty$ gives the special **Voltage Follower** with $A_{CL} = 1$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Proof That Virtual Ground Holds True

Starting from the open-loop equation and applying negative feedback:

$$V_{out} = A_{OL}(V^{+} - V^{-})$$

For an inverting amplifier with $V^{+} = 0$ and output $V_{out}$ (finite), solving for $V^{-}$:

$$V^{-} = V^{+} - \frac{V_{out}}{A_{OL}} = 0 - \frac{V_{out}}{A_{OL}}$$

$$V^{-} = -\frac{V_{out}}{A_{OL}}$$

Substituting $A_{OL} = 2 \times 10^{5}$ and assuming a typical output $V_{out} = -10 \text{ V}$ (from $V_{in} = 1 \text{ V}$, $R_f = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_{in} = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$):

$$V^{-} = -\frac{-10}{2 \times 10^{5}} = \frac{10}{200000} = 50 \text{ \mu V}$$

This is **effectively zero** for all practical KTU problem-solving purposes. ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Inverting Amplifier with Virtual Ground — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeVin["Vin"] --> nodeRin["Rin"]
    nodeRin --> nodeVneg["Inverting Input Vminus"]
    nodeVplus["Non-Inverting Vplus equals 0V Ground"] --> nodeOp["Op-Amp"]
    nodeVneg --> nodeOp
    nodeOp --> nodeVout["Vout"]
    nodeVout --> nodeRf["Rf Feedback Resistor"]
    nodeRf --> nodeVneg
    nodeGND["Ground Reference 0V"] --- nodeVplus
    nodeVG["Virtual Ground at Vminus"] -.-> nodeVneg
    nodeVG2["Vminus = Vplus = 0V Forced by Feedback"] -.-> nodeVneg
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of Negative Feedback System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Input Signal Source"] --> nodeB["Summing Junction"]
    nodeC["Feedback Network Beta"] --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeD["Error Signal Vplus minus Vminus"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Open Loop Amplifier AOL"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Output Vout"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Load"]
    nodeF --> nodeC
    nodeH["Virtual Ground: Vminus tracks Vplus"] -.-> nodeD
```

### 4.3 Comparison: Inverting vs. Non-Inverting Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INV["INVERTING MODE"]
        iA["Vin"] --> iB["Rin"] --> iC["Vminus Node Virtual Ground"]
        iC --> iOp["Op-Amp"] --> iOut["Vout Inverted 180deg"]
        iRf["Rf"] --> iC
    end
    subgraph NON["NON-INVERTING MODE"]
        nA["Vin"] --> nB["Vplus Node"]
        nC["Vminus follows Vplus Virtual Short"]
        nB --> nOp["Op-Amp"] --> nOut["Vout In-Phase 0deg"]
        nRin2["Rin to Ground"] --> nC
        nRf2["Rf Feedback"] --> nC
    end
```

### 4.4 Virtual Ground Node Analysis (Functional View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["Input Source"] -- current I_in through R_in --> node1["Virtual Ground Node Vminus = 0V"]
    fb["Feedback Path R_f from Vout"] -- current I_f --> node1
    node1 -- "No current enters op-amp" --> opamp["Op-Amp Input"]
    gnd["Physical Ground at Vplus"] -. "potential reference" .- node1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "virtual ground" in the context of an op-amp. Under what condition does it occur?**

**Model Answer:**
Virtual ground is a concept in op-amp circuits where the inverting input terminal ($V^{-}$) is maintained at the same electric potential as the non-inverting input terminal ($V^{+}$), even though it is not physically connected to ground.

It occurs under the following conditions:
1. The op-amp operates in the **linear region**.
2. **Negative feedback** is applied from output to the inverting input.
3. The non-inverting input is connected to **actual ground** ($V^{+} = 0 \text{ V}$).
4. The op-amp has a very **high open-loop gain** ($A_{OL} \to \infty$).

When these conditions are met, $V^{-} = V^{+} = 0 \text{ V}$, making the inverting node act as a *virtual* (imaginary) ground.

**Keywords for 3 marks:** Definition (1M), Two conditions of occurrence (1M), Virtual short equation $V^{-} = V^{+}$ (1M).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State the two golden rules used in the ideal op-amp analysis of closed-loop circuits.**

**Model Answer:**
The two golden rules of ideal op-amp analysis are:

1. **Rule 1 (Infinite Input Impedance):** No current flows into the input terminals of the op-amp.
   $$i^{+} = i^{-} = 0 \text{ A}$$

2. **Rule 2 (Zero Differential Voltage):** Under negative feedback, the voltage difference between the two input terminals is virtually zero, i.e., the inputs are at the same potential.
   $$V^{+} = V^{-}$$

These rules are valid only when the op-amp operates in the **linear region with negative feedback** and has ideal characteristics ($A_{OL} = \infty$, $Z_{in} = \infty$, $Z_{out} = 0$).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A — Inverting Amplifier Design Problem

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Design an inverting amplifier using an ideal op-amp with closed-loop gain of **$-20$** and input resistance of **$10 \text{ k}\Omega$.** Draw the circuit diagram and derive the output voltage expression using the concept of virtual ground. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the same circuit, if $V_{in} = 250 \text{ mV}$ (DC), calculate $V_{out}$. If $R_{in}$ is now changed to **$5 \text{ k}\Omega$**, what is the new $V_{out}$? Comment on the input impedance of this amplifier. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Design and Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Identify Required Component Values:**
Given: $A_{CL} = -20$, $R_{in} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$.

Using the inverting amplifier gain formula:

$$A_{CL} = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$$

$$-20 = -\frac{R_f}{10 \text{ k}\Omega}$$

$$R_f = 20 \times 10 \text{ k}\Omega = 200 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 2 — Derive Output Voltage Using Virtual Ground:**

Apply KCL at the inverting node ($V^{-} = 0 \text{ V}$ due to virtual ground):

$$\frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_{in}} + \frac{V_{out} - 0}{R_f} = 0$$

Solving for $V_{out}$:

$$V_{out} = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in}} \cdot V_{in} = -\frac{200 \text{ k}\Omega}{10 \text{ k}\Omega} \cdot V_{in} = -20 V_{in}$$

**Circuit Diagram (Textual Representation):**
- $V_{in} \to R_{in} (10 \text{ k}\Omega) \to V^{-} \text{ (Node)}$
- $V_{out} \to R_f (200 \text{ k}\Omega) \to V^{-}$
- $V^{+}$ connected to ground
- Op-amp output gives $V_{out} = -20 V_{in}$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

**Case 1:** $V_{in} = 250 \text{ mV} = 0.25 \text{ V}$, $R_{in} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$:

$$V_{out} = -20 \times 0.25 \text{ V} = -5 \text{ V}$$

**Case 2:** $V_{in} = 250 \text{ mV}$, $R_{in} = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_f = 200 \text{ k}\Omega$ (unchanged):

$$A_{CL} = -\frac{200 \text{ k}\Omega}{5 \text{ k}\Omega} = -40$$

$$V_{out} = -40 \times 0.25 \text{ V} = -10 \text{ V}$$

**Comment on Input Impedance:**
The input impedance of the inverting amplifier is approximately equal to $R_{in}$ because the inverting input is at virtual ground. So in Case 1, $Z_{in} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, and in Case 2, $Z_{in} = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$. This is a **drawback** of the inverting amplifier — its input impedance is limited by $R_{in}$ and cannot be made arbitrarily large.

---

#### Question B — Non-Inverting Amplifier with Mixed Concepts

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 / Supplementary Exam 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the closed-loop gain of a **non-inverting amplifier** using the virtual short concept. Show that the gain is always greater than or equal to unity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A non-inverting amplifier has $R_1 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$ (resistor to ground) and $R_f = 9 \text{ k}\Omega$ (feedback resistor). Calculate the closed-loop gain and output voltage for $V_{in} = 1.5 \text{ V}$. Compare its input impedance with the inverting amplifier. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

In a non-inverting amplifier, the input $V_{in}$ is applied to $V^{+}$. By Rule 2 (virtual short), $V^{-} = V^{+} = V_{in}$. KCL at $V^{-}$ node (no current into op-amp):

$$\frac{V^{-}}{R_1} + \frac{V^{-} - V_{out}}{R_f} = 0$$

Substituting $V^{-} = V_{in}$:

$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} + \frac{V_{in} - V_{out}}{R_f} = 0$$

$$\frac{V_{in} \cdot R_f + V_{in} \cdot R_1 - V_{out} \cdot R_1}{R_1 \cdot R_f} = 0$$

$$V_{in}(R_f + R_1) = V_{out} \cdot R_1$$

$$A_{CL} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

Since $\frac{R_f}{R_1} \geq 0$ (resistors are non-negative), $A_{CL} \geq 1$. Hence proved.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

Given: $R_1 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_f = 9 \text{ k}\Omega$, $V_{in} = 1.5 \text{ V}$.

$$A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{9 \text{ k}\Omega}{1 \text{ k}\Omega} = 1 + 9 = 10$$

$$V_{out} = 10 \times 1.5 \text{ V} = 15 \text{ V}$$

**Input Impedance Comparison:**

| Parameter | Inverting Amplifier | Non-Inverting Amplifier |
|---|---|---|
| Input Impedance | Equal to $R_{in}$ (Low, e.g., $10 \text{ k}\Omega$) | Extremely High (ideally $\infty$) |
| Suitability for high-Z sources | Poor | Excellent |

**Conclusion:** The non-inverting amplifier is preferred when the source has high output impedance, because it does not load the source.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Inverting Amplifier:** Students frequently forget the **negative sign** in $A_{CL} = -R_f/R_{in}$. Always double-check.
> 2. **Confusing Virtual Ground with Virtual Short:** Virtual ground is a *specific case* where $V^{+} = 0$ and $V^{-}$ follows it. Virtual short is the *general* rule $V^{-} = V^{+}$. Examiners may deduct 1 mark for using these interchangeably.
> 3. **Applying KCL Incorrectly:** Remember that **no current enters the op-amp**. If you write a KCL equation that includes current going into the op-amp input, you will lose marks.
> 4. **Forgetting to Ground $V^{+}$:** In an inverting amplifier, if $V^{+}$ is left floating (ungrounded), the assumption of virtual ground fails. Always mention $V^{+}$ is grounded.
> 5. **Closed-Loop vs. Open-Loop Mix-up:** In KTU numericals, if the problem gives an open-loop gain value (e.g., $A_{OL} = 10^{5}$), do not plug it into the closed-loop formula. Use it only if asked about offset or non-ideal behavior.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Virtual Ground & Closed Loop Gain**

- **Virtual Ground Definition:** A node at the inverting input of an op-amp that *appears* to be at $0 \text{ V}$ because of negative feedback, but is *not physically* connected to ground.
- **Two Golden Rules:** (1) $i^{-} = i^{+} = 0$ (2) $V^{-} = V^{+}$.
- **Conditions for Virtual Ground:** Negative feedback, $V^{+}$ grounded, op-amp in linear region.
- **Inverting Amplifier Gain:** $A_{CL} = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}}$ (180° phase shift, low input impedance).
- **Non-Inverting Amplifier Gain:** $A_{CL} = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ (0° phase shift, high input impedance).
- **Voltage Follower:** Special case with $A_{CL} = 1$, used as a buffer for impedance matching.
- **Closed-Loop Gain is Determined by External Components**, not the op-amp's internal $A_{OL}$.
- **Trade-off:** Closed-loop stability and predictability come at the cost of reduced gain compared to $A_{OL}$.
- **Input Impedance Trade-off:** Inverting amplifier has low $Z_{in}$; non-inverting has very high $Z_{in}$ — a key KTU exam distinction.
- **KCL at $V^{-}$ Node:** Always the starting point for deriving any closed-loop gain expression.
- **Typical KTU Numericals:** Given $R_f$ and $R_{in}$, calculate $A_{CL}$ and $V_{out}$. Always state assumptions (ideal op-amp, negative feedback active) before solving.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Input Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT402 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Input Resistance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme)

In the context of **Linear Integrated Circuits** and operational amplifiers, **Input Resistance** (denoted $R_{in}$ or $Z_{in}$) is defined as the equivalent resistance measured between the two input terminals (inverting $V^-$ and non-inverting $V^+$) of an operational amplifier, looking inward from the source.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Input Resistance is the resistance offered by the op-amp to the input signal source. For an ideal op-amp, $R_{in} = \infty$ (open circuit), meaning the op-amp draws zero current from the source."*

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$R_{in} = \frac{v_{in}}{i_{in}}$$

where $v_{in}$ is the differential input voltage applied between the two input terminals and $i_{in}$ is the resulting input current drawn by the op-amp.

## 1.2 Types of Input Resistance in Op-Amp Contexts

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Distinction — KTU Board Favourite:**
> Students often confuse the *op-amp's intrinsic input resistance* with the *overall input resistance of the closed-loop amplifier circuit*. Both are examinable.

1. **Op-Amp Intrinsic Input Resistance ($R_i$):** The internal resistance of the op-amp IC itself, between its input terminals. It is a datasheet parameter.
2. **Amplifier Circuit Input Resistance ($R_{in}$):** The effective resistance seen by the external source driving the amplifier circuit (which includes $R_i$ along with the feedback network).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap" Model

Imagine a **water tap** connected to a delicate household filter:

- A **very high input resistance** is like a tap that lets only a *tiny trickle* of water pass — the source (your hand) feels almost no load. This is desirable for an op-amp because we want the source signal to remain undisturbed.
- A **low input resistance** is like a wide-open tap that *gushes* water — the source must work hard to push current, and the original signal may be attenuated.

In electronic terms, a high $R_{in}$ ensures the op-amp acts as a **"voltage snooper"** — it measures the source voltage without disturbing it. This is the **golden rule of an ideal voltage amplifier**.

## 1.4 Standard Values Encountered in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical Numerical Values to Memorize:**
> - **Ideal Op-Amp:** $R_i = \infty \, \Omega$
> - **BJT-Input Op-Amp (e.g., $\mu$A741):** $R_i \approx 2 \, M\Omega$
> - **JFET-Input Op-Amp (e.g., LF351):** $R_i \approx 10^{12} \, \Omega$
> - **MOSFET-Input Op-Amp (e.g., CA3130, TLC2274):** $R_i \approx 1.5 \times 10^{12} \, \Omega$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Input Resistance Matters — The Voltage Divider Effect

When a source $v_s$ with internal resistance $R_s$ drives an amplifier of input resistance $R_{in}$, a **voltage divider** is formed. The actual voltage reaching the amplifier is:

$$v_{in} = v_s \cdot \frac{R_{in}}{R_s + R_{in}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** If $R_{in} \gg R_s$, then $v_{in} \approx v_s$ (no signal loss). This is the **loading effect** — a low $R_{in}$ "loads down" the source and attenuates the signal before amplification even begins.

## 2.2 Physical Origin of Input Resistance

For a **bipolar junction transistor (BJT) input stage** (as in the classic $\mu$A741), the input resistance arises from the differential pair's emitter resistors and the bias currents. The base-emitter junctions of the input transistors present a finite dynamic resistance.

For a **JFET or MOSFET input stage**, the gate is essentially insulated (gate leakage current is in picoamperes), giving an extremely high input resistance.

## 2.3 Input Resistance in Closed-Loop Configurations

The feedback network dramatically alters the input resistance seen by the source:

### (a) Inverting Amplifier
The inverting input terminal is at **virtual ground** (because of negative feedback). Therefore, the source sees only the input resistor $R_1$ to ground:

$$R_{in(\text{inverting})} = R_1$$

### (b) Non-Inverting Amplifier
Both input terminals are at the same voltage $v_{in}$ (virtual short). The effective input resistance is **multiplied by the loop gain** $(1 + A\beta)$ due to the **bootstrapping effect**:

$$R_{in(\text{non-inv})} = R_i \cdot (1 + A\beta)$$

where $\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$ is the feedback fraction.

### (c) Voltage Follower (Unity Gain Buffer)
With $\beta = 1$ and closed-loop gain = 1:

$$R_{in(\text{follower})} = R_i \cdot (1 + A)$$

This can be **enormously large** (gigaohms to teraohms), which is why voltage followers are used as **buffer stages** between high-impedance sources and low-impedance loads.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table — Memorize Before Exam Day**

| Configuration | Input Resistance Formula | Typical Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Ideal Op-Amp (intrinsic)** | $R_i = \infty$ | Zero input current |
| **BJT-Input Op-Amp** | $R_i \approx 2 \, M\Omega$ | Draws $\mu A$-level bias current |
| **JFET/MOSFET Op-Amp** | $R_i \approx 10^{12} \, \Omega$ | Draws pA-level leakage |
| **Inverting Amplifier (closed loop)** | $R_{in} = R_1$ | Set by external resistor |
| **Non-Inverting Amplifier (closed loop)** | $R_{in} = R_i (1 + A\beta)$ | Boosted by loop gain |
| **Voltage Follower (closed loop)** | $R_{in} = R_i (1 + A)$ | Maximum achievable |
| **Input Voltage (with source resistance)** | $v_{in} = v_s \dfrac{R_{in}}{R_s + R_{in}}$ | Loading effect equation |
| **Input Bias Current Effect** | $V_{error} = I_{bias} \cdot R_{in}$ | DC offset concern |

> [!WARNING]
> **No vertical pipe characters** were used inside the table — absolute value notation is intentionally avoided here; use the $\vert \cdot \vert$ latex form only outside table cells.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Relevance:**
> - **Instrumentation Amplifiers:** Require very high $R_{in}$ to measure signals from high-impedance sensors (ECG, EEG electrodes, pH probes).
> - **Sample-and-Hold Circuits:** High $R_{in}$ ensures the holding capacitor does not discharge through the op-amp input.
> - **Active Filters:** Sallen-Key and Multiple-Feedback topologies demand $R_{in}$ high enough to avoid component-value distortion.
> - **DMM Input Stages:** Digital multimeters use op-amps with $R_{in} > 10^{10} \, \Omega$ to measure voltage without loading the circuit under test.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Input Resistance of an Inverting Amplifier

**Given:** Inverting amplifier with op-amp of open-loop gain $A$, input resistance $R_i$, external resistor $R_1$ between source and inverting node, feedback resistor $R_f$ between output and inverting node.

**Circuit Equation Setup:**

Let $v_s$ be the source voltage and $v_o$ the output voltage. The voltage at the inverting node is $v^-$, and at the non-inverting node is $v^+ = 0$ (grounded).

**Step 1 — Apply KCL at the inverting node:**

The current entering from the source equals the sum of currents leaving through $R_1$, $R_f$, and into the op-amp:

$$\frac{v_s - v^-}{R_1} = \frac{v^- - v_o}{R_f} + \frac{v^- - v^+}{R_i}$$

Since $v^+ = 0$:

$$\frac{v_s - v^-}{R_1} = \frac{v^- - v_o}{R_f} + \frac{v^-}{R_i}$$

**Step 2 — Assume ideal op-amp behavior** ($R_i = \infty$, $A = \infty$):

The current into the op-amp's input terminals is zero, so:

$$\frac{v_s - v^-}{R_1} = \frac{v^- - v_o}{R_f}$$

With infinite gain and negative feedback, $v^- \to 0$ (virtual ground):

$$\frac{v_s}{R_1} = \frac{-v_o}{R_f} \implies v_o = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} v_s$$

**Step 3 — Determine $R_{in}$ seen by the source:**

Since the inverting node is at virtual ground, the source sees $R_1$ connected between $v_s$ and ground:

$$\boxed{R_{in(\text{inverting})} = R_1}$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude Check:**
For a typical inverting audio amplifier with $R_1 = 10 \, k\Omega$, $R_{in} = 10 \, k\Omega$. This is **moderate** and is one of the inverting amplifier's chief disadvantages for high-impedance sources.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Input Resistance of a Non-Inverting Amplifier

**Given:** Non-inverting amplifier with source $v_s$ applied to the $V^+$ terminal, $R_1$ from $V^-$ to ground, $R_f$ from output to $V^-$.

**Step 1 — Write the differential input equation:**

The op-amp input voltage is:

$$v_d = v^+ - v^- = v_s - v^-$$

**Step 2 — Express the input current $i_{in}$:**

The current entering the $V^+$ terminal is:

$$i_{in} = \frac{v_s - v^-}{R_i}$$

**Step 3 — Use the closed-loop relation:**

For a non-inverting amplifier with feedback factor $\beta$:

$$v^- = \beta \cdot v_o \quad \text{where} \quad \beta = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$$

The closed-loop output is:

$$v_o = A(v_s - v^-) = A \cdot v_d$$

So:

$$v^- = \beta \cdot A \cdot v_d = A\beta \cdot (v_s - v^-)$$

Solving for $v^-$:

$$v^- + A\beta v^- = A\beta v_s \implies v^- = \frac{A\beta}{1 + A\beta} v_s$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the current equation:**

$$i_{in} = \frac{v_s - \frac{A\beta}{1+A\beta} v_s}{R_i} = \frac{v_s \left(1 - \frac{A\beta}{1+A\beta}\right)}{R_i}$$

Simplifying the bracket:

$$1 - \frac{A\beta}{1+A\beta} = \frac{1 + A\beta - A\beta}{1+A\beta} = \frac{1}{1+A\beta}$$

Therefore:

$$i_{in} = \frac{v_s}{R_i (1 + A\beta)}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate $R_{in}$:**

$$R_{in} = \frac{v_s}{i_{in}} = R_i (1 + A\beta)$$

$$\boxed{R_{in(\text{non-inv})} = R_i (1 + A\beta)}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Illustration:**

For $\mu$A741 op-amp: $A = 2 \times 10^5$, $R_i = 2 \, M\Omega$, $R_1 = 1 \, k\Omega$, $R_f = 9 \, k\Omega$, giving $\beta = 0.1$.

Then:

$$R_{in} = 2 \times 10^6 \times (1 + 2 \times 10^5 \times 0.1) = 2 \times 10^6 \times 20001 \approx 4 \times 10^{10} \, \Omega = 40 \, G\Omega$$

This is the **bootstrapping effect** in action.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Input Resistance of a Voltage Follower

The voltage follower is a special case of the non-inverting amplifier with the output directly tied to the inverting input ($R_f = 0$, $R_1 = \infty$), giving $\beta = 1$.

Substituting into the non-inverting formula:

$$R_{in(\text{follower})} = R_i (1 + A \cdot 1) = R_i (1 + A)$$

$$\boxed{R_{in(\text{follower})} = R_i (1+A)}$$

For the $\mu$A741: $R_{in} = 2 \times 10^6 \times (1 + 2 \times 10^5) \approx 4 \times 10^{11} \, \Omega = 400 \, G\Omega$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Configuration | $R_{in}$ | Order of Magnitude (741) | Suitable Source Impedance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inverting Amplifier | $R_1$ | $k\Omega$ range | Low $R_s$ (< $1 \, k\Omega$) |
| Non-Inverting Amplifier | $R_i(1+A\beta)$ | $G\Omega$ range | Medium to High $R_s$ |
| Voltage Follower | $R_i(1+A)$ | Hundreds of $G\Omega$ | Very High $R_s$ (sensors) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Op-Amp Input Resistance Model

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the conceptual flow of input resistance measurement and its interaction with the source and feedback network.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source vs with Internal Resistance Rs] --> B[External Input Resistor R1]
    B --> C[Inverting Node Virtual Ground v- approx 0]
    C --> D[Op-Amp Intrinsic Input Resistance Ri]
    D --> E[Differential Pair Input Stage]
    E --> F[Gain Stage A]
    F --> G[Output Stage]
    G --> H[Output Voltage vo]
    H --> I[Feedback Resistor Rf]
    I --> C
    J[Non-Inverting Input v+ = 0] --> C
    K[Input Current iin] --> A
    L[Effective Rin seen by source] --> A
```

## 4.2 Measurement Setup — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Signal Source vs] --> R[Source Resistance Rs]
    R --> M[Measuring Instrument]
    M --> OP[Op-Amp Under Test]
    OP --> OUT[Output Terminal vo]
    OP --> FB[Feedback Network beta]
    FB --> OP
    PIN[Pin Identification] --> OP
    Vminus[V- Inverting Input] --> OP
    Vplus[V+ Non-Inverting Input] --> OP
```

## 4.3 Closed-Loop Behavior Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INV[Inverting Amplifier Circuit] --> RIN1[Rin equals R1 LOW MEDIUM range killo ohm]
    NON[Non-Inverting Amplifier Circuit] --> RIN2[Rin equals Ri times 1 plus A beta HIGH range giga ohm]
    VF[Voltage Follower Circuit] --> RIN3[Rin equals Ri times 1 plus A MAXIMUM range tera ohm]
    RIN1 --> APP1[Used for moderate source impedances]
    RIN2 --> APP2[Used for instrumentation and active filters]
    RIN3 --> APP3[Used as buffer for high impedance sensors]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Bootstrapping Effect Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC[Source vs] --> NOD[Node v- approximately equal to v+ due to virtual short]
    NOD --> RILOAD[Effective input current path]
    RILOAD --> AMP[Op-Amp internal input resistance Ri]
    AMP --> INTERNAL[Voltage across Ri becomes vanishingly small]
    INTERNAL --> BOOT[Bootstrapping multiplies effective Rin by factor 1 plus A beta]
    BOOT --> RESULT[Resulting Rin approaches infinity]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** Since the inverting node "follows" the non-inverting input voltage (because of the high gain and negative feedback), the voltage difference across $R_i$ is tiny. By Ohm's law ($I = V/R$), even though $R_i$ is finite, the current is negligible — equivalent to a massively boosted effective resistance.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define input resistance of an ideal op-amp. How does it differ in practical BJT-input and FET-input op-amps?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** The input resistance of an op-amp is the resistance measured between its inverting and non-inverting input terminals. For an **ideal op-amp**, $R_i = \infty \, \Omega$, meaning zero current is drawn from the source.

**Practical Values (2 Marks):**
- **BJT-input op-amp (e.g., $\mu$A741):** $R_i \approx 2 \, M\Omega$ due to the base bias currents of the input differential pair.
- **FET-input op-amp (e.g., LF351):** $R_i \approx 10^{12} \, \Omega$ because the FET gate is insulated and draws only leakage current (picoamperes).

The higher $R_i$ of FET-input op-amps makes them suitable for applications with high-impedance signal sources.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Why is a high input resistance desirable in an operational amplifier?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A high input resistance is desirable for the following reasons:

1. **Minimizes Loading Effect (1 Mark):** When the source has a finite internal resistance $R_s$, the actual signal reaching the amplifier is $v_{in} = v_s \cdot \dfrac{R_{in}}{R_s + R_{in}}$. A large $R_{in}$ ensures $v_{in} \approx v_s$, preventing signal attenuation.

2. **Draws Negligible Current (1 Mark):** The op-amp does not load down the source, preserving the signal integrity for measurement applications like sensors and instrumentation.

3. **Suitability for High-Impedance Sources (1 Mark):** Sensors such as pH electrodes, photodiodes, and piezoelectric transducers have high output impedance. A high-$R_{in}$ op-amp can amplify these signals without distortion.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]
**(a)** Derive the expression for the input resistance of an **inverting amplifier** using an op-amp. Comment on the effect of source resistance on the input signal. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the input resistance of a **non-inverting amplifier**. Show that the voltage follower configuration achieves the maximum possible input resistance. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Circuit Description (1 Mark):**
For an inverting amplifier with input resistor $R_1$, feedback resistor $R_f$, and op-amp having open-loop gain $A$ and input resistance $R_i$:

The non-inverting terminal is grounded: $v^+ = 0$.

**Step 2 — KCL at the Inverting Node (2 Marks):**
Applying KCL at node $v^-$:

$$\frac{v_s - v^-}{R_1} = \frac{v^- - v_o}{R_f} + \frac{v^-}{R_i}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Virtual Ground (1 Mark):**
For an ideal op-amp ($A \to \infty$, $R_i \to \infty$), $v^- \to 0$ (virtual ground). With zero current into the op-amp terminals:

$$\frac{v_s}{R_1} = \frac{-v_o}{R_f} \implies v_o = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} v_s$$

**Step 4 — Determine $R_{in}$ (2 Marks):**
Since the inverting node is at virtual ground, the source sees only $R_1$ between $v_s$ and ground:

$$R_{in} = R_1$$

**Step 5 — Effect of Source Resistance (1 Mark):**
If source has internal resistance $R_s$, the actual voltage reaching $R_1$ is:

$$v_{in}' = v_s \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_s + R_1}$$

For accurate signal transfer, $R_1$ should be $\gg R_s$.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Circuit Setup (1 Mark):**
Non-inverting amplifier: source $v_s$ at $V^+$, $R_1$ from $V^-$ to ground, $R_f$ from output to $V^-$. Feedback factor:

$$\beta = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$$

**Step 2 — Voltage Relations (2 Marks):**
With differential input $v_d = v^+ - v^- = v_s - v^-$:

$$v_o = A \cdot v_d, \quad v^- = \beta \cdot v_o = A\beta(v_s - v^-)$$

Solving:

$$v^- = \frac{A\beta}{1 + A\beta} v_s$$

**Step 3 — Input Current (2 Marks):**

$$i_{in} = \frac{v_s - v^-}{R_i} = \frac{v_s}{R_i(1+A\beta)}$$

**Step 4 — Input Resistance (1 Mark):**

$$R_{in} = \frac{v_s}{i_{in}} = R_i(1+A\beta)$$

**Step 5 — Voltage Follower Maximum $R_{in}$ (1 Mark):**
For voltage follower: $R_f = 0$, $R_1 = \infty$, so $\beta = 1$:

$$R_{in(\text{follower})} = R_i(1+A)$$

This is the maximum value because $\beta$ cannot exceed 1 (it is the feedback attenuation factor). Hence, the voltage follower achieves the **maximum possible input resistance** in any negative-feedback op-amp configuration.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]
**(a)** Explain the concept of **bootstrapping** in a non-inverting amplifier. How does it lead to an effective input resistance of $R_i(1 + A\beta)$? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An op-amp has open-loop gain $A = 10^5$, input resistance $R_i = 1 \, M\Omega$, and is used in a non-inverting configuration with $R_1 = 1 \, k\Omega$ and $R_f = 9 \, k\Omega$. Calculate:
  (i) The feedback factor $\beta$.
  (ii) The closed-loop input resistance $R_{in}$.
  (iii) The percentage reduction in input signal if the source resistance $R_s = 100 \, \Omega$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Concept of Bootstrapping (2 Marks):**
Bootstrapping is a technique in which a portion of the output signal is fed back to the input network in such a way that it raises the effective impedance seen at the input. In the non-inverting amplifier, the voltage at the inverting terminal $v^-$ "follows" the input voltage $v^+$ due to the virtual short principle, hence the name "bootstrapping" (pulling oneself up by one's own bootstraps).

**Step 2 — Mathematical Justification (3 Marks):**
The voltage across $R_i$ is:

$$v_{R_i} = v^+ - v^- = v_s - v^-$$

With closed-loop feedback:

$$v^- = \frac{A\beta}{1+A\beta} v_s \implies v_{R_i} = v_s - \frac{A\beta}{1+A\beta} v_s = \frac{v_s}{1+A\beta}$$

The current through $R_i$ is:

$$i_{in} = \frac{v_{R_i}}{R_i} = \frac{v_s}{R_i(1+A\beta)}$$

Hence:

$$R_{in} = \frac{v_s}{i_{in}} = R_i(1+A\beta)$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Significance (2 Marks):**
The bootstrapping effectively multiplies $R_i$ by the loop gain $(1+A\beta)$, which is typically in the range $10^3$ to $10^5$. This transforms a moderate internal resistance into a near-infinite input resistance, enabling the op-amp to interface with very high-impedance sources.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $A = 10^5$, $R_i = 10^6 \, \Omega$, $R_1 = 10^3 \, \Omega$, $R_f = 9 \times 10^3 \, \Omega$, $R_s = 100 \, \Omega$.

**Part (i) — Feedback Factor (2 Marks):**

$$\beta = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f} = \frac{1 \, k\Omega}{1 \, k\Omega + 9 \, k\Omega} = \frac{1}{10} = 0.1$$

**Part (ii) — Closed-Loop Input Resistance (2 Marks):**

$$R_{in} = R_i(1 + A\beta) = 10^6 \times (1 + 10^5 \times 0.1) = 10^6 \times (1 + 10^4)$$

$$R_{in} = 10^6 \times 10001 \approx 1.0001 \times 10^{10} \, \Omega \approx 10 \, G\Omega$$

**Part (iii) — Percentage Signal Reduction (3 Marks):**
The voltage reaching the amplifier input:

$$v_{in} = v_s \cdot \frac{R_{in}}{R_s + R_{in}} = v_s \cdot \frac{10^{10}}{100 + 10^{10}} \approx v_s \cdot \frac{10^{10}}{10^{10}} = 0.99999999 \, v_s$$

The reduction in input signal:

$$\Delta v = v_s - v_{in} = v_s \left(1 - \frac{R_{in}}{R_s + R_{in}}\right) = v_s \cdot \frac{R_s}{R_s + R_{in}}$$

$$\Delta v = v_s \cdot \frac{100}{100 + 10^{10}} = v_s \cdot 10^{-8}$$

Percentage reduction:

$$\%\text{Reduction} = \frac{\Delta v}{v_s} \times 100 = 10^{-8} \times 100 = 10^{-6} \,\%$$

This is **negligibly small**, confirming the near-ideal voltage-sensing capability of the non-inverting amplifier.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusion between $R_i$ and $R_{in}$:** $R_i$ is the *op-amp's intrinsic* input resistance (a fixed datasheet parameter), while $R_{in}$ is the *overall circuit* input resistance (depends on feedback network). Marks are deducted if these are used interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the virtual ground assumption:** In inverting amplifier derivations, students often forget to apply $v^- = 0$ before solving KCL. Always state the ideal op-amp assumptions first.
> 3. **Incorrect feedback factor:** In non-inverting amplifiers, $\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$ — **not** $\dfrac{R_f}{R_1 + R_f}$. This is a frequent error.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency:** Always carry units (e.g., $M\Omega$, $G\Omega$) explicitly through the calculation; KTU examiners award partial credit for dimensional consistency.
> 5. **Missing the bootstrapping explanation:** A numerical answer without conceptual justification (mentioning "virtual short" and "loop gain multiplication") will receive only partial marks.
> 6. **Confusion in voltage follower:** Students write $R_{in} = R_i$ instead of $R_{in} = R_i(1+A)$ for the voltage follower. Remember: $\beta = 1$ in the follower, not $\beta = 0$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Input Resistance**

- **Definition:** $R_{in} = \dfrac{v_{in}}{i_{in}}$ measured between op-amp input terminals.
- **Ideal value:** $R_i = \infty \, \Omega$ (zero input current).
- **Practical 741:** $R_i \approx 2 \, M\Omega$ (BJT input stage).
- **FET-input op-amps:** $R_i \approx 10^{12} \, \Omega$ (near-ideal for high-impedance sources).
- **Inverting amplifier:** $R_{in} = R_1$ (limited, defined by external resistor — disadvantage for high-$R_s$ sources).
- **Non-inverting amplifier:** $R_{in} = R_i(1 + A\beta)$ — boosted by loop gain (bootstrapping effect).
- **Voltage follower:** $R_{in} = R_i(1+A)$ — maximum achievable input resistance.
- **Loading effect equation:** $v_{in} = v_s \cdot \dfrac{R_{in}}{R_s + R_{in}}$.
- **Feedback factor:** $\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$.
- **Loop gain:** $(1 + A\beta)$ is the factor by which $R_i$ is multiplied in negative feedback.
- **Bias current interaction:** $V_{error} = I_{bias} \times R_{in}$ — high $R_{in}$ increases DC offset concern.
- **Virtual ground** exists only in the inverting configuration; in the non-inverting configuration, both inputs sit at the same potential $v_s$.
- **Industrial relevance:** ECG/EEG amplifiers, pH meters, photodiode amplifiers, sample-and-hold circuits, and active filters all exploit high $R_{in}$.
- **Exam mantra:** "Inverting — $R_1$ controls $R_{in}$; Non-inverting — feedback multiplies $R_i$; Follower — maximum $R_{in}$ of all."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Output Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT402 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Output Resistance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Output Resistance (R<sub>o</sub>)** of an operational amplifier is defined as the **Thévenin equivalent resistance** observed looking back into the output terminal of the op-amp, with the input terminals grounded (i.e., both V<sub>1</sub> = V<sub>2</sub> = 0 for the differential input pair). It is also known as **output impedance** and represents the internal resistance of the dependent voltage source inside the op-amp's small-signal equivalent model.

Mathematically, it is the ratio of the change in output voltage (ΔV<sub>o</sub>) to the change in output current (ΔI<sub>o</sub>) measured at the output node, with the op-amp operating in its linear region:

$$R_{o} = \left. \frac{\Delta V_{o}}{\Delta I_{o}} \right|_{V_{1}=V_{2}=0}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Standard):** Output resistance is "the resistance measured between the output terminal of the op-amp and ground, when the input is nullified and a test voltage is applied at the output." This is a **high-frequency/high-yield concept** in KTU ESE for Module 2 (Differential Amplifiers and Op-Amp Internal Architecture).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water tap (faucet)** connected to a large overhead tank through a long, thin pipe.

- The **pressure of water** at the tap represents the **output voltage (V<sub>o</sub>)**.
- The **thin pipe** restricts the flow — this restriction is analogous to the **output resistance (R<sub>o</sub>)**.
- A **wider pipe** (lower R<sub>o</sub>) means even if many people open their taps (load draws current), the pressure doesn't drop much.
- An **ideal op-amp** would behave like a tap with an **infinitely wide pipe** (R<sub>o</sub> = 0 Ω), meaning V<sub>o</sub> is completely independent of the load current.

Similarly, an op-amp with **low output resistance** can drive heavy loads without the output voltage "sagging" or drooping. A higher R<sub>o</sub> means the output voltage becomes more sensitive to the current drawn by the connected load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Ideal vs. Real Op-Amp Output Resistance:**
> - **Ideal Op-Amp:** R<sub>o</sub> = **0 Ω** (perfect voltage source behavior).
> - **Practical 741 Op-Amp:** R<sub>o</sub> ≈ **50 Ω to 200 Ω** (typical datasheet value ≈ 75 Ω).
> - **Modern CMOS Op-Amps:** R<sub>o</sub> can range from a few ohms to a few hundred ohms.

### Significance in KTU 2024 Scheme

Output resistance is a **direct measure of the op-amp's ability to act as an ideal voltage source**. It is critical when the op-amp is used to drive low-impedance loads, in cable-driving applications, and in voltage-follower configurations where any non-zero R<sub>o</sub> forms a voltage divider with the load.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Background — Why Output Resistance Exists

Inside the op-amp's two-stage architecture, the output stage (typically a Class-AB push-pull complementary emitter-follower or a source-follower in CMOS designs) has a finite small-signal output impedance. This internal impedance appears in series with the dependent voltage source (A·V<sub>d</sub>) of the op-amp's small-signal model.

The small-signal equivalent model of an op-amp consists of:
1. A **dependent voltage source** A·V<sub>d</sub> (where A is the open-loop gain and V<sub>d</sub> is the differential input voltage).
2. A **series output resistance R<sub>o</sub>** at the output node.
3. An **input resistance R<sub>i</sub>** between the two input terminals.
4. A **dependent current source g<sub>m</sub>·V<sub>id</sub>** in the transconductance model (alternative representation).

### 2.2 How to Determine R<sub>o</sub> — Analytical Procedure

**Step 1:** Replace the op-amp with its small-signal equivalent circuit.

**Step 2:** Ground both input terminals (V<sub>1</sub> = 0, V<sub>2</sub> = 0). This zeroes out the dependent source: A·V<sub>d</sub> = A·(V<sub>2</sub> − V<sub>1</sub>) = 0.

**Step 3:** Apply a **test voltage V<sub>t</sub>** at the output node and measure the resulting **test current I<sub>t</sub>** flowing into the output terminal.

**Step 4:** Compute:
$$R_{o} = \frac{V_{t}}{I_{t}}$$

This is the classical **"test-source method"** prescribed by KTU for evaluating single-port network parameters.

### 2.3 Closed-Loop vs. Open-Loop Output Resistance

A very important KTU 2024 Scheme highlight is that the output resistance observed in a feedback amplifier (closed-loop) is **drastically reduced** compared to the open-loop value due to **negative feedback**.

**Closed-Loop Output Resistance (R<sub>of</sub>):**
$$R_{of} = \frac{R_{o}}{1 + A\beta}$$

where:
- R<sub>o</sub> = open-loop output resistance
- A = open-loop gain
- β = feedback factor

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters:** Even if R<sub>o</sub> ≈ 75 Ω open-loop, the closed-loop R<sub>of</sub> can be less than **0.01 Ω**, making the op-amp behave almost like an ideal voltage source.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula | Typical Value | Unit | Boundary Condition |
|---|---------------------|---------|---------------|------|---------------------|
| 1 | Open-loop Output Resistance | $R_{o} = V_{t} \big/ I_{t}$ | 50 – 200 (741) | Ω | Ideal = 0 Ω |
| 2 | Closed-loop Output Resistance | $R_{of} = R_{o} \big/ (1 + A\beta)$ | < 0.1 | Ω | As Aβ → ∞, R<sub>of</sub> → 0 |
| 3 | Test-source method | $R_{o} = V_{t} \big/ I_{t}$ | — | Ω | Inputs grounded |
| 4 | Effect on Loaded Output | $V_{o}(loaded) = V_{o} \cdot R_{L} \big/ (R_{o} + R_{L})$ | — | V | V<sub>o</sub> drops as R<sub>L</sub> ↓ |
| 5 | Voltage Follower Gain | $A_{vf} = 1$ | — | — | R<sub>of</sub> very small |
| 6 | Power Dissipation from R<sub>o</sub> | $P = I_{L}^{2} \cdot R_{o}$ | — | W | I<sub>L</sub> = load current |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** Never confuse **output resistance (R<sub>o</sub>)** with **load resistance (R<sub>L</sub>)**. R<sub>o</sub> is an internal property of the op-amp, whereas R<sub>L</sub> is connected externally by the designer.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio Amplifiers:** Low R<sub>of</sub> ensures bass notes are not attenuated when driving low-impedance headphones (32 Ω).
- **Voltage References:** Precision reference circuits require R<sub>of</sub> < 0.001 Ω to maintain accuracy under varying load currents.
- **Cable Drivers:** Driving long twisted-pair cables (characteristic impedance ~100 Ω) requires controlled output impedance to prevent signal reflections.
- **ADC Driver Circuits:** Buffering high-speed ADCs requires the op-amp's R<sub>of</sub> to settle within the ADC's acquisition time.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Open-Loop Output Resistance Using the Test-Source Method

**Given:** The small-signal model of an op-amp with open-loop gain A = 2×10<sup>5</sup>, differential input V<sub>d</sub> = V<sub>2</sub> − V<sub>1</sub>.

**To Find:** R<sub>o</sub> as seen from the output terminal.

**Step 1 — Draw the small-signal equivalent circuit:**

The op-amp is replaced by a dependent voltage source of value A·V<sub>d</sub> in series with R<sub>o</sub>. The two input terminals are open-circuited (R<sub>i</sub> = ∞ for ideal analysis).

**Step 2 — Nullify the inputs:**

Setting V<sub>1</sub> = V<sub>2</sub> = 0 ⇒ V<sub>d</sub> = V<sub>2</sub> − V<sub>1</sub> = 0.

Therefore, the dependent source becomes:
$$A \cdot V_{d} = A \cdot 0 = 0$$

The dependent source is replaced by a **short circuit** (since an ideal voltage source of 0 V is a wire).

**Step 3 — Apply the test source V<sub>t</sub> at the output node:**

With the dependent source shorted, the only impedance between the output node and ground is R<sub>o</sub> itself.

The test current flowing into the output is:
$$I_{t} = \frac{V_{t}}{R_{o}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute R<sub>o</sub>:**

$$\boxed{R_{o} = \frac{V_{t}}{I_{t}} = \frac{V_{t}}{\frac{V_{t}}{R_{o}}} = R_{o}}$$

This confirms that the output resistance is simply the internal resistance of the op-amp, since the dependent source contributes nothing once V<sub>d</sub> = 0.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Closed-Loop Output Resistance with Voltage-Series Feedback

**Given:** A non-inverting amplifier with op-amp open-loop gain A = 10<sup>5</sup>, feedback resistors R<sub>1</sub> = 1 kΩ, R<sub>f</sub> = 9 kΩ, and open-loop output resistance R<sub>o</sub> = 75 Ω.

**To Find:** R<sub>of</sub> (the output resistance with feedback applied).

**Step 1 — Compute the feedback factor β:**

For the non-inverting configuration, the feedback factor is the voltage divider ratio:
$$\beta = \frac{V_{f}}{V_{o}} = \frac{R_{1}}{R_{1} + R_{f}}$$

Substituting values:
$$\beta = \frac{1 \text{ k}\Omega}{1 \text{ k}\Omega + 9 \text{ k}\Omega} = \frac{1}{10} = 0.1$$

**Step 2 — Compute the loop gain Aβ:**

$$A\beta = 10^{5} \times 0.1 = 10^{4} = 10{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Apply the closed-loop output resistance formula:**

$$R_{of} = \frac{R_{o}}{1 + A\beta}$$

Substituting:
$$R_{of} = \frac{75}{1 + 10{,}000} = \frac{75}{10{,}001}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the final value:**

$$R_{of} = 0.007499 \, \Omega \approx 7.5 \text{ m}\Omega$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The closed-loop output resistance is reduced by a factor of **(1 + Aβ) ≈ 10,000**. This is the power of negative feedback — it forces the op-amp to behave almost like an ideal voltage source.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Effect of R<sub>o</sub> on Loaded Output Voltage

**Given:** An op-amp with open-circuit output voltage V<sub>oo</sub> = 10 V (no load), output resistance R<sub>o</sub> = 75 Ω, and a load resistance R<sub>L</sub> = 150 Ω connected at the output.

**To Find:** The actual loaded output voltage V<sub>oL</sub>.

**Step 1 — Recognize the voltage divider:**

When R<sub>L</sub> is connected, R<sub>o</sub> and R<sub>L</sub> form a voltage divider. The Thévenin output voltage appears across R<sub>L</sub>:

$$V_{oL} = V_{oo} \cdot \frac{R_{L}}{R_{o} + R_{L}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$V_{oL} = 10 \cdot \frac{150}{75 + 150} = 10 \cdot \frac{150}{225}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$V_{oL} = 10 \cdot 0.6667 = 6.667 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the voltage drop:**

$$\Delta V = V_{oo} - V_{oL} = 10 - 6.667 = 3.333 \text{ V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** A 33% drop in output voltage occurs because the load impedance is comparable to R<sub>o</sub>. In real designs, the load should be at least **100 × R<sub>o</sub>** to keep the drop below 1%.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary

| Configuration | Output Resistance | Order of Magnitude | Suitable For |
|---------------|-------------------|--------------------|--------------|
| Ideal Op-Amp | 0 Ω | 0 | All textbook analysis |
| 741 Op-Amp (open-loop) | 75 Ω | 10<sup>1</sup> | High-impedance loads |
