| Non-inverting amp (Aβ = 10<sup>4</sup>) | 7.5 mΩ | 10<sup>-3</sup> | General-purpose |
| Voltage Follower (β = 1, Aβ = 10<sup>5</sup>) | 0.75 mΩ | 10<sup>-3</sup> | Buffers, ADC drivers |
| Open-collector comparator | > 1 kΩ | 10<sup>3</sup> | Not for driving loads |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Op-Amp Small-Signal Model Showing Output Resistance

```mermaid
graph LR
    InP[Input Plus V1] --> RiNode[Input Resistance Ri]
    InN[Input Minus V2] --> RiNode
    RiNode --> DepSrc{Dependent Source A times Vd}
    DepSrc --> RoNode[Output Resistance Ro]
    RoNode --> OutNode[Output Terminal Vo]
    DepSrc --> Gnd1[Ground]
    RoNode --> Gnd2[Ground]
    InP --> Gnd3[Ground]
    InN --> Gnd4[Ground]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The two inputs V<sub>1</sub> and V<sub>2</sub> feed into the differential input.
- The dependent source generates a voltage A·(V<sub>2</sub> − V<sub>1</sub>).
- R<sub>o</sub> is in series between the dependent source and the output terminal.
- When measuring R<sub>o</sub>, all inputs are grounded, the dependent source becomes a short, and only R<sub>o</sub> remains between the output and ground.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Test-Source Method for Finding Output Resistance

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start: Small-Signal Op-Amp Model] --> B[Ground Both Inputs V1 and V2]
    B --> C[Set Vd equal to V2 minus V1 equal to 0]
    C --> D[Dependent Source A times Vd equals 0 V]
    D --> E[Replace Dependent Source with Short Circuit]
    E --> F[Apply Test Voltage Vt at Output Node]
    F --> G[Measure Resulting Test Current It]
    G --> H[Compute Ro equal to Vt divided by It]
    H --> I[Result: Internal Output Resistance of Op-Amp]
```

**Flow interpretation:** This flowchart visualizes the textbook procedure mandated by KTU for solving "find the output resistance" problems. Every step corresponds to a one-line explanation in your answer script.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Effect of Negative Feedback on Output Resistance

```mermaid
graph LR
    OpenLoop[Open Loop Ro equals 75 ohm] --> ApplyFB[Apply Negative Feedback]
    ApplyFB --> CalcAB[Compute Loop Gain A times Beta]
    CalcAB --> Reduce[Divide Ro by 1 plus A times Beta]
    Reduce --> ClosedLoop[Closed Loop Rof approx 7.5 milliohm]
    ClosedLoop --> IdealSrc[Behaves Nearly Like Ideal Voltage Source]
```

**Interpretation:** This block-level diagram emphasizes the **engineering impact** of feedback. The numerical reduction from 75 Ω to 7.5 mΩ is a factor of 10,000 — a classic KTU "show the calculation" question.

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Block — Real-World Application Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    Sensor[Low Impedance Sensor] --> Buffer[Op-Amp Voltage Follower]
    Buffer --> Load1[Heavy Load R L small]
    Buffer --> Load2[Long Cable 100 ohm]
    Buffer --> ADC[High Speed ADC Input]
    Note1[Buffer provides Ro f approx 1 milliohm] --> Buffer
```

**Interpretation:** A unity-gain buffer (voltage follower) is the most common application where the op-amp's closed-loop R<sub>of</sub> is exploited. It isolates a high-impedance source from a low-impedance load.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "output resistance" of an operational amplifier. State its ideal value and the typical value for a 741 op-amp.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Output resistance R<sub>o</sub> of an op-amp is the Thévenin equivalent resistance measured at the output terminal with respect to ground, when both inputs are grounded (V<sub>1</sub> = V<sub>2</sub> = 0). It is the small-signal output impedance of the internal output stage.

- **Ideal value:** R<sub>o</sub> = **0 Ω** (an ideal op-amp is a perfect voltage source).
- **Practical value for μA741:** R<sub>o</sub> ≈ **75 Ω** (typical, as per datasheet).
- **Range:** 50 Ω to 200 Ω depending on operating conditions and frequency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU Pattern):** Definition: 1 mark, Ideal value: 1 mark, Practical 741 value: 1 mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop output resistance of an op-amp. Why does negative feedback reduce R<sub>o</sub>?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Open-Loop R<sub>o</sub> | Closed-Loop R<sub>of</sub> |
|-----------|--------------------------|------------------------------|
| Definition | R<sub>o</sub> without feedback | R<sub>of</sub> with negative feedback |
| Formula | $R_{o}$ (intrinsic) | $R_{of} = R_{o} \big/ (1 + A\beta)$ |
| Typical value (741) | 75 Ω | Few milliohms |
| Stability | — | More stable, less load-sensitive |

**Why negative feedback reduces R<sub>o</sub>:** Negative feedback samples the output voltage and feeds back a portion to the inverting input. Any drop in V<sub>o</sub> due to load current is detected, amplified by A, and corrected automatically. The internal error-correction mechanism effectively cancels the IR drop across R<sub>o</sub>, making the op-amp behave as if its internal resistance were divided by (1 + Aβ).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of the small-signal equivalent circuit, derive the expression for the **open-loop output resistance R<sub>o</sub>** of an op-amp using the test-source method. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** An op-amp with open-loop gain A = 2 × 10<sup>5</sup> and open-loop output resistance R<sub>o</sub> = 100 Ω is used in a **non-inverting amplifier** with R<sub>1</sub> = 1 kΩ and R<sub>f</sub> = 99 kΩ. Calculate the **closed-loop output resistance R<sub>of</sub>** and the **closed-loop gain**. Comment on the ability of the circuit to drive a 50 Ω load. **\[7 marks\]**

---

**Solution to (a):**

**Step 1:** Replace the op-amp by its small-signal model consisting of a dependent voltage source A·V<sub>d</sub> in series with the output resistance R<sub>o</sub>, and a high input resistance R<sub>i</sub> between the two input terminals.

**Step 2:** Ground both inputs: V<sub>1</sub> = 0, V<sub>2</sub> = 0.

Therefore V<sub>d</sub> = V<sub>2</sub> − V<sub>1</sub> = 0, and the dependent source becomes:
$$A \cdot V_{d} = A \cdot 0 = 0$$

A zero-volt ideal voltage source is equivalent to a **short circuit**.

**Step 3:** Apply a test voltage V<sub>t</sub> at the output node. The current through R<sub>o</sub> is:
$$I_{t} = \frac{V_{t} - 0}{R_{o}} = \frac{V_{t}}{R_{o}}$$

(Here 0 V is across the shorted dependent source.)

**Step 4:** Hence, the output resistance is:
$$\boxed{R_{o} = \frac{V_{t}}{I_{t}} = R_{o}}$$

This confirms that the **open-loop output resistance** is simply the internal resistance of the op-amp's output stage, with no contribution from the dependent source once the inputs are nullified.

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute feedback factor β:**

$$\beta = \frac{R_{1}}{R_{1} + R_{f}} = \frac{1}{1 + 99} = \frac{1}{100} = 0.01$$

**Step 2 — Compute loop gain Aβ:**

$$A\beta = 2 \times 10^{5} \times 0.01 = 2000$$

**Step 3 — Closed-loop output resistance:**

$$R_{of} = \frac{R_{o}}{1 + A\beta} = \frac{100}{1 + 2000} = \frac{100}{2001} \approx 0.0499 \, \Omega \approx 50 \text{ m}\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Closed-loop gain:**

$$A_{CL} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta} = \frac{2 \times 10^{5}}{2001} \approx 99.95 \approx 100$$

Alternatively, using the ideal formula:
$$A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{R_{f}}{R_{1}} = 1 + 99 = 100$$

**Step 5 — Loading analysis:**

With R<sub>of</sub> ≈ 50 mΩ, the voltage divider formed with a 50 Ω load produces a drop:

$$\text{Drop} = V_{oo} \times \frac{R_{of}}{R_{of} + R_{L}} = V_{oo} \times \frac{0.05}{0.05 + 50} \approx V_{oo} \times 0.001$$

This is only a **0.1% drop** — the circuit can drive a 50 Ω load with negligible loss in output voltage. 

> [!TIP]
> **Comment:** Such a non-inverting amplifier is well-suited as a buffer between a high-impedance source and a low-impedance 50 Ω load (e.g., a coaxial cable or RF mixer input).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **effect of output resistance on the loaded output voltage** of an op-amp. An op-amp has R<sub>o</sub> = 200 Ω and delivers a no-load output of 8 V. Find the output voltage when a 600 Ω load is connected. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **concept of negative feedback in reducing output resistance**. A voltage follower uses an op-amp with A = 10<sup>5</sup> and R<sub>o</sub> = 50 Ω. Calculate R<sub>of</sub>. **\[7 marks\]**

---

**Solution to (a):**

**Explanation:**
When a load resistance R<sub>L</sub> is connected at the output of an op-amp, it forms a voltage divider with the op-amp's internal output resistance R<sub>o</sub>. The actual output voltage delivered to the load is therefore less than the ideal no-load value.

The loaded output voltage is given by:
$$V_{oL} = V_{oo} \cdot \frac{R_{L}}{R_{o} + R_{L}}$$

**Numerical calculation:**

Given: R<sub>o</sub> = 200 Ω, V<sub>oo</sub> = 8 V, R<sub>L</sub> = 600 Ω.

$$V_{oL} = 8 \cdot \frac{600}{200 + 600} = 8 \cdot \frac{600}{800} = 8 \times 0.75 = 6 \text{ V}$$

The voltage drop is:
$$\Delta V = V_{oo} - V_{oL} = 8 - 6 = 2 \text{ V}$$

This 25% drop is significant and would be unacceptable in precision applications, motivating the use of feedback or a buffer stage.

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Concept:**
In a voltage follower (unity-gain buffer), the output is fed back directly to the inverting input, making β = 1. The non-inverting input receives the signal. Any deviation of V<sub>o</sub> from the input (due to IR drop across R<sub>o</sub>) is amplified by A and applied with opposite polarity to the input, automatically restoring V<sub>o</sub>. This servo action makes the effective output resistance divide by (1 + Aβ).

**Calculation:**

Given: A = 10<sup>5</sup>, R<sub>o</sub> = 50 Ω, β = 1 (voltage follower).

Loop gain:
$$A\beta = 10^{5} \times 1 = 10^{5}$$

Closed-loop output resistance:
$$R_{of} = \frac{R_{o}}{1 + A\beta} = \frac{50}{1 + 10^{5}} = \frac{50}{100001} \approx 0.5 \text{ m}\Omega$$

This extremely small R<sub>of</sub> makes the voltage follower an excellent buffer, capable of driving loads as low as a few ohms without measurable loss.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to ground both inputs** before applying the test source (loses 1–2 marks).
> 2. **Confusing R<sub>o</sub> (internal) with R<sub>L</sub> (external load)** — examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Wrong formula for closed-loop R<sub>of</sub>:** Some students write R<sub>of</sub> = R<sub>o</sub>(1 + Aβ). The correct formula has R<sub>o</sub> in the **numerator**, not the denominator.
> 4. **Not mentioning units** (Ω, mΩ) — KTU strictly deducts 0.5 marks for missing units.
> 5. **Skipping the "comment" step** in design questions — KTU awards 1 mark for the practical interpretation.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Output Resistance R<sub>o</sub>** = Thévenin resistance at the output with both inputs grounded.
- **Ideal value:** R<sub>o</sub> = **0 Ω**.
- **Practical 741 value:** R<sub>o</sub> ≈ **75 Ω** (range 50–200 Ω).
- **Test-source method:** Apply V<sub>t</sub>, measure I<sub>t</sub>, compute R<sub>o</sub> = V<sub>t</sub> / I<sub>t</sub>.
- **Closed-loop formula:** $R_{of} = R_{o} \big/ (1 + A\beta)$ — the cornerstone of feedback theory.
- **Voltage follower** has β = 1, giving the **largest reduction** in R<sub>o</sub>.
- **Loaded output voltage:** $V_{oL} = V_{oo} \cdot R_{L} \big/ (R_{o} + R_{L})$ — always less than V<sub>oo</sub>.
- **Design rule of thumb:** Choose R<sub>L</sub> ≥ **100 × R<sub>o</sub>** to keep output drop below 1%.
- **High R<sub>o</sub> consequence:** Output voltage droops when load current increases — bad for voltage references.
- **Low R<sub>of</sub> advantage:** Near-ideal voltage source behavior; suitable for cable drivers and ADC buffers.
- **Frequency dependence:** R<sub>o</sub> increases at high frequencies because the open-loop gain A rolls off — important for RF op-amp selection.
- **KTU key formula triplet** to memorize: (i) $R_{o} = V_{t}/I_{t}$, (ii) $R_{of} = R_{o}/(1+A\beta)$, (iii) $V_{oL} = V_{oo} \cdot R_{L}/(R_{o}+R_{L})$.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bandwidth and Output Offset Voltage of Feedback Configurations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEBT402 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Bandwidth and Output Offset Voltage of Feedback Configurations # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Bandwidth of Feedback Amplifiers **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology):** The **Bandwidth (BW)** of a feedback amplifier is the range of frequencies over which the closed-loop gain remains within **3 dB** of its mid-band value. When a negative feedback loop with loop-gain $A\beta$ is applied around an op-amp, the **closed-loop bandwidth** $f_f$ is extended by a factor of $(1 + A\beta)$ relative to the open-loop bandwidth $f_H$, while the **mid-band gain** $A_f$ is reduced by the same factor. $$A_f \cdot f_f = A \cdot f_H \quad \text{(Gain–Bandwidth Product is invariant)}$$ ## 1.2 Output Offset Voltage **Formal Academic Definition:** The **Output Offset Voltage ($V_{OO}$)** is the unwanted DC voltage present at the output of a closed-loop op-amp circuit when both input terminals are grounded (i.e., $V_{in} = 0$). It arises primarily from three non-ideal op-amp parameters: the **Input Offset Voltage ($V_{IO}$)**, the **Input Bias Current ($I_B$)**, and the **Input Offset Current ($I_{IO} = \mid I_{B}^{-} - I_{B}^{+} \mid$)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – Feedback Configurations):** > Students are expected to derive $A_f$, $R_{if}$, $R_{of}$, and the **bandwidth extension factor** for all four feedback topologies (Voltage-Series, Voltage-Shunt, Current-Series, Current-Shunt), and to compute the **total output offset voltage** including the bias-current compensation resistor technique. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition **The Steering-Wheel Analogy for Bandwidth:** Imagine you are driving a sports car with a very "twitchy" steering wheel — the car responds sharply, but you over-correct on every bump (high gain, narrow bandwidth, easily destabilised). Now, fit a power-steering reducer (negative feedback). The car becomes easier to control (lower gain), but it glides smoothly over a much wider range of road conditions (wider bandwidth). The "total sharpness × smoothness" (gain × bandwidth) of the system is conserved — you trade one for the other. **The "Weighing-Scale Zero-Error" Analogy for Offset Voltage:** An ideal weighing scale should read **0.000 kg** when nothing is placed on it. In reality, a real scale reads **0.012 kg** (the *zero error / offset*). This residual reading, multiplied by the scale's amplification factor, gives an **output offset**. Similarly, an op-amp has a tiny internal imbalance ($V_{IO}$) that gets amplified by the closed-loop gain to become the **output offset voltage $V_{OO}$** — directly limiting how small a DC signal the circuit can faithfully detect. ## 1.4 The Four Feedback Configurations (Quick Reference) | Configuration | Input Mixing | Output Sampling | Ideal Quantity Sampled | Closed-Loop Quantity | | :-- | :-- | :-- | :-- | :-- | | **Voltage-Series** | Series | Shunt | Voltage | Voltage Amplifier | | **Voltage-Shunt** | Shunt | Shunt | Voltage | Transresistance (V→I) | | **Current-Series** | Series | Series | Current | Transconductance (I→V) | | **Current-Shunt** | Shunt | Series | Current | Current Amplifier | > [!NOTE] > The **bandwidth-extension theorem** $(1 + A\beta)$ and **GBP invariance** apply *identically* to all four topologies — only the *expression* for $A_f$ and the *type* of output offset (voltage vs. current) differ. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Negative Feedback Extends Bandwidth The open-loop transfer function of a single-pole op-amp is $$A(s) = \frac{A_0}{1 + s/\omega_H}$$ Closing the loop with feedback factor $\beta$ gives $$A_f(s) = \frac{A(s)}{1 + A(s)\beta} = \frac{A_0}{1 + A_0\beta} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + s/\omega_H(1+A_0\beta)}$$ **Operational Logic — Why this happens:** - The feedback network **continuously senses the output** and subtracts a scaled replica $\beta V_{out}$ from the input. - At high frequencies where the loop gain $\vert A\beta \vert$ falls, the feedback "loosens its grip," so the closed-loop response is dominated by the slowly varying $\beta$-network rather than the falling op-amp response. - Mathematically, the **effective pole** moves from $\omega_H$ to $\omega_H(1+A_0\beta)$, pushing the $-3$ dB corner to a higher frequency. ## 2.2 The Closed-Loop –3 dB Bandwidth For an op-amp with open-loop $-3$ dB frequency $f_H$ (Hz) and open-loop DC gain $A_0$: $$f_f = f_H \,(1 + A_0\beta) \quad \text{— bandwidth increases by the } (1+A\beta) \text{ factor}$$ For *deep* feedback ($A_0\beta \gg 1$): $$A_f \approx \frac{1}{\beta} \quad \text{and} \quad f_f \approx \beta \cdot A_0 \cdot f_H = A_f \cdot f_H$$ ## 2.3 The Four Topologies — Closed-Loop Gain and Bandwidth | Topology | Closed-Loop Gain $A_f$ | $-3$ dB Bandwidth $f_f$ | Gain–Bandwidth Product | | :-- | :-- | :-- | :-- | | Voltage-Series (Non-Inverting) | $1 + R_2/R_1$ | $f_H(1 + A_0\beta)$ | $A_f \cdot f_f$ | | Voltage-Shunt (Inverting) | $-R_f/R_1$ | $f_H(1 + A_0\beta)$ | $\vert A_f \vert \cdot f_f$ | | Current-Series (Transcond.) | $g_m \cdot R_L / (1 + R_2/R_1)$ | $f_H(1 + A_0\beta)$ | $A_f \cdot f_f$ | | Current-Shunt (Transres.) | $-R_f / R_1$ | $f_H(1 + A_0\beta)$ | $\vert A_f \vert \cdot f_f$ | > [!TIP] > **Engineering Utility:** Bandwidth extension is the *real reason* op-amps are used with feedback. A raw op-amp like the $\mu$A741 has $f_H \approx 5$ Hz; with a closed-loop gain of 10, the bandwidth becomes $5 \times (1 + 200000/10) \approx 100$ kHz — four orders of magnitude improvement. ## 2.4 Sources of Output Offset Voltage 1. **Input Offset Voltage $V_{IO}$** — the differential DC voltage that must be applied between the input pins to force the output to zero (typically $1$–$5$ mV for a 741). 2. **Input Bias Current $I_B$** — the average DC current flowing into the input terminals ($I_B = (I_{B}^{+} + I_{B}^{-})/2$, typically $80$ nA for 741). 3. **Input Offset Current $I_{IO} = \vert I_{B}^{-} - I_{B}^{+} \vert$** — the *difference* between the two bias currents (typically $20$ nA for 741). ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat-Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below are **board-exam favourites**. Memorise the *form*, the *boundary conditions*, and the *sign convention*. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Condition | | :-- | :-- | :-- | :-- | | 1 | Closed-loop gain | $A_f = A_0 / (1 + A_0\beta)$ | dimensionless | | 2 | Loop gain | $T = A_0\beta$ | dimensionless | | 3 | Closed-loop $-3$ dB BW | $f_f = f_H (1 + A_0\beta)$ | Hz | | 4 | GBP (invariant) | $A_f \cdot f_f = A_0 \cdot f_H$ | Hz | | 5 | Inverting-amp $V_{OO}$ (V) | $(1 + R_f/R_1) V_{IO} + R_f \cdot I_B$ | Volts | | 6 | Non-inv $V_{OO}$ (V) | $(1 + R_f/R_1)[ V_{IO} + R_s \cdot I_B ]$ | $R_s = R_1 \mid\mid R_f$ | | 7 | Transcond. $I_{OO}$ (A) | $(1 + R_2/R_1) V_{IO}/R_s + (1 + R_2/R_1) I_B$ | Amperes | | 8 | Current-amp $I_{OO}$ (A) | $(1 + R_f/R_1) V_{IO}/R_s + R_f \cdot I_B / R_L$ | Amperes | | 9 | Bias-comp. resistor | $R_s = R_1 \mid\mid R_f$ | Ohms | | 10 | Total worst-case offset | $V_{OO} = V_{IO} \cdot A_f \pm I_{IO} \cdot R_{eq} \cdot A_f$ | Worst-case sign | > **Boundary condition:** The formulas above assume a single-pole op-amp, $A_0\beta \gg 1$ (deep feedback), and that the *compensation resistor* $R_s$ has been added to the non-inverting input to null the $I_B$ contribution. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Instrumentation amplifiers** (medical ECG/EEG front-ends) use low-offset precision op-amps (e.g., OP-07, $V_{IO} = 25\ \mu$V) precisely because the output offset is *directly multiplied* by the gain — a $5$ mV offset × gain 1000 = $5$ V error. - **Audio pre-amplifiers** rely on bandwidth extension: a 5532 op-amp has GBP $= 10$ MHz, so at a closed-loop gain of 10, bandwidth = 1 MHz (far beyond audio range). - **Active filters** depend on GBP being much larger than the filter cutoff, otherwise the filter $Q$ factor and frequency get distorted. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — Bandwidth Extension from a Single-Pole Op-Amp ### Step 1: Open-Loop Transfer Function For an op-amp with DC gain $A_0$ and a dominant pole at $\omega_H = 2\pi f_H$: $$A(s) = \frac{A_0}{1 + s/\omega_H}$$ The $-3$ dB bandwidth of the open-loop response is $f_H$. ### Step 2: Close the Loop with Negative Feedback $\beta$ The closed-loop transfer function is, by definition: $$A_f(s) = \frac{A(s)}{1 + A(s)\,\beta}$$ ### Step 3: Substitute the Open-Loop Expression $$A_f(s) = \frac{\dfrac{A_0}{1 + s/\omega_H}}{1 + \beta \cdot \dfrac{A_0}{1 + s/\omega_H}}$$ Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1 + s/\omega_H)$: $$A_f(s) = \frac{A_0}{(1 + s/\omega_H) + \beta A_0}$$ ### Step 4: Group the Constant and Frequency-Dependent Terms $$A_f(s) = \frac{A_0}{1 + \beta A_0} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{s/\omega_H}{1 + \beta A_0}} = \frac{A_0}{1 + \beta A_0} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + s/\omega_H(1 + \beta A_0)}$$ ### Step 5: Identify the New DC Gain and New Pole Frequency Comparing with the standard form $A_f(s) = \dfrac{A_f}{1 + s/\omega_{Hf}}$: $$\boxed{\,A_f = \frac{A_0}{1 + A_0\beta} \quad ; \quad \omega_{Hf} = \omega_H(1 + A_0\beta)\,}$$ Hence the **$-3$ dB bandwidth** becomes: $$f_f = f_H \cdot (1 + A_0\beta) \;\;\text{Hz}$$ ### Step 6: Multiply Gain × Bandwidth to Prove Invariance $$A_f \cdot f_f = \frac{A_0}{1 + A_0\beta} \cdot f_H(1 + A_0\beta) = A_0 \cdot f_H = \text{GBP}$$ The product is independent of $\beta$ — **the gain–bandwidth product is conserved**. > [!NOTE] > **Conversion logic recap:** We pulled the constant $1 + A_0\beta$ out of the denominator, leaving the new pole at a *higher* frequency. This is the algebraic signature of negative feedback — it pushes the pole outward along the negative real axis. --- ## 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — Output Offset Voltage of a Voltage-Shunt (Inverting) Amplifier ### Circuit & Assumptions Inverting amplifier with $R_1$ (input), $R_f$ (feedback), and a compensation resistor $R_s = R_1 \mid\mid R_f$ at the non-inverting input to cancel the *common-mode* $I_B$ effect. The op-amp is modelled as ideal **plus** three error sources: $V_{IO}$ (between input pins), $I_B^{+}$ (into + pin), $I_B^{-}$ (into – pin). ### Step 1: Write the Node Equation at the Inverting Input Define $V^{-}$ and $V^{+}$ as the actual node voltages (not the ideal virtual short). Then: $$V^{+} - V^{-} = V_{IO} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad V^{-} = V^{+} - V_{IO}$$ ### Step 2: Compute $V^{+}$ The non-inverting input is connected to ground through $R_s$; the bias current $I_B^{+}$ flows *into* the + pin, so by KCL $V^{+} = -I_B^{+} R_s$. $$V^{+} = -I_B^{+} R_s$$ $$V^{-} = -I_B^{+} R_s - V_{IO}$$ ### Step 3: KCL at the Inverting Node Currents leaving the node sum to zero (with $I_B^{-}$ flowing *into* the – pin, the current *leaving* through the op-amp input is $-I_B^{-}$): $$\frac{0 - V^{-}}{R_1} + \frac{V_{out} - V^{-}}{R_f} - I_B^{-} = 0$$ ### Step 4: Substitute $V^{-}$ and Solve for $V_{out}$ $$\frac{V^{-}}{R_1} = \frac{V^{-} - V_{out}}{R_f} + I_B^{-}$$ Multiply through by $R_f$: $$\frac{R_f}{R_1} V^{-} = V^{-} - V_{out} + R_f I_B^{-}$$ Rearrange: $$V_{out} = V^{-}\left(1 - \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) - R_f I_B^{-}$$ ### Step 5: Substitute $V^{-} = -I_B^{+} R_s - V_{IO}$ $$V_{out} = \left(-I_B^{+} R_s - V_{IO}\right)\left(1 - \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) - R_f I_B^{-}$$ ### Step 6: Extract the Output Offset (set $V_{in} = 0$) Since $V_{in} = 0$ was already used, $V_{out}$ is purely the **output offset voltage**: $$\boxed{\,V_{OO} = -\left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{IO} \;-\; R_f \, I_B^{-} \;+\; \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) I_B^{+} R_s\,}$$ ### Step 7: Apply the Compensation Condition $R_s = R_1 \mid\mid R_f$ Substitute $R_s = \dfrac{R_1 R_f}{R_1 + R_f}$. Then: $$\left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) R_s = \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) \cdot \frac{R_1 R_f}{R_1 + R_f} = R_f$$ So the bias terms combine: $$V_{OO} = -\left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{IO} - R_f (I_B^{-} - I_B^{+})$$ ### Step 8: Introduce the Offset Current $I_{IO}$ By definition $I_{IO} = I_B^{-} - I_B^{+}$ (and $I_B = (I_B^{+} + I_B^{-})/2$). The final worst-case form is: $$\boxed{\,V_{OO} = -\left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{IO} \;\pm\; R_f \, I_{IO}\,}$$ Or, separating the bias-current contribution (when compensation is *not* used): $$V_{OO} = \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{IO} + R_f \, I_B$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conversion logic recap:** The $I_B$ contribution *vanishes* when $R_s$ is exactly $R_1 \mid\mid R_f$, leaving only the *offset current* $I_{IO}$ — which is typically an order of magnitude smaller than $I_B$. This is why the bias-compensation resistor is a **mandatory** design step in KTU problems. --- ## 3.3 DERIVATION 3 — Output Offset Voltage of a Voltage-Series (Non-Inverting) Amplifier ### Step 1: Define the Equivalent Input Errors With the + input driven by source resistance $R_s = R_1 \mid\mid R_f$ (compensation): $$V^{+} = -I_B^{+} R_s$$ $$V^{-} = V^{+} - V_{IO} = -I_B^{+} R_s - V_{IO}$$ ### Step 2: KCL at the Inverting Node With input grounded ($V_{in} = 0$): $$\frac{0 - V^{-}}{R_1} + \frac{V_{out} - V^{-}}{R_f} - I_B^{-} = 0$$ This is **identical in form** to the inverting case, giving: $$V_{out} = V^{-}\left(1 - \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) - R_f I_B^{-}$$ ### Step 3: Substitute and Apply $R_s = R_1 \mid\mid R_f$ $$V_{OO} = \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{IO} + \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) R_s \, I_B^{+} - R_f I_B^{-}$$ With $R_s = R_1 \mid\mid R_f$, $\;(1 + R_f/R_1) R_s = R_f$, and $I_B^{-} = I_B^{+} + I_{IO}$: $$\boxed{\,V_{OO} = \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{IO} \;\pm\; \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) R_s \, I_{IO}\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > The $I_B$ term drops out under compensation, but now the $I_{IO}$ term carries the *closed-loop gain factor* $(1 + R_f/R_1)$ — making the non-inverting configuration **slightly more sensitive** to offset current than the inverting one (where $I_{IO}$ is multiplied by $R_f$ alone). --- ## 3.4 DERIVATION 4 — Output Offset of Current-Series Feedback (Transconductance Amplifier) ### Configuration Series input / series output. The output is a *current* $I_{out}$ flowing into a load $R_L$. Closed-loop gain is $A_f = G_M \cdot R_L / (1 + R_2/R_1)$. ### Step 1: Offset Current at the Output The input offset $V_{IO}$ acts between the input terminals. The differential input voltage is therefore $V_d = V_{IO}$ (when both input sources are zero). The op-amp output current is: $$\Delta I_{out} = g_m \cdot V_d = g_m \, V_{IO}$$ ### Step 2: Apply Negative Feedback Closing the loop with current-sampling feedback $\beta$ (in units of A/A): $$I_{out,off} = \frac{g_m \, V_{IO}}{1 + g_m \beta R_L / (1 + R_2/R_1)} \approx \left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) \frac{V_{IO}}{R_s}$$ ### Step 3: Add the Bias-Current Contribution The input bias current $I_B$ flows through $R_s$ to develop an additional input voltage, which is amplified in the same way: $$\boxed{\,I_{OO} = \left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) \left[\frac{V_{IO}}{R_s} + I_B\right] \pm \left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) \frac{I_{IO}}{2}\,}$$ --- ## 3.5 DERIVATION 5 — Output Offset of Current-Shunt Feedback (Current Amplifier) The input is a *current*, the output is a *current*. The closed-loop current gain is $A_{If} = -R_f / R_1$. ### Step 1: Convert $V_{IO}$ to an Equivalent Input Current The input source has resistance $R_s$. The $V_{IO}$ at the input is equivalent to a current $V_{IO} / R_s$ injected at the input node. ### Step 2: The Bias Currents Flow Through the Source The bias current $I_B$ also creates a voltage drop $I_B R_s$ that the op-amp amplifies. ### Step 3: Combine $$\boxed{\,I_{OO} = \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right)\left[\frac{V_{IO} + I_B R_s}{R_s}\right] \pm \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) \frac{I_{IO}}{2}\,}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > For *all four topologies*, the **structural pattern** is identical: the *closed-loop gain* multiplies an *equivalent input offset* that consists of $V_{IO}$ plus a bias-current term. Memorise the *pattern*, not just the formula. --- ## 3.6 Summary Comparison Matrix — Output Offset Across the Four Topologies | Topology | Input Mixing | Output Offset Quantity | $V_{IO}$ Term Multiplier | $I_B$ Term | Best Compensation | | :-- | :-- | :-- | :-- | :-- | :-- | | **Voltage-Series** | Series | Voltage $V_{OO}$ | $(1 + R_f/R_1)$ | $(1 + R_f/R_1) R_s$ | $R_s = R_1 \mid\mid R_f$ | | **Voltage-Shunt** | Shunt | Voltage $V_{OO}$ | $(1 + R_f/R_1)$ | $R_f$ | $R_s = R_1 \mid\mid R_f$ | | **Current-Series** | Series | Current $I_{OO}$ | $(1 + R_2/R_1)/R_s$ | $(1 + R_2/R_1)$ | $R_s = R_1 \mid\mid R_f$ | | **Current-Shunt** | Shunt | Current $I_{OO}$ | $(1 + R_f/R_1)/R_s$ | $(1 + R_f/R_1)$ | $R_s = R_1 \mid\mid R_f$ | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Negative Feedback Loop with Bandwidth Extension ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeVin["Vin Source"] --> nodeInSum(("Summing Junction")) nodeInSum --> nodeA["Open-Loop Amplifier
Gain A of s
Bandwidth fH"] nodeA --> nodeOut["Vout"] nodeOut --> nodeBeta["Feedback Network
Beta"] nodeBeta --> nodeInSum nodeA -.->|"3 dB corner at fH"| nodeMarkerA[Original Pole] nodeBeta -.->|"Feedback pushes pole to
fH x 1 + AB"| nodeMarkerF["New Pole at ff"] style nodeA fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 style nodeBeta fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000 style nodeInSum fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000 style nodeMarkerF fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid — Bandwidth Extension Mechanism (Frequency-Domain View) ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart[Open-Loop Response] --> nodeA["A0: 200000
fH: 5 Hz"] nodeA --> nodeForm["A s equals A0 divided by 1 plus s over omegaH"] nodeForm --> nodeClose[Close Loop with Beta 0.1] nodeClose --> nodeAf["Af equals 10"] nodeAf --> nodeff["ff equals 50000 Hz
Gain drops 20000x
Bandwidth rises 10000x"] nodeff --> nodeGBP["GBP Invariant
A0 fH equals Af ff
equals 1 MHz"] style nodeStart fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d,color:#000 style nodeff fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 style nodeGBP fill:#cff4fc,stroke:#0dcaf0,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid — The Four Feedback Topologies (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TOPO1["VOLTAGE-SERIES"] V1["Vin Series at + input"] --> A1["Amplifier A"] A1 --> R1["Output Voltage Vout"] R1 --> B1["Beta Network"] B1 --> V1 end subgraph TOPO2["VOLTAGE-SHUNT"] V2["Iin Shunt at - input"] --> A2["Amplifier A"] A2 --> R2["Output Voltage Vout"] R2 --> B2["Beta Network"] B2 --> V2 end subgraph TOPO3["CURRENT-SERIES"] V3["Vin Series at + input"] --> A3["Amplifier A"] A3 --> R3["Output Current Iout"] R3 --> B3["Beta Network"] B3 --> V3 end subgraph TOPO4["CURRENT-SHUNT"] V4["Iin Shunt at - input"] --> A4["Amplifier A"] A4 --> R4["Output Current Iout"] R4 --> B4["Beta Network"] B4 --> V4 end style TOPO1 fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 style TOPO2 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000 style TOPO3 fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 style TOPO4 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid — Output Offset-Voltage Build-Up Mechanism (Inverting Amplifier) ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeGND["Ground 0 V"] --> nodeR1["R1"] nodeR1 --> nodeInv["Inverting Node V-"] nodeInv --> nodeRf["Rf to Vout"] nodeInv --> nodeVio["VIO Source
between + and - inputs"] nodeRs["Compensation Resistor Rs = R1 parallel Rf"] --> nodeNonInv["Non-Inverting Node V+"] nodeNonInv --> nodeIBp["Bias Current IB+ flows in"] nodeInv --> nodeIBn["Bias Current IB- flows in"] nodeIBp --> nodeIBn nodeIBn --> nodeOffset["IO = IB- minus IB+"] nodeVio --> nodeSumV["VOO = -1 + Rf by R1 times VIO"] nodeOffset --> nodeSumI["VOO = plus or minus Rf times IIO"] nodeSumV --> nodeFinal["Total Output Offset Voltage VOO"] nodeSumI --> nodeFinal style nodeGND fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d,color:#000 style nodeFinal fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000 style nodeVio fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 ``` ## 4.5 ASCII Block Diagram — Closed-Loop BW Invariance (Quick Visual) ```text GAIN BANDWIDTH A0 = 200 000 * fH = 5 Hz = GBP = 1 000 000 Hz | | /\ | / \ A0 | / \ | /______\___________ | fH | v Apply Beta = 0.1 ______________________ Af = 10 * ff = 100 000 Hz = GBP = 1 000 000 Hz | | /\ | / \ Af | / \ | /______\____________________ | ff ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **State the relationship between the closed-loop gain, the loop gain, and the bandwidth extension factor of a negative feedback amplifier. Why is the Gain–Bandwidth Product considered invariant?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** For an op-amp with open-loop DC gain $A_0$, open-loop $-3$ dB bandwidth $f_H$, and feedback factor $\beta$: $$A_f = \frac{A_0}{1 + A_0\beta} \quad ; \quad f_f = f_H (1 + A_0\beta)$$ Multiplying the two: $$A_f \cdot f_f = \frac{A_0}{1 + A_0\beta} \cdot f_H(1 + A_0\beta) = A_0 f_H$$ The factor $(1 + A_0\beta)$ appears in both the gain and bandwidth expressions with *opposite signs* — the closed-loop gain is reduced by the same factor by which the bandwidth is extended. Hence the **GBP is invariant** under negative feedback, and the result is a fundamental *trade-off* rather than a free improvement. *(1 mark: state formulas; 1 mark: derive invariance; 1 mark: physical interpretation.)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **List the three non-ideal op-amp parameters that contribute to the output offset voltage of a closed-loop amplifier. Why is a compensation resistor $R_s = R_1 \mid\mid R_f$ added at the non-inverting input?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Input Offset Voltage $V_{IO}$** — internal mismatch between the differential input stage. 2. **Input Bias Current $I_B$** — base/gate current required to bias the input transistors. 3. **Input Offset Current $I_{IO} = \vert I_B^{-} - I_B^{+} \vert$** — mismatch between the two bias currents. A resistor $R_s = R_1 \mid\mid R_f$ is placed at the non-inverting input so that the *DC resistances seen from both inputs to ground are equal*. This makes the two $I_B$-induced voltage drops identical, leaving only the much smaller $I_{IO}$ contribution to the output offset. *(1 mark per parameter; 1 mark for compensation logic.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Voltage-Series Feedback: Bandwidth and Output Offset **[KTU University Exam – Model Question aligned with 2024 Scheme, Module 2]** **(a)** For a non-inverting amplifier using an op-amp with open-loop DC gain $A_0 = 2 \times 10^5$ and open-loop $-3$ dB bandwidth $f_H = 5$ Hz, with $R_1 = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$ and $R_f = 9\ \text{k}\Omega$:     **(i)** Compute the closed-loop gain $A_f$ and the feedback factor $\beta$.     **(ii)** Compute the closed-loop $-3$ dB bandwidth $f_f$ and the gain–bandwidth product.     **(iii)** If the input offset voltage is $V_{IO} = 5$ mV, input bias current $I_B = 100$ nA, and input offset current $I_{IO} = 20$ nA, compute the **total output offset voltage** assuming a compensation resistor $R_s = R_1 \mid\mid R_f$ is used. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the output offset voltage expression of a voltage-shunt (inverting) feedback amplifier with $R_1$, $R_f$, and compensation resistor $R_s = R_1 \mid\mid R_f$, starting from the equivalent circuit with $V_{IO}$ and bias-current sources. Show that the bias-current term cancels and only the $I_{IO}$ contribution remains. **(7 marks)** --- #### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A ##### Part (a) (7 marks) **(i) Closed-loop gain and feedback factor (2 marks):** $$A_f = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 1 + \frac{9\text{ k}\Omega}{1\text{ k}\Omega} = 10$$ $$\beta = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f} = \frac{1}{1 + 9} = 0.1$$ **Check using exact formula:** $$A_f = \frac{A_0}{1 + A_0\beta} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{1 + 2 \times 10^5 \times 0.1} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{20001} \approx 9.9995 \approx 10$$ **(ii) Closed-loop bandwidth and GBP (2 marks):** $$f_f = f_H(1 + A_0\beta) = 5\,(1 + 2 \times 10^5 \times 0.1) = 5 \times 20001 = 100{,}005 \text{ Hz} \approx 100 \text{ kHz}$$ $$\text{GBP} = A_0 \cdot f_H = 2 \times 10^5 \times 5 = 10^6 \text{ Hz} = 1 \text{ MHz}$$ **Verification:** $A_f \cdot f_f = 10 \times 10^5 = 10^6$ Hz ✓ **(iii) Output offset voltage (3 marks):** Compensation resistor: $$R_s = R_1 \mid\mid R_f = \frac{1 \times 9}{1 + 9} = 0.9 \text{ k}\Omega = 900\ \Omega$$ From the derived formula for a non-inverting amplifier *with* compensation: $$V_{OO} = \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{IO} \pm \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) R_s \, I_{IO}$$ Substitute values: $$V_{OO} = 10 \times 5 \text{ mV} \pm 10 \times 900 \times 20 \text{ nA}$$ $$V_{OO} = 50 \text{ mV} \pm 0.18 \text{ mV}$$ Worst-case magnitude: $$\boxed{\,V_{OO} = 50 \pm 0.18 \text{ mV} = 50.18 \text{ mV (max)}\,}$$ The $I_{IO}$ contribution is **negligible** compared to the $V_{IO}$ contribution — this is precisely why bias-current compensation is worth doing. ##### Part (b) (7 marks) — Derivation of Voltage-Shunt Output Offset Refer to **DERIVATION 2 in Section 3.2** of these notes for the complete step-by-step derivation. The key checkpoints for full marks are: 1. **Define** the equivalent circuit: $V_{IO}$ between input pins, $I_B^{+}$ entering + input, $I_B^{-}$ entering – input. **(1 mark)** 2. **Express** $V^{+} = -I_B^{+} R_s$ and $V^{-} = V^{+} - V_{IO} = -I_B^{+} R_s - V_{IO}$. **(1 mark)** 3. **KCL** at the inverting node, with the input terminal grounded. **(2 marks)** 4. **Solve** for $V_{out}$ to obtain: $$V_{OO} = -\left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{IO} - R_f I_B^{-} + \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) R_s I_B^{+}$$ **(1 mark)** 5. **Apply** $R_s = R_1 \mid\mid R_f \Rightarrow (1 + R_f/R_1) R_s = R_f$. Combine bias terms: $R_f(I_B^{+} - I_B^{-}) = -R_f I_{IO}$. **(1 mark)** 6. **Final result:** $V_{OO} = -(1 + R_f/R_1) V_{IO} \pm R_f I_{IO}$ — the $I_B$ term *cancels*. **(1 mark)** --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — All Four Topologies: Bandwidth and Offset Comparison **[KTU University Exam – Model Question aligned with 2024 Scheme, Module 2]** **(a)** Tabulate the closed-loop gain expressions, the feedback factor $\beta$, and the closed-loop $-3$ dB bandwidth for **all four** feedback topologies (voltage-series, voltage-shunt, current-series, current-shunt). For an op-amp with $A_0 = 10^5$ and $f_H = 10$ Hz, find the closed-loop bandwidth in each case when the closed-loop gain magnitude is fixed at 10. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An op-amp used in a transconductance (current-series feedback) amplifier has $V_{IO} = 2$ mV, $I_B = 50$ nA, and $I_{IO} = 5$ nA. The feedback network has $R_1 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ and $R_2 = 90\ \text{k}\Omega$, the source resistance $R_s = 9\ \text{k}\Omega$, and the load $R_L = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$. Calculate the **worst-case output offset current** $I_{OO}$ of the amplifier. **(7 marks)** --- #### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B ##### Part (a) (7 marks) | Topology | $A_f$ | $\beta$ | $f_f$ at $\vert A_f \vert = 10$ | | :-- | :-- | :-- | :-- | | Voltage-Series | $1 + R_f/R_1$ | $R_1/(R_1 + R_f)$ | $10^5 \times 10 \times 10 = 10$ MHz | | Voltage-Shunt | $-R_f/R_1$ | $R_1/(R_1 + R_f)$ | $10^5 \times 10 \times 10 = 10$ MHz | | Current-Series | $\dfrac{g_m R_L}{1 + R_2/R_1}$ | $\dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ | $10$ MHz | | Current-Shunt | $-R_f/R_1$ | $R_1/(R_1 + R_f)$ | $10$ MHz | **Common computation** for each topology, with $A_0 = 10^5$, $f_H = 10$ Hz, $\vert A_f \vert = 10$: $$\beta \approx \frac{1}{A_f} = \frac{1}{10} = 0.1$$ $$1 + A_0\beta = 1 + 10^5 \times 0.1 = 10{,}001 \approx 10^4$$ $$f_f = f_H (1 + A_0\beta) = 10 \times 10^4 = 10^5 \text{ Hz} = 100 \text{ kHz}$$ $$\text{GBP} = A_f \cdot f_f = 10 \times 10^5 = 10^6 \text{ Hz} = 1 \text{ MHz} = A_0 f_H \;\checkmark$$ **Interpretation (1 mark):** All four topologies share the same GBP, confirming the topology-independent nature of the bandwidth-extension theorem. ##### Part (b) (7 marks) — Transconductance Amplifier Output Offset Current The current-series feedback topology with compensation $R_s = R_1 \mid\mid R_2$: $$R_s = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{10 \times 90}{10 + 90} = 9 \text{ k}\Omega \quad \checkmark$$ Closed-loop current gain factor: $$1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1} = 1 + \frac{90}{10} = 10$$ **$V_{IO}$ contribution to $I_{OO}$:** $$I_{OO,V_{IO}} = \left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) \frac{V_{IO}}{R_s} = 10 \times \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{9 \times 10^3} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-2}}{9 \times 10^3} = 2.222\ \mu\text{A}$$ **$I_B$ contribution to $I_{OO}$:** $$I_{OO,I_B} = \left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) I_B = 10 \times 50\ \text{nA} = 500\ \text{nA} = 0.5\ \mu\text{A}$$ **$I_{IO}$ contribution to $I_{OO}$** (worst-case): $$I_{OO,I_{IO}} = \left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) \frac{I_{IO}}{2} = 10 \times 2.5\ \text{nA} = 25\ \text{nA} = 0.025\ \mu\text{A}$$ **Total worst-case output offset current:** $$\boxed{\,I_{OO} = 2.222 + 0.5 + 0.025 = 2.747\ \mu\text{A}\,}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Bandwidth & Offset Problems:** > 1. **Sign convention:** Many students drop the negative sign in the inverting-amp formula — KTU awards *zero* marks for the offset sign if the derivation leading to it is wrong, even if the magnitude is right. Always derive the expression symbolically first, *then* plug in numbers. > 2. **Units mismatch:** $I_B$ in nA vs. resistance in kΩ gives offsets in **mV** — students often forget to convert and report $\mu$V. Show units at every step. > 3. **Forgetting the compensation resistor:** When a problem specifies $R_s = R_1 \mid\mid R_f$, you *must* apply it to cancel the $I_B$ term. Missing this costs 2 marks. > 4. **GBP trap:** Examiners often rephrase as "If the closed-loop gain is doubled, what happens to the bandwidth?" — the correct answer is *halved* (GBP constant), not "slightly reduced." > 5. **Confusing the four topologies:** Voltage-Series ≠ Current-Series. Memorise the *shunt/shunt, series/series* pattern; the first word is the **input** mixing, the second is the **output** sampling. > 6. **Worst-case offset:** Always take the **positive** sign of the $I_{IO}$ term — the question almost always asks for the *magnitude* of the worst-case offset. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Bandwidth extension factor** $= (1 + A_0\beta)$ — same for *all* four feedback topologies. - [x] **Gain–Bandwidth Product** $A_f f_f = A_0 f_H$ is **invariant** — you trade gain for bandwidth, nothing is "free." - [x] **Voltage-Series** = non-inverting, $A_f = 1 + R_f/R_1$, **high input impedance**, **low output impedance**. - [x] **Voltage-Shunt** = inverting, $A_f = -R_f/R_1$, **low input impedance**, **low output impedance**. - [x] **Current-Series** = transconductance, $A_f = g_m R_L / (1 + R_2/R_1)$, **high Z in & out**. - [x] **Current-Shunt** = current amplifier, $A_f = -R_f/R_1$, **low Z in, high Z out**. - [x] **Output offset** has **three sources**: $V_{IO}$, $I_B$, $I_{IO}$ — all are amplified by the closed-loop gain. - [x] **Bias-current compensation** by $R_s = R_1 \mid\mid R_f$ cancels the $I_B$ contribution, leaving only the much smaller $I_{IO}$. - [x] **Inverting-amp $V_{OO}$:** $\;\;V_{OO} = (1 + R_f/R_1) V_{IO} \pm R_f I_{IO}$. - [x] **Non-inverting-amp $V_{OO}$:** $\;\;V_{OO} = (1 + R_f/R_1)[V_{IO} \pm R_s I_{IO}]$. - [x] **Transconductance $I_{OO}$:** $\;\;I_{OO} = (1 + R_2/R_1)[V_{IO}/R_s + I_B \pm I_{IO}/2]$. - [x] **Current-amplifier $I_{OO}$:** $\;\;I_{OO} = (1 + R_f/R_1)[(V_{IO} + I_B R_s)/R_s \pm I_{IO}/2]$. - [x] **Worst-case offset** always takes the **plus sign** for $I_{IO}$. - [x] **GBP trade-off** is the *single most important concept* of Module 2 — expect it in **every** KTU paper. - [x] **Engineering tip:** To minimise output offset in a precision circuit, *first* use a low-$V_{IO}$ op-amp (e.g., OP-07, $V_{IO} = 25\ \mu$V); the bias-current compensation then makes the $I_{IO}$ term negligible.

topics.title: Voltage Follower
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEBT402 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Voltage Follower # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## Formal Definition A **Voltage Follower** is a specific configuration of an operational amplifier in which the output voltage is made to follow the input voltage exactly, producing a **unity voltage gain (Aᵥ = 1)**. It is implemented by taking the **non-inverting input (+)** as the signal input and connecting the output directly back to the **inverting input (−)**, thereby applying **100% negative feedback**. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the Voltage Follower is a special case of the **Non-Inverting Amplifier** where the feedback resistor **R_f = 0 Ω** and the input resistor **R_1 = ∞ Ω** (open circuit). It is also known as a **Unity Gain Buffer** or **Source Follower** (in op-amp terms). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Voltage Follower is treated as a fundamental building block in linear IC design. It is the simplest op-amp topology and the *first* configuration a KTU student must master before moving on to inverting/non-inverting amplifiers, summers, and integrators. ## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are speaking softly to a large, noisy crowd. Even though your voice is clear, the crowd cannot hear you well because of the **impedance mismatch** between your mouth (high internal impedance) and the air/crowd (low impedance, but lossy). To solve this, you use a **megaphone**. You whisper into it, and it produces the *exact same words* at a *much louder volume* without changing what you said. The Voltage Follower is the **electronic megaphone**: - The **input signal** is your soft voice. - The **output signal** is the amplified voice — same content, same level (voltage), but with **vastly increased drive capability**. - It **does not amplify voltage**, but it **isolates** the source from the load, protecting the source from being "loaded down." > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics:** > - Ideal Input Impedance (Z_in): **∞ Ω (Infinite)** > - Ideal Output Impedance (Z_out): **0 Ω (Zero)** > - Ideal Voltage Gain (Aᵥ): **1 (Unity)** > - Ideal Bandwidth: **Infinite** (practically limited by Gain-Bandwidth Product, GBP) > - Ideal Current Gain: **∞ (Infinite)** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## Operational Breakdown — The "Why" and "How" A Voltage Follower operates on the principle of **Shunt-Shunt (Voltage-Voltage) Negative Feedback**. Here is the step-by-step logic: 1. **Signal Entry:** The input signal V_in is applied to the **non-inverting (+) terminal** of the op-amp. 2. **Feedback Path:** The output V_out is connected **directly** to the **inverting (−) terminal** through a wire (zero-resistance feedback). This means V⁻ = V_out. 3. **Virtual Short Action:** Due to the negative feedback, the op-amp uses its internal high open-loop gain (A_OL ≈ 10⁵ to 10⁶ for a 741 op-amp) to force the two input terminals to be at the same potential: V⁺ = V⁻. 4. **Equilibrium Condition:** Since V⁺ = V_in and V⁻ = V_out, the op-amp continuously adjusts its output until **V_out = V_in**. 5. **No Loading Effect:** Because the op-amp's input impedance is extremely high, almost no current flows into the (+) terminal. The source therefore "sees" a near-infinite impedance and is unaffected by the load. 6. **Strong Drive:** The op-amp's output stage can source/sink significant current to drive low-impedance loads, something the original source could never do alone. ## Real-World Utility in Engineering - **Impedance Buffering** between high-impedance sensors (e.g., pH probes, piezoelectric sensors) and low-impedance ADCs. - **Sample-and-Hold circuits** in data acquisition systems. - **Reference voltage drivers** in DACs and precision regulators. - **Active filter isolation** to prevent filter stage interaction. - **Driving long cables** in instrumentation without signal degradation. ## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Ideal Value** | **Formula / Relationship** | **Unit** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Closed-Loop Voltage Gain | A_v | 1 | $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} = 1 + \dfrac{0}{\infty} = 1$ | Dimensionless | | Input Impedance | Z_in | $\infty$ | $Z_{in} \approx (1 + A_{OL}\beta) \cdot Z_{in\_OL}$ | $\Omega$ | | Output Impedance | Z_out | 0 | $Z_{out} \approx \dfrac{Z_{out\_OL}}{1 + A_{OL}\beta}$ | $\Omega$ | | Feedback Factor | $\beta$ | 1 | $\beta = \dfrac{V_f}{V_{out}} = 1$ (100% feedback) | Dimensionless | | Loop Gain | $A_{OL}\beta$ | $\infty$ (ideal) | $A_{OL}\beta \approx 10^5$ to $10^6$ for 741 | Dimensionless | | Bandwidth (Closed Loop) | BW | GBP (full) | $BW = \dfrac{GBP}{A_v} = GBP$ (since A_v = 1) | Hz | | Output Voltage | V_out | = V_in | $V_{out} = V_{in}$ | V | > [!NOTE] > **Examination Pearl:** When Aᵥ = 1, the **closed-loop bandwidth equals the Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP)**. This is a hugely important concept for KTU numerical problems involving frequency response. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## Derivation 1: Closed-Loop Voltage Gain Starting from the **Non-Inverting Amplifier** general configuration, the voltage gain is: $$ A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} $$ **For a Voltage Follower:** - The feedback resistor R_f is replaced by a short circuit → **R_f = 0 Ω** - The input resistor R_1 is removed (open circuit) → **R_1 = ∞ Ω** Substituting: $$ A_v = 1 + \frac{0}{\infty} $$ $$ A_v = 1 + 0 $$ $$ \boxed{A_v = 1} $$ **Logic:** With R_f = 0, the full output is fed back. With R_1 removed, there is no resistor to form a voltage divider. Hence, 100% of the output is subtracted from the input, and the op-amp must produce an output exactly equal to the input to nullify the differential input. ## Derivation 2: Input Impedance with Feedback For a non-inverting topology, the input impedance with feedback is given by: $$ Z_{in} = Z_{in\_OL} \cdot (1 + A_{OL}\beta) $$ **For a Voltage Follower**, $\beta = 1$: $$ Z_{in} = Z_{in\_OL} \cdot (1 + A_{OL} \cdot 1) $$ $$ Z_{in} = Z_{in\_OL} \cdot (1 + A_{OL}) $$ $$ \boxed{Z_{in} \approx A_{OL} \cdot Z_{in\_OL}} $$ **Numerical Example (KTU Style):** For a 741 op-amp with $Z_{in\_OL} = 2 \text{ M}\Omega$ and $A_{OL} = 2 \times 10^5$: $$ Z_{in} = 2 \times 10^6 \cdot (1 + 2 \times 10^5) $$ $$ Z_{in} = 2 \times 10^6 \cdot 200001 $$ $$ Z_{in} \approx 4 \times 10^{11} \ \Omega = 400 \text{ G}\Omega $$ This **near-infinite input impedance** is the defining advantage of the voltage follower. ## Derivation 3: Output Impedance with Feedback $$ Z_{out} = \frac{Z_{out\_OL}}{1 + A_{OL}\beta} $$ For Voltage Follower ($\beta = 1$): $$ Z_{out} = \frac{Z_{out\_OL}}{1 + A_{OL}} $$ $$ \boxed{Z_{out} \approx \frac{Z_{out\_OL}}{A_{OL}}} $$ **Numerical Example:** For a 741 op-amp with $Z_{out\_OL} = 75 \ \Omega$ and $A_{OL} = 2 \times 10^5$: $$ Z_{out} = \frac{75}{1 + 2 \times 10^5} $$ $$ Z_{out} = \frac{75}{200001} $$ $$ Z_{out} \approx 3.75 \times 10^{-4} \ \Omega \approx 0.000375 \ \Omega $$ This **near-zero output impedance** allows the voltage follower to drive low-impedance loads without voltage drop. ## Derivation 4: Closed-Loop Bandwidth The **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP)** is a constant for a given op-amp: $$ GBP = A_{OL} \cdot f_{OL} $$ The closed-loop bandwidth is: $$ BW_{CL} = \frac{GBP}{A_{CL}} $$ For Voltage Follower, $A_{CL} = 1$: $$ \boxed{BW_{CL} = \frac{GBP}{1} = GBP} $$ **Logic:** Since the voltage follower has unity gain, it does not trade gain for bandwidth. It therefore enjoys the **maximum possible bandwidth** the op-amp can offer — a critical feature in high-speed applications. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## Circuit Schematic (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[V_in Source
High Z] -->|Applied to Plus| nodeB[Op-Amp
Internal A_OL] nodeB -->|V_out| nodeC[Load
Low Z] nodeB -.->|Direct Wire Feedback| nodeD[V_minus Terminal] nodeD -.->|Virtual Short| nodeE[V_plus Terminal] nodeE --> nodeA style nodeA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style nodeB fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style nodeC fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style nodeD fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style nodeE fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` ## Functional Block Diagram (Detailed) ```mermaid flowchart TD inp[Input Signal V_in] --> noninv[Non-Inverting Terminal] noninv --> diffamp[Differential Amplifier Stage] diffamp --> gainstage[High Gain A_OL Stage] gainstage --> outputbuf[Output Buffer Stage] outputbuf --> vout[Output V_out] vout --> load[Drives Load R_L] vout -. 100 percent Negative Feedback .-> inv[Inverting Terminal] inv --> diffamp note1[Note Z_in very high, Z_out very low] -.-> vout style inp fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000000 style noninv fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000000 style inv fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000000 style diffamp fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000 style gainstage fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000 style outputbuf fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000 style vout fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#000000 style load fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#000000 style note1 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000000 ``` ## Key Signal Flow Summary ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Input Source] -->|High Z Seen| B[Voltage Follower] B -->|Same V, Low Z Output| C[Load] B -->|100 percent Feedback| B style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000000 style B fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000 style C fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#000000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Architecture Insight:** The three-block structure (Input Stage → Gain Stage → Output Buffer) is the canonical op-amp internal architecture. The voltage follower exercises the *entire* feedback loop because the output is fed directly to the inverting input, making it the most demanding configuration for op-amp stability analysis. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define a voltage follower. Why is it also called a unity gain buffer?** **Model Answer:** A voltage follower is an op-amp configuration in which the output is connected directly to the inverting input, while the input signal is applied to the non-inverting terminal. The closed-loop voltage gain is unity (Aᵥ = 1), so the output voltage exactly follows the input voltage without any amplification. It is called a **unity gain buffer** because (i) it provides a voltage gain of exactly 1, and (ii) it **buffers** (isolates) the high-impedance source from the low-impedance load, preventing loading effects. It offers high input impedance (≈ ∞), low output impedance (≈ 0), and 100% negative feedback with feedback factor β = 1. **(3 marks)** ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Mention any three applications of a voltage follower.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Impedance matching** between high-impedance sources (e.g., sensors) and low-impedance loads (e.g., ADCs). 2. **Isolation buffer** in cascaded amplifier stages to prevent inter-stage loading. 3. **Sample-and-Hold circuits** in data acquisition systems, or as a **reference voltage driver** in DACs and voltage regulators. **(3 marks)** --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Derive the expression for the closed-loop voltage gain of a voltage follower. Show that its input impedance is multiplied by a factor of (1 + A_OL) and its output impedance is divided by (1 + A_OL). **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 741 op-amp with open-loop gain A_OL = 2 × 10⁵, Z_in_OL = 2 MΩ, and Z_out_OL = 75 Ω is used as a voltage follower. Calculate the input and output impedances. A 1 V, 1 kHz sinusoidal source with internal resistance 100 kΩ drives a 50 Ω load through this follower. Find the actual voltage delivered to the load. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a):** The voltage follower is a special case of the non-inverting amplifier. The closed-loop gain is: $$ A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} $$ With R_f = 0 (direct wire) and R_1 = ∞ (open): $$ A_v = 1 + \frac{0}{\infty} = 1 $$ For a non-inverting configuration, the input impedance with feedback is: $$ Z_{in} = Z_{in\_OL} \cdot (1 + A_{OL}\beta) $$ Since β = 1 for a voltage follower: $$ Z_{in} = Z_{in\_OL} \cdot (1 + A_{OL}) $$ The output impedance with feedback is: $$ Z_{out} = \frac{Z_{out\_OL}}{1 + A_{OL}\beta} = \frac{Z_{out\_OL}}{1 + A_{OL}} $$ Thus, Z_in is multiplied by (1 + A_OL) and Z_out is divided by (1 + A_OL). **(7 marks)** **Part (b):** **Step 1 — Input Impedance:** $$ Z_{in} = 2 \times 10^6 \cdot (1 + 2 \times 10^5) = 2 \times 10^6 \cdot 200001 $$ $$ Z_{in} \approx 4.0 \times 10^{11} \ \Omega = 400 \ \text{G}\Omega $$ **Step 2 — Output Impedance:** $$ Z_{out} = \frac{75}{1 + 2 \times 10^5} = \frac{75}{200001} $$ $$ Z_{out} \approx 3.75 \times 10^{-4} \ \Omega \approx 0.000375 \ \Omega $$ **Step 3 — Voltage at the load:** Since Z_in ≫ 100 kΩ, the voltage at the non-inverting input is essentially V_in = 1 V (negligible drop across source resistance). The output voltage of the follower is V_out = 1 V. Voltage at load (after voltage divider between Z_out and R_L = 50 Ω): $$ V_{load} = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_L}{R_L + Z_{out}} = 1 \cdot \frac{50}{50 + 0.000375} $$ $$ V_{load} \approx 0.9999925 \ \text{V} \approx 1 \ \text{V} $$ **The voltage delivered to the load is approximately 1 V**, with negligible loss. **(7 marks)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a voltage follower. Discuss its ideal input and output impedance characteristics. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain why a voltage follower has a bandwidth equal to the Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP) of the op-amp. A voltage follower is built using an op-amp whose GBP is 1 MHz. If the same op-amp were used as an inverting amplifier with a gain of −10, what would be the new closed-loop bandwidth? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a):** In a voltage follower, the input V_in is applied to the non-inverting (+) terminal, and the output V_out is connected directly to the inverting (−) terminal. This provides 100% negative feedback. The op-amp, with its high open-loop gain, forces V⁻ = V_out to track V⁺ = V_in. Hence, V_out = V_in, giving unity voltage gain. **Ideal characteristics:** - **Input Impedance Z_in = ∞:** The op-amp draws no current from the source, so the source is never loaded. - **Output Impedance Z_out = 0:** The output acts as an ideal voltage source, delivering V_in to any load without voltage drop. These two properties make it an **ideal buffer** for impedance isolation. **(7 marks)** **Part (b):** The Gain-Bandwidth Product of an op-amp is a constant: $$ GBP = A_{OL} \cdot f_{OL} = A_{CL} \cdot BW_{CL} $$ For a voltage follower, A_CL = 1: $$ BW_{CL} = \frac{GBP}{1} = GBP $$ So the closed-loop bandwidth equals the full GBP of the op-amp. This is the **maximum bandwidth** achievable with that op-amp in any closed-loop configuration. **Numerical Calculation:** Given GBP = 1 MHz. For an inverting amplifier with gain magnitude |A_v| = 10: $$ BW_{CL} = \frac{GBP}{\vert A_v \vert} = \frac{1 \ \text{MHz}}{10} = 100 \ \text{kHz} $$ **The closed-loop bandwidth drops to 100 kHz** when the same op-amp is configured for a gain of 10. **(7 marks)** --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cause Mark Deductions in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Confusing Voltage Follower with Voltage Comparator:** A voltage follower uses **negative feedback** (linear operation). A comparator uses **no feedback** (saturated operation). Mixing these up costs full marks. > 2. **Forgetting that β = 1:** Many students leave the feedback factor generic (β = R_1/(R_1 + R_f)) and fail to substitute β = 1 for the voltage follower. This makes subsequent Z_in and Z_out calculations incorrect. > 3. **Sign error on the input terminal:** The input MUST be applied to the **non-inverting (+)** terminal. Applying it to the inverting terminal makes it a different (inverting) configuration. > 4. **Ignoring the bandwidth implication:** Examiners often include a "bonus" sub-question on why a voltage follower achieves maximum bandwidth. Students who don't mention A_v = 1 → BW = GBP lose 2 marks. > 5. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers (Ω vs. kΩ vs. MΩ). Always write the unit explicitly. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Voltage Follower = Op-amp with 100% negative feedback** (output tied directly to inverting input). - **Closed-loop gain A_v = 1** (unity, dimensionless). - **Feedback factor β = 1.** - **Ideal Z_in = ∞** (multiplied by 1 + A_OL due to feedback). - **Ideal Z_out = 0** (divided by 1 + A_OL due to feedback). - **V_out = V_in** at all times (output faithfully follows input). - **Maximum bandwidth** = Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP) of the op-amp. - **Special case** of non-inverting amplifier with R_f = 0 and R_1 = ∞. - **Primary use:** Impedance buffering / isolation between cascaded stages. - **Not used for voltage amplification** — use a non-inverting or inverting amplifier for that. - **Stability note:** With 100% feedback, the loop gain is at its maximum, making stability analysis critical in practical designs. - **Must operate in linear region** — keep input within the op-amp's common-mode and supply-voltage limits. - **No phase inversion** between input and output (unlike the inverting amplifier). - **Common KTU keywords:** "unity gain buffer", "source follower", "impedance transformer", "isolation amplifier".

topics.title: Summing Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT402 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Summing Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Summing Amplifier** (also called an *Adder* or *Summing Inverter*) is a linear operational amplifier (op-amp) configuration based on the classical **inverting amplifier topology**, in which multiple input voltage signals are applied to a common virtual ground node through individual input resistors. The circuit produces a single output voltage that is the **weighted algebraic sum** (with a sign inversion) of all the input signals, scaled by the ratio of the feedback resistor to each respective input resistor.

Mathematically, for an inverting summer with *n* inputs:

$$V_{out} = - \left( \frac{R_f}{R_1} V_1 + \frac{R_f}{R_2} V_2 + \cdots + \frac{R_f}{R_n} V_n \right)$$

The summing amplifier is constructed around an op-amp with **negative feedback** (closed-loop operation) and operates entirely within the **linear region**, relying on two golden rules of ideal op-amp analysis:
- **Virtual Short:** $V^+ = V^-$
- **Virtual Open:** Input bias currents $I_{B^+} = I_{B^-} = 0$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The summing amplifier is a **high-weightage** topic in Module 2 (Active Building Blocks and Applications). It frequently appears as a 7-mark sub-question or as the core of a 14-mark Part B question, often combined with the difference amplifier or D/A converter.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water tank with multiple inlet pipes** and a single outlet. Each inlet pipe pours water at a different rate. The tank has a smart **float valve** (the feedback mechanism) that ensures the water level (output) is precisely the *negative* weighted sum of all inflows. If you open pipe-1 more (raise $V_1$), the float drops the outlet (output decreases). The *weights* are determined by the **pipe diameters** (resistor values).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "inverting"?** The minus sign in the equation comes from the fact that the inverting input terminal of the op-amp is the active summing node. Positive inputs at this node *reduce* the output, hence the inversion.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle (The "Why" and "How")

The summing amplifier works because of the **virtual ground** (or virtual earth) at the inverting node:

1. **The non-inverting input ($V^+$) is grounded**, so $V^+ = 0\,\text{V}$.
2. By the **virtual short** principle, the inverting input $V^-$ is also held at $0\,\text{V}$ (even though it is not physically connected to ground). This is the *virtual ground*.
3. Because the input bias current of an ideal op-amp is zero, **no current flows into the $V^-$ terminal**.
4. By **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** at the virtual ground node, the sum of all input currents must equal the feedback current flowing through $R_f$.
5. The op-amp adjusts $V_{out}$ until this KCL condition is satisfied, producing the summed output.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Configuration | Output Voltage Equation | Key Condition | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inverting Summer (n inputs)** | $V_{out} = -\sum_{i=1}^{n}\dfrac{R_f}{R_i}V_i$ | All inputs at $V^-$ node | Audio mixer, DAC, adder |
| **Inverting Summer (equal gains)** | $V_{out} = -\dfrac{R_f}{R}(V_1 + V_2 + \cdots + V_n)$ | $R_1 = R_2 = \cdots = R_n = R$ | Pure summing, no weighting |
| **Scaler / Weighted Summer** | $V_{out} = -\left(a_1 V_1 + a_2 V_2 + \cdots\right)$ | Gains $a_i = R_f / R_i$ arbitrary | Custom signal combination |
| **Average Amplifier** | $V_{out} = -\dfrac{V_1 + V_2 + \cdots + V_n}{n}$ | $R_f = R/n$ and $R_i = R$ for all $i$ | Computing arithmetic mean |
| **Non-Inverting Summer** | $V_{out} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}V_i$ (if symmetric) | Specialized resistor network | DC level shifting, averaging without inversion |
| **Difference Amplifier (1-op-amp)** | $V_{out} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}(V_2 - V_1)$ | $R_1 = R_3$, $R_2 = R_4$ | Subtraction (related circuit) |
| **D/A Converter (Binary-Weighted)** | $V_{out} = -V_{ref}\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \dfrac{b_i \cdot 2^i}{2^{n-1}}$ | $R_i = 2^{i} R$ | Digital-to-analog conversion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbols used in the table above:** $R_f$ = feedback resistor, $R_i$ = $i$-th input resistor, $V_i$ = $i$-th input voltage, $n$ = number of inputs, $b_i$ = $i$-th binary bit (0 or 1).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Summing amplifiers are fundamental building blocks in numerous practical systems:
- **Digital-to-Analog Converters (DAC):** Binary-weighted or R-2R ladder networks use the summing principle to convert digital codes to analog voltages.
- **Audio Mixers:** Multiple microphone/instrument signals are summed in a recording console.
- **Beamforming in Phased-Array Antennas:** Weighted summation of multiple antenna signals for directional reception.
- **Active Filters:** Cascaded filter sections (Sallen-Key, Multiple Feedback) depend on proper input summing at the active node.
- **Analog Computers:** Summation is a primary mathematical operation for solving differential equations.
- **Servo Control Systems:** Error signals from multiple sensors are combined with individual weights.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: General Inverting Summer (n Inputs)

Consider the inverting summing amplifier with $n$ input branches, each with input resistor $R_i$ and input voltage $V_i$, all feeding into the inverting node. The feedback resistor is $R_f$, and the non-inverting terminal is grounded.

**Step 1 — Identify the virtual ground condition:**
Because the non-inverting terminal is grounded, $V^+ = 0\,\text{V}$. Using the virtual short:

$$V^- = V^+ = 0\,\text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at the inverting node ($V^-$):**
Since no current enters the op-amp, the algebraic sum of currents flowing *into* the node must equal zero:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} I_{R_i} + I_{R_f} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Express each current using Ohm's Law:**
For the $i$-th input branch, the voltage across $R_i$ is $V_i - V^- = V_i - 0 = V_i$, so:

$$I_{R_i} = \frac{V_i - V^-}{R_i} = \frac{V_i}{R_i}$$

For the feedback branch, the voltage across $R_f$ is $V^- - V_{out} = 0 - V_{out}$, so:

$$I_{R_f} = \frac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_f} = \frac{-V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into KCL and solve:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{V_i}{R_i} + \frac{-V_{out}}{R_f} = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{V_{out}}{R_f} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{V_i}{R_i}$$

Multiplying both sides by $R_f$:

$$\boxed{\,V_{out} = -R_f \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{V_i}{R_i} = -\left( \frac{R_f}{R_1} V_1 + \frac{R_f}{R_2} V_2 + \cdots + \frac{R_f}{R_n} V_n \right)\,}$$

The **negative sign** appears because the chosen current direction (flowing into the node) is opposite to the conventional direction of $I_{R_f}$ in the feedback branch.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Two-Input Inverting Summer (Special Case)

The most common configuration used in KTU examinations. Set $n = 2$ in the general formula:

$$V_{out} = -\left( \frac{R_f}{R_1} V_1 + \frac{R_f}{R_2} V_2 \right)$$

**If $R_1 = R_2 = R_f = R$ (unity gains):**

$$V_{out} = -(V_1 + V_2)$$

This is the **pure inverting adder** used in audio mixers and the foundational building block for DACs.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Average Amplifier (Three Inputs)

For an average amplifier, we want $V_{out} = -\dfrac{V_1 + V_2 + V_3}{3}$. Comparing with the general formula:

$$V_{out} = -\left( \frac{R_f}{R_1} V_1 + \frac{R_f}{R_2} V_2 + \frac{R_f}{R_3} V_3 \right) = -\frac{1}{3}(V_1 + V_2 + V_3)$$

Therefore each coefficient must equal $\dfrac{1}{3}$, i.e., $\dfrac{R_f}{R_i} = \dfrac{1}{3}$, which gives:

$$R_i = 3 R_f \quad \text{for all } i = 1, 2, 3$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Binary-Weighted DAC (4-Bit Example)

In a binary-weighted DAC, the input resistors are chosen as powers of two, so that the output voltage directly represents the digital input word.

For a 4-bit DAC with inputs $b_0, b_1, b_2, b_3$ (LSB to MSB) and reference $V_{ref}$:

$$R_0 = R, \quad R_1 = \frac{R}{2}, \quad R_2 = \frac{R}{4}, \quad R_3 = \frac{R}{8}$$

Using the inverting summer formula (with $V_i = b_i V_{ref}$):

$$V_{out} = -R_f \sum_{i=0}^{3} \frac{b_i V_{ref}}{R/2^i} = -\frac{R_f V_{ref}}{R} \sum_{i=0}^{3} b_i 2^i$$

If we set $R_f = R$:

$$V_{out} = -V_{ref} \left( b_0 \cdot 2^0 + b_1 \cdot 2^1 + b_2 \cdot 2^2 + b_3 \cdot 2^3 \right)$$

The output is the **binary-weighted sum** of the input bits, producing an analog voltage proportional to the digital code.

## 3.5 Key Design Constraints

- **Input Bias Current Compensation:** For BJT-input op-amps, a resistor $R_c = R_1 \vert\vert R_2 \vert\vert R_f$ is often added between the non-inverting terminal and ground to minimize offset due to input bias currents.
- **Slew Rate Limitation:** The output cannot change faster than the op-amp's slew rate. For high-frequency summing, choose a wideband op-amp (e.g., LM318, TL072).
- **Resistor Tolerance:** Mismatched resistors produce gain errors. Use **0.1% metal-film resistors** or laser-trimmed networks for precision summing.
- **Output Saturation:** The output must stay within the op-amp's linear range: $-V_{sat} \le V_{out} \le +V_{sat}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a 3-Input Inverting Summer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Input V1 with Resistor R1] --> nodeSum[Virtual Ground Node Vminus]
    A2[Input V2 with Resistor R2] --> nodeSum
    A3[Input V3 with Resistor R3] --> nodeSum
    nodeSum -->|Feedback Path via Rf| Aout[Output Node Vout]
    opamp[Op-Amp with Negative Feedback] --> Aout
    gnd[Ground Reference 0V] --> opamp
    Aout -.->|Negative Feedback Loop| nodeSum
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Binary-Weighted DAC

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ref[Vref Reference Source] --> switch0[Switch b0]
    ref --> switch1[Switch b1]
    ref --> switch2[Switch b2]
    ref --> switch3[Switch b3]
    switch0 --> R0[Resistor R]
    switch1 --> R1[Resistor R/2]
    switch2 --> R2[Resistor R/4]
    switch3 --> R3[Resistor R/8]
    R0 --> vnode[Virtual Ground Summing Node]
    R1 --> vnode
    R2 --> vnode
    R3 --> vnode
    vnode -->|Feedback Rf| opampIC[Op-Amp]
    opampIC --> vDAC[Analog Output Vout]
```

## 4.3 Signal-Flow Block Diagram (Functional)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS
        V1[Signal V1] --> X1[Gain Stage minus Rf by R1]
        V2[Signal V2] --> X2[Gain Stage minus Rf by R2]
        V3[Signal V3] --> X3[Gain Stage minus Rf by R3]
    end
    X1 --> SUM[Algebraic Summation Junction]
    X2 --> SUM
    X3 --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT[Combined Output Vout]
```

## 4.4 Physical Circuit Schematic (Text Representation)

```
                  R1           Rf
   V1 o----/\/\/\-------+------/\/\/\-------+
                        |                    |
                  R2    |              +-----+------+
   V2 o----/\/\/\-------+              |  Op-Amp   |----o Vout
                        +-----|-\      |           |
                            |  >------|-/          |
                  R3    +---|+/      |             |
   V3 o----/\/\/\-------+   |        +-------------+
                        |   |  GND
                       GND  |
                            |
                           GND
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** All input resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3$ meet at a single node — the **inverting input** of the op-amp, which sits at **virtual ground** ($0\,\text{V}$) due to negative feedback. The non-inverting input is physically tied to the actual ground. The feedback resistor $R_f$ connects the output back to the inverting node.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Define a summing amplifier. Derive the expression for the output voltage of a three-input inverting summing amplifier.**

**Model Answer:**

A summing amplifier is an op-amp circuit that produces an output voltage proportional to the weighted algebraic sum of two or more input voltages. The inputs are applied to the inverting terminal through individual resistors, while the non-inverting terminal is grounded.

**Derivation:**
Let the three inputs be $V_1, V_2, V_3$ with input resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3$, and feedback resistor $R_f$. Because the non-inverting terminal is grounded, the inverting terminal is at **virtual ground** ($V^- = 0$).

Applying **KCL** at the inverting node:

$$\frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_3}{R_3} + \frac{V_{out}}{R_f} = 0$$

Solving for $V_{out}$:

$$V_{out} = -\left(\frac{R_f}{R_1} V_1 + \frac{R_f}{R_2} V_2 + \frac{R_f}{R_3} V_3\right)$$

The negative sign indicates **phase inversion**.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Explain the significance of the virtual ground concept in the operation of an inverting summing amplifier.**

**Model Answer:**

The **virtual ground** is the most critical concept that enables the summing amplifier to function. It refers to the fact that the inverting input terminal of the op-amp is held at **$0\,\text{V}$** (same potential as ground) even though it is not physically connected to ground. This happens because of the negative feedback action and the virtual short principle ($V^+ = V^-$).

Its significance in a summing amplifier is as follows:
1. **Independent Input Channels:** Because $V^-$ is fixed at $0\,\text{V}$, each input voltage sees an independent load to ground through its own resistor. This means **the inputs do not load each other**, allowing true algebraic summation.
2. **KCL Application Becomes Trivial:** With $V^- = 0$, the current through each resistor is simply $V_i / R_i$, simplifying analysis.
3. **No Cross-Talk:** Signals at one input do not affect the operation of other input channels.
4. **Linear Operation:** It guarantees that the circuit stays in the **linear region** by preventing the inverting node from drifting away from $V^+$.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, derive the expression for the output voltage of an inverting summing amplifier with **three inputs**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design a summing amplifier to produce the output $V_{out} = -(2V_1 + 5V_2 + 10V_3)$. Choose $R_f = 100\,\text{k}\Omega$ and find the required input resistor values. Comment on the practicality of the design. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A (a):**

**Circuit Description:** The three inputs $V_1, V_2, V_3$ are applied to the inverting terminal via resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3$. The feedback resistor $R_f$ is connected between the output and the inverting input. The non-inverting input is grounded.

**Derivation:**

Because $V^+ = 0$ and the op-amp is ideal, $V^- = 0$ (virtual ground) and $I_{in} = 0$.

Applying KCL at the inverting node, the sum of currents entering the node must equal zero:

$$I_1 + I_2 + I_3 + I_f = 0$$

Substituting Ohm's law expressions:

$$\frac{V_1 - V^-}{R_1} + \frac{V_2 - V^-}{R_2} + \frac{V_3 - V^-}{R_3} + \frac{V_{out} - V^-}{R_f} = 0$$

Since $V^- = 0$:

$$\frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_3}{R_3} + \frac{V_{out}}{R_f} = 0$$

Solving for $V_{out}$:

$$\boxed{\,V_{out} = -\left(\frac{R_f}{R_1} V_1 + \frac{R_f}{R_2} V_2 + \frac{R_f}{R_3} V_3\right)\,}$$

---

### **Model Solution for Question A (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify the required gains:**
Comparing with the standard form $V_{out} = -\left(\dfrac{R_f}{R_1} V_1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_2} V_2 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_3} V_3\right)$:

$$\frac{R_f}{R_1} = 2, \quad \frac{R_f}{R_2} = 5, \quad \frac{R_f}{R_3} = 10$$

**Step 2 — Solve for input resistors** with $R_f = 100\,\text{k}\Omega$:

$$R_1 = \frac{R_f}{2} = \frac{100\,\text{k}\Omega}{2} = 50\,\text{k}\Omega$$

$$R_2 = \frac{R_f}{5} = \frac{100\,\text{k}\Omega}{5} = 20\,\text{k}\Omega$$

$$R_3 = \frac{R_f}{10} = \frac{100\,\text{k}\Omega}{10} = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Practicality Comments:**
- **Large gain on $V_3$ ($10\times$):** This amplifies any noise present in $V_3$ by a factor of 10. Such a high gain can also push the op-amp into saturation easily if $V_3$ is large.
- **Bandwidth consideration:** The closed-loop bandwidth shrinks as gain increases. The gain-bandwidth product is constant, so a $10\times$ gain section has $1/10$th the bandwidth of a unity-gain op-amp.
- **Output saturation:** For example, if $V_1 = 1\,\text{V}$, $V_2 = 1\,\text{V}$, $V_3 = 1\,\text{V}$, then $V_{out} = -(2 + 5 + 10) = -17\,\text{V}$, which exceeds the typical $\pm 15\,\text{V}$ supply. The op-amp will saturate, and the output will clip near $-V_{sat}$.
- **Resistor spread:** The ratio between the largest and smallest input resistor is $50/10 = 5$, which is reasonable. For very high gain ratios, the smallest resistor can become comparable to $R_f$, leading to loading issues.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
