> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign:** Many students drop the inversion and write $V_{out} = +(R_f/R_1)V_1 + \ldots$ — instant loss of 1 mark.
> 2. **Not stating virtual ground explicitly:** Examiners in KTU expect a clear mention of $V^- = V^+ = 0$ before applying KCL. Skipping this is a half-mark deduction.
> 3. **Mixing up $R_f$ and input resistor positions:** In the formula $R_f$ is the feedback resistor. Some students incorrectly place $R_f$ in the input position and vice versa.
> 4. **Not designing for the practical output swing:** Design questions require a final sentence about saturation and supply rails.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Internal Choice Alternative]`

**(a)** Explain how an inverting summing amplifier can be used as a **3-bit binary-weighted Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)**. Derive the output expression. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a 3-bit DAC using $R_f = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $R = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ as the largest resistor, calculate the output voltage for the digital input word $b_2 b_1 b_0 = 101$. Take $V_{ref} = 5\,\text{V}$. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B (a):**

**Concept:** A binary-weighted DAC uses the inverting summing amplifier topology. Each bit of the digital input controls a switch that either connects a reference voltage $V_{ref}$ to an input resistor or grounds the resistor. The input resistors are binary-weighted, i.e., they follow a $1{:}2{:}4{:}8\ldots$ ratio.

**Circuit:** For a 3-bit DAC, the input resistors are $R$, $R/2$, and $R/4$ corresponding to the LSB, middle bit, and MSB respectively. Each resistor is connected to a switch that selects either $V_{ref}$ (when bit = 1) or $0\,\text{V}$ (when bit = 0).

**Derivation:**
Let the input bits be $b_0$ (LSB), $b_1$, and $b_2$ (MSB). The corresponding input voltages are $V_i = b_i \cdot V_{ref}$. Using the inverting summer formula:

$$V_{out} = -\left(\frac{R_f}{R} \cdot b_0 V_{ref} + \frac{R_f}{R/2} \cdot b_1 V_{ref} + \frac{R_f}{R/4} \cdot b_2 V_{ref}\right)$$

Simplifying each term:

$$V_{out} = -\frac{R_f V_{ref}}{R}\left(b_0 \cdot 2^0 + b_1 \cdot 2^1 + b_2 \cdot 2^2\right)$$

If we set $R_f = R$:

$$\boxed{\,V_{out} = -V_{ref}\left(b_0 + 2 b_1 + 4 b_2\right)\,}$$

The output voltage is a **negative analog equivalent** of the digital input code, with the sign inverted due to the inverting topology.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify the input resistors:**
With $R = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ as the **largest** resistor (used for LSB):

$$R_0 = R = 10\,\text{k}\Omega \quad \text{(LSB, } b_0\text{)}$$

$$R_1 = \frac{R}{2} = 5\,\text{k}\Omega \quad \text{(bit } b_1\text{)}$$

$$R_2 = \frac{R}{4} = 2.5\,\text{k}\Omega \quad \text{(MSB, } b_2\text{)}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the input word:**
For $b_2 b_1 b_0 = 101$ (binary): $b_0 = 1$, $b_1 = 0$, $b_2 = 1$.

**Step 3 — Calculate the output:**

$$V_{out} = -V_{ref}\left(b_0 + 2 b_1 + 4 b_2\right) = -5 \cdot (1 + 2 \cdot 0 + 4 \cdot 1)$$

$$V_{out} = -5 \cdot (1 + 0 + 4) = -5 \cdot 5 = -25\,\text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Practicality check:**
The output magnitude of $25\,\text{V}$ exceeds the typical $\pm 15\,\text{V}$ supply rails. This means the op-amp will **saturate** at approximately $-V_{sat} \approx -13$ to $-14\,\text{V}$ (depending on the op-amp). The DAC therefore operates in saturation for the input code 101, and the actual output will be clipped. To operate in the linear region, the supply rails must be increased, or a lower $V_{ref}$ must be used.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — DAC-Specific Mistakes:**
> 1. **Wrong resistor-to-bit assignment:** The smallest resistor corresponds to the MSB (most significant bit), not the LSB. Mixing this up is a common error.
> 2. **Ignoring $R_f$ ratio:** If $R_f \ne R$, the formula gains an extra factor of $R_f / R$ which must be retained.
> 3. **Not specifying the reference:** Examiners expect a clear $V_{ref}$ value, not just a generic $V_{in}$.
> 4. **Forgetting output inversion:** The DAC output is *inverted*; mention this explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Summing Amplifier:**

- **Core Formula (MUST MEMORIZE):** $V_{out} = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{R_f}{R_i} V_i$
- **Golden Rules of Ideal Op-Amp:**
   - $V^+ = V^-$ (virtual short)
   - $I_{B^+} = I_{B^-} = 0$ (virtual open)
- **Virtual Ground:** The inverting node of a negative-feedback op-amp with $V^+$ grounded sits at $0\,\text{V}$ even without a physical ground connection.
- **KCL is the Key Tool:** Always start analysis by writing KCL at the inverting node.
- **Inversion is Always There:** The output of an inverting summer is always the **negative** weighted sum.
- **Two Special Cases:**
   - *Average Amplifier:* $R_f = R/n$, all $R_i = R$ → $V_{out} = -(V_1 + V_2 + \ldots + V_n)/n$.
   - *Unity Adder:* All resistors equal → $V_{out} = -(V_1 + V_2 + \ldots + V_n)$.
- **DAC Connection:** Binary-weighted DAC uses $R_i \propto 1/2^i$ (i.e., smallest resistor for MSB).
- **Practical Design Notes:**
   - Choose $R_f$ first (typically **$10\,\text{k}\Omega$** to **$100\,\text{k}\Omega$**).
   - Then compute $R_i = R_f / a_i$ for desired gain $a_i$.
   - Always check the output against supply rails: $-V_{sat} \le V_{out} \le +V_{sat}$.
   - For high-precision summing, use $0.1\%$ metal-film resistors or precision op-amps (e.g., OP-07, OP-27).
- **Common KTU Question Patterns:**
   - 3-mark: Definition + short derivation of a 2-input or 3-input summer.
   - 7-mark: Derivation with circuit diagram for an *n*-input summer.
   - 7-mark: Design problem (choose resistor values for a given output equation).
   - 7-mark: DAC application (binary-weighted summing network).
- **Trap Questions to Watch For:**
   - "What is the polarity of the output?" → Always **negative** (inverting summer).
   - "What happens if $R_1$ is open?" → That input contributes zero; output = sum of remaining inputs.
   - "What is the input impedance of the $i$-th input?" → $R_i$ (each input sees its own resistor to virtual ground).
   - "Can the summer add more than two voltages?" → **Yes**, theoretically unlimited inputs; practically limited by op-amp bias current and resistor tolerances.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Difference Amplifiers with One Op Amp and 3 Op Amps - Instrumentation Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT402 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Difference Amplifiers with One Op Amp and 3 Op Amps - Instrumentation Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Difference Amplifier — Formal Definition

A **Difference Amplifier** is an electronic circuit that amplifies the *algebraic difference* between two input voltage signals while rejecting (ideally) any common signal present at both inputs. Mathematically, for two inputs $V_1$ and $V_2$:

$$V_{out} = A_d (V_2 - V_1) + A_c \left(\frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}\right)$$

where:
- $A_d$ = **Differential Gain** (the gain applied to the difference)
- $A_c$ = **Common-Mode Gain** (ideally zero)
- $\frac{V_1+V_2}{2}$ = the common-mode (average) component

> [!NOTE]
> **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** is the key figure of merit of a difference amplifier:
> $$\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10} \left(\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right) \text{ dB}$$
> A higher CMRR means the circuit is more selective — it amplifies the difference but ignores noise shared by both signals.

---

## 1.2 The Two Architectures We Will Study

| # | Architecture | Core Purpose | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **One Op-Amp Difference Amplifier** | Simple subtraction of two signals | Low input impedance; gain change requires altering **two** resistors |
| **2** | **Three Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier (IA)** | Precision difference amplification with **high input impedance** and **single-resistor gain control** | More components; needs matched resistors for high CMRR |

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Stethoscope and the Noisy Room"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: A Doctor Using a Stethoscope in a Crowded Ward**
> 
> Imagine two patients **Patient A** and **Patient B** connected to a heart monitor. The signals (ECG) are weak — about **1 mV** — but the room is full of electrical noise from fluorescent lights, mobile phones, and other machines producing a common **50 Hz hum** that appears on *both* patients equally.
> 
> - A **One Op-Amp Difference Amplifier** is like a basic stethoscope — it can pick up the difference between the two chest sounds, **but the doctor must press the chest-pieces firmly** (high input impedance is missing), and the hum still bleeds in.
> 
> - A **Three Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier** is like a **high-end electronic stethoscope with active noise-cancelling headphones** — it has very high input impedance (does not load the patient sensors), amplifies the *tiny* heart signal thousands of times, and completely cancels the **50 Hz room hum** because the noise is common to both inputs.
> 
> The single resistor that controls the volume of the stethoscope? That is the **gain-setting resistor $R_G$** — change one resistor, and the entire amplification changes. The other resistors stay matched and perfect for noise rejection.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Applications (KTU 2024 High-Yield)**
> 
> 1. **Biomedical Instrumentation** — ECG, EEG, EMG signal conditioning (signals are typically **0.1 mV – 5 mV**)
> 2. **Industrial Process Control** — reading strain-gauge bridge outputs in pressure/weight sensors
> 3. **Audio Engineering** — balanced audio lines (XLR cables) where hum rejection is critical
> 4. **Data Acquisition Systems** — ADC front-ends that must reject ground-loop noise
> 5. **Motor Control** — current-sensing across shunt resistors in H-bridge drivers

---

## 1.5 Pre-Requisite Concepts

| Pre-Requisite | Reason Needed |
|---|---|
| Ideal Op-Amp assumptions ($I_+ = I_- = 0$, $V_+ = V_-$) | Foundation of every node equation |
| Superposition Theorem | Used in deriving 1 Op-Amp difference amplifier output |
| Inverting & Non-Inverting Amplifier | Sub-blocks inside the 3 Op-Amp IA |
| Voltage Divider Rule | Used in node analysis |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 1 Op-Amp Difference Amplifier — Architecture

The classic 1 Op-Amp difference amplifier uses **four precision resistors** connected to a single op-amp. Two inputs ($V_1$ applied through $R_1$ to the inverting node, and $V_2$ applied through $R_3$ to the non-inverting node) are combined to produce a single output.

**Node Connections:**
- Inverting input ($V^-$): connected to $V_1$ via $R_1$ and to output via feedback $R_2$
- Non-inverting input ($V^+$): connected to $V_2$ via $R_3$ and to ground via $R_4$

**Key Design Goal — Matching Condition:**
For the difference amplifier to reject common-mode signals, the resistor ratios must satisfy:
$$\frac{R_2}{R_1} = \frac{R_4}{R_3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Matching Condition** is what makes the circuit reject the common-mode signal. If $R_1 \neq R_3$ or $R_2 \neq R_4$, the CMRR collapses rapidly. A mismatch of just **1%** between resistor pairs can reduce CMRR from **infinity** to roughly **40 dB** (a factor of 100 loss in noise rejection).

---

## 2.2 The 1 Op-Amp Difference Amplifier — Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Apply $V_1$ alone** (set $V_2 = 0$): the circuit reduces to a standard inverting amplifier with gain $-\frac{R_2}{R_1}$.
2. **Apply $V_2$ alone** (set $V_1 = 0$): the voltage at the non-inverting node becomes a divider output $\frac{R_4}{R_3+R_4} V_2$, which is then amplified by a non-inverting gain of $1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}$.
3. **Superpose** the two contributions.
4. **Enforce the matching condition** $\frac{R_2}{R_1} = \frac{R_4}{R_3}$ to eliminate the common-mode term.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is input impedance low?** The input $V_1$ sees $R_1$ directly to the inverting node, and the inverting node is a **virtual ground**. So the input impedance at $V_1$ is simply $R_1$ (typically 10–100 kΩ). This **loads down** the source — unacceptable for high-impedance sensors like biomedical electrodes.

---

## 2.3 The 3 Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier — Architecture

The IA overcomes the input impedance problem by **buffering** both inputs with non-inverting amplifiers first. It consists of:

- **Stage 1 — Input Buffers with Variable Gain**: Two op-amps ($A_1$, $A_2$) configured as non-inverting amplifiers. A single resistor $R_G$ (gain-setting) is placed between their inverting inputs. Both buffers share the same $R_G$, so changing it changes the gain of the **first stage only**.
- **Stage 2 — Unity-Gain Difference Amplifier**: A single op-amp ($A_3$) configured exactly as the 1 Op-Amp difference amplifier from Section 2.1, with $\frac{R_2}{R_1} = \frac{R_4}{R_3} = 1$ (typically all four resistors are equal — say $R = 10$ kΩ).

**Node Connections of Stage 1:**
- $A_1$ non-inverting input ← $V_1$
- $A_2$ non-inverting input ← $V_2$
- $A_1$ inverting input ← one end of $R_G$
- $A_2$ inverting input ← other end of $R_G$
- Each op-amp output feeds back to its own inverting input through a feedback resistor $R_1$

**Key Design Insight:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is input impedance infinite (ideally)?** Both inputs go directly to the **non-inverting (+) terminals** of op-amps $A_1$ and $A_2$. The input impedance of an op-amp's non-inverting terminal is on the order of **10¹² Ω**. Therefore, the IA does not load the source at all — perfect for high-impedance sensors.

---

## 2.4 Working Principle of the 3 Op-Amp IA (Step-by-Step)

1. **Currents through $R_G$**: Since both inverting inputs are at the same voltage (virtual short), the voltage across $R_G$ is $V_2 - V_1$. The current through $R_G$ is $I = \frac{V_2 - V_1}{R_G}$.
2. **Same current through feedback resistors**: This same current must also flow through the two feedback resistors $R_1$ (one on each side), because no current enters the op-amp inputs.
3. **Differential output of Stage 1**: The output of $A_1$ is lower than $V_1$ by $I \cdot R_1$, and the output of $A_2$ is higher than $V_2$ by $I \cdot R_1$. So the differential output is $(V_2 - V_1) \cdot \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)$.
4. **Stage 2 difference amplifier**: This differential signal is converted to a single-ended output with unity differential gain (when all four resistors of $A_3$ are equal).

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter / Configuration | Formula | Condition / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 Op-Amp Diff-Amp Output (general) | $V_{out} = \frac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot \frac{(R_1+R_3)V_2 - (R_2+R_4)V_1}{R_3+R_4}$ | No matching assumption |
| 2 | 1 Op-Amp Diff-Amp Output (matched) | $V_{out} = \frac{R_2}{R_1}(V_2 - V_1)$ | Requires $\frac{R_2}{R_1} = \frac{R_4}{R_3}$ |
| 3 | Differential Gain of 1 Op-Amp | $A_d = \frac{R_2}{R_1}$ | When matched |
| 4 | Common-Mode Gain of 1 Op-Amp | $A_c = \frac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot \frac{R_4 R_1 - R_2 R_3}{R_1(R_3+R_4)}$ | Zero when matched |
| 5 | 3 Op-Amp IA — Stage 1 Gain | $G_1 = 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}$ | Adjustable via $R_G$ |
| 6 | 3 Op-Amp IA — Stage 2 Gain | $G_2 = \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$ (typically = 1) | Fixed difference amplifier |
| 7 | 3 Op-Amp IA — Total Gain | $A_d = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right) \cdot \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$ | $\frac{R_f}{R_{in}}=1$ usually |
| 8 | 3 Op-Amp IA — Simplified Output | $V_{out} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)(V_2 - V_1)$ | When Stage 2 = unity |
| 9 | Common-Mode Input Range | $V_{CM}$ within op-amp supply rails minus headroom | IA must handle sensor bias |
| 10 | CMRR (dB) | $\text{CMRR} = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$ | Higher is better; 80–120 dB typical |
| 11 | Input Impedance of 1 Op-Amp Diff-Amp | $Z_{in} = R_1$ (at $V_1$) or $R_3 + R_4 \Vert$ something (at $V_2$) | **Low** — main weakness |
| 12 | Input Impedance of 3 Op-Amp IA | $Z_{in} \approx 10^{12} \, \Omega$ (at both inputs) | **Very high** — main strength |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of the IA

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Instrumentation Amplifier is one of the most commercially successful analog ICs in history. IC packages like the **INA128, AD620, AD8221, INA333** (Texas Instruments / Analog Devices) are dedicated 3 Op-Amp IAs on a single silicon die. The reason is that **resistor matching** in the difference stage of $A_3$ is done at the silicon level using laser-trimmed thin-film resistors — achieving **CMRR > 100 dB** at DC and **> 80 dB at 1 kHz**, which discrete designs struggle to match.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation #1: 1 Op-Amp Difference Amplifier Output

### 3.1.1 Circuit Setup
Consider the standard 1 Op-Amp difference amplifier:
- $V_1$ feeds the inverting input via resistor $R_1$
- Feedback resistor $R_2$ connects output to inverting input
- $V_2$ feeds the non-inverting input via resistor $R_3$
- Resistor $R_4$ connects the non-inverting input to ground

### 3.1.2 Applying Superposition — Contribution of $V_1$ (set $V_2 = 0$)

With $V_2 = 0$, the non-inverting node is grounded through $R_3$ in series with $R_4$ (since the source is shorted). The inverting node becomes a virtual ground ($V^- = 0$). The circuit reduces to a standard inverting amplifier:

$$V_{out1} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1} V_1$$

**Explanation:** In the inverting configuration, the gain is $-\frac{R_2}{R_1}$ because the current through $R_1$ is $\frac{V_1 - 0}{R_1}$, and this same current must flow through $R_2$ (no current enters the op-amp), producing $V_{out} = 0 - I \cdot R_2 = -\frac{R_2}{R_1}V_1$.

### 3.1.3 Applying Superposition — Contribution of $V_2$ (set $V_1 = 0$)

With $V_1 = 0$, the inverting node is connected to ground through $R_1$ and to the output through $R_2$. The non-inverting node sees a voltage divider formed by $R_3$ (to $V_2$) and $R_4$ (to ground):

$$V^+ = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}$$

This $V^+$ is amplified by a non-inverting amplifier with gain $\left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)$:

$$V_{out2} = \left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) \cdot V_2 \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}$$

**Explanation:** The non-inverting amplifier gain formula is derived from the fact that the inverting node follows $V^+$ (virtual short), and the current through $R_1$ must equal the current through $R_2$ (no current into op-amp).

### 3.1.4 Total Output by Superposition

$$V_{out} = V_{out1} + V_{out2}$$

$$V_{out} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1} V_1 + \left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \cdot V_2$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = \frac{R_4(R_1+R_2)}{R_1(R_3+R_4)} \cdot V_2 - \frac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot V_1}$$

### 3.1.5 Applying the Matching Condition

To get a clean difference output, we enforce:
$$\frac{R_2}{R_1} = \frac{R_4}{R_3} \equiv k$$

Substituting $R_2 = k R_1$ and $R_4 = k R_3$:

$$V_{out} = \frac{(k R_3)(R_1 + k R_1)}{R_1(R_3 + k R_3)} V_2 - k V_1 = \frac{k R_1 R_3 (1+k)}{R_1 R_3 (1+k)} V_2 - k V_1 = k V_2 - k V_1$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = \frac{R_2}{R_1}(V_2 - V_1)}$$

**Result:** Under matched conditions, the gain of the 1 Op-Amp difference amplifier is $\frac{R_2}{R_1}$ and it amplifies only the difference.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation #2: 3 Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier Output

### 3.2.1 Stage 1 Analysis

Let:
- Output of $A_1$ be $V_{o1}$
- Output of $A_2$ be $V_{o2}$

**Step 1 — Voltage at the inverting nodes:** By the virtual short principle:
$$V^-_{A_1} = V^+_{A_1} = V_1$$
$$V^-_{A_2} = V^+_{A_2} = V_2$$

**Step 2 — Current through $R_G$:** The voltage across $R_G$ is $V_2 - V_1$. By Ohm's law:

$$I_{RG} = \frac{V_2 - V_1}{R_G}$$

**Step 3 — Current through the feedback resistors:** Since op-amp inputs draw no current, the current flowing into the inverting node of $A_1$ from its feedback resistor must equal the current flowing out of that node through $R_G$. Therefore, the current through each feedback resistor (let's call it $R_1$, the same on both sides) is the same $I_{RG}$:

$$I_{R_1} = I_{RG} = \frac{V_2 - V_1}{R_G}$$

**Step 4 — Voltage drop across each feedback resistor:** Each feedback resistor $R_1$ has the same current $I_{R_1}$ flowing through it:

$$V_{drop,R_1} = I_{R_1} \cdot R_1 = \frac{R_1 (V_2 - V_1)}{R_G}$$

**Step 5 — Outputs of $A_1$ and $A_2$:** Since the output of each op-amp is its inverting-node voltage *plus* the drop across the feedback resistor (current flows from output to inverting node, so output is *higher*):

$$V_{o1} = V_1 - \frac{R_1 (V_2 - V_1)}{R_G}$$

$$V_{o2} = V_2 + \frac{R_1 (V_2 - V_1)}{R_G}$$

**Explanation of sign:** $A_1$'s output is *below* $V_1$ because the inverting input is *higher* than the output (current $I$ flows from $V_1$ to $V_{o1}$ through $R_1$ on the inverting side, but the current must also flow from $V_{o1}$ through $R_G$ to $V_2$ — so $V_{o1}$ is the lowest potential). Conversely, $V_{o2}$ is *above* $V_2$.

**Step 6 — Differential output of Stage 1:**

$$V_{o2} - V_{o1} = \left[V_2 + \frac{R_1 (V_2 - V_1)}{R_G}\right] - \left[V_1 - \frac{R_1 (V_2 - V_1)}{R_G}\right]$$

$$V_{o2} - V_{o1} = (V_2 - V_1) + \frac{2 R_1 (V_2 - V_1)}{R_G}$$

$$V_{o2} - V_{o1} = (V_2 - V_1) \left(1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_G}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\text{Stage 1 Differential Gain: } G_1 = 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}}$$

### 3.2.2 Stage 2 Analysis

Stage 2 is a standard 1 Op-Amp difference amplifier with all four resistors equal to $R$:
- $V_{o1}$ feeds inverting input via $R$
- Feedback resistor $R$ from $A_3$ output to inverting input
- $V_{o2}$ feeds non-inverting input via $R$
- Resistor $R$ from non-inverting input to ground

By the matched difference amplifier result from Section 3.1.5:

$$V_{out} = \frac{R}{R}(V_{o2} - V_{o1}) = V_{o2} - V_{o1}$$

### 3.2.3 Combined IA Output

$$\boxed{V_{out} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)(V_2 - V_1)}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Gain Variation with $R_G$ in the 3 Op-Amp IA

Assume $R_1 = 25 \text{ k}\Omega$ (typical for AD620 family). The following table shows the achievable gains and their use-cases:

| $R_G$ (Ω) | Total Differential Gain $A_d$ | $A_d$ (dB) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\infty$ (open) | $1$ | $0$ dB | Buffer / signal monitor |
| 49900 | $2$ | $6$ dB | Line-level audio |
| 12120 | $5.13$ | $14.2$ dB | General purpose |
| 4990 | $11$ | $20.8$ dB | Sensor pre-amp |
| 1100 | $46.5$ | $33.4$ dB | Strain-gauge bridge |
| 499 | $101$ | $40.1$ dB | ECG / EEG front-end |
| 100 | $501$ | $54$ dB | High-impedance transducer |
| 49.9 | $1003$ | $60$ dB | Photodiode amplification |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb for $R_G$ selection:** In commercial IA datasheets (e.g., AD620), the gain-setting formula is given as:
> $$R_G = \frac{49.4 \text{ k}\Omega}{G - 1}$$
> This is because $2R_1 = 49.4 \text{ k}\Omega$ is laser-trimmed inside the IC. Use this when solving KTU numerical problems that involve commercial IAs.

---

## 3.4 CMRR Analysis Due to Resistor Mismatch (Quantitative)

If the Stage 2 resistors of the IA are mismatched by a fraction $\varepsilon$ (e.g., $\frac{R_2}{R_1} = k(1+\varepsilon)$ vs $\frac{R_4}{R_3} = k$), the common-mode gain becomes:

$$A_c = \frac{\varepsilon \cdot k}{1 + k}$$

For $\varepsilon = 0.001$ (0.1% mismatch — typical of 0.1% resistors) and $k = 1$:

$$A_c = \frac{0.001 \times 1}{2} = 0.0005$$

If $A_d = 100$, then:

$$\text{CMRR} = \frac{100}{0.0005} = 200000 = 20\log_{10}(200000) \approx 106 \text{ dB}$$

**Implication:** To achieve CMRR $\geq 80$ dB with discrete resistors, you need at least **0.1% tolerance** resistors. This is why commercial IAs use **laser-trimmed** thin-film resistors on-chip.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — 1 Op-Amp Difference Amplifier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1["INPUT V1"] --> R1["R1"]
    R1 --> NodeA["INVERTING NODE Vmin"]
    R2["R2 FEEDBACK"] --> NodeA
    NodeA --> OPAMP["OPAMP A1"]
    OPAMP --> Vout["OUTPUT Vout"]
    V2["INPUT V2"] --> R3["R3"]
    R3 --> NodeB["NON-INVERTING NODE Vplus"]
    R4["R4 TO GROUND"] --> NodeB
    NodeB --> OPAMP
    Vout --> Vout2["Vout = R2 BY R1 TIMES V2 MINUS V1"]
    style OPAMP fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style NodeA fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style NodeB fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

> **Read-Through:** $V_1$ enters the inverting input through $R_1$; $V_2$ enters the non-inverting input through $R_3$ with $R_4$ forming a divider to ground. Output is the amplified difference.

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow — 3 Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1["STAGE 1 - INPUT BUFFERS WITH VARIABLE GAIN"]
        direction LR
        V1["V1 INPUT"] --> A1plus["NON INV INPUT OF A1"]
        A1["OPAMP A1"] --> A1out["OUTPUT VO1"]
        A1out --> R1a["FEEDBACK RESISTOR R1 LEFT"]
        R1a --> A1min["INV INPUT OF A1"]
        A1min --> RG["GAIN RESISTOR RG MIDDLE"]
        RG --> A2min["INV INPUT OF A2"]
        A2min --> R1b["FEEDBACK RESISTOR R1 RIGHT"]
        R1b --> A2out["OUTPUT VO2"]
        A2out --> A2["OPAMP A2"]
        A2plus["NON INV INPUT OF A2"] --> V2["V2 INPUT"]
    end
    subgraph S2["STAGE 2 - UNITY GAIN DIFFERENCE AMPLIFIER"]
        direction LR
        A1out --> R5["R AT A3 INV INPUT"]
        A2out --> R6["R AT A3 NON INV INPUT"]
        R7["R TO GROUND"] --> A3plus["NON INV INPUT OF A3"]
        R8["FEEDBACK R AT A3"] --> A3out["FINAL OUTPUT Vout"]
        A3["OPAMP A3"] --> A3out
        R5 --> A3min["INV INPUT OF A3"]
        A3min --> A3
    end
    A1out -.-> R5
    A2out -.-> R6
    style A1 fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A2 fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A3 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style RG fill:#ea9999,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Read-Through:** Both inputs see **infinite** input impedance (non-inverting terminals). $R_G$ is the **single** resistor that controls gain. $A_3$ converts the differential signal to a single-ended output.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Comparison of the Two Architectures

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A["1 OP AMP DIFFERENCE AMPLIFIER"]
        A1["4 RESISTORS R1 R2 R3 R4"] --> A2["ONE OPAMP"]
        A2 --> A3["Vout"]
    end
    subgraph B["3 OP AMP INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER"]
        B1["TWO NON INV BUFFERS"] --> B2["ONE DIFFERENCE AMP"]
        B2 --> B3["Vout"]
    end
    A3 --> ACOMP["LOW INPUT Z ABOUT 10K OHM"]
    B3 --> BCOMP["HIGH INPUT Z ABOUT 10 TO THE 12 OHM"]
    style ACOMP fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style BCOMP fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

> **Read-Through:** The IA is the **engineered upgrade** — it adds two extra op-amps purely to give the inputs high impedance while still providing the same difference-amplification function.

---

## 4.4 Signal Path Visualization (What Happens to a Common-Mode Signal)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VS["Vcm COMMON MODE PLUS Vd DIFFERENTIAL"] --> IA["3 OPAMP INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER"]
    IA --> Vout["Vout EQUALS GAIN TIMES Vd ONLY Vcm CANCELLED BY CMRR"]
    style VS fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style Vout fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Common Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) of a difference amplifier. What is its ideal value?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

CMRR is defined as the ratio of the differential-mode gain $A_d$ to the common-mode gain $A_c$ of a difference amplifier, expressed in decibels:

$$\text{CMRR} = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right) \text{ dB}$$

It quantifies the amplifier's ability to **reject signals common to both inputs** (such as noise or interference) while amplifying the **difference** between the two inputs. The **ideal value of CMRR is infinity** (∞ dB), meaning the common-mode gain is zero and the circuit amplifies only the differential component.

**Mark Split:** Definition = 2 marks; Ideal value = 1 mark.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Compare the 1 Op-Amp difference amplifier and 3 Op-Amp instrumentation amplifier based on input impedance and gain adjustment.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | 1 Op-Amp Diff. Amp | 3 Op-Amp IA |
|---|---|---|
| Input Impedance | Low ($Z_{in} = R_1$, typically 10–100 kΩ) | Very high (≈ 10¹² Ω, non-inverting inputs) |
| Gain Adjustment | Requires changing **two** matched resistors | Requires changing **only one** resistor $R_G$ |
| CMRR | Moderate (depends on resistor matching) | Very high (especially in IC implementations) |

The 3 Op-Amp IA is preferred for **high-impedance, low-level signal sources** like biomedical sensors.

**Mark Split:** Any 3 valid comparison points = 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the matching condition** in 1 Op-Amp diff-amp derivations → 0 marks for the final expression.
> 2. **Sign errors** when applying superposition (especially the inverting vs non-inverting contributions) → lose 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Writing $R_1$ twice** in the IA: students often confuse the *feedback* $R_1$ in Stage 1 with the *input* $R_1$ in Stage 2. Use different subscripts (e.g., $R_1$ for IA feedback, $R$ for stage 2) to avoid ambiguity.
> 4. **Not mentioning CMRR** in IA explanations — the KTU marking scheme often awards a separate 1–2 mark component for CMRR discussion.
> 5. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the IA gain formula: the gain is $1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}$, not $1 + \frac{R_1}{R_G}$. This is a very common slip.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 — Modified]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the output voltage of a **1 Op-Amp difference amplifier** with four resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$, applying superposition. State and explain the matching condition required for high CMRR. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 1 Op-Amp difference amplifier with $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_3 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, and $R_4 = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, find the output voltage when $V_1 = 2 \text{ V}$ and $V_2 = 3 \text{ V}$. Comment on the CMRR of this configuration. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A (a)

**Step 1 — State the circuit topology:** The 1 Op-Amp difference amplifier has:
- $V_1$ → inverting input via $R_1$
- Output → inverting input via $R_2$
- $V_2$ → non-inverting input via $R_3$
- Non-inverting input → ground via $R_4$

**Step 2 — Apply superposition with $V_2 = 0$:**
The non-inverting node is grounded. The inverting node is a virtual ground. Output:
$$V_{out1} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1} V_1$$

**Step 3 — Apply superposition with $V_1 = 0$:**
Voltage at non-inverting node is the divider:
$$V^+ = \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} V_2$$
This is amplified by the non-inverting gain $1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}$:
$$V_{out2} = \left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) \frac{R_4}{R_3+R_4} V_2$$

**Step 4 — Total output:**
$$V_{out} = \frac{R_4(R_1+R_2)}{R_1(R_3+R_4)} V_2 - \frac{R_2}{R_1} V_1$$

**Step 5 — Matching condition for high CMRR:**
To reduce the expression to a clean difference $k(V_2 - V_1)$, we require:
$$\frac{R_2}{R_1} = \frac{R_4}{R_3}$$
This equalizes the two resistor ratios so that any common-mode signal $V_1 = V_2$ produces zero output, making $A_c = 0$ and $\text{CMRR} \to \infty$.

**Final expression under matched condition:**
$$V_{out} = \frac{R_2}{R_1}(V_2 - V_1)$$

**Mark Distribution:** Superposition steps = 4 marks; Final expression = 2 marks; Matching condition explanation = 1 mark.

---

#### Solution to Question A (b)

**Given:** $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_3 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_4 = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, $V_1 = 2 \text{ V}$, $V_2 = 3 \text{ V}$.

**Check matching condition:**
$$\frac{R_2}{R_1} = \frac{100}{10} = 10, \quad \frac{R_4}{R_3} = \frac{5}{1} = 5$$
The ratios are **not equal** (10 ≠ 5), so the matching condition is **violated**.

**Step 1 — Compute $V_{out}$ using the general (unmatched) formula:**

$$V_{out} = \frac{R_4(R_1+R_2)}{R_1(R_3+R_4)} V_2 - \frac{R_2}{R_1} V_1$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$\frac{R_4(R_1+R_2)}{R_1(R_3+R_4)} = \frac{5 \text{k} \times (10 \text{k} + 100 \text{k})}{10 \text{k} \times (1 \text{k} + 5 \text{k})} = \frac{5 \times 110}{10 \times 6} = \frac{550}{60} = 9.1667$$

$$\frac{R_2}{R_1} = 10$$

**Step 3 — Compute the output:**

$$V_{out} = 9.1667 \times 3 - 10 \times 2 = 27.5 - 20 = 7.5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Differential and Common-Mode decomposition:**

The differential input: $V_d = V_2 - V_1 = 1 \text{ V}$
The common-mode input: $V_c = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2} = 2.5 \text{ V}$

The ideal output (if matched) would be $10 \times 1 = 10 \text{ V}$. The actual output is $7.5 \text{ V}$, which is significantly less. This is because part of the common-mode signal is being amplified and **subtracting** from the differential output.

**Step 5 — Comment on CMRR:**

The mismatched resistor ratios ($10$ vs $5$) cause a non-zero common-mode gain. Specifically, the mismatch factor is:
$$\varepsilon = \frac{R_4/R_3 - R_2/R_1}{R_2/R_1} = \frac{5 - 10}{10} = -0.5$$

This is a **50% mismatch** — extremely poor. CMRR will be very low (around **6–10 dB**), making this configuration **unsuitable for any practical application** requiring noise rejection.

**Conclusion:** The given resistor values violate the matching condition, leading to poor CMRR. To fix, choose $R_3 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_4 = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$ so both ratios equal 10.

**Mark Distribution:** Formula application = 2 marks; Computation = 2 marks; CMRR comment = 3 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024] — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a **3 Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier (IA)**. Derive the expression for its output voltage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a 3 Op-Amp IA, the feedback resistors are $R_1 = 25 \text{ k}\Omega$ and the gain-setting resistor is $R_G = 1.1 \text{ k}\Omega$. The Stage 2 difference amplifier uses four equal resistors of $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$. Calculate: (i) the total differential gain, (ii) the output voltage when $V_1 = 5.05 \text{ V}$ and $V_2 = 5.15 \text{ V}$, and (iii) the new value of $R_G$ required to double the gain. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B (a)

**Circuit Description:** The 3 Op-Amp IA consists of:
- **Stage 1:** Two op-amps $A_1$ and $A_2$ configured as non-inverting amplifiers. Their non-inverting inputs receive $V_1$ and $V_2$ respectively. A single gain-setting resistor $R_G$ connects their inverting inputs. Each op-amp has a feedback resistor $R_1$ from its output to its inverting input.
- **Stage 2:** A standard 1 Op-Amp difference amplifier with $A_3$. $V_{o1}$ goes to inverting input via $R$, feedback via $R$. $V_{o2}$ goes to non-inverting input via $R$, with $R$ to ground.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Current through $R_G$:**
By virtual short, both inverting nodes equal their respective non-inverting inputs. So the voltage across $R_G$ is $V_2 - V_1$:
$$I = \frac{V_2 - V_1}{R_G}$$

**Step 2 — Outputs of $A_1$ and $A_2$:**
The same current flows through both feedback resistors $R_1$ (KCL at inverting nodes, since no current enters the op-amp):

$$V_{o1} = V_1 - I R_1 = V_1 - \frac{R_1 (V_2 - V_1)}{R_G}$$

$$V_{o2} = V_2 + I R_1 = V_2 + \frac{R_1 (V_2 - V_1)}{R_G}$$

**Step 3 — Stage 1 differential output:**

$$V_{o2} - V_{o1} = (V_2 - V_1) + \frac{2 R_1 (V_2 - V_1)}{R_G} = \left(1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_G}\right)(V_2 - V_1)$$

**Step 4 — Stage 2 output (unity gain since all four $R$ are equal):**

$$V_{out} = V_{o2} - V_{o1} = \left(1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_G}\right)(V_2 - V_1)$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = \left(1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_G}\right)(V_2 - V_1)}$$

**Key Advantages of the IA:**
1. **Infinite input impedance** (both inputs to non-inverting terminals).
2. **Single-resistor gain control** — only $R_G$ needs to be varied.
3. **High CMRR** because Stage 2's matched resistors are at the output (driving low-impedance nodes), minimizing the effect of resistor mismatch.

**Mark Distribution:** Circuit description = 2 marks; Derivation = 4 marks; Final expression + advantages = 1 mark.

---

#### Solution to Question B (b)

**Given:** $R_1 = 25 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_G = 1.1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $V_1 = 5.05 \text{ V}$, $V_2 = 5.15 \text{ V}$.

**(i) Total Differential Gain:**

**Step 1 — Stage 1 gain:**

$$G_1 = 1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_G} = 1 + \frac{2 \times 25 \text{ k}\Omega}{1.1 \text{ k}\Omega} = 1 + \frac{50}{1.1} = 1 + 45.4545 = 46.4545$$

**Step 2 — Stage 2 gain:**

Since all four resistors are equal ($R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$):
$$G_2 = \frac{R}{R} = 1$$

**Step 3 — Total gain:**

$$\boxed{A_d = G_1 \times G_2 = 46.4545 \times 1 = 46.4545}$$

**(ii) Output Voltage:**

**Step 1 — Compute differential input:**

$$V_d = V_2 - V_1 = 5.15 - 5.05 = 0.10 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute output:**

$$V_{out} = A_d \times V_d = 46.4545 \times 0.10 = 4.645 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} \approx 4.65 \text{ V}}$$

**(iii) New $R_G$ to Double the Gain:**

**Step 1 — New required gain:**

$$A_{d,new} = 2 \times 46.4545 = 92.909$$

**Step 2 — Solve for new $R_G$:**

$$A_{d,new} = 1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_{G,new}} \Rightarrow 92.909 = 1 + \frac{2 \times 25\text{k}}{R_{G,new}}$$

$$\frac{50 \text{ k}}{R_{G,new}} = 91.909 \Rightarrow R_{G,new} = \frac{50 \text{ k}}{91.909} \approx 544 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$\boxed{R_{G,new} \approx 544 \text{ }\Omega}$$

**Verification check:** Doubling the gain means halving the gain-setting resistor approximately (since $A_d \approx \frac{2R_1}{R_G}$ for large gains). Original $R_G = 1.1 \text{ k}\Omega$; half is $0.55 \text{ k}\Omega = 550 \text{ }\Omega$. The computed value $544 \text{ }\Omega$ matches this approximation.

**Mark Distribution:** (i) = 2 marks; (ii) = 2 marks; (iii) = 3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- [x] **1 Op-Amp Diff-Amp output (matched):** $V_{out} = \frac{R_2}{R_1}(V_2 - V_1)$.
- [x] **1 Op-Amp Diff-Amp output (general/unmatched):** $V_{out} = \frac{R_4(R_1+R_2)}{R_1(R_3+R_4)} V_2 - \frac{R_2}{R_1} V_1$.
- [x] **Matching condition:** $\frac{R_2}{R_1} = \frac{R_4}{R_3}$ → CMRR = ∞.
- [x] **3 Op-Amp IA output:** $V_{out} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)(V_2 - V_1)$ for unity Stage 2.
- [x] **Factor of 2** in the IA gain formula is the most-missed term — remember it.
- [x] **Single-resistor gain control** is the IA's signature feature — vary $R_G$, not $R_1$.
- [x] **Input impedance:** Low ($R_1$) for 1 Op-Amp; ~$10^{12} \, \Omega$ for 3 Op-Amp IA.
- [x] **CMRR formula:** $\text{CMRR} = 20\log_{10}(A_d / A_c)$ in dB.
- [x] **Practical resistor tolerance:** Use **0.1%** resistors minimum for CMRR $\geq 80$ dB; for $>100$ dB use laser-trimmed IC IAs.
- [x] **Virtual short** applies to both stages of the IA — use it to find currents through $R_G$ and feedback resistors.
- [x] **KCL at inverting nodes** in Stage 1 gives the same current through both feedback resistors.
- [x] **Common IC IA packages:** INA128, AD620, AD8221, INA333 — remember the $R_G = \frac{49.4 \text{ k}\Omega}{G-1}$ formula for AD620.
- [x] **Applications to mention in exams:** ECG/EEG signal conditioning, strain-gauge bridges, data acquisition front-ends, audio balanced lines, motor current sensing.
- [x] **The 1 Op-Amp diff-amp has THREE weaknesses:** low input impedance at $V_1$, gain change requires changing two resistors, moderate CMRR. The 3 Op-Amp IA **fixes all three**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Log and Antilog Amplifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT402 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Log and Antilog Amplifiers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Log Amplifier** is an electronic circuit built using an operational amplifier in which the output voltage is a **logarithmic function** of the input voltage. Mathematically, the output is expressed as:

$$V_{out} = -K \ln\left(\frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}}\right)$$

Conversely, an **Antilog Amplifier** (or Exponential Amplifier) performs the inverse mathematical operation — its output voltage is an **exponential function** of the input voltage:

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot e^{\left(\frac{V_{in}}{K}\right)}$$

Both circuits exploit the inherently logarithmic current-voltage ($I$-$V$) characteristic of a semiconductor **PN-junction diode** or the base-emitter junction of a **BJT** placed in the negative feedback path of an op-amp.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCEBT402 – Module 2):**
> The student must be able to *analyze, design, and derive the transfer function* of log and antilog amplifiers using both diode and BJT configurations. Numerical problems on temperature compensation, dynamic range, and compression/expansion are frequently asked (typically 7 to 14 marks).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **dam with a logarithmic spillway**. When the water level (input voltage) is low, a small opening lets water out easily. But as the water level rises, the opening widens *disproportionately fast*, so that the water flow (output) compresses a huge range of water levels into a manageable range. That is exactly what a log amplifier does — it **compresses a wide dynamic range of input signals into a smaller, more manageable output range**.

In audio engineering, this is called **compression**; in mathematics, it is the **logarithm function**. Conversely, an antilog amplifier **expands** a compressed signal back to its original dynamic range — like stretching the spillway reading back to a real water level.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use log amplifiers in real engineering?**
> - **Signal compression** in audio, communication, and instrumentation systems.
> - **Analog multiplication/division** (using log and antilog blocks in series).
> - **True RMS detection** of AC signals.
> - **Linearization** of transducer outputs that follow an exponential law.
> - Used as a **building block** in analog computers.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- Thermal Voltage at room temperature (T = **300 K**): $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx \mathbf{25.85 \ mV}$ (commonly taken as **26 mV** in KTU problems).
- Boltzmann Constant: $k = \mathbf{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \ J/K}$
- Electron Charge: $q = \mathbf{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \ C}$
- Silicon diode ideality factor: $\eta \in [\mathbf{1, \ 2}]$ (typically **1** for transistors, **2** for diodes at low current).

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — The Logarithmic Element

The cornerstone of every log/antilog amplifier is the **exponential I-V relationship of a PN junction**. For a forward-biased diode:

$$I_D = I_S \left(e^{\frac{V_D}{\eta V_T}} - 1\right) \approx I_S \cdot e^{\frac{V_D}{\eta V_T}}$$

where:
- $I_S$ = reverse saturation current (order of **nA** to **pA**).
- $V_D$ = forward voltage across the diode.
- $V_T$ = thermal voltage ≈ **26 mV** at 300 K.
- $\eta$ = ideality factor.

The approximation $e^{V_D/\eta V_T} \gg 1$ holds for $V_D > 0.1 \ V$, allowing us to write:

$$V_D = \eta V_T \ln\left(\frac{I_D}{I_S}\right)$$

This equation is the **logarithmic relationship** that the op-amp exploits. If the input current $I_D$ is proportional to $V_{in}$, then $V_D$ becomes the log of $V_{in}$.

### 2.2 Basic Log Amplifier (Diode in Feedback)

**Circuit Topology**: The diode is placed in the **negative feedback path** of an inverting op-amp configuration. The input voltage $V_{in}$ is applied through input resistor $R_1$, and the output is taken across the diode.

**Why inverting configuration?** Because the virtual ground at the inverting node forces the current through $R_1$ to be the same as the diode current, allowing precise control.

The current through $R_1$ is:

$$I_{in} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$

By KCL at the virtual ground, $I_D = I_{in}$:

$$I_D = \frac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$

The voltage across the diode is the output voltage (with sign):

$$V_{out} = -V_D = -\eta V_T \ln\left(\frac{V_{in}}{R_1 \cdot I_S}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Negative sign explanation:** The inverting input is at virtual ground (0 V). The cathode of the diode is connected to the output. So $V_D = V_- - V_{out} = 0 - V_{out} = -V_{out}$. Hence $V_{out} = -V_D$.

### 2.3 BJT-Based Log Amplifier (Preferred Configuration)

**Drawbacks of diode log amplifier**:
- Limited dynamic range (only ~2 decades due to leakage currents).
- Saturation current $I_S$ is **temperature sensitive** (~ doubles every 10 °C).
- Diode resistance $r_d = \eta V_T / I_D$ varies with current, causing non-ideal behaviour.

**BJT-based version** overcomes these limitations. By using a transistor in the feedback path, the dynamic range extends to **6–8 decades**, and the log conformance is much tighter.

For a BJT in the **active region**, the collector current is:

$$I_C = I_S \cdot e^{\frac{V_{BE}}{V_T}}$$

The base-emitter voltage:

$$V_{BE} = V_T \ln\left(\frac{I_C}{I_S}\right)$$

Since the op-amp's inverting node is at virtual ground, the emitter is at ground potential. The output is taken at the collector, which equals $-V_{BE}$ (because the collector is connected to the output and emitter is at ground, with the BJT in CB or emitter-follower feedback mode).

$$V_{out} = -V_{BE} = -V_T \ln\left(\frac{V_{in}}{R_1 \cdot I_S}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> For BJT log amplifiers, the ideality factor $\eta$ is typically **1** (since the base current effect is negligible for $\beta \to \infty$), giving a cleaner logarithmic relationship.

### 2.4 Antilog Amplifier (Exponential Amplifier)

The antilog amplifier is the **dual** of the log amplifier. The diode (or BJT) is now placed at the **input** instead of the feedback, and the feedback element is a resistor.

**Working Principle**: Apply a negative input voltage to the diode so that it is forward biased. The diode current becomes:

$$I_D = I_S \cdot e^{\frac{V_{in}}{\eta V_T}}$$

This current flows through the feedback resistor $R_f$, generating the output voltage:

$$V_{out} = -I_D \cdot R_f = -R_f \cdot I_S \cdot e^{\frac{V_{in}}{\eta V_T}}$$

For BJT version:

$$V_{out} = -R_f \cdot I_S \cdot e^{\frac{V_{in}}{V_T}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention Alert:** For the antilog amplifier, $V_{in}$ must be **negative** so that the diode/BJT is forward biased (since the inverting node is at virtual ground, the diode cathode is at virtual ground, and the input is at the anode).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Log Amplifier Formula | Antilog Amplifier Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|-----------|---------------------|--------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Input current | $I_{in} = V_{in} / R_1$ | $I_D = I_S \cdot e^{V_{in}/\eta V_T}$ | Amperes |
| 2 | Output voltage (Diode) | $V_{out} = -\eta V_T \ln\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1 I_S}\right)$ | $V_{out} = -R_f I_S \cdot e^{V_{in}/\eta V_T}$ | Volts |
| 3 | Output voltage (BJT) | $V_{out} = -V_T \ln\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1 I_S}\right)$ | $V_{out} = -R_f I_S \cdot e^{V_{in}/V_T}$ | Volts |
| 4 | Thermal voltage | $V_T = kT / q$ | $V_T = kT / q$ | ≈ 26 mV @ 300 K |
| 5 | Dynamic range (Diode) | ~ 2 decades | ~ 2 decades | Limited by leakage |
| 6 | Dynamic range (BJT) | 6 to 8 decades | 6 to 8 decades | Preferred in practice |
| 7 | Temperature sensitivity | Output shifts with $I_S$ | Output shifts with $I_S$ | Needs compensation |
| 8 | Constant K | $K = -\eta V_T$ | $K = -R_f I_S$ | Used in compact form |
| 9 | Compact form | $V_{out} = K \ln(V_{in}) + C$ | $V_{out} = K \cdot e^{V_{in}/K}$ | Standard notation |
| 10 | Condition for proper bias | $V_{in} > 0$ (log) | $V_{in} < 0$ (antilog) | Sign convention |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Transfer Function of a Diode Log Amplifier

**Given:** Op-amp in inverting configuration with input resistor $R_1$ and a forward-biased diode in the feedback path.

**Step 1 — Apply KCL at the inverting node (virtual ground).**

Since the op-amp input impedance is infinite, no current flows into the op-amp. Therefore, the current through $R_1$ must equal the current through the diode.

$$I_{R_1} = I_D$$

**Step 2 — Express the current through $R_1$.**

The inverting node is at virtual ground (0 V). One end of $R_1$ is at $V_{in}$ and the other end is at 0 V.

$$I_{R_1} = \frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_1} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$

**Step 3 — Express the diode current using Shockley's equation.**

The voltage across the diode is $V_D = V_{diode\ anode} - V_{diode\ cathode}$. The anode is at virtual ground (0 V) and the cathode is at $V_{out}$. So:

$$V_D = 0 - V_{out} = -V_{out}$$

The diode current (forward direction, from anode to cathode) is:

$$I_D = I_S \left(e^{\frac{V_D}{\eta V_T}} - 1\right) \approx I_S \cdot e^{\frac{V_D}{\eta V_T}}$$

(The $-1$ term is neglected because $V_D \gg \eta V_T$ for forward bias.)

Substituting $V_D = -V_{out}$:

$$I_D = I_S \cdot e^{\frac{-V_{out}}{\eta V_T}}$$

**Step 4 — Equate $I_{R_1}$ and $I_D$.**

$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} = I_S \cdot e^{\frac{-V_{out}}{\eta V_T}}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $V_{out}$.**

Rearranging:

$$e^{\frac{-V_{out}}{\eta V_T}} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_1 \cdot I_S}$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\frac{-V_{out}}{\eta V_T} = \ln\left(\frac{V_{in}}{R_1 \cdot I_S}\right)$$

Multiplying both sides by $-\eta V_T$:

$$V_{out} = -\eta V_T \cdot \ln\left(\frac{V_{in}}{R_1 \cdot I_S}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Write in standard KTU compact form.**

Using the identity $\ln(a/b) = \ln a - \ln b$:

$$V_{out} = -\eta V_T \ln(V_{in}) + \eta V_T \ln(R_1 I_S)$$

Or, defining a constant offset $C = \eta V_T \ln(R_1 I_S)$:

$$\boxed{V_{out} = -\eta V_T \ln\left(\frac{V_{in}}{R_1 I_S}\right) = -K \ln(V_{in}) + C}$$

where $K = \eta V_T$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: BJT Log Amplifier Transfer Function

**Step 1 — Identify circuit topology.**

A BJT is connected in the feedback path with the **base and collector** tied together (diode-connected) OR with the **base grounded** and the **collector** at the output. We use the latter configuration: emitter at virtual ground, collector at output, base grounded.

**Step 2 — Write the BJT collector current equation.**

For an NPN transistor in the active region, with base grounded:

$$I_C = I_S \cdot e^{\frac{V_{BE}}{V_T}}$$

**Step 3 — Identify $V_{BE}$ in terms of $V_{out}$.**

Since base is grounded ($V_B = 0$) and emitter is at virtual ground ($V_E = 0$), the base-emitter voltage is:

$$V_{BE} = V_B - V_E = 0 - 0 = 0$$

Wait — this is incorrect for a log amplifier. Let us reconsider. The correct BJT log amplifier configuration has the **emitter** connected to the inverting input (virtual ground) and the **collector** connected to the output. With the **base grounded**:

$$V_{BE} = V_B - V_E = 0 - V_E$$

The emitter is NOT at ground; it is connected through the feedback loop. Let us correct the topology:

**Correct topology:** Base is grounded, emitter is at the inverting node (virtual ground = 0 V), collector is at the output. So:

$$V_{BE} = V_B - V_E = 0 - 0 = 0$$

This gives $I_C = I_S$ (constant), which is wrong. So actually, the correct configuration is:

**Correct BJT log amplifier topology**: 
- Base is connected to the inverting input node (virtual ground = 0 V).
- Emitter is connected to the output.
- Collector is grounded (or to a negative supply).

With this topology:

$$V_{BE} = V_B - V_E = 0 - V_{out} = -V_{out}$$

**Step 4 — Apply KCL at the inverting node.**

$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} = I_C = I_S \cdot e^{\frac{V_{BE}}{V_T}} = I_S \cdot e^{\frac{-V_{out}}{V_T}}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $V_{out}$.**

$$V_{out} = -V_T \ln\left(\frac{V_{in}}{R_1 I_S}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why BJT is preferred over diode:**
> In a BJT, the collector current $I_C$ is controlled exponentially by $V_{BE}$ with high accuracy over **6–8 decades**, and the saturation current $I_S$ of a BJT is more stable and predictable than that of a diode. Additionally, the ideality factor $\eta$ is typically closer to 1 for BJTs.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Antilog Amplifier Transfer Function (BJT Version)

**Step 1 — Circuit description.** Input voltage $V_{in}$ is applied to the base of the BJT, emitter is at virtual ground (via feedback), collector is connected to the output through resistor $R_f$.

**Step 2 — Write the input current (BJT collector current).**

$$I_C = I_S \cdot e^{\frac{V_{in}}{V_T}}$$

(Since $V_{BE} = V_{in}$ in this configuration.)

**Step 3 — Apply KCL at inverting node.**

The current through the feedback resistor equals the collector current:

$$\frac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f} = I_C$$

$$V_{out} = -R_f \cdot I_C = -R_f \cdot I_S \cdot e^{\frac{V_{in}}{V_T}}$$

**Step 4 — Compact KTU form.**

$$\boxed{V_{out} = -R_f I_S \cdot \exp\left(\frac{V_{in}}{V_T}\right)}$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A log amplifier uses a silicon diode ($\eta = 2$, $I_S = 10 \ \mu A$, $V_T = 26 \ mV$) with input resistor $R_1 = 10 \ k\Omega$. Calculate the output voltage when $V_{in} = 2 \ V$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the product $R_1 \cdot I_S$.

$$R_1 I_S = (10 \times 10^3) \cdot (10 \times 10^{-6}) = 0.1 \ V$$

**Step 2:** Apply the log amplifier equation.

$$V_{out} = -\eta V_T \ln\left(\frac{V_{in}}{R_1 I_S}\right)$$

$$V_{out} = -(2)(26 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot \ln\left(\frac{2}{0.1}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the logarithm.

$$\ln\left(\frac{2}{0.1}\right) = \ln(20) = 2.9957$$

**Step 4:** Compute the final answer.

$$V_{out} = -(2)(0.026)(2.9957) = -0.1558 \ V \approx -156 \ mV$$

**Answer:** $V_{out} \approx \mathbf{-0.156 \ V}$ or **-156 mV**.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison Between Configurations

| Parameter | Diode Log Amplifier | BJT Log Amplifier | Diode Antilog | BJT Antilog |
|-----------|---------------------|-------------------|---------------|-------------|
| Dynamic range | 1–2 decades | 6–8 decades | 1–2 decades | 6–8 decades |
| Ideality factor $\eta$ | ~ 2 | ~ 1 | ~ 2 | ~ 1 |
| Temperature stability | Poor | Moderate (needs compensation) | Poor | Moderate |
| Leakage current effect | Significant | Negligible | Significant | Negligible |
| Application suitability | Low-frequency, low-precision | High-precision, wide range | Low-precision | High-precision |
| KTU exam frequency | Common (theory) | Most common (numerical) | Occasional | Common |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Log Amplifier

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **signal flow** through a log amplifier, from input to output, including the role of the logarithmic element (BJT) in the feedback path.

```mermaid
    B --> C["INVERTING NODE virtual ground"]
    C --> D["OP AMP open loop gain A"]
    D --> E["OUTPUT NODE Vout negative DC"]
    E --> F["FEEDBACK PATH contains BJT"]
    F --> C
    E --> G["OUTPUT BUFFER optional"]
    G --> H["FINAL Vout equals minus VT times ln of Vin over R1 times IS"]

    style H fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00
    style C fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025
```

```mermaid
    end
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    Rin --> Vsum["Virtual ground node sum of currents equals zero"]
    Vsum --> OpAmpCore["Op amp in open loop high gain"]
    OpAmpCore --> VoutFinal["Vout negative collector of BJT"]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    Icollector --> RfNode["Virtual ground node"]

```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```

> **Engineering Insight:** The cascaded log-summing-antilog architecture is the classical analog computer technique for performing multiplication, division, raising to a power, and root extraction. A KTU viva favourite question is: *"How would you realize an analog multiplier using log and antilog amplifiers?"* — the answer is the structure above.
