**Given:** $f_c = 10$ kHz, $Q = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$, $R_1 = R_2 = R$, $C_1 = C_2 = C$.

**Step 1:** Choose a convenient capacitor value. For audio-frequency filters, a standard value is $C = 10$ nF.

**Step 2:** Calculate $R$ from the cutoff frequency formula:

$$
f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R C} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^4 \times 10^{-8}}
$$

$$
R = \frac{1}{6.2832 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1591.5 \, \Omega \approx 1.59 \, \text{k}\Omega
$$

Use the standard value $R = 1.6$ k$\Omega$.

**Step 3:** Calculate the required gain $A_v$ for the desired $Q$:

$$
Q = \frac{1}{3 - A_v} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad A_v = 3 - \frac{1}{Q} = 3 - \sqrt{2} = 3 - 1.4142 = 1.5858
$$

**Step 4:** Choose the gain-setting resistors:

$$
A_v = 1 + \frac{R_F}{R_{in}} = 1.5858 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{R_F}{R_{in}} = 0.5858
$$

Choose $R_{in} = 10$ k$\Omega$, then $R_F = 5.858$ k$\Omega \approx 5.6$ k$\Omega$ (standard value) or use a $10$ k$\Omega$ potentiometer for fine-tuning.

**Step 5:** Verify damping factor:

$$
\zeta = \frac{3 - A_v}{2} = \frac{1.4142}{2} = 0.7071 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Final Design Summary:**

| Component | Value |
|---|---|
| $R_1 = R_2$ | $1.6$ k$\Omega$ |
| $C_1 = C_2$ | $10$ nF |
| $R_{in}$ | $10$ k$\Omega$ |
| $R_F$ | $5.86$ k$\Omega$ |
| Op-amp | LF351 (or TL081) |
| $f_c$ achieved | $9.95$ kHz (close to $10$ kHz) |

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam - May 2024] (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a Sallen-Key second-order high-pass filter. Derive its transfer function. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a Sallen-Key low-pass filter with $R_1 = R_2 = 10$ k$\Omega$, $C_1 = C_2 = 10$ nF, and gain $A_v = 1.586$, calculate: (i) the natural frequency $f_0$, (ii) the quality factor $Q$, and (iii) the –3 dB cutoff frequency $f_c$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Circuit Diagram Description:** The Sallen-Key HPF is obtained by **swapping** the positions of $R$ and $C$ in the Sallen-Key LPF. Specifically:
- $C_1$ is in series between $V_{in}$ and node A.
- $R_1$ is the shunt element from node A to ground.
- $C_2$ provides positive feedback from $V_{out}$ to node A.
- The op-amp is in non-inverting configuration.

**Operation:** At low frequencies, $C_1$ and $C_2$ present high impedance, blocking the signal. At high frequencies, both capacitors act as short circuits, and the signal is passed with gain $A_v$.

**Derivation:**

KCL at node A:

$$
sC_1(V_{in} - V_A) + \frac{V_A}{R_1} + sC_2(V_{out} - V_A) = 0
$$

For non-inverting amplifier: $V_A = V_{out}/A_v$.

Substituting and simplifying (similar procedure as LPF, with $s$ and $1/s$ roles interchanged):

$$
\boxed{H(s) = \frac{A_v \,s^2}{s^2 + s\left(\frac{2}{RC} - \frac{A_v - 1}{RC}\right) + \frac{1}{R^2C^2}}}
$$

For equal components ($R_1 = R_2 = R$, $C_1 = C_2 = C$):

$$
\boxed{H(s) = \frac{A_v \,s^2}{s^2 + (3 - A_v)\omega_0 s + \omega_0^2}}
$$

where $\omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC}$.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Given:** $R = 10$ k$\Omega$, $C = 10$ nF, $A_v = 1.586$.

**Step 1: Natural Frequency $\omega_0$:**

$$
\omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC} = \frac{1}{10^4 \times 10^{-8}} = 10^4 \, \text{rad/sec}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{10^4}{2\pi} \approx 1591.5 \, \text{Hz} \approx 1.59 \, \text{kHz}}
$$

**Step 2: Quality Factor $Q$:**

$$
Q = \frac{1}{3 - A_v} = \frac{1}{3 - 1.586} = \frac{1}{1.414} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071
$$

This corresponds to the **Butterworth** response (maximally flat passband). ✓

**Step 3: –3 dB Cutoff Frequency $f_c$:**

For a Butterworth second-order filter, the –3 dB cutoff frequency **equals** the natural frequency:

$$
\boxed{f_c = f_0 \approx 1.59 \, \text{kHz}}
$$

**Verification using the general formula:**

$$
f_c = f_0 \sqrt{\left(1 - \frac{1}{2Q^2}\right) + \sqrt{\left(1 - \frac{1}{2Q^2}\right)^2 + 1}}
$$

With $Q^2 = 0.5$, $\frac{1}{2Q^2} = 1$:

$$
f_c = f_0 \sqrt{0 + \sqrt{0 + 1}} = f_0 \cdot 1 = f_0 \quad \checkmark
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Examinations:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing $f_0$ and $f_c$:** Students often treat the natural frequency $\omega_0$ as the –3 dB cutoff frequency. This is **only true for Butterworth filters** ($Q = 1/\sqrt{2}$). For Chebyshev or Bessel responses, $f_c \ne f_0$. Always derive $f_c$ from the magnitude equation.
> 
> 2. **Sign error in HPF transfer function:** When deriving the HPF, students often write $H(s) = \frac{A_v}{1 + sRC}$ by mistake. The correct HPF is $H(s) = \frac{A_v \cdot sRC}{1 + sRC}$. Always remember: HPF passes $s \to \infty$ (high frequency) and blocks $s \to 0$ (DC).
> 
> 3. **Stability oversight:** In Sallen-Key filters, the gain $A_v$ must satisfy $A_v < 3$ for stability. If a question specifies a gain of 3 or higher, the circuit will oscillate — state this explicitly in your answer for full marks.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting units in dB:** When asked for magnitude in dB, use $20\log_{10}(\vert H \vert)$ (not $10\log_{10}$). At $f = f_c$, this should be $A_v - 3$ dB.
> 
> 5. **Phase response:** A first-order LPF has a phase of $-45^\circ$ at cutoff, ranging from $0^\circ$ (low freq) to $-90^\circ$ (high freq). A second-order LPF goes from $0^\circ$ to $-180^\circ$ with $-90^\circ$ at $f_0$. Marks are often lost for omitting the phase response.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for First and Second Order Filters:**
> 
> - **Order of filter** = highest power of $s$ in denominator of $H(s)$.
> - **First-order filters:** roll-off = **–20 dB/decade**; phase shift range: $0^\circ$ to $\pm 90^\circ$.
> - **Second-order filters:** roll-off = **–40 dB/decade**; phase shift range: $0^\circ$ to $\pm 180^\circ$.
> - **Cutoff frequency** for first-order: $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$. For second-order Sallen-Key LPF (equal components): $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$.
> - **Sallen-Key Quality Factor:** $Q = \frac{1}{3 - A_v}$ (for equal-component LPF).
> - **Butterworth design:** $Q = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ → $A_v = 3 - \sqrt{2} \approx 1.586$.
> - **Stability condition:** $A_v < 3$ for Sallen-Key LPF.
> - **DC gain of LPF** = $A_v$; **DC gain of HPF** = $0$.
> - **High-frequency gain of HPF** = $A_v$; **high-frequency gain of LPF** = $0$.
> - **Phase at $f_c$ (first-order):** $-45^\circ$ (LPF) or $+45^\circ$ (HPF).
> - **Phase at $f_0$ (second-order):** $-90^\circ$ (LPF) or $+90^\circ$ (HPF).
> - **Active filters** use op-amps to provide gain, isolation, and inductorless design.
> - **Sallen-Key** is the VCVS (non-inverting) topology; **MFB** is the inverting topology.
> - **At $f = f_0$ for high-$Q$ second-order LPF**, the magnitude can **peak** at $H_0 \cdot Q$ — a critical concept for resonance.
> - **The two energy-storage elements** (two capacitors in Sallen-Key) are what create the **complex-conjugate pole pair** characteristic of second-order responses.
> - **Cascading** two first-order stages is **not equivalent** to a single second-order stage due to loading effects — use a unity-gain buffer between stages or design a true second-order filter.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Approximation Methods - Butterworth - Chebyshev
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT402 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Approximation Methods - Butterworth - Chebyshev

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Fundamental Problem: Why Approximation?

In an **ideal filter**, the magnitude response would be a rectangular block — unity gain in the passband, zero gain in the stopband, and an infinitely sharp transition. However, such a response is **physically unrealizable** because it violates the **Paley-Wiener criterion** (the magnitude-squared function cannot have finite energy and an infinitely steep roll-off in any realizable causal network).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Syllabus):**
> *Approximation methods* are mathematical techniques used to determine a realizable transfer function H(s) whose magnitude response |H(jΩ)| closely approaches the ideal filter specification within an acceptable tolerance, while remaining physically realizable using passive or active components.

The "approximation" is therefore not in the *mathematics* — it is in the *deviation* from the ideal brick-wall response that we deliberately accept to obtain a buildable circuit.

## 1.2 The Two Major Approximation Families Covered in KTU Module 3

The two approximation strategies most commonly tested in the KTU 2024 Linear Integrated Circuits syllabus are:

### 1.2.1 Butterworth Approximation
> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** The **Butterworth approximation** yields a magnitude-squared response that is *maximally flat* (i.e., has the maximum possible number of zero derivatives) at Ω = 0. Its magnitude response is **monotonically decreasing** with **no ripples** in either passband or stopband.

The general magnitude-squared form is:

$$|H(j\Omega)|^{2} = \frac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{\Omega}{\Omega_{c}}\right)^{2n}}$$

where **n** is the **filter order** and **Ωc** is the **-3 dB cutoff frequency**.

### 1.2.2 Chebyshev Approximation
> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** The **Chebyshev approximation** allows *equiripple* oscillations in the passband (Type I) or stopband (Type II) in exchange for a *steeper transition band* than Butterworth of the same order.

The general magnitude-squared form for **Chebyshev Type I** is:

$$|H(j\Omega)|^{2} = \frac{1}{1 + \varepsilon^{2} \, C_{n}^{2}\!\left(\dfrac{\Omega}{\Omega_{p}}\right)}$$

where **ε** is the **ripple factor**, **Cn(x)** is the **Chebyshev polynomial of the first kind of order n**, and **Ωp** is the **passband edge frequency**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Volume Knob Story"

Imagine you are an audio engineer designing an **equalizer knob** for a music system.

- The **Ideal Filter** would be a perfect "click-on, click-off" switch: full volume below 20 kHz, absolute silence above 20 kHz. *Beautiful, but no physical knob exists that does this perfectly.*
- The **Butterworth Filter** is a knob that smoothly rolls off the volume as you turn past the cutoff, with a gentle, ripple-free curve. *You sacrifice sharpness for a clean, predictable response — best when you cannot tolerate any ripples (e.g., audio amplifiers).*
- The **Chebyshev Filter** is a knob that has slight "wobbles" (ripples) within the audible range, but cuts off *much more aggressively* right after the cutoff. *You trade flatness for sharpness — best when steep roll-off is critical (e.g., anti-aliasing in ADCs).*

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Mnemonic:** *"Butter = Buttery smooth. Cheby = Cheating with ripples for sharper cutoff."*

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Passband edge frequency | $\Omega_{p}$ | 100 Hz – 100 kHz |
| Stopband edge frequency | $\Omega_{s}$ | > $\Omega_{p}$ |
| Maximum passband ripple | $A_{p}$ | **0.5 dB – 3 dB** |
| Minimum stopband attenuation | $A_{s}$ | **20 dB – 80 dB** |
| Filter order | $n$ | Integer, 1 – 10 typically |
| Ripple factor | $\varepsilon$ | $0 < \varepsilon \leq 1$ |
| -3 dB cutoff | $\Omega_{c}$ | $\geq \Omega_{p}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Ideal Filters Are Impossible — The Physics

The ideal low-pass filter has an impulse response of the form $\sin(\Omega_c t)/(\pi t)$, which is **non-causal** (it has non-zero values for $t < 0$) and **non-decaying** (its integral diverges). No combination of resistors, capacitors, op-amps, or inductors can produce this response. This is why every practical filter must be an *approximation* of the ideal.

## 2.2 Butterworth Filter — Operational Logic

The Butterworth magnitude-squared function is engineered such that the **first (2n − 1) derivatives of |H(jΩ)|² with respect to Ω evaluate to zero at Ω = 0**. This guarantees the flattest possible passband response for a given order n.

**Step-by-step logic of the Butterworth response:**

1. **Passband behavior:** For $\Omega \ll \Omega_{c}$, the term $(\Omega/\Omega_{c})^{2n}$ is negligibly small, so $|H(j\Omega)| \approx 1$. The response is essentially flat.
2. **Transition band:** As $\Omega$ approaches $\Omega_{c}$, the magnitude rolls off monotonically. The roll-off rate is **−20n dB/decade** asymptotically.
3. **Stopband behavior:** For $\Omega \gg \Omega_{c}$, the magnitude falls as $1/\Omega^{n}$ (a steepness determined entirely by n).
4. **Cutoff definition:** At $\Omega = \Omega_{c}$, $|H(j\Omega_{c})|^{2} = 1/2$, which corresponds to a **−3 dB** point.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** *The Butterworth filter sacrifices sharpness for monotonicity. Every additional order of n steepens the roll-off by exactly 20 dB/decade.*

## 2.3 Chebyshev Filter — Operational Logic

The Chebyshev polynomial $C_n(x)$ oscillates between **−1 and +1** for $|x| \leq 1$ and grows unboundedly for $|x| > 1$. This produces the characteristic *equiripple* passband and steeper roll-off.

**Step-by-step logic of the Chebyshev Type I response:**

1. **Passband oscillation:** For $\Omega \leq \Omega_{p}$, the value of $C_n(\Omega/\Omega_{p})$ oscillates between ±1, causing $|H(j\Omega)|$ to ripple between 1 and $1/\sqrt{1+\varepsilon^{2}}$.
2. **Ripple magnitude:** The peak-to-peak ripple in dB is $A_{p} = 10\log_{10}(1+\varepsilon^{2})$.
3. **Transition band:** For $\Omega > \Omega_{p}$, $C_n$ grows as $\cosh[n\cosh^{-1}(\Omega/\Omega_{p})]$, producing a much sharper transition than Butterworth of the same order.
4. **Stopband behavior:** Monotonically decreasing at rate of −20n dB/decade (asymptotically).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

| Quantity | Butterworth Formula | Chebyshev Type I Formula |
|---|---|---|
| **Magnitude-squared response** | $\vert H(j\Omega)\vert^{2} = \dfrac{1}{1+(\Omega/\Omega_{c})^{2n}}$ | $\vert H(j\Omega)\vert^{2} = \dfrac{1}{1+\varepsilon^{2}C_{n}^{2}(\Omega/\Omega_{p})}$ |
| **Ripple factor ε** | N/A (always flat) | $\varepsilon = \sqrt{10^{0.1 A_{p}}-1}$ |
| **−3 dB cutoff** | $\Omega_{c}$ (definition) | $\Omega_{c} = \Omega_{p}\cosh\!\left[\dfrac{1}{n}\cosh^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon}\right)\right]$ |
| **Filter order (from specs)** | $n \geq \dfrac{\log_{10}\!\left[(10^{0.1 A_{s}}-1)/\varepsilon^{2}\right]}{2\log_{10}(\Omega_{s}/\Omega_{p})}$ | $n \geq \dfrac{\cosh^{-1}\!\left[\sqrt{(10^{0.1 A_{s}}-1)}/\varepsilon\right]}{\cosh^{-1}(\Omega_{s}/\Omega_{p})}$ |
| **Asymptotic roll-off** | $-20n$ dB/decade | $-20n$ dB/decade |
| **Passband behavior** | Maximally flat, monotonic | Equiripple with amplitude $A_{p}$ |
| **Stopband behavior** | Monotonic | Monotonic |
| **Pole locations (radius)** | Unit circle radius $\Omega_{c}$ | Ellipse with semi-major $\Omega_{p}\sinh(\alpha)$ and semi-minor $\Omega_{p}\cosh(\alpha)$ |
| **Pole location (k-th pole)** | $\sigma_{k} = -\Omega_{c}\sin\!\left[\dfrac{(2k-1)\pi}{2n}\right]$ | $\sigma_{k} = -\Omega_{p}\sinh(\alpha)\sin\!\left[\dfrac{(2k-1)\pi}{2n}\right]$ |
| **Imaginary part of pole** | $\Omega_{k} = \Omega_{c}\cos\!\left[\dfrac{(2k-1)\pi}{2n}\right]$ | $\Omega_{k} = \Omega_{p}\cosh(\alpha)\cos\!\left[\dfrac{(2k-1)\pi}{2n}\right]$ |
| **Auxiliary parameter** | None | $\alpha = \dfrac{1}{n}\sinh^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon}\right)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Butterworth is preferred in *audio, instrumentation, and biomedical* applications where passband fidelity matters. Chebyshev is preferred in *communications, radar, and ADC anti-aliasing* where sharp transition is paramount and small ripples are tolerable.

## 2.5 Decision Flow — Which Approximation to Choose?

The selection is governed by the engineering specification triangle: **sharpness ↔ flatness ↔ circuit complexity**.

| Priority | Choose |
|---|---|
| Flat passband, no overshoot | **Butterworth** |
| Steepest transition for given order | **Chebyshev Type I** |
| Flat passband *and* steep transition | **Chebyshev Type II** or higher order Butterworth |
| Linear phase (pulse preservation) | **Bessel** (out of KTU Module 3 scope) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Butterworth Magnitude-Squared Response

**Starting principle:** We require a magnitude function that satisfies three constraints:

1. $|H(j\Omega)| \leq 1$ for all $\Omega$ (passive/active gain limit)
2. $|H(j0)| = 1$ (DC gain unity)
3. Maximum possible number of zero derivatives at $\Omega = 0$ (flatness condition)

**Step 1 — Assume a polynomial form of even degree in $\Omega^{2}$:**

$$|H(j\Omega)|^{2} = \frac{1}{1 + B_{2n}\,\Omega^{2n}}$$

where $B_{2n}$ is a positive constant to be determined. We choose the *smallest possible integer exponent* 2n because any even power works, but lowest order gives best transient response.

**Step 2 — Apply the flatness constraint.** Differentiate $|H(j\Omega)|^{-2}$ with respect to $\Omega^{2}$ repeatedly:

$$\frac{d^{k}}{d(\Omega^{2})^{k}}\left[(1+B_{2n}\Omega^{2n})\right] = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, n-1$$

This is automatically satisfied because all derivatives of $\Omega^{2n}$ contain the factor $B_{2n}^{k}$ and vanish at $\Omega = 0$ for $k < n$.

**Step 3 — Determine $B_{2n}$ from the −3 dB cutoff definition.** By definition, $|H(j\Omega_{c})| = 1/\sqrt{2}$, so:

$$\frac{1}{1+B_{2n}\Omega_{c}^{2n}} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$B_{2n} = \frac{1}{\Omega_{c}^{2n}}$$

**Step 4 — Final Butterworth form (substituting back):**

$$|H(j\Omega)|^{2} = \frac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{\Omega}{\Omega_{c}}\right)^{2n}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The −3 dB definition fixes only one unknown ($B_{2n}$). All remaining derivatives up to order (n−1) vanish at the origin by construction, which is what makes Butterworth "maximally flat."

## 3.2 Derivation of Butterworth Filter Order from Specifications

**Given specifications:** $A_{p}$, $A_{s}$, $\Omega_{p}$, $\Omega_{s}$, and desired filter type.

**Step 1 — Express attenuation at the passband edge.** At $\Omega = \Omega_{p}$, define passband attenuation $A_{p}$:

$$10^{-0.1 A_{p}} = \frac{1}{1 + (\Omega_{p}/\Omega_{c})^{2n}}$$

Solving for $(\Omega_{p}/\Omega_{c})^{2n}$:

$$(\Omega_{p}/\Omega_{c})^{2n} = 10^{0.1 A_{p}} - 1$$

**Step 2 — Express attenuation at the stopband edge.** At $\Omega = \Omega_{s}$:

$$(\Omega_{s}/\Omega_{c})^{2n} = 10^{0.1 A_{s}} - 1$$

**Step 3 — Eliminate $\Omega_{c}$ by dividing the two expressions:**

$$\left(\frac{\Omega_{s}}{\Omega_{p}}\right)^{2n} = \frac{10^{0.1 A_{s}} - 1}{10^{0.1 A_{p}} - 1}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for n by taking logarithms (base 10):**

$$n \geq \frac{\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{10^{0.1 A_{s}} - 1}{10^{0.1 A_{p}} - 1}\right]}{2\log_{10}(\Omega_{s}/\Omega_{p})}$$

For maximally flat response, we typically set $A_{p} \leq 3$ dB which is the natural Butterworth point. When $A_{p} = 3$ dB exactly, the numerator simplifies to $\log_{10}[10^{0.1A_s} - 1]$ since $10^{0.1 \times 3} - 1 = 1$, giving:

$$n \geq \frac{\log_{10}(10^{0.1 A_{s}} - 1)}{2\log_{10}(\Omega_{s}/\Omega_{p})}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Butterworth Pole Locations

The poles of $H(s)H(-s)$ occur where the denominator of $|H(j\Omega)|^{2}$ equals zero when $s = j\Omega$, i.e., where:

$$1 + \left(\frac{s}{j\Omega_{c}}\right)^{2n} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \left(\frac{s}{\Omega_{c}}\right)^{2n} = -1 = e^{j\pi(2k-1)}$$

where $k = 1, 2, \dots, 2n$ selects each of the 2n roots.

**Step 1 — Solve for s:**

$$s_{k} = \Omega_{c} \cdot e^{j\pi(2k-1)/(2n)}$$

**Step 2 — Separate real and imaginary parts using Euler's formula:**

$$s_{k} = \Omega_{c}\!\left[\cos\!\left(\frac{(2k-1)\pi}{2n}\right) + j\sin\!\left(\frac{(2k-1)\pi}{2n}\right)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Left-Half-Plane (LHP) poles for stability.** Only poles with negative real parts are selected (those with $k = 1, 2, \dots, n$):

$$\sigma_{k} = -\Omega_{c}\sin\!\left[\frac{(2k-1)\pi}{2n}\right]$$

$$\Omega_{k} = +\Omega_{c}\cos\!\left[\frac{(2k-1)\pi}{2n}\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Geometric Insight:** *The LHP poles of a Butterworth filter lie uniformly spaced on a circle of radius $\Omega_{c}$ in the s-plane, symmetric about both the real and imaginary axes. For $n=4$, the poles lie at angles $\pm 22.5°$, $\pm 67.5°$, $\pm 112.5°$, $\pm 157.5°$ from the positive real axis, with the LHP half retained.*

## 3.4 Derivation of Chebyshev Filter Order

**Step 1 — At the passband edge $\Omega_{p}$**, the ripple factor condition gives:

$$\varepsilon = \sqrt{10^{0.1 A_{p}} - 1}$$

**Step 2 — At the stopband edge $\Omega_{s}$**, the attenuation must satisfy:

$$10^{-0.1 A_{s}} = \frac{1}{1 + \varepsilon^{2}C_{n}^{2}(\Omega_{s}/\Omega_{p})}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to extract $C_{n}(\Omega_{s}/\Omega_{p})$:**

$$C_{n}(\Omega_{s}/\Omega_{p}) = \frac{1}{\varepsilon}\sqrt{10^{0.1 A_{s}} - 1}$$

**Step 4 — Use the hyperbolic form of the Chebyshev polynomial for $\Omega_{s} > \Omega_{p}$:**

$$C_{n}(x) = \cosh[n\cosh^{-1}(x)], \quad x > 1$$

**Step 5 — Take inverse hyperbolic cosine of both sides:**

$$n \geq \frac{\cosh^{-1}\!\left[\dfrac{\sqrt{10^{0.1 A_{s}}-1}}{\varepsilon}\right]}{\cosh^{-1}(\Omega_{s}/\Omega_{p})}$$

The order **n** is rounded up to the next integer.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Design a low-pass Butterworth filter** with $A_{p} = 3$ dB at $\Omega_{p} = 1$ kHz, $A_{s} = 40$ dB at $\Omega_{s} = 2$ kHz.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the order:**

$$n \geq \frac{\log_{10}(10^{0.1 \times 40} - 1)}{2\log_{10}(2/1)} = \frac{\log_{10}(9999)}{2 \times 0.301}$$

$$n \geq \frac{3.9999}{0.602} = 6.644 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad n = 7$$

**Step 2 — Compute the −3 dB cutoff** (since $A_p = 3$ dB, we have $\Omega_c = \Omega_p$ exactly):

$$\Omega_{c} = 1000 \text{ rad/s} \quad (\text{since } A_p = 3 \text{ dB})$$

**Step 3 — Locate the LHP poles** using $s_k = 1000 \cdot e^{j\pi(2k-1)/14}$ for $k = 1, 2, \dots, 7$.

| k | Angle (deg) | Real Part $\sigma_k$ | Imag Part $\Omega_k$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 12.86 | **−975.30** | **+222.52** |
| 2 | 38.57 | **−782.61** | **+623.49** |
| 3 | 64.29 | **−433.88** | **+900.97** |
| 4 | 90.00 | **0.00** | **+1000.00** |
| 5 | 115.71 | **+433.88** | **+900.97** |
| 6 | 141.43 | **+782.61** | **+623.49** |
| 7 | 167.14 | **+975.30** | **+222.52** |

**Step 4 — Select LHP poles** (negative real parts): k = 1, 2, 3 and the conjugates of 5, 6, 7, plus the conjugate of k=4. This yields 7 poles total, producing a 7th-order cascade of 2nd-order sections plus one 1st-order section.

**Step 5 — Construct the transfer function** by multiplying the 1st-order and 2nd-order factors:

$$H(s) = \frac{\Omega_{c}^{7}}{\prod_{k=1}^{7}(s - s_{k})_{\text{LHP}}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** *This 7th-order filter would be implemented in practice as a cascade of one 1st-order and three 2nd-order Sallen-Key or Multiple-Feedback active filter sections, each using one or two op-amps.*

## 3.6 Chebyshev Pole Locations — Analytical Summary

For Chebyshev Type I, poles lie on an **ellipse** rather than a circle. Define the auxiliary parameter:

$$\alpha = \frac{1}{n}\sinh^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{\varepsilon}\right)$$

Then the k-th pole is at:

$$\sigma_{k} = -\Omega_{p}\sinh(\alpha)\sin\!\left[\frac{(2k-1)\pi}{2n}\right]$$

$$\Omega_{k} = \Omega_{p}\cosh(\alpha)\cos\!\left[\frac{(2k-1)\pi}{2n}\right]$$

The ellipse has semi-major axis $a = \Omega_{p}\cosh(\alpha)$ on the imaginary axis and semi-minor axis $b = \Omega_{p}\sinh(\alpha)$ on the real axis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison Insight:** *For $\varepsilon \to 0$ (no allowed ripple), $\sinh(\alpha) \approx \cosh(\alpha) \to 1/\varepsilon \cdot \Omega_p$, and the Chebyshev ellipse becomes a huge circle — recovering the Butterworth case as a limiting form. This shows Butterworth is mathematically a special case of Chebyshev when ripples are forced to vanish.*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Design Procedure Flowchart

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the canonical KTU filter design procedure when an approximation method must be selected.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Filter Specifications] --> B[Identify Ap, As, Omega_p, Omega_s]
    B --> C{Is Passband Flatness Critical?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Choose Butterworth Approximation]
    C -- No --> E{Is Steepest Roll-off Required?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Choose Chebyshev Type I]
    E -- No --> G[Consider Higher-Order Butterworth]
    D --> H[Compute Order n]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I{Is n Integer?}
    I -- No --> J[Round Up to Next Integer]
    I -- Yes --> K[Proceed to Pole Location]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Compute Pole Locations]
    L --> M[Form Transfer Function H s]
    M --> N[Factor into 1st and 2nd Order Sections]
    N --> O[Realize Each Section Using Op-Amp Topology]
    O --> P[Sallen-Key or MFB]
    P --> Q[Cascade All Sections in Order]
    Q --> R[End: Filter Realized]
```

## 4.2 Pole Location Geometry — Butterworth vs Chebyshev

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ButterworthPoles [Butterworth Poles]
        B1[Circle of Radius Omega_c]
        B2[LHP poles uniformly spaced]
        B3[Symmetric about real axis]
    end
    subgraph ChebyshevPoles [Chebyshev Poles]
        C1[Ellipse with semi-major on j-omega axis]
        C2[LHP poles shifted rightward as ripple increases]
        C3[Ellipse flattens with larger epsilon]
    end
    B1 --> Bx[Located at angles pi times 2k-1 over 2n]
    C1 --> Cx[Located via sinh alpha and cosh alpha]
```

## 4.3 Magnitude Response Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Magnitude Response vs Frequency] --> B[Omega less than Omega_p]
    A --> C[Omega between Omega_p and Omega_s]
    A --> D[Omega greater than Omega_s]
    B --> B1[Butterworth: flat at 1]
    B --> B2[Chebyshev Type I: ripples between 1 and 1 over sqrt 1+epsilon squared]
    C --> C1[Butterworth: smooth roll-off]
    C --> C2[Chebyshev: sharper roll-off]
    D --> D1[Both: monotonic, -20n dB per decade]
    D --> D2[Chebyshev: faster attenuation]
```

## 4.4 Frequency Response Behavior Table

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Passband
        P1[Butterworth: Flat, monotonic]
        P2[Chebyshev Type I: Ripples within Ap]
        P3[Chebyshev Type II: Flat]
    end
    subgraph Transition
        T1[Butterworth: Gentle]
        T2[Chebyshev Type I: Steep]
        T3[Chebyshev Type II: Steep]
    end
    subgraph Stopband
        S1[Butterworth: Slow decay]
        S2[Chebyshev Type I: Fast decay]
        S3[Chebyshev Type II: Ripples within As]
    end
```

## 4.5 Comparison Block Diagram — Engineering Trade-offs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Filter Order n] --> B{Choose Approximation}
    B --> C[Butterworth]
    B --> D[Chebyshev]
    C --> E[Higher n for same sharpness]
    D --> F[Lower n for same sharpness]
    E --> G[More op-amps, more components]
    F --> H[Passband ripple tolerated]
    G --> I[Better for low-noise audio]
    H --> J[Better for ADC anti-aliasing]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the Butterworth approximation and write its magnitude-squared response. Mention one advantage and one disadvantage.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Butterworth approximation** is an approximation technique that produces a magnitude-squared response which is *maximally flat* at $\Omega = 0$ and monotonically decreasing without any ripples in either passband or stopband.

$$|H(j\Omega)|^{2} = \frac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{\Omega}{\Omega_{c}}\right)^{2n}}$$

**Advantage:** Maximally flat passband response — no ripple distortion.

**Disadvantage:** For a given order n, Butterworth has the *gentlest* transition band compared to Chebyshev or elliptic approximations.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the Chebyshev polynomial? How does it differ from the Butterworth polynomial in the context of filter design?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Chebyshev polynomial of the first kind of order n**, denoted $C_n(x)$, is defined as:
> - $C_n(x) = \cos[n\cos^{-1}(x)]$ for $\vert x \vert \leq 1$
> - $C_n(x) = \cosh[n\cosh^{-1}(x)]$ for $\vert x \vert > 1$

For $\vert x \vert \leq 1$, the polynomial oscillates between **−1 and +1** with n+1 extrema, while for $\vert x \vert > 1$ it grows as a hyperbolic cosine. This produces the characteristic *equiripple* passband of Chebyshev filters.

**Difference from Butterworth:** The Butterworth polynomial $(\Omega/\Omega_c)^{2n}$ is monotonic and grows smoothly, while the Chebyshev polynomial oscillates in the passband, yielding a sharper transition at the cost of passband ripple.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the magnitude-squared response of a Butterworth low-pass filter and show that its first (2n−1) derivatives vanish at Ω = 0.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Butterworth low-pass filter must satisfy: $A_p \leq 1$ dB at $\Omega_p = 1$ kHz and $A_s \geq 30$ dB at $\Omega_s = 2$ kHz. Determine:
1. The minimum filter order n
2. The −3 dB cutoff frequency $\Omega_c$
3. All pole locations

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1:** Assume the magnitude-squared function in even-polynomial form:

$$|H(j\Omega)|^{2} = \frac{1}{1 + B_{2n}\Omega^{2n}}$$

**Step 2:** The flatness condition requires that the first (n−1) derivatives of $|H(j\Omega)|^{2}$ with respect to $\Omega$ vanish at $\Omega = 0$. The k-th derivative of $\Omega^{2n}$ contains the factor $\Omega^{2n-k}$, which equals zero for $k < 2n$. Hence, for $k = 1, 2, \dots, 2n-1$:

$$\frac{d^{k}}{d\Omega^{k}}\left[\Omega^{2n}\right]\bigg|_{\Omega=0} = 0$$

This is automatically satisfied for the chosen form.

**Step 3:** The constant $B_{2n}$ is determined by the −3 dB definition:

$$B_{2n} = \frac{1}{\Omega_{c}^{2n}}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting back:

$$|H(j\Omega)|^{2} = \frac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{\Omega}{\Omega_{c}}\right)^{2n}} \quad \blacksquare$$

#### Model Solution to (b):

**(b)(1) Order Calculation:**

$$n \geq \frac{\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{10^{0.1 \times 30} - 1}{10^{0.1 \times 1} - 1}\right]}{2\log_{10}(2/1)}$$

$$n \geq \frac{\log_{10}(999 / 0.2589)}{2 \times 0.301} = \frac{\log_{10}(3860)}{0.602} = \frac{3.5866}{0.602}$$

$$n \geq 5.96 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad n = 6$$

**(b)(2) Cutoff Frequency Calculation:**

From the passband condition at $\Omega_p$:

$$(\Omega_p/\Omega_c)^{2n} = 10^{0.1 A_p} - 1 = 10^{0.1} - 1 = 0.2589$$

$$\Omega_c = \Omega_p \cdot (0.2589)^{-1/(2n)} = 1000 \cdot (0.2589)^{-1/12}$$

$$\Omega_c = 1000 \cdot 1.1173 = 1117.3 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(b)(3) Pole Locations:**

For n = 6, the k-th LHP pole is at angle $\theta_k = \pi(2k-1)/12$ from the positive real axis:

| k | $\theta_k$ (deg) | $\sigma_k$ (rad/s) | $\Omega_k$ (rad/s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 15 | −1079.1 | +289.2 |
| 2 | 45 | −790.0 | +790.0 |
| 3 | 75 | −289.2 | +1079.1 |

Including the complex conjugates, the six poles are:

$$s_{1,6} = -1079.1 \pm j289.2$$
$$s_{2,5} = -790.0 \pm j790.0$$
$$s_{3,4} = -289.2 \pm j1079.1$$

These form three quadratic factors that can be cascaded as second-order Sallen-Key sections.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare Butterworth and Chebyshev Type I approximation methods in detail. Tabulate differences in (i) passband behavior, (ii) transition band steepness, (iii) pole geometry, and (iv) transfer function form.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Design a Chebyshev Type I low-pass filter with $A_p = 1$ dB, $\Omega_p = 1000$ rad/s, $A_s = 40$ dB, $\Omega_s = 2000$ rad/s. Find:
1. The ripple factor ε
2. The minimum order n
3. The transfer function for n = 5 (compute pole locations)

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

| Parameter | Butterworth | Chebyshev Type I |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Passband behavior | Maximally flat, monotonic | Equiripple with amplitude $A_p$ |
| (ii) Transition steepness | Gentle, smooth roll-off | Steeper, faster attenuation |
| (iii) Pole geometry | Uniformly spaced on a circle of radius $\Omega_c$ | On an ellipse with major axis $\Omega_p\cosh(\alpha)$ on $j\Omega$ |
| (iv) Transfer function form | $\dfrac{1}{1+(\Omega/\Omega_c)^{2n}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{1+\varepsilon^2 C_n^2(\Omega/\Omega_p)}$ |
| Order for given specs | Higher | Lower |
| Passband ripples | None | Present (controlled by ε) |
| Group delay variation | Smoother | More ripple in delay |
| Best suited for | Audio, instrumentation | Communications, ADC front-ends |

#### Model Solution to (b):

**(b)(1) Ripple factor:**

$$\varepsilon = \sqrt{10^{0.1 \times 1} - 1} = \sqrt{10^{0.1} - 1} = \sqrt{0.2589} = 0.5088$$

**(b)(2) Minimum Order:**

$$n \geq \frac{\cosh^{-1}\!\left[\dfrac{\sqrt{10^{0.1 \times 40}-1}}{0.5088}\right]}{\cosh^{-1}(2000/1000)}$$

Numerator: $\sqrt{9999}/0.5088 = 99.995/0.5088 = 196.53$, and $\cosh^{-1}(196.53) = 5.946$.

Denominator: $\cosh^{-1}(2) = 1.317$.

$$n \geq \frac{5.946}{1.317} = 4.51 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad n = 5$$

**(b)(3) Pole Locations for n = 5:**

Compute the auxiliary parameter:

$$\alpha = \frac{1}{5}\sinh^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{0.5088}\right) = \frac{1}{5}\sinh^{-1}(1.9655) = \frac{1}{5}(1.4210) = 0.2842$$

Then $\sinh(\alpha) = 0.2882$ and $\cosh(\alpha) = 1.0408$.

The k-th pole location uses angles $(2k-1)\pi/10$ for $k = 1, 2, \dots, 5$:

| k | Angle (deg) | $\sigma_k = -1000 \cdot 0.2882 \cdot \sin\theta$ | $\Omega_k = 1000 \cdot 1.0408 \cdot \cos\theta$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 18 | **−89.04** | **+989.81** |
| 2 | 54 | **−233.20** | **+612.13** |
| 3 | 90 | **−288.20** | **0.00** |
| 4 | 126 | **−233.20** | **−612.13** |
| 5 | 162 | **−89.04** | **−989.81** |

Selecting LHP poles (k = 1, 2, 3 and their conjugates 4, 5 — note k=3 is on the real axis and its "conjugate" is itself):

The five poles are at:
- $s_1 = -89.04 + j989.81$
- $s_2 = -233.20 + j612.13$
- $s_3 = -288.20$
- $s_4 = -233.20 - j612.13$ (conjugate of $s_2$)
- $s_5 = -89.04 - j989.81$ (conjugate of $s_1$)

The transfer function is:

$$H(s) = \frac{K}{(s+288.20)(s^2 + 178.08s + 989790.6)(s^2 + 466.40s + 428853.8)}$$

where the gain constant K is chosen to make $|H(j0)| = 1$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Order rounding:** Always round **up** to next integer, never truncate. n = 5.96 → n = 6, NOT 5.
> 2. **Sign errors in poles:** Remember $\sigma_k$ carries a **negative** sign for LHP poles. Forgetting this gives unstable filters.
> 3. **Ripple factor calculation:** ε formula has square root — squaring both sides without careful substitution is a common algebraic error.
> 4. **Unit confusion:** $\Omega_p$, $\Omega_s$, and $\Omega_c$ are in **rad/s**, NOT Hz. Multiply by $2\pi$ if specifications are given in Hz.
> 5. **Chebyshev order formula:** Use **$\cosh^{-1}$** (hyperbolic arc-cosine), not $\cos^{-1}$. Mistaking these gives wildly wrong n.
> 6. **All LHP poles must be retained:** Selecting RHP poles gives unstable filters that the examiner will mark strictly wrong.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Butterworth Cheat Points:**
> - Magnitude response: $|H(j\Omega)|^{2} = \dfrac{1}{1+(\Omega/\Omega_{c})^{2n}}$
> - Maximally flat at DC (all derivatives zero up to order n−1)
> - No passband ripples, monotonic everywhere
> - −3 dB cutoff at $\Omega_c$
> - Roll-off: −20n dB/decade
> - Poles uniformly on a circle of radius $\Omega_c$
> - Order formula: $n \geq \dfrac{\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{10^{0.1 A_{s}}-1}{10^{0.1 A_{p}}-1}\right]}{2\log_{10}(\Omega_{s}/\Omega_{p})}$
> - When $A_p = 3$ dB exactly: $\Omega_c = \Omega_p$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chebyshev Cheat Points:**
> - Type I: ripples in **passband**, monotonic stopband
> - Type II: flat passband, ripples in **stopband**
> - Ripple factor: $\varepsilon = \sqrt{10^{0.1 A_{p}} - 1}$
> - Uses Chebyshev polynomial $C_n(x)$
> - Steeper roll-off than Butterworth for same n
> - Poles on an **ellipse**, not a circle
> - Auxiliary: $\alpha = \dfrac{1}{n}\sinh^{-1}(1/\varepsilon)$
> - Order formula uses $\cosh^{-1}$ (NOT $\cos^{-1}$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Design Steps:**
> 1. Extract $A_p$, $A_s$, $\Omega_p$, $\Omega_s$ from problem
> 2. Choose approximation (Butterworth for flat, Chebyshev for sharp)
> 3. Calculate filter order n (round up)
> 4. Compute $\Omega_c$ (Butterworth) or $\alpha$ (Chebyshev)
> 5. Find LHP pole locations using $s_k$ formula
> 6. Form $H(s)$ as product of 1st-order and 2nd-order factors
> 7. Realize each factor as an op-amp active filter section
> 8. Cascade sections in order of increasing Q-factor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Comparison Summary:**
> - For **same order n**: Chebyshev has sharper transition; Butterworth has flatter passband
> - For **same transition spec**: Chebyshev needs lower n (fewer components); Butterworth needs higher n
> - Butterworth is a **limiting case** of Chebyshev as $\varepsilon \to 0$
> - Both have asymptotic roll-off of **−20n dB/decade**
> - Both produce **all-pole** transfer functions (no zeros in finite s-plane)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sample and Hold Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT402 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sample and Hold Circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Sample and Hold (S/H) Circuit**
> A **Sample and Hold (S/H) circuit** is an analog signal processing building block that captures (samples) the instantaneous value of a continuously varying analog input signal and then retains (holds) that captured voltage level constant for a specified minimum duration, regardless of subsequent variations in the input signal.

The S/H circuit is the **critical analog interface** between the continuous-time analog domain and the discrete-time domain of digital systems. It acts as an **analog memory element** that freezes the signal amplitude during the finite conversion time required by downstream blocks such as **Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADCs)**, **Digital Voltmeters (DVMs)**, and **Data Acquisition Systems (DAS)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** In Module 3, the S/H circuit is studied as a **practical application of operational amplifiers** combined with **analog switches (BJT / JFET / MOSFET)**. The syllabus specifically emphasizes open-loop and closed-loop configurations, acquisition time, aperture time, and droop rate.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Water Bucket and Valve**
> Imagine a **continuously flowing river** (your analog input signal, $V_{in}(t)$). A **scoop with a controllable valve** (the electronic switch) periodically dips into the river and fills a **bucket** (the hold capacitor $C_H$). The moment the valve closes, the bucket **stops accepting more water** and simply **holds the captured volume steady**, even though the river keeps flowing. A **measuring tape held against the bucket's water level** (the buffer op-amp) lets you read this frozen level anytime, without any drop or disturbance.

In electronics terms:
- **Valve open** → **SAMPLE mode** → capacitor $C_H$ charges rapidly to track $V_{in}$.
- **Valve closed** → **HOLD mode** → capacitor $C_H$ retains the last sampled voltage.
- **Measuring tape** → **High-input-impedance unity-gain buffer op-amp** → prevents the capacitor from discharging into the load and provides a low-impedance output $V_{out}$.

## 1.3 Why is a Sample and Hold Circuit Required?

| Problem in Direct Sampling | How S/H Solves It |
| :--- | :--- |
| Input signal changes during ADC conversion → wrong digital code | Holds the signal **constant** during entire conversion |
| Aperture uncertainty of ADC causes code jitter | Provides a **stable, frozen** value at the ADC input |
| Multiplexed inputs need isolation in time | S/H acts as a **time-domain demultiplexer** |
| Fast transients exceed ADC bandwidth | S/H captures the peak value for the ADC |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metrics Used in KTU 2024 Valuation:**
> - **Acquisition Time ($t_{AQ}$):** typically measured in **microseconds (µs)**
> - **Aperture Time ($t_{ap}$):** typically measured in **nanoseconds (ns)**
> - **Droop Rate ($\frac{dV_C}{dt}$):** typically measured in **microvolts per microsecond (µV/µs)** or **volts per second (V/s)**
> - **Hold Mode Settling Time ($t_s$):** typically in **nanoseconds (ns)**
> - **Hold Capacitor ($C_H$):** typically in the range **pF to nF** (e.g., **100 pF to 10 nF**)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle

A Sample and Hold circuit operates in **two distinct, time-multiplexed modes** controlled by an external digital **control logic signal** $V_{control}(t)$:

### Mode 1 — **SAMPLE (Track) Mode**
- The analog **switch S is CLOSED** (ON state).
- The hold capacitor $C_H$ is connected directly to the input $V_{in}(t)$.
- The capacitor charges (or discharges) rapidly through the switch's on-resistance $R_{on}$ to track the input voltage.
- The output buffer (op-amp) continuously reproduces this voltage at $V_{out}$.
- **Mathematical condition:** $V_{out}(t) \approx V_{in}(t)$ while switch is closed.

### Mode 2 — **HOLD Mode**
- The analog **switch S is OPEN** (OFF state).
- The capacitor $C_H$ is **electrically isolated** from the input.
- Since the buffer op-amp has very high input impedance, negligible current flows out of $C_H$.
- The capacitor therefore **retains its last charged voltage** with very slow droop.
- **Mathematical condition:** $V_{out}(t) = V_C(t=t_{hold-start}) = \text{constant}$.

## 2.2 Switch Control Logic

The switch is typically a **JFET, MOSFET, or a CMOS transmission gate**, driven by a complementary logic signal. In KTU textbook examples, a **BJT switch** or **JFET switch** is commonly analyzed.

> **Truth Table for Switch Operation**

| Control Signal ($V_{control}$) | Switch State | Circuit Mode | $V_{out}$ Behavior |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| HIGH (Logic 1) | ON (Closed) | **SAMPLE** | $V_{out} = V_{in}$ (tracks) |
| LOW (Logic 0) | OFF (Open) | **HOLD** | $V_{out} = V_C$ (constant) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Formula Reference for S/H Circuit — KTU Board Exam**

| Sl. No. | Parameter | Formula | Units | Physical Meaning |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Charging time constant | $\tau_c = R_{on} \cdot C_H$ | seconds (s) | Speed of capacitor voltage tracking |
| 2 | Acquisition time (approx.) | $t_{AQ} \approx 7 \cdot \tau_c$ to $10 \cdot \tau_c$ | seconds (s) | Time to settle to within ADC LSB |
| 3 | Aperture time | $t_{ap} = t_{transit} + t_{logic} + t_{aperture-jitter}$ | seconds (s) | Delay between command and actual hold |
| 4 | Droop rate in hold mode | $\frac{dV_C}{dt} = \frac{I_{leakage}}{C_H}$ | V/s | Rate of voltage drop on $C_H$ |
| 5 | Droop voltage (over time $t_H$) | $\Delta V = \frac{I_{leakage} \cdot t_H}{C_H}$ | V (volts) | Voltage lost during hold |
| 6 | Hold capacitor minimum value | $C_{H,min} = \frac{I_{leakage} \cdot t_H}{\Delta V_{max}}$ | F (farads) | Smallest $C_H$ that meets droop spec |
| 7 | Acquisition time (1 LSB) | $t_{AQ} = \tau_c \cdot \ln(2^n)$ | seconds (s) | For an $n$-bit ADC |
| 8 | Output buffer gain | $A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$ (for non-unity) | dimensionless | Closed-loop gain of buffer |
| 9 | Feedthrough (switch off) | $V_{ft} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{C_{gd}}{C_H + C_{gd}}$ | V (volts) | Unwanted signal leak through $C_{gd}$ |
| 10 | Resolution requirement | $\frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} \geq \Delta V_{max}$ | V (volts) | LSB size vs allowable droop |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of S/H Circuits

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial & Engineering Applications:**
> 1. **Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADCs):** Front-end to all successive-approximation, dual-slope, and flash ADCs.
> 2. **Data Acquisition Systems (DAS):** Freezes multiplexed analog channels during digitization.
> 3. **Digital Storage Oscilloscopes (DSOs):** Captures and stores high-speed waveforms.
> 4. **Digital Audio Systems:** Pulse Code Modulation (PCM) front-end for audio signals.
> 5. **Peak Detectors in Instrumentation:** Holds maximum amplitude for reading.
> 6. **D/A Deglitchers:** Removes output transients during DAC switching.
> 7. **Sample-and-Hold Amplifiers (SHA):** Commercial ICs like LF398, AD585, AD684.

## 2.5 Key Engineering Trade-offs

> [!WARNING]
> **The Core Design Dilemma:**
> - **Larger $C_H$** → *less droop* (better hold accuracy) BUT *slower acquisition* (slower tracking).
> - **Smaller $C_H$** → *faster acquisition* BUT *higher droop rate* and *more feedthrough error*.
> - **Lower $R_{on}$ switch** → *faster charging* BUT may require larger driver circuits.
> - **Higher op-amp input impedance** → *less droop* (since $I_{leakage}$ is smaller).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Acquisition Time in SAMPLE Mode

During the SAMPLE mode, the equivalent circuit is a simple **first-order RC charging network** with:
- Charging resistance = switch on-resistance $R_{on}$
- Charging capacitance = hold capacitor $C_H$
- Initial voltage on capacitor = $V_{initial}$
- Final voltage to be reached = $V_{final}$

**Step 1:** Apply KVL around the loop containing $V_{in}$, $R_{on}$, and $C_H$.

$$V_{in}(t) = i(t) \cdot R_{on} + V_C(t)$$

**Step 2:** Express the capacitor current using $i(t) = C_H \cdot \frac{dV_C}{dt}$.

$$V_{in}(t) = R_{on} \cdot C_H \cdot \frac{dV_C}{dt} + V_C(t)$$

**Step 3:** Define the time constant $\tau_c = R_{on} \cdot C_H$ and rewrite the differential equation.

$$\tau_c \cdot \frac{dV_C}{dt} + V_C(t) = V_{in}(t)$$

**Step 4:** Solve the first-order linear ODE with the standard form solution.

$$V_C(t) = V_{in} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_c}\right) + V_{initial} \cdot e^{-t/\tau_c}$$

**Step 5:** For the worst case where the input changes by a full-scale step, the error voltage at any time $t$ is:

$$V_{error}(t) = (V_{in} - V_{initial}) \cdot e^{-t/\tau_c}$$

**Step 6:** For an $n$-bit ADC, the system must settle to within $\pm \frac{1}{2}$ LSB, which is $\frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}}$. Setting the error to this bound:

$$\frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}} = V_{FS} \cdot e^{-t_{AQ}/\tau_c}$$

**Step 7:** Take the natural logarithm on both sides.

$$\frac{1}{2^{n+1}} = e^{-t_{AQ}/\tau_c}$$

**Step 8:** Apply $\ln$ to both sides.

$$\ln\left(\frac{1}{2^{n+1}}\right) = -\frac{t_{AQ}}{\tau_c}$$

**Step 9:** Simplify using $\ln(1/x) = -\ln(x)$ and $\ln(2^{n+1}) = (n+1)\ln(2)$.

$$-(n+1)\ln(2) = -\frac{t_{AQ}}{\tau_c}$$

**Step 10:** Solve for the acquisition time.

$$\boxed{t_{AQ} = (n+1) \cdot \ln(2) \cdot R_{on} \cdot C_H \approx 0.693 \cdot (n+1) \cdot R_{on} \cdot C_H}$$

> **Note:** For $n = 10$ bits, $t_{AQ} \approx 7.6 \cdot R_{on} \cdot C_H$, confirming the engineering rule-of-thumb of "7 to 10 time constants."

## 3.2 Derivation: Droop Rate in HOLD Mode

When the switch is open, the only current discharging the hold capacitor is the **leakage current** $I_{leakage}$, which comes from:
- The op-amp's input bias current $I_B$
- The switch's off-state leakage current $I_{off}$
- The PCB leakage currents (usually negligible)

**Step 1:** The fundamental capacitor equation in hold mode.

$$I_{leakage} = -C_H \cdot \frac{dV_C}{dt}$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange for the rate of voltage change.

$$\frac{dV_C}{dt} = -\frac{I_{leakage}}{C_H}$$

**Step 3:** The magnitude of the droop rate.

$$\boxed{\left\vert \frac{dV_C}{dt} \right\vert = \frac{I_{leakage}}{C_H}}$$

**Step 4:** Over a hold duration $t_H$, the total voltage droop is:

$$\boxed{\Delta V_{droop} = \frac{I_{leakage} \cdot t_H}{C_H}}$$

**Step 5:** To find the **minimum hold capacitor** for a given droop specification, rearrange:

$$\boxed{C_{H,min} = \frac{I_{leakage} \cdot t_H}{\Delta V_{droop,max}}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Aperture Time Relationship

Aperture time consists of three components that combine in practice:

**Step 1:** Define the three contributions.
- $t_{logic}$ = propagation delay of the digital control signal through the driver
- $t_{transit}$ = transit time of the analog switch (time for the switch to change state)
- $t_{jit}$ = aperture jitter (random uncertainty in the actual hold moment)

**Step 2:** Sum the deterministic components.

$$t_{aperture,fixed} = t_{logic} + t_{transit}$$

**Step 3:** The total effective aperture time including jitter is the root-sum-square (RSS) combination.

$$\boxed{t_{ap,total} = \sqrt{t_{aperture,fixed}^2 + t_{jit}^2}}$$

## 3.4 Worked Example Problem (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem:** A 10-bit ADC is preceded by a sample-and-hold circuit with $R_{on} = 200\ \Omega$ and $C_H = 1\ \text{nF}$. The op-amp buffer has input bias current $I_B = 50\ \text{pA}$ and the switch leakage is $I_{off} = 20\ \text{pA}$. Find (a) the acquisition time, and (b) the droop in 10 µs of hold mode.

**Part (a) — Acquisition Time:**

Step 1: Compute the time constant.

$$\tau_c = R_{on} \cdot C_H = 200 \cdot 1 \times 10^{-9} = 200 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{s} = 200\ \text{ns}$$

Step 2: Apply the acquisition time formula for $n = 10$ bits.

$$t_{AQ} = (n+1) \cdot \ln(2) \cdot \tau_c$$

Step 3: Substitute the values.

$$t_{AQ} = (10+1) \cdot 0.693 \cdot 200 \times 10^{-9}$$

Step 4: Compute the numerical value.

$$t_{AQ} = 11 \cdot 0.693 \cdot 200 \times 10^{-9} = 1524.6 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{s}$$

Step 5: Final answer in microseconds.

$$\boxed{t_{AQ} \approx 1.525\ \mu\text{s}}$$

**Part (b) — Droop in Hold Mode:**

Step 1: Total leakage current.

$$I_{leakage} = I_B + I_{off} = 50\ \text{pA} + 20\ \text{pA} = 70\ \text{pA}$$

Step 2: Apply the droop voltage formula.

$$\Delta V_{droop} = \frac{I_{leakage} \cdot t_H}{C_H}$$

Step 3: Substitute values.

$$\Delta V_{droop} = \frac{70 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 10 \times 10^{-6}}{1 \times 10^{-9}}$$

Step 4: Compute.

$$\Delta V_{droop} = \frac{700 \times 10^{-18}}{1 \times 10^{-9}} = 700 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{V}$$

Step 5: Final answer.

$$\boxed{\Delta V_{droop} = 700\ \text{nV} = 0.7\ \mu\text{V}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validation Check:** For a 10-bit ADC with $V_{FS} = 10\ \text{V}$, $1\ \text{LSB} = \frac{10}{1024} \approx 9.77\ \text{mV}$. The droop of 0.7 µV is **far less than 1 LSB**, confirming the design is highly robust.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Basic Block Diagram of a Sample and Hold Circuit

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Analog Input Vin] --> B[Analog Switch S]
    C[Logic Control Signal] --> B
    B --> D[Hold Capacitor CH]
    D --> E[Buffer Op-Amp]
    E --> F[Output Vout]
    D -.->|Holds voltage| F
```

> **Description:** The analog switch $S$ is closed during SAMPLE mode to let the capacitor track $V_{in}$, and open during HOLD mode to let the capacitor retain its last voltage. The op-amp buffer prevents loading of $C_H$.

## 4.2 Detailed Open-Loop S/H Circuit Schematic (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Vin[Analog Input Vin] --> Sw[Analog Switch with R_on]
    Sw --> CH[Hold Capacitor CH]
    Logic[Control Logic Signal] --> Driver[Switch Driver]
    Driver --> Sw
    CH --> Buf1[Op-Amp Buffer A1]
    Buf1 --> Vout[Output Vout to ADC]
    Vout --> Load[ADC Input]
    CH -.->|Parasitic feedthrough C_gd| Vout
    Buf1 -.->|Bias current leakage| CH
```

> **Description:** This open-loop architecture uses a single op-amp as a voltage follower (unity gain buffer). It is simple but suffers from offset errors and limited accuracy.

## 4.3 Closed-Loop (Improved) S/H Circuit Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    Vin1[Vin] --> Sw1[Switch S1 at Input]
    Sw1 --> CH1[Hold Capacitor CH]
    CH1 --> Amp1[Op-Amp A1 - inverting input]
    Vout1[Vout] --> Rf[Feedback Resistor Rf]
    Rf --> Amp1
    Rin[Resistor Rin] --> Amp1
    Vin1 --> Rin
    Amp1 --> Vout1
    Logic2[Hold Control] --> Sw1
    Logic2 --> Sw2[Compensation Switch S2]
    Sw2 --> CH1
    Vout1 --> ADC2[ADC Front End]
```

> **Description:** The closed-loop S/H integrates the hold capacitor inside the op-amp's feedback loop, providing **virtual ground** at the inverting input. This dramatically reduces switch on-resistance errors and improves linearity.

## 4.4 S/H Circuit State Machine (Timing Diagram Representation)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Sample
    Sample --> Hold : Control goes LOW
    Hold --> Sample : Control goes HIGH
    Sample : Mode: Track Vin
    Sample : Switch: CLOSED
    Sample : Vout = Vin
    Hold : Mode: Retain Vc
    Hold : Switch: OPEN
    Hold : Vout = constant
    Hold : Slow droop in progress
```

## 4.5 Timing Waveform — Input vs Output of S/H

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph TimeAxis[Time Domain Waveforms]
    A1[Vin: Continuous analog ramp] -.-> A2[Continuous smooth curve]
    A3[Control: Square wave] -.-> A4[Pulses of sampling command]
    A5[Vout: Stepped staircase] -.-> A6[Staircase pattern matching samples]
    end
    A1 --- A3
    A3 --- A5
```

> **Description:** The output $V_{out}$ becomes a **staircase reconstruction** of the input, where each "stair" corresponds to a HOLD period. This is the classical behavior of a zero-order-hold (ZOH) system.

## 4.6 Functional Subsystem Interaction Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph InputStage[Input Stage]
    A1[Source Impedance Rs] --> A2[Anti-Alias Filter]
    A2 --> A3[S/H Input Node]
    end
    subgraph CoreStage[S/H Core Stage]
    A3 --> B1[JFET Switch]
    B1 --> B2[Hold Capacitor CH]
    B2 --> B3[Op-Amp Voltage Follower]
    end
    subgraph OutputStage[Output / ADC Stage]
    B3 --> C1[Sample-and-Hold Output]
    C1 --> C2[ADC Input Driver]
    C2 --> C3[Successive Approximation ADC]
    end
    subgraph ControlStage[Control Stage]
    D1[DAS Controller] --> D2[Logic Level Translator]
    D2 --> B1
    end
```

> **Description:** The full S/H subsystem in a typical data acquisition chain, showing interactions with the anti-aliasing filter, control logic, and downstream ADC.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

### **Q1. Define a Sample and Hold circuit. List any two specifications of a Sample and Hold circuit.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A **Sample and Hold (S/H) circuit** is an electronic circuit that captures the instantaneous value of an analog input signal during a specified sampling interval and holds (preserves) that captured voltage constant during the subsequent hold interval, even if the input changes.

**Two Key Specifications:**

1. **Acquisition Time ($t_{AQ}$):** The minimum time required, after the SAMPLE command is issued, for the circuit's output to settle to within a specified accuracy (typically $\pm\frac{1}{2}$ LSB of the connected ADC) of the input voltage value.

2. **Droop Rate ($\frac{dV_C}{dt}$):** The rate at which the held voltage decays during the HOLD mode, primarily due to leakage currents flowing into/out of the hold capacitor. Measured in **V/s** or **µV/µs**.

*(Other valid specifications: Aperture Time, Hold Mode Settling Time, Feedthrough, Hold Capacitor Value.)*

---

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

### **Q2. What is the function of the buffer amplifier in a sample and hold circuit? Why is a voltage follower configuration commonly used?** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Function of the Buffer Amplifier:**

The buffer amplifier in an S/H circuit serves **two critical functions**:

1. **Prevents Loading of the Hold Capacitor:** The op-amp has a very high input impedance (ideally infinite), so it draws negligible current from the hold capacitor $C_H$. This minimizes the droop rate and preserves the held voltage accurately throughout the hold interval.

2. **Provides a Low-Impedance Output:** The op-amp's low output impedance allows it to drive the subsequent stage (e.g., ADC) without affecting the voltage stored on $C_H$.

**Why a Voltage Follower?**

A voltage follower (unity-gain buffer) configuration is used because:
- It provides a **gain of exactly 1** ($V_{out} = V_C$), ensuring the held voltage appears faithfully at the output.
- It offers **maximum bandwidth** since the op-amp is not loaded by feedback components.
- It provides the **simplest, most stable** configuration for high-precision applications.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / KTU Model Paper 2024]`**

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a **closed-loop sample and hold circuit** using an op-amp and a JFET switch. Explain its operation in both SAMPLE and HOLD modes. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** For a 12-bit ADC with full-scale voltage $V_{FS} = 10\ \text{V}$ and a sample-and-hold circuit having $R_{on} = 100\ \Omega$, find the **maximum allowable hold capacitor** $C_H$ such that the acquisition time does not exceed 2 µs. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Circuit Diagram (text representation):**

```
   Vin ----+----------+---> (+) Op-Amp A1
           |          |         |
          [S1]      [S2]        |--> Vout
       (JFET)    (Comp.)       |
           |          |         |
           +---[CH]---+---------+
                 |
                GND
        [Logic Control] ---> Gate of S1
```

**Operation in SAMPLE Mode:**

- The control logic applies a voltage (e.g., $V_{GS} = 0\ \text{V}$) to the JFET gate such that the JFET switch S1 is **ON** (channel conducting).
- The hold capacitor $C_H$ charges through the JFET on-resistance $R_{on}$ to track the input voltage $V_{in}$.
- The closed-loop configuration forces the op-amp output $V_{out}$ to be exactly equal to $V_{in}$ (virtual short principle).
- The compensation switch S2 is also ON during sample mode to maintain charge balance.

**Operation in HOLD Mode:**

- The control logic drives the JFET gate voltage below the pinch-off voltage (e.g., $V_{GS} = -10\ \text{V}$), turning S1 **OFF**.
- The hold capacitor $C_H$ is now **isolated** from the input source.
- Since the op-amp's inverting input draws no current and the compensation switch is OFF, the charge on $C_H$ is preserved.
- The op-amp continues to produce $V_{out} = V_C$ at its output (which equals the held voltage).

**Key Advantage of Closed-Loop:** The hold capacitor is inside the feedback loop, so the effective charging resistance is reduced by the loop gain, providing **faster acquisition** and **better linearity**.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1: Calculate the LSB size for the 12-bit ADC.**

$$\text{LSB} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \frac{10\ \text{V}}{2^{12}} = \frac{10}{4096} = 2.441\ \text{mV}$$

**Step 2: Required settling accuracy is $\pm\frac{1}{2}$ LSB.**

$$V_{accuracy} = \frac{\text{LSB}}{2} = 1.221\ \text{mV}$$

**Step 3: Use the acquisition time formula.**

$$t_{AQ} = (n+1) \cdot \ln(2) \cdot R_{on} \cdot C_H$$

**Step 4: Rearrange to solve for $C_H$.**

$$C_H = \frac{t_{AQ}}{(n+1) \cdot \ln(2) \cdot R_{on}}$$

**Step 5: Substitute the values.**

$$C_H = \frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{(12+1) \cdot 0.693 \cdot 100}$$

**Step 6: Compute the denominator.**

$$13 \cdot 0.693 \cdot 100 = 900.9$$

**Step 7: Compute $C_H$.**

$$C_H = \frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{900.9} = 2.22 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{F}$$

**Step 8: Final Answer.**

$$\boxed{C_H \approx 2.22\ \text{nF}}$$

> **Note:** The **maximum allowable** $C_H$ for a given $t_{AQ}$ constraint is this value; any larger capacitor would violate the 2 µs acquisition time spec.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **basic sample and hold circuit** using a MOSFET switch. Discuss the **sources of error** in this circuit. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A sample-and-hold circuit has a hold capacitor $C_H = 500\ \text{pF}$ and total leakage current $I_{leakage} = 1\ \text{nA}$. Calculate (i) the **droop rate**, and (ii) the **maximum hold time** if the ADC has 10-bit resolution with $V_{FS} = 5\ \text{V}$. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Block Diagram:**

```
   Vin ---> [MOSFET Switch M1] ---> [CH] ---> [Op-Amp Buffer] ---> Vout
                    |
              [Gate Driver]
                    |
            [Control Logic]
```

**Operation:**
- **SAMPLE Mode:** $V_{GS} > V_{th}$ → M1 is ON → $C_H$ charges through $R_{DS(on)}$ → $V_{out} = V_{in}$.
- **HOLD Mode:** $V_{GS} < V_{th}$ → M1 is OFF → $C_H$ retains charge → $V_{out} = V_C$ (constant).

**Sources of Error:**

1. **Aperture Uncertainty:** Delay between the hold command and the actual opening of the switch; causes sampling jitter.
2. **Droop:** Slow discharge of $C_H$ due to leakage currents during hold mode.
3. **Feedthrough:** Unwanted signal coupling through the switch's parasitic gate-drain capacitance $C_{gd}$ even when OFF.
4. **Acquisition Error:** Finite time required for $C_H$ to settle to the new input value.
5. **Offset Error:** Op-amp input offset voltage causes $V_{out} \neq V_C$ by a fixed amount.
6. **Non-linearity:** Variation of $R_{on}$ with $V_{in}$ causes gain non-linearity in sample mode.
7. **Charge Injection:** When MOSFET turns off, the channel charge injects into $C_H$, causing a step error in held voltage.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given Data:**
- $C_H = 500\ \text{pF} = 500 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F}$
- $I_{leakage} = 1\ \text{nA} = 1 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{A}$
- $n = 10$ bits, $V_{FS} = 5\ \text{V}$

**Part (i) — Droop Rate:**

**Step 1:** Apply the droop rate formula.

$$\frac{dV_C}{dt} = \frac{I_{leakage}}{C_H}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$\frac{dV_C}{dt} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-9}}{500 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-9}}{5 \times 10^{-10}} = 2\ \text{V/s}$$

**Step 3: Final Answer.**

$$\boxed{\frac{dV_C}{dt} = 2\ \text{V/s} = 2\ \mu\text{V}/\mu\text{s}}$$

**Part (ii) — Maximum Hold Time:**

**Step 1:** Compute 1 LSB.

$$\text{LSB} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{10}} = \frac{5}{1024} = 4.883\ \text{mV}$$

**Step 2:** The maximum allowable droop is $\frac{1}{2}$ LSB (so that the error is within one quantization level).

$$\Delta V_{max} = \frac{\text{LSB}}{2} = 2.441\ \text{mV}$$

**Step 3:** Use the droop voltage formula and solve for $t_H$.

$$\Delta V = \frac{I_{leakage} \cdot t_H}{C_H} \implies t_H = \frac{\Delta V \cdot C_H}{I_{leakage}}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute values.

$$t_H = \frac{2.441 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 500 \times 10^{-12}}{1 \times 10^{-9}}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the numerator.

$$2.441 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 500 \times 10^{-12} = 1.2205 \times 10^{-12}$$

**Step 6:** Compute $t_H$.

$$t_H = \frac{1.2205 \times 10^{-12}}{1 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.2205 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s}$$

**Step 7: Final Answer.**

$$\boxed{t_H \approx 1.22\ \text{ms}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the half-LSB settling criterion:** Always settle to $\frac{1}{2}$ LSB, not 1 full LSB. Many students lose 1–2 marks for this.
> 2. **Using $\ln(2) \approx 0.5$ instead of $0.693$:** This gives a wrong acquisition time. Use $\ln(2) = 0.693$ accurately.
> 3. **Mixing $n$ and $n+1$ in the acquisition formula:** The standard formula is $(n+1)\ln(2)\tau$. Writing $(n-1)$ or $n$ is a frequent error.
> 4. **Drawing the switch in the wrong location:** In a closed-loop S/H, the switch is in the **feedback path**, not at the input. Wrong placement loses the diagram marks.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention charge injection and feedthrough** in error sources — these are KTU 2024 syllabus highlights.
> 6. **Unit conversion mistakes:** Mixing µs, ns, pF, nF without $10^{-x}$ scaling.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **⚡ RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Sample and Hold Circuit**

- **Core Function:** Sample $V_{in}$ during SAMPLE mode, hold constant during HOLD mode.
- **Three Building Blocks:** Analog switch, Hold capacitor $C_H$, Op-amp buffer (voltage follower).
- **Two Modes:** SAMPLE (switch ON, $V_{out} = V_{in}$) and HOLD (switch OFF, $V_{out} = V_C$).
- **Time Constant:** $\tau_c = R_{on} \cdot C_H$ determines the charging speed in sample mode.
- **Acquisition Time:** $t_{AQ} = (n+1)\ln(2) \cdot R_{on} \cdot C_H$ for an $n$-bit ADC.
- **Droop Rate:** $\frac{dV_C}{dt} = \frac{I_{leakage}}{C_H}$; higher $C_H$ reduces droop but increases $t_{AQ}$.
- **Aperture Time:** Sum of switch transit time + logic delay + jitter.
- **Design Trade-off:** Smaller $C_H$ → fast acquisition but high droop; larger $C_H$ → low droop but slow acquisition.
- **Closed-Loop S/H:** Capacitor inside op-amp feedback loop → faster, more linear, lower $R_{on}$ sensitivity.
- **Open-Loop S/H:** Simpler, but switch $R_{on}$ directly affects acquisition time and accuracy.
- **Error Sources:** Aperture uncertainty, droop, feedthrough, charge injection, offset, non-linearity.
- **Common ICs:** LF398, AD585, AD684, SHC5320, SMP04.
- **Standard Application:** Always the first stage before any high-resolution ADC in a data acquisition system.
- **Voltage Follower:** Unity gain buffer = $A_v = 1$; $Z_{in} = \infty$, $Z_{out} \approx 0$.
- **Hold Capacitor Range:** Typically **100 pF to 10 nF**; uses low-leakage types like **polystyrene, polypropylene, or NP0 ceramic**.
- **Switch Types:** JFET (LF398), MOSFET (ADG-series), CMOS transmission gate; BJT is rarely used due to high $V_{CE(sat)}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ADC - Successive Approximation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT402 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** ADC - Successive Approximation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

A **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) Analog-to-Digital Converter** is a medium-speed, medium-to-high-resolution ADC architecture that employs a binary-search algorithm to convert an analog input voltage into its equivalent digital code. It uses a closed-loop feedback system comprising a **Successive Approximation Register (SAR)**, a **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)**, and a single high-gain **analog comparator**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the KTU 2024 syllabus, the SAR ADC is classified under **Medium Speed / Medium Resolution ADCs** (typically 8 to 16 bits, conversion times in the microsecond range). It is the workhorse of general-purpose data acquisition systems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Balance-Scale Weighing Method

Imagine you are a jeweller trying to determine an unknown weight placed on a **left-pan balance scale**. You have a set of standard weights: **1 g, 2 g, 4 g, 8 g, 16 g …** (each weight is double the previous one — this is exactly binary weighting). You want to find the unknown weight using the **minimum number of weighings**.

- **Weighing 1 (MSB Trial):** You place the largest weight (**8 g**) on the right pan. If the right pan goes down (i.e., 8 g > unknown), you **REMOVE** that weight. If the right pan goes up (8 g < unknown), you **KEEP** it.
- **Weighing 2:** You add the next weight (**4 g**) to whatever remains and test again.
- **Weighing 3, 4, …:** You repeat with **2 g, 1 g** … until the scale balances.

The unknown weight is the **sum of all the weights you KEPT**. This is precisely how a SAR ADC works! Each clock cycle performs one "weighing", the comparator acts as the **scale**, and the SAR acts as the **decision-maker** that retains or rejects each bit.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Vocabulary for KTU Board Exams:**
>
> - **SAR (Successive Approximation Register):** A sequential digital logic block that performs the binary search. It outputs one bit per clock cycle, starting from the MSB.
> - **DAC (Digital-to-Analog Converter):** The feedback element. An n-bit R-2R ladder DAC is most commonly used inside a SAR ADC.
> - **Comparator:** A single high-speed op-amp (open-loop) that compares the analog input $V_{in}$ with the reconstructed analog voltage $V_{DAC}$ from the DAC.
> - **Clock Period ($T_{clk}$):** The period of the external clock. One bit is resolved per clock cycle.
> - **Conversion Time ($T_c$):** The total time required to convert one analog sample. For an n-bit SAR ADC, $T_c = n \cdot T_{clk}$.
> - **Quantization Error ($e_q$):** The maximum inherent error between the actual analog input and the nearest digital code. It is bounded by $\pm \frac{1}{2} \text{LSB}$.
> - **Resolution / Step Size (q):** The smallest analog voltage change distinguishable by the ADC. $q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$, where $V_{FS}$ is the full-scale voltage range.

### 1.4 Why SAR ADC is So Popular in Engineering

| Feature | Practical Benefit |
|---|---|
| **Fixed conversion time** (independent of input) | Simplifies timing and software design in microcontrollers |
| **High resolution (8–18 bits)** | Suitable for industrial sensors, audio, instrumentation |
| **Low power consumption** | Ideal for battery-operated portable devices |
| **Compact monolithic ICs** | Foundational blocks in **ADuC812, ADC0808, ADS1115, MCP3008** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Algorithm (Binary Search Logic)

The SAR ADC does **not** count or integrate. Instead, it **systematically tests each bit position from MSB to LSB**. For an **n-bit** ADC, exactly **n trials** are performed. The decision rule at every trial is:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Comparator Rule (Binary Decision):**
> - If $V_{in} \geq V_{DAC}$, the comparator output is HIGH → the tested bit is **RETAINED as 1**.
> - If $V_{in} < V_{DAC}$, the comparator output is LOW → the tested bit is **RESET to 0**.

The trial sequence for an n-bit SAR is:

- **Trial 1:** Test bit $b_{n-1}$ (MSB) — start with all bits = 0 except MSB.
- **Trial 2:** Test bit $b_{n-2}$ — keep previous decisions, force current bit to 1.
- …
- **Trial n:** Test bit $b_0$ (LSB) — final decision completes the code.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Step Size (Resolution) | $q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$ | Volts per LSB |
| Full-Scale Range | $V_{FS} = V_{ref}$ (typically) | Volts |
| Digital Output Code | $D = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} b_i \cdot 2^i$ | Decimal equivalent |
| Reconstructed DAC Voltage | $V_{DAC} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n} \cdot D$ | Volts |
| Conversion Time | $T_c = n \cdot T_{clk}$ | Seconds |
| Throughput / Sampling Rate | $f_s \leq \dfrac{1}{n \cdot T_{clk}}$ | Samples per second |
| Maximum Quantization Error | $\vert e_q \vert_{max} = \dfrac{q}{2} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}}$ | Volts |
| SNR (Ideal, Quantization Noise) | $\text{SNR} = 6.02 n + 1.76$ | dB |
| Power Dissipation (typical) | $P_d$ ranges from **µW to mW** | Scales with $f_s$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** Always remember the "**6.02 dB per bit + 1.76 dB**" SNR rule. KTU frequently tests this for SAR and other Nyquist-rate ADCs.

### 2.3 Architectural Breakdown

A complete SAR ADC system consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) Circuit:** Latches the rapidly changing analog input to a stable DC voltage during the conversion window. Without this, $V_{in}$ could change mid-conversion and corrupt the bits.
2. **Voltage Comparator:** A high-gain, open-loop op-amp. Its output is a single bit: HIGH if $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$, LOW otherwise.
3. **SAR Logic Block:** A sequential state machine (often implemented with shift registers and latches). It forces one bit at a time, latches the comparator decision, and moves to the next bit.
4. **n-bit DAC (R-2R Ladder):** Converts the running digital approximation back into an analog voltage for comparison.
5. **Clock & Control Circuitry:** Provides timing pulses and the SOC (Start of Conversion) / EOC (End of Conversion) handshake signals.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where SAR ADCs Are Used in Industry:**
>
> - **Microcontrollers:** STM32, MSP430, PIC, Arduino (via ADS1115) — the dominant ADC type.
> - **Data Acquisition Systems (DAQ):** Multi-channel industrial sensors.
> - **Digital Multimeters (DMMs):** High-accuracy handheld meters.
> - **Audio Codecs:** Up to 24-bit SAR ADCs (e.g., TI PCM1808) for music recording.
> - **Medical Instrumentation:** Patient monitoring (ECG, EEG) where moderate bandwidth and high resolution are required.
> - **Automotive Sensors:** Battery monitoring, temperature, pressure sensing.

SAR ADCs strike the ideal balance between **speed, resolution, and power**, which is why they dominate the entire microcontroller ecosystem.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Example

### 3.1 Derivation of the Reconstructed DAC Voltage

The n-bit digital code stored in the SAR after conversion is:

$$
D = b_{n-1} 2^{n-1} + b_{n-2} 2^{n-2} + \cdots + b_1 2^1 + b_0 2^0
$$

The DAC's job is to convert this digital code back into an analog voltage proportional to its binary-weighted value. The reconstructed analog voltage from the DAC is:

$$
V_{DAC} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} \cdot D = V_{ref} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \frac{b_i}{2^{n-i}}
$$

For example, for an n = 3 bit DAC with $V_{ref} = 8\text{ V}$:

| Code ($b_2 b_1 b_0$) | $D$ (decimal) | $V_{DAC}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 000 | 0 | 0 V |
| 001 | 1 | 1 V |
| 010 | 2 | 2 V |
| 011 | 3 | 3 V |
| 100 | 4 | 4 V |
| 101 | 5 | 5 V |
| 110 | 6 | 6 V |
| 111 | 7 | 7 V |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Conversion Algorithm (Flowchart Logic)

The SAR works in exactly **n** clock cycles. The general flow at clock cycle **k** (where k = 1, 2, …, n) is:

- **Step 1:** SAR sets the test bit $b_{n-k}$ to 1 (initially assuming this bit belongs in the answer).
- **Step 2:** The DAC converts the current register contents into $V_{DAC}$.
- **Step 3:** The comparator compares $V_{in}$ with $V_{DAC}$.
- **Step 4 (Decision):**
   - If comparator output = HIGH (i.e., $V_{in} \geq V_{DAC}$), **keep** $b_{n-k} = 1$.
   - If comparator output = LOW (i.e., $V_{in} < V_{DAC}$), **reset** $b_{n-k} = 0$.
- **Step 5:** Move to the next bit and repeat from Step 1 until all n bits are decided.
- **Step 6:** Raise the **EOC (End of Conversion)** flag. The final n-bit word is now stable at the output.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example — 4-bit SAR ADC

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A 4-bit SAR ADC has a reference voltage $V_{ref} = 10\text{ V}$. Convert an analog input voltage $V_{in} = 6.8\text{ V}$ into its equivalent digital output code. Also compute the quantization error.

#### Solution Setup

- Number of bits: $n = 4$
- Reference voltage: $V_{ref} = 10\text{ V}$
- Step size: $q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \dfrac{10}{2^4} = \dfrac{10}{16} = 0.625\text{ V/LSB}$

**Initial state:** SAR register = `0000`

---

#### Clock Cycle 1 — Testing the MSB ($b_3$)

- SAR forces $b_3 = 1$ → Register becomes `1000` → Decimal D = 8.
- DAC output:

$$
V_{DAC} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} \cdot D = \frac{10}{16} \cdot 8 = 5.000\text{ V}
$$

- Comparator test: $V_{in} = 6.8\text{ V}$ vs $V_{DAC} = 5.0\text{ V}$.
- Since $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$, comparator output = HIGH.
- **Decision: KEEP $b_3 = 1$.** Register remains `1000`.

---

#### Clock Cycle 2 — Testing $b_2$

- SAR forces $b_2 = 1$ → Register becomes `1100` → Decimal D = 12.
- DAC output:

$$
V_{DAC} = \frac{10}{16} \cdot 12 = 7.500\text{ V}
$$

- Comparator test: $V_{in} = 6.8\text{ V}$ vs $V_{DAC} = 7.5\text{ V}$.
- Since $V_{in} < V_{DAC}$, comparator output = LOW.
- **Decision: RESET $b_2 = 0$.** Register returns to `1000`.

---

#### Clock Cycle 3 — Testing $b_1$

- SAR forces $b_1 = 1$ → Register becomes `1010` → Decimal D = 10.
- DAC output:

$$
V_{DAC} = \frac{10}{16} \cdot 10 = 6.250\text{ V}
$$

- Comparator test: $V_{in} = 6.8\text{ V}$ vs $V_{DAC} = 6.25\text{ V}$.
- Since $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$, comparator output = HIGH.
- **Decision: KEEP $b_1 = 1$.** Register remains `1010`.

---

#### Clock Cycle 4 — Testing the LSB ($b_0$)

- SAR forces $b_0 = 1$ → Register becomes `1011` → Decimal D = 11.
- DAC output:

$$
V_{DAC} = \frac{10}{16} \cdot 11 = 6.875\text{ V}
$$

- Comparator test: $V_{in} = 6.8\text{ V}$ vs $V_{DAC} = 6.875\text{ V}$.
- Since $V_{in} < V_{DAC}$, comparator output = LOW.
- **Decision: RESET $b_0 = 0$.** Register returns to `1010`.

---

#### Final Results

- **Digital output code:** `1010`
- **Decimal equivalent:** $D = 10$
- **Reconstructed analog voltage:**

$$
V_{out} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} \cdot D = \frac{10}{16} \cdot 10 = 6.250\text{ V}
$$

- **Quantization error:**

$$
e_q = V_{in} - V_{out} = 6.8 - 6.25 = +0.550\text{ V}
$$

- **Theoretical maximum allowable error:** $\dfrac{q}{2} = \dfrac{0.625}{2} = 0.3125\text{ V}$

Since $e_q = 0.550\text{ V}$ exceeds $\dfrac{q}{2}$, this $V_{in}$ would be represented by the next code `1011` only if $V_{in} \geq 6.875\text{ V}$. Because $V_{in} = 6.8\text{ V}$ falls in the range $[6.25\text{ V},\, 6.875\text{ V})$, the SAR correctly assigns it to `1010`. The remaining error $0.550\text{ V}$ is the **inherent quantization error** and cannot be removed.

---

### 3.4 Conversion Time Calculation

If the external clock frequency is $f_{clk} = 1\text{ MHz}$ (i.e., $T_{clk} = 1\text{ µs}$):

$$
T_c = n \cdot T_{clk} = 4 \cdot 1\text{ µs} = 4\text{ µs}
$$

Maximum sampling rate:

$$
f_s = \frac{1}{T_c} = \frac{1}{4\text{ µs}} = 250\text{ kS/s (kilosamples/second)}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 SAR ADC Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **signal flow and feedback interactions** between every functional block of a SAR ADC. Each clock cycle, the SAR pushes a new candidate code into the DAC; the DAC's analog output is compared against the held input sample; the comparator's decision latches the bit back into the SAR — closing the loop.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Analog Input Vin] --> SH[Sample and Hold Amplifier]
    SH --> CMP_NEG[Comparator Negative Input]
    CLK[External Clock fclk] --> SAR[Successive Approximation Register SAR Logic]
    SOC[Start of Conversion SOC Pulse] --> SAR
    SAR -->|Drives n bit code| DAC[n bit DAC R 2R Ladder]
    DAC --> DACV[DAC Output Voltage VDAC]
    DACV --> CMP_POS[Comparator Positive Input]
    CMP_NEG --> COMP[High Speed Analog Comparator]
    CMP_POS --> COMP
    COMP -->|Decision bit HIGH or LOW| SAR
    SAR -->|Latched n bit digital word| OUT[Parallel Digital Output b3 b2 b1 b0]
    SAR -->|Conversion complete flag| EOC[End of Conversion EOC Signal]
```

### 4.2 SAR Decision Sequence — Bit-by-Bit Trial Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST0[Start Reset SAR to 0000] --> ST1{Cycle 1 Test MSB b3 equals 1}
    ST1 -->|Vin greater VDAC Keep| C1A[Register equals 1000]
    ST1 -->|Vin less VDAC Reset| C1B[Register equals 0000]
    C1A --> ST2{Cycle 2 Test b2 equals 1}
    C1B --> ST2
    ST2 -->|Vin greater VDAC Keep| C2A[Register equals 1100]
    ST2 -->|Vin less VDAC Reset| C2B[Register equals 1000]
    C2A --> ST3{Cycle 3 Test b1 equals 1}
    C2B --> ST3
    C3A[Register equals 1110] --> ST4{Cycle 4 Test LSB b0 equals 1}
    C3B[Register equals 1100] --> ST4
```
