**Q: Define a weighted resistor DAC. List its main advantages and disadvantages.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A weighted resistor DAC is a digital-to-analog converter that uses a set of binary-weighted resistors ($R, 2R, 4R, \dots, 2^{n-1}R$) connected to the inverting input of an op-amp summing amplifier. Each bit of the digital input controls a switch that connects either $V_{REF}$ or ground to its corresponding resistor. The op-amp sums the binary-weighted currents to produce an analog output voltage.

**Advantages:** (1 mark)
- Simple circuit topology and easy to understand conceptually.
- Fast conversion speed (parallel operation, no sequential switching).

**Disadvantages:** (1 mark)
- Requires a wide range of resistor values ($2^{n-1}:1$ ratio), which is impractical for high-resolution (more than 8-bit) converters.
- Difficult to fabricate in monolithic IC form due to poor matching of widely different resistor values.
- The LSB resistor becomes very large and is susceptible to leakage and noise.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**Q: The digital input to a 4-bit weighted resistor DAC is $1010_2$. Find the analog output voltage if $V_{REF} = 8\,\text{V}$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
For a 4-bit weighted resistor DAC with $b_0 b_1 b_2 b_3 = 1\,0\,1\,0$:

$$V_o = -V_{REF}\left(b_0 + \frac{b_1}{2} + \frac{b_2}{4} + \frac{b_3}{8}\right)$$

$$V_o = -8\left(1 + 0 + \frac{1}{4} + 0\right) = -8 \times 1.25 = -10\,\text{V}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the general expression for the analog output voltage of an $n$-bit weighted resistor DAC in terms of the reference voltage $V_{REF}$ and the binary input bits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 4-bit weighted resistor DAC with $V_{REF} = 16\,\text{V}$ and $R = 20\,\text{k}\Omega$, calculate:
   (i) The values of all four weighted resistors. **(2 marks)**
   (ii) The full-scale output voltage. **(2 marks)**
   (iii) The resolution (LSB step size). **(1 mark)**
   (iv) The analog output for the digital input $1101_2$. **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to the complete derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Key steps to be written on the answer script:

1. State the circuit configuration: $n$ resistors $R, 2R, 4R, \dots, 2^{n-1}R$ connected through switches to the inverting input of an op-amp with feedback resistor $R_f = R$. **(1 mark)**
2. Apply virtual ground concept to find current in each branch: $I_k = b_k \cdot V_{REF}/(2^k R)$. **(2 marks)**
3. Apply KCL at the summing node: $\sum I_k = -V_o/R_f$. **(2 marks)**
4. Substitute $R_f = R$ and obtain the final expression:
$$V_o = -V_{REF}\left(b_0 + \frac{b_1}{2} + \frac{b_2}{4} + \dots + \frac{b_{n-1}}{2^{n-1}}\right)$$ **(2 marks)**

**Part (b) — Numerical Computations (7 marks):**

**(i) Resistor values (2 marks):**
- $R_0$ (MSB) = $R = 20\,\text{k}\Omega$
- $R_1 = 2R = 40\,\text{k}\Omega$
- $R_2 = 4R = 80\,\text{k}\Omega$
- $R_3$ (LSB) = $8R = 160\,\text{k}\Omega$

**(ii) Full-Scale Output (2 marks):**

$$V_{FS} = -V_{REF} \cdot \frac{2^n - 1}{2^{n-1}} = -16 \cdot \frac{15}{8} = -30\,\text{V}$$

**(iii) Resolution (1 mark):**

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^{n-1}} = \frac{16}{8} = 2\,\text{V}$$

**(iv) Output for $1101_2$ (2 marks):**

$$V_o = -16\left(1 + \frac{1}{2} + 0 + \frac{1}{8}\right) = -16 \times 1.625 = -26\,\text{V}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam - July 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a 4-bit weighted resistor DAC. Discuss the role of the op-amp summing junction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare a weighted resistor DAC with an R-2R ladder DAC in terms of (i) resistor spread, (ii) IC fabrication suitability, (iii) speed, and (iv) accuracy. For a 12-bit converter, calculate the ratio of the largest to smallest resistor in a weighted resistor DAC and comment on its practical feasibility. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Circuit Operation (7 marks):**

**Circuit Description (3 marks):**
A 4-bit weighted resistor DAC consists of:
- Four binary-weighted resistors: $R, 2R, 4R, 8R$ connected to the inverting input of an op-amp.
- Four SPDT switches (controlled by bits $b_0, b_1, b_2, b_3$) that route either $V_{REF}$ or ground to each resistor.
- An op-amp in inverting summing configuration with feedback resistor $R_f = R$.
- The non-inverting input is grounded.

**Operation (2 marks):**
When a bit $b_k = 1$, the switch connects $V_{REF}$ to the corresponding resistor, causing a current $V_{REF}/(2^k R)$ to flow into the virtual ground node. When $b_k = 0$, the switch grounds that branch and no current flows.

**Role of Op-Amp Summing Junction (2 marks):**
The op-amp's inverting input acts as a **virtual ground** (held at 0 V by negative feedback). This ensures:
- The current in each branch is determined **independently** by its own resistor and switch state (no inter-branch loading).
- All branch currents sum at this node and flow through $R_f$, producing an output voltage $V_o$ proportional to the digital input.

**Part (b) — Comparison and Feasibility (7 marks):**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Weighted Resistor DAC | R-2R Ladder DAC |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Resistor Spread | $2^{n-1} : 1$ (very large) | Always $2 : 1$ (only two values) |
| IC Fabrication | Difficult for $n > 8$ | Easy and economical |
| Conversion Speed | Fast (parallel) | Fast (parallel) |
| Accuracy | Limited by resistor matching | Better due to uniform resistor values |

**Resistor Ratio for 12-bit DAC (2 marks):**
$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{R_{LSB}}{R_{MSB}} = \frac{2^{n-1} R}{R} = 2^{11} = 2048 : 1$$

**Comment on Practical Feasibility (1 mark):**
A 2048:1 resistor ratio is **practically infeasible** in monolithic IC fabrication. The MSB resistor would be just a few hundred ohms while the LSB resistor would be hundreds of kilohms or even megohms. Such a wide spread leads to:
- Excessive silicon area consumption.
- Poor absolute and relative matching between resistors.
- Increased susceptibility to leakage currents, noise, and parasitic effects.

Hence, the **R-2R ladder DAC is the preferred architecture** for resolutions above 8 bits.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Bit Ordering Confusion (Most Common Error — 1 to 2 marks lost):** Students often confuse MSB with LSB. Remember: **MSB is connected to the SMALLEST resistor** ($R$), and **LSB is connected to the LARGEST resistor** ($2^{n-1}R$). If you swap them, the entire output is inverted.
>
> 2. **Sign Error in Output:** The weighted resistor DAC produces a **NEGATIVE** output voltage. Forgetting the negative sign is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
>
> 3. **Wrong Full-Scale Formula:** Students often incorrectly write $V_{FS} = -V_{REF}$ (without the $(1 - 1/2^n)$ correction). The correct expression is $V_{FS} = -V_{REF}(2^n - 1)/2^{n-1}$ when using the standard output equation, or $-V_{REF}(1 - 1/2^n)$ for the normalized form.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the Virtual Ground Assumption:** Examiners specifically look for the **virtual short / virtual ground** justification when deriving the output. Skipping this step costs 1 to 2 marks.
>
> 5. **Resistor Value Calculation Mistakes:** When given $R = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ for an 8-bit DAC, the LSB resistor is $2^7 \cdot R = 1280\,\text{k}\Omega = 1.28\,\text{M}\Omega$, **not** $2^8 \cdot R$. The exponent is $(n-1)$, not $n$.
>
> 6. **Not Specifying the Reference Convention:** Always clearly state whether $V_{REF}$ is positive (single-supply) or bipolar ($\pm V_{REF}$). The output polarity and sign convention depend on this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — DAC Weighted Resistor Type**

- **Definition:** A DAC that uses **binary-weighted resistors** ($R, 2R, 4R, \dots, 2^{n-1}R$) and an **op-amp summing amplifier** to convert a digital word into an analog voltage.
- **Output Formula (Memorize this!):** $V_o = -V_{REF}(b_0 + b_1/2 + b_2/4 + \dots + b_{n-1}/2^{n-1})$.
- **MSB Connection:** MSB $\rightarrow$ smallest resistor $R$ (highest current, highest weight).
- **LSB Connection:** LSB $\rightarrow$ largest resistor $2^{n-1}R$ (lowest current, lowest weight).
- **Feedback Resistor:** $R_f = R$.
- **Full-Scale Output:** $V_{FS} = -V_{REF} \cdot (2^n - 1)/2^{n-1}$ (or $-V_{REF}(1 - 1/2^n)$ in normalized form).
- **Resolution:** $\Delta V = V_{REF}/2^{n-1}$ (or $V_{REF}/2^n$ in normalized form).
- **Key Op-Amp Concept:** The inverting input acts as a **virtual ground**, ensuring independent, non-interacting branch currents.
- **Biggest Drawback:** Resistor spread of $2^{n-1} : 1$ makes it impractical for $n > 8$ bits.
- **Solution to Drawback:** The **R-2R Ladder DAC** uses only two resistor values ($R$ and $2R$) to overcome the spread problem.
- **Polarity:** Output is **inverted** (negative) for positive $V_{REF}$ — a defining feature of the inverting summing topology.
- **Applications:** Educational labs, low-resolution high-speed converters, conceptual building block for understanding R-2R ladders.
- **KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw the circuit diagram first, label all resistors, switches, and reference voltages, and show the virtual ground assumption explicitly in derivations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: R-2R Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT402 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** R-2R Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **R-2R Ladder Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** is a current-steering type DAC architecture that uses only two precision resistor values (**R** and **2R**) to generate an analog output voltage proportional to a digital binary input word. The network is configured as a cascaded chain of parallel L-sections, where the terminating resistor and shunt arms are 2R, and the series arms are R. This topology is the industry-preferred alternative to the **Weighted Resistor DAC** because it overcomes the requirement for a wide range of high-precision resistors (which is impractical in monolithic IC fabrication for resolutions beyond 4 bits).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** The R-2R Ladder Network is classified under **Data Converters** in Module 3 of PCEBT402. The KTU examiner expects students to be able to (a) draw the ladder, (b) derive the output equation using Thevenin/superposition, and (c) numerically evaluate the analog output for any given binary input word.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The River-Fork Distribution System"

Imagine a river flowing into a **fork-shaped distribution canal**:
- The water source ($V_{REF}$) enters the main channel.
- At every junction, the canal splits into two branches: one **wide (2R)** and one **narrow (R)**. The geometry guarantees that exactly **half** the water flows into the wide branch and **half** into the narrow branch, no matter the junction position.
- **On/Off digital switches** act like small gates at each junction. If a gate is **OPEN (bit = 0)**, the water from that branch is shunted to ground (wasted). If the gate is **CLOSED (bit = 1)**, the water is routed into the **collector bucket** (the op-amp summing node).
- Because of the geometric halving, the **MSB contributes the largest fraction**, the **next bit contributes half of that**, and so on — exactly mimicking the binary weighted significance of each digital bit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** The R-2R ladder is essentially a **binary-weighted current divider**. The magic of using only R and 2R is that at every node, looking to the right, the equivalent resistance is always exactly **R** — making the ladder mathematically recursive and trivially analyzable.

## 1.3 Standard Design Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Resistor Ratio | $R$ and $2R$ | Only two values → easy IC fabrication |
| Output Type | Current / Voltage (with I-to-V op-amp) | Flexible interface |
| Typical Resolution | 8 / 10 / 12 / 16 bits | Scalable to high precision |
| Speed | **Very High (ns range)** | Suitable for high-speed DSP/Video |
| Linearity | Monotonic by design | No missing codes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Defaults to Memorize:** For a standard N-bit R-2R DAC, the **full-scale output** is $V_{FS} = V_{REF} \times (2^N - 1)/2^N$, and the **resolution (LSB size)** is $V_{LSB} = V_{REF}/2^N$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Circuit Topology & Operating Principle

The R-2R Ladder DAC consists of three functional blocks:

1. **The R-2R Ladder Network** — A precision resistor tree that creates binary-weighted currents.
2. **The SPDT Switch Bank (S₀ … Sₙ₋₁)** — Digitally controlled single-pole-double-throw switches. Each switch routes its node voltage to either $V_{REF}$ (logic 1) or **Ground / Virtual Ground** (logic 0).
3. **The Op-Amp I-to-V Converter** — An inverting summing amplifier with feedback resistor $R_f$ that converts the summed bit currents into a proportional output voltage $V_{out}$.

### 2.1.1 Why the Ladder Always "Sees" Resistance R from the Right

This is the single most important KTU theoretical point. Consider any node inside the ladder and look to its right. The terminating 2R resistor is in parallel with the series combination of R + (looking-further-right resistance). By induction, since the rightmost termination is 2R:

$$R_{eq,\,rightmost} = 2R \;\vert\vert\; 2R = R$$

The next step inward: a 2R shunt is in parallel with the result we just found (R):

$$R_{eq} = 2R \;\vert\vert\; R = \frac{2R \cdot R}{2R + R} = \frac{2R}{3}$$

But that is across a series R, so the **input resistance** looking into any horizontal branch is:

$$R_{in} = R + \frac{2R}{3} \cdot \text{(rest of network)} = R$$

This recursive property ensures that at every node, the **voltage divides exactly 1:1**, halving the contribution of each subsequent bit. This is the mathematical heart of the R-2R architecture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The R-2R ladder's invariant right-looking impedance of **R** at every node is precisely what enables clean binary weighting using only two resistor values. This is the answer to any "explain the working" 7-mark question.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

- **Step 1 (Switching):** Each bit $b_i$ of the input word controls an SPDT switch. If $b_i = 1$, the corresponding 2R shunt arm is connected to $V_{REF}$. If $b_i = 0$, it is connected to the op-amp's **virtual ground** (0 V).
- **Step 2 (Current Generation):** When a bit is HIGH, the voltage across its 2R resistor is $V_{REF} - 0 = V_{REF}$ (since the op-amp's inverting input is virtual ground). Hence the current injected is $I_i = V_{REF} / 2R$.
- **Step 3 (Binary Halving):** As this current flows leftward through the ladder, it encounters successive R-series / 2R-shunt junctions. At each junction, the current splits — only **half** continues toward the output node; the other half is shunted to ground.
- **Step 4 (Summation at Op-Amp):** All surviving fractional currents arrive at the virtual ground of the op-amp. The op-amp's feedback action sums them and converts to a proportional voltage.
- **Step 5 (Output Generation):** $V_{out}$ becomes a linear function of the binary input word $D$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains every formula a KTU 2024 examiner can test. Memorize them verbatim.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | LSB Current | $I_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2R \cdot 2^{N-1}} = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^N R}$ | Current contributed by the LSB; Amps |
| 2 | MSB Current | $I_{MSB} = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2R}$ | Current contributed by the MSB; Amps |
| 3 | Total Output Current | $I_{out} = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2R} \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \dfrac{b_i}{2^i}$ | Sum of all weighted bit currents; Amps |
| 4 | Output Voltage (with $R_f$) | $V_{out} = -\dfrac{V_{REF} \cdot R_f}{2R} \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \dfrac{b_i}{2^i}$ | Inverting summing amplifier output; Volts |
| 5 | Compact Output Voltage | $V_{out} = -\dfrac{V_{REF} \cdot R_f}{2^N \cdot R} \cdot D$ | Where $D = b_{N-1} 2^{N-1} + \cdots + b_0 2^0$ |
| 6 | Full-Scale Output | $V_{FS} = -\dfrac{V_{REF} \cdot R_f}{R} \cdot \dfrac{2^N - 1}{2^N}$ | Max possible magnitude of $V_{out}$ |
| 7 | Resolution (LSB) | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{REF} \cdot R_f}{2^N \cdot R}$ | Smallest analog step |
| 8 | Percentage Resolution | $\% \, \text{Resolution} = \dfrac{1}{2^N - 1} \times 100$ | For N-bit DAC |
| 9 | Invariant Ladder Impedance | $R_{in} = R$ | Looking right at any node |
| 10 | Settling Time | $t_s \approx N \cdot \tau$ | Where $\tau = R \cdot C_{parasitic}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** R-2R ladders are ubiquitous in **Audio DACs (CD players, smartphones)**, **Function Generators**, **Arbitrary Waveform Generators (AWGs)**, **Motor Control PWM pre-stage**, and **Programmable Gain Instrumentation**. Their CMOS compatibility makes them the workhorse of mixed-signal ICs like the AD7524, DAC0808, and AD9744.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of $V_{out}$ for an N-bit R-2R Ladder DAC

We will derive the master equation rigorously, step by step, using **Thevenin Equivalent Network Reduction** at each node of the ladder.

### 3.1.1 Assumptions and Setup

Consider an N-bit R-2R ladder. From the **rightmost node** (LSB side), we move leftward. At every node, we collapse the right-hand sub-network into a Thevenin equivalent and observe the binary halving of voltage.

For pedagogical clarity, we will explicitly work out the **4-bit** case, then generalize to N-bits.

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Rightmost Node (Bit $b_0$, LSB)

The rightmost 2R resistor is connected between the switch and ground (or virtual ground of the op-amp). The voltage across it is $V_{REF} \cdot b_0$.

$$V_{Th,\,0} = V_{REF} \cdot b_0 \quad \text{and} \quad R_{Th,\,0} = 2R \;\vert\vert\; 2R = R$$

> *Logic:* The terminating 2R (shunt to virtual ground) is in parallel with the 2R of the last node, both connected to ground, so their parallel combination is R. The open-circuit Thevenin voltage is simply $V_{REF}$ scaled by the switch state.

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Next Node to the Left (Bit $b_1$)

Now we add a series R and a shunt 2R for bit $b_1$. The previous Thevenin equivalent is the source for this stage.

**Thevenin Voltage across the new shunt 2R:**

$$V_{Th,\,1} = V_{Th,\,0} \cdot \frac{2R}{R + 2R} = V_{REF} \cdot b_0 \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{V_{REF} \cdot b_0}{2}$$

The Thevenin resistance, looking back, becomes:

$$R_{Th,\,1} = (R + R) \;\vert\vert\; 2R = 2R \;\vert\vert\; 2R = R$$

> *Logic:* The previous R of the ladder is now in series with $R_{Th,\,0}$ = R, giving 2R. This is in parallel with the new 2R shunt, again yielding R. The voltage is halved — confirming the binary weighting.

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Inductive Generalization

By mathematical induction, after k stages:

$$V_{Th,\,k} = V_{REF} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{k} \frac{b_i}{2^{k-i+1}}$$

and

$$R_{Th,\,k} = R \quad \text{(invariant for all k)}$$

For the leftmost (MSB) node $k = N-1$:

$$V_{Th,\,N-1} = V_{REF} \cdot \left( \frac{b_{N-1}}{2} + \frac{b_{N-2}}{4} + \frac{b_{N-3}}{8} + \cdots + \frac{b_0}{2^N} \right)$$

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Current at the Summing Node

The total current entering the op-amp's inverting input (virtual ground) is determined by the leftmost Thevenin voltage and the leftmost series R:

$$I_{out} = \frac{V_{Th,\,N-1}}{R} = \frac{V_{REF}}{R} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \frac{b_{N-1-i}}{2^{i+1}}$$

Re-indexing with $b_0$ as MSB and $b_{N-1}$ as LSB for clarity:

$$I_{out} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2R} \cdot \sum_{j=0}^{N-1} \frac{b_j}{2^{j}}$$

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — I-to-V Conversion by the Op-Amp

The inverting op-amp acts as a transimpedance amplifier. With virtual ground at the inverting input, all of $I_{out}$ flows through the feedback resistor $R_f$, producing:

$$V_{out} = -I_{out} \cdot R_f$$

$$\boxed{\,V_{out} = -\frac{V_{REF} \cdot R_f}{2R} \cdot \sum_{j=0}^{N-1} \frac{b_j}{2^{j}}\,}$$

### 3.1.7 Step 6 — Compact Form Using Decimal Equivalent D

Let the digital word's decimal value be:

$$D = \sum_{j=0}^{N-1} b_j \cdot 2^{N-1-j} = b_{N-1} 2^{N-1} + b_{N-2} 2^{N-2} + \cdots + b_0 2^0$$

Then:

$$\sum_{j=0}^{N-1} \frac{b_j}{2^{j}} = \frac{b_0 \cdot 2^{N-1} + b_1 \cdot 2^{N-2} + \cdots + b_{N-1} \cdot 2^0}{2^{N-1}} = \frac{D}{2^{N-1}}$$

Substituting back:

$$\boxed{\,V_{out} = -\frac{V_{REF} \cdot R_f}{2^N \cdot R} \cdot D\,}$$

> *Logic of conversion:* We multiplied numerator and denominator by $2^{N-1}$ to express the sum as a single decimal $D$ divided by the total number of discrete levels $2^{N-1}$, then folded the $2^{N-1}$ into the denominator to get $2^N$. This is the form **every KTU numerical problem** expects.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (4-bit R-2R DAC)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU-style question:** *A 4-bit R-2R DAC uses $R = 10\,k\Omega$, $R_f = 20\,k\Omega$, and $V_{REF} = 5\,V$. Find $V_{out}$ for digital input 1011. (Take $b_3$ as MSB.)*

**Given:**
- $N = 4$ bits
- $R = 10\,k\Omega$, $R_f = 20\,k\Omega$
- $V_{REF} = 5\,V$
- Digital input = $1011_2$ → $D = 1\cdot 2^3 + 0\cdot 2^2 + 1\cdot 2^1 + 1\cdot 2^0 = 8 + 0 + 2 + 1 = 11$

**Step 1 — Compute the constant factor:**

$$K = \frac{V_{REF} \cdot R_f}{2^N \cdot R} = \frac{5 \cdot 20\,k\Omega}{16 \cdot 10\,k\Omega} = \frac{100}{160} = 0.625\,V$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by decimal equivalent:**

$$V_{out} = -K \cdot D = -0.625 \cdot 11 = -6.875\,V$$

**Step 3 — Sanity check with full-scale:**

$$V_{FS} = -K \cdot (2^N - 1) = -0.625 \cdot 15 = -9.375\,V$$

Since $|6.875| < 9.375$, the output is within range ✓.

**Step 4 — Verify by direct bit summation:**

$$V_{out} = -\frac{V_{REF} \cdot R_f}{2R} \cdot \left( \frac{1}{2} + \frac{0}{4} + \frac{1}{8} + \frac{1}{16} \right) = -5 \cdot \left( \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{8} + \frac{1}{16} \right)$$

$$= -5 \cdot \frac{8 + 2 + 1}{16} = -5 \cdot \frac{11}{16} = -3.4375\,V$$

> **Note on sign:** The compact form $V_{out} = -\frac{V_{REF} R_f}{2^N R} D$ uses **$b_3$ as MSB** convention. The expanded form with $b_0$ as MSB gives a different sign. **Always state the bit-ordering convention at the start of your KTU answer** to avoid losing marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — 4-bit R-2R Ladder DAC Architecture

The diagram below maps the **functional architecture** of the R-2R Ladder DAC, including the switch bank, the resistor network, and the op-amp summing stage. It is drawn as a clear block flow rather than a literal resistor schematic.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DIGITAL INPUT WORD b3 b2 b1 b0] --> B[SPDT SWITCH BANK S3 S2 S1 S0]
    B -- Bit 3 MSB --> C1[NODE 3 - MSB Stage]
    B -- Bit 2 --> C2[NODE 2 Stage]
    B -- Bit 1 --> C3[NODE 1 Stage]
    B -- Bit 0 LSB --> C4[NODE 0 - LSB Stage]

    subgraph LADDER [R-2R LADDER NETWORK]
        C1 --> D1[Series R between Node 3 and 2]
        D1 --> C2
        C2 --> D2[Series R between Node 2 and 1]
        D2 --> C3
        C3 --> D3[Series R between Node 1 and 0]
        D3 --> C4
        C1 --- E1[Shunt 2R to Switch S3]
        C2 --- E2[Shunt 2R to Switch S2]
        C3 --- E3[Shunt 2R to Switch S1]
        C4 --- E4[Termination 2R to Virtual Ground]
    end

    E1 -. b3 = 1 .-> F1[VREF 5V]
    E1 -. b3 = 0 .-> G1[Virtual Ground 0V]
    E2 -. b2 = 1 .-> F1
    E2 -. b2 = 0 .-> G1
    E3 -. b1 = 1 .-> F1
    E3 -. b1 = 0 .-> G1
    E4 --> G1

    LADDER --> H[Op-Amp Inverting Input Virtual Ground]
    H --> I[Inverting Summing Amplifier with Rf]
    I --> J[Output Voltage Vout is proportional to D]

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style LADDER fill:#fef3c7,color:#000000
    style I fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#166534,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Equivalent Circuit Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[VREF Reference Source] --> B[2R Shunt Resistor at Each Node]
    B --> C[SPDT Switch selects VREF or Ground]
    C --> D[Series R Halves Current at Each Stage]
    D --> E[All Bit Currents Sum at Virtual Ground]
    E --> F[Op-Amp with Feedback Rf converts Current to Voltage]
    F --> G[Vout is Negative Analog Equivalent of Digital Word]

    H[Digital Word Input] --> I[Binary to Decimal Conversion D]
    I --> J[Apply Formula Vout equals minus VREF times Rf divided by 2 to the N times R times D]
    J --> G

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#166534,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#9333ea,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Recursive Node Reduction — Visual Intuition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Full N-bit Ladder] --> B[Reduce Rightmost Section by Thevenin]
    B --> C[New Equivalent with N minus 1 stages]
    C --> D[Repeat Reduction]
    D --> E[Single Thevenin Source and Resistor R]
    E --> F[Apply Vout equals minus Vth divided by R times Rf]

    style A fill:#fef3c7,color:#000000
    style E fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#166534,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Block Diagrams Instead of Schematics?** The R-2R ladder's physical schematic has many crossing wires and is difficult to render in plain text/Mermaid without ambiguity. The block diagrams above faithfully convey the **signal flow, switching logic, and recursive reduction** — all of which are what KTU examiners actually test.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Q: Compare Weighted Resistor DAC and R-2R Ladder DAC. Mention two advantages of the R-2R type.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Weighted Resistor DAC | R-2R Ladder DAC |
|---|---|---|
| Resistor Values | $R, 2R, 4R, 8R, \ldots, 2^{N-1}R$ | Only $R$ and $2R$ |
| IC Fabrication | Difficult for N > 4 | Easy for any N |
| Cost | High for high resolution | Low |
| Speed | Slower (parasitic mismatch) | Faster |

**Two Advantages of R-2R Ladder DAC:**
1. **Only two resistor values (R and 2R) are required** — drastically simplifying IC fabrication and improving matching accuracy.
2. **Easier to scale to higher resolutions** (8, 10, 12, 16 bits) because no exponential spread of resistor values is needed.

*(1 mark for tabular comparison + 1 mark per advantage = 3 marks)*

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**Q: Why is the R-2R Ladder DAC preferred in monolithic ICs even though the Weighted Resistor DAC has a simpler equation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- In a **monolithic IC**, all resistors are made of the same material with the same sheet resistance, and absolute values have poor tolerance (often ±20% or more). However, **matching between adjacent resistors on the same chip is excellent (typically < 0.1%)**.
- The R-2R ladder relies on **ratio matching** between $R$ and $2R$ (which are physically laid out as identical unit resistors in series/parallel), making it **highly accurate and process-tolerant**.
- The weighted resistor DAC requires values ranging from $R$ to $2^{N-1}R$ — a range that is **impractical and inaccurate** for $N > 4$ in monolithic form.
- Hence, despite its slightly more complex equation, the R-2R ladder is the **industry and academic standard** for high-resolution DACs. *(3 marks — 1 mark per valid point)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a **4-bit R-2R Ladder DAC** using an op-amp. Explain its working principle with the help of Thevenin's theorem. Derive the expression for the output voltage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **6-bit R-2R Ladder DAC** uses $R = 5\,k\Omega$, $R_f = 10\,k\Omega$, and $V_{REF} = 8\,V$. Determine: (i) the output voltage for digital input **101101**, (ii) the percentage resolution, and (iii) the full-scale output voltage. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Circuit Diagram (to be drawn on answer sheet):**
A 4-bit R-2R ladder with 4 SPDT switches $S_3, S_2, S_1, S_0$ connected to bits $b_3, b_2, b_1, b_0$. Series resistors of value $R$ between successive nodes. Shunt resistors of $2R$ at each node connecting to the switches. Op-amp in inverting configuration with feedback $R_f$, inverting input connected to the leftmost node, non-inverting input grounded.

**Working Principle:**

The R-2R ladder operates on the principle of **binary-weighted current division**. Each bit switch connects its associated 2R shunt arm to either $V_{REF}$ (bit = 1) or ground (bit = 0). The op-amp's inverting input acts as **virtual ground**, so the voltage across any 2R shunt connected to a HIGH bit is exactly $V_{REF}$, producing a current $V_{REF}/2R$ in that branch.

**Thevenin Reduction (from right to left):**
- Starting from the rightmost 2R (LSB), we observe that the right-looking impedance at every node is always **R** (proved by induction: $R \;\vert\vert\; 2R = 2R/3$, then add series R, get R again).
- At each step leftward, the Thevenin voltage is **halved**, and the Thevenin resistance remains R.
- After N-1 reductions, the voltage seen at the leftmost node is:

$$V_{Th} = V_{REF} \cdot \left( \frac{b_{N-1}}{2} + \frac{b_{N-2}}{4} + \cdots + \frac{b_0}{2^N} \right)$$

**Final Output Derivation:**

This Thevenin source drives a series R into the op-amp's virtual ground, generating current $I_{out} = V_{Th}/R$. The op-amp's feedback resistor converts this to:

$$V_{out} = -I_{out} \cdot R_f = -\frac{V_{REF} \cdot R_f}{2R} \cdot \sum_{j=0}^{N-1} \frac{b_j}{2^j} = -\frac{V_{REF} \cdot R_f}{2^N \cdot R} \cdot D$$

where $D$ is the decimal equivalent of the binary input word. *(7 marks: 2 marks for diagram + 3 marks for working + 2 marks for derivation)*

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- $N = 6$ bits, $R = 5\,k\Omega$, $R_f = 10\,k\Omega$, $V_{REF} = 8\,V$
- Input: $b_5 b_4 b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0 = 101101_2$

**Step 1 — Decimal Equivalent D:**

$$D = 1\cdot 32 + 0\cdot 16 + 1\cdot 8 + 1\cdot 4 + 0\cdot 2 + 1\cdot 1 = 32 + 8 + 4 + 1 = 45$$

**Step 2 — Constant Factor K:**

$$K = \frac{V_{REF} \cdot R_f}{2^N \cdot R} = \frac{8 \cdot 10\,k}{64 \cdot 5\,k} = \frac{80}{320} = 0.25\,V$$

**Step 3 — Output Voltage:**

$$V_{out} = -K \cdot D = -0.25 \cdot 45 = -11.25\,V$$

**Step 4 — Percentage Resolution:**

$$\% \, \text{Resolution} = \frac{1}{2^N - 1} \times 100 = \frac{1}{63} \times 100 \approx 1.587\%$$

**Step 5 — Full-Scale Output:**

$$V_{FS} = -K \cdot (2^N - 1) = -0.25 \cdot 63 = -15.75\,V$$

**Verification:** $|V_{out}| = 11.25 < 15.75 = |V_{FS}|$ ✓

*(7 marks: 2 marks for D + 2 marks for $V_{out}$ + 1.5 marks for % resolution + 1.5 marks for $V_{FS}$)*

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an **R-2R Ladder DAC** using an op-amp in current-to-voltage converter mode. Show that the output voltage is proportional to the binary input word. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An **8-bit R-2R Ladder DAC** has $R = 10\,k\Omega$, $R_f = 20\,k\Omega$, and $V_{REF} = 10\,V$. Compute: (i) the analog output for digital input **11001011**, (ii) the LSB step size in volts, and (iii) the analog output for the input where **only the MSB is HIGH** (i.e., $10000000_2$). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Circuit Diagram:**
Draw an R-2R ladder with N SPDT switches, ladder network, and op-amp as I-to-V converter. Label $V_{REF}$, $R$, $2R$, $R_f$, and digital inputs $b_0 \ldots b_{N-1}$.

**Operation Explanation:**

The op-amp's inverting input is held at **virtual ground** by the feedback action. Each SPDT switch routes the bottom of its 2R shunt to either $V_{REF}$ (logic 1) or virtual ground (logic 0).

For any bit $b_i = 1$, the voltage across the corresponding 2R is $V_{REF}$, so the current injected into that node is:

$$I_i = \frac{V_{REF}}{2R}$$

This current then **halves** at each ladder junction as it propagates leftward. The fraction reaching the op-amp's summing node from bit $i$ is $I_i / 2^i$.

**Summing at the Op-Amp:**

By Kirchhoff's Current Law at the virtual ground:

$$I_{total} = \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \frac{b_i \cdot V_{REF}}{2R \cdot 2^i} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2R} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \frac{b_i}{2^i}$$

The op-amp's feedback converts this to voltage:

$$V_{out} = -I_{total} \cdot R_f = -\frac{V_{REF} \cdot R_f}{2R} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \frac{b_i}{2^i}$$

**Proportionality:**

Since $D = \sum b_i 2^{N-1-i}$, we can rewrite:

$$V_{out} = -\frac{V_{REF} \cdot R_f}{2^N \cdot R} \cdot D \;\propto\; D$$

Hence, $V_{out}$ is **linearly proportional** to the binary input word's decimal value $D$. *(7 marks: 2 marks for diagram + 3 marks for current derivation + 2 marks for proportionality proof)*

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- $N = 8$ bits, $R = 10\,k\Omega$, $R_f = 20\,k\Omega$, $V_{REF} = 10\,V$

**Step 1 — Compute Constant Factor K:**

$$K = \frac{V_{REF} \cdot R_f}{2^N \cdot R} = \frac{10 \cdot 20\,k}{256 \cdot 10\,k} = \frac{200}{2560} = 0.078125\,V$$

**Step 2 — Decimal Equivalent of 11001011₂:**

$$D = 1\cdot 128 + 1\cdot 64 + 0\cdot 32 + 0\cdot 16 + 1\cdot 8 + 0\cdot 4 + 1\cdot 2 + 1\cdot 1$$
$$= 128 + 64 + 8 + 2 + 1 = 203$$

**Step 3 — Output Voltage for 11001011:**

$$V_{out} = -K \cdot D = -0.078125 \cdot 203 = -15.8594\,V$$

**Step 4 — LSB Step Size:**

$$\Delta V = K = 0.078125\,V \approx 78.125\,mV$$

(Or equivalently, $V_{REF}/2^N = 10/256 = 0.0391\,V$ when $R_f = R$; with $R_f = 2R$, the factor doubles.)

**Step 5 — Output for Input 10000000₂:**

For $10000000_2$, only the MSB ($b_7$) is HIGH, so $D = 128$.

$$V_{out} = -K \cdot D = -0.078125 \cdot 128 = -10.0\,V$$

**Verification:** The MSB-only output is $V_{REF} \cdot R_f / 2R = 10 \cdot 2 = 20\,V$? No, the correct half-scale for unsigned binary is $V_{FS}/2 = -V_{REF} \cdot R_f \cdot (2^N-1)/(2 \cdot 2^N \cdot R) \approx -9.96\,V$. Recalculating: the MSB contributes $V_{REF} \cdot R_f / (2R) = 10 \cdot 20k / (2 \cdot 10k) = 10\,V$ in magnitude. So $V_{out} = -10.0\,V$ for MSB-only input. ✓

*(7 marks: 1 mark for K + 1 mark for D + 1.5 marks for $V_{out}$ + 1.5 marks for LSB + 2 marks for MSB-only case)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Frequently Lose Marks on R-2R DAC Problems:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the Sign of the Output:** Because the op-amp is in **inverting** configuration, $V_{out}$ is **negative**. Students often write the magnitude only and lose 1 mark.
> 
> 2. **Confusing Bit Ordering:** The expanded sum $V_{out} = -\frac{V_{REF} R_f}{2R} \sum \frac{b_i}{2^i}$ uses $b_0$ = **MSB**, while the compact form $V_{out} = -\frac{V_{REF} R_f}{2^N R} D$ uses the **standard MSB-to-LSB convention**. Mixing these two gives numerical errors. **Always state your convention explicitly at the start of the answer.**
> 
> 3. **Using the Wrong $2^N$ Factor:** A common mistake is using $2^{N-1}$ instead of $2^N$ in the denominator. The $2^N$ arises from folding the $2^{N-1}$ from the summation index into the denominator — do this step carefully.
> 
> 4. **Not Proving the Invariant Impedance:** A 7-mark "explain working" question **demands** the inductive proof that the right-looking impedance at every node is R. Skipping this loses 2-3 marks.
> 
> 5. **Ignoring the Op-Amp's Virtual Ground:** Many students write $V_{REF}$ across the 2R even when the switch routes it to ground. Always check: the op-amp inverting input is virtual ground **only if the non-inverting input is grounded and feedback is present**.
> 
> 6. **Not Drawing the Switches Correctly:** SPDT switches are mandatory in the diagram. Drawing simple SPST switches or omitting them entirely loses 1-2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — R-2R Ladder DAC**

- **Two resistor values only:** $R$ and $2R$. This is the **defining feature** of the R-2R architecture.
- **Invariant right-looking impedance = R** at every ladder node (proved by induction). This is the **core theorem** that makes binary weighting work.
- **Switches are SPDT** connecting each 2R shunt to either $V_{REF}$ (logic 1) or **virtual ground** (logic 0).
- **Op-amp acts as I-to-V converter** with feedback resistor $R_f$, producing $V_{out} = -I_{out} \cdot R_f$.
- **Master Equation (Expanded):**

$$V_{out} = -\frac{V_{REF} \cdot R_f}{2R} \cdot \sum_{j=0}^{N-1} \frac{b_j}{2^j}$$

- **Master Equation (Compact):**

$$V_{out} = -\frac{V_{REF} \cdot R_f}{2^N \cdot R} \cdot D \quad \text{where } D = \text{decimal equivalent of input word}$$

- **LSB size (Resolution in Volts):**

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{REF} \cdot R_f}{2^N \cdot R}$$

- **Full-Scale Output:**

$$V_{FS} = -\frac{V_{REF} \cdot R_f}{R} \cdot \frac{2^N - 1}{2^N}$$

- **Percentage Resolution:**

$$\% \, \text{Resolution} = \frac{1}{2^N - 1} \times 100\%$$

- **Advantages over Weighted Resistor DAC:** (i) only two resistor values → easy IC fabrication, (ii) scalable to high resolution, (iii) better matching, (iv) faster settling.
- **Disadvantages:** (i) requires more switches, (ii) output is inverting (negative) — needs level shifting for unipolar applications.
- **Typical Commercial ICs:** DAC0808 (8-bit), AD7524 (8-bit), AD9744 (14-bit), DAC8565 (16-bit).
- **Sign Convention:** Output is **negative** for positive $V_{REF}$. State this explicitly in KTU answers.
- **Settling Time:** Roughly $t_s \approx N \cdot R \cdot C_{parasitic}$. Faster for smaller R.
- **Monotonicity:** R-2R ladder is **monotonic by design** (no missing codes) if switches and op-amp are ideal.
- **Common KTU Trick Questions:** (a) "What if the MSB resistor is 2R and LSB is R?" — answer: the ladder is reversed; (b) "What if $R_f = R$?" — answer: $V_{FS} = -V_{REF} \cdot (2^N-1)/2^N$, very close to $-V_{REF}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When solving numerical problems, **always compute the constant factor $K = V_{REF} R_f / (2^N R)$ first**, then $V_{out} = -K \cdot D$. This two-step approach is faster, less error-prone, and matches the KTU marker's expected solution flow.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multivibrators - Astable and Monostable - Design and Working
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT402 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Multivibrators - Astable and Monostable - Design and Working

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Multivibrators — Formal KTU Definition

A **multivibrator** is a two-stage regenerative electronic switching circuit that employs positive feedback to produce a rectangular output waveform. It is the fundamental building block of all digital timing, pulse-generation, and sequential logic subsystems used in modern linear and digital integrated circuits. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context for *Linear Integrated Circuits (PCEBT402)*, multivibrators are realized using the **555 Timer IC** — the most ubiquitous analog timing integrated circuit ever designed (introduced by Signetics in 1972 by Hans Camenzind).

The word *multivibrator* literally means **"many vibrations"** because its output is rich in harmonics (a square wave is mathematically the sum of an infinite series of odd harmonics of the fundamental frequency).

### 1.2 The Three Families of Multivibrators

| Type | Stable States | External Trigger? | Output Nature | Use Case |
|------|--------------|-------------------|---------------|---------|
| **Astable** (Free-running) | **None** (self-oscillating) | Not required | Continuous square wave | Clock generators, LED flashers, tone generators |
| **Monostable** (One-shot) | **One** stable state | **Required** | Single pulse of fixed width | Missing-pulse detectors, debouncers, timers |
| **Bistable** (Flip-flop) | **Two** stable states | **Required** (Set / Reset) | Toggles between 0 and V_CC | Latches, memory cells, Schmitt triggers |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The current module explicitly focuses on the **Astable** and **Monostable** configurations. The **Bistable** multivibrator is covered under digital electronics / sequential logic courses and is *not* part of the Module 3 syllabus.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **bathroom flush tank**:
- The **Astable** multivibrator is like a **leaking bucket with automatic refill** — it constantly alternates between filling and emptying, producing a continuous cycle without anyone touching it.
- The **Monostable** multivibrator is like a **push-button doorbell** — it sits quietly in one state, but the moment you press the button (trigger), it produces exactly **one** ring (pulse) of fixed duration, then automatically returns to silence.
- The **Bistable** is like a **ratchet light switch** — push once for ON, push again for OFF; it remembers its state.

### 1.4 The 555 Timer IC — Physical Identity

The 555 is an **8-pin mini-DIP** (Dual In-line Package) integrated circuit that contains roughly **25 transistors, 2 diodes, and 15 resistors** on a single silicon die. It can operate from a single supply of **+4.5 V to +16 V**, deliver output currents up to **200 mA**, and source/sink up to **±200 mA** at the output pin.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "555" name comes from its internal **three 5 kΩ resistors** which form a precision voltage divider. This is a critical KTU 2024 board-exam fact — students often confuse 5 kΩ with 50 kΩ.

### 1.5 Pin Configuration of the 555 Timer

| Pin No. | Pin Name | Function | KTU Board Significance |
|---------|----------|----------|------------------------|
| **1** | **GND** | Ground reference (0 V) | Common reference for all signals |
| **2** | **TRIG (Trigger)** | Starts the timing cycle when voltage falls **below (1/3)V_CC** | Lower comparator input |
| **3** | **OUT (Output)** | Push-pull output drives the load (HIGH ≈ V_CC − 1.7 V, LOW ≈ 0.1 V) | Where the rectangular wave appears |
| **4** | **RESET** | Active-LOW reset; if pulled below **0.4 V**, forces output LOW | Master clear (asynchronous) |
| **5** | **CONT (Control Voltage)** | External modulation input to upper comparator (normally bypassed by **0.01 µF** to GND) | Allows frequency/width modulation |
| **6** | **THR (Threshold)** | Resets the flip-flop when voltage rises **above (2/3)V_CC** | Upper comparator input |
| **7** | **DIS (Discharge)** | Open-collector transistor; sinks current to discharge the timing capacitor | Acts as a controlled short to GND |
| **8** | **V_CC** | Positive supply (**+4.5 V to +16 V** nominal, +15 V typical) | Powers the entire IC |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin 5 (Control Voltage)** is a frequent KTU question. By default, it sets the upper comparator reference to **(2/3)V_CC** and the lower comparator reference to **(1/3)V_CC**. Externally applying a voltage to Pin 5 directly overrides these thresholds, which is the basis of **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** using the 555.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Internal Block Architecture of the 555 Timer

The 555 has **four functional sub-blocks** working in concert:

1. **Voltage Divider Network** — Three equal 5 kΩ resistors in series between V_CC and GND. This creates two stable reference voltages:
   - Upper reference: $(1)\;V_{ref1} = (2/3)V_{CC}$
   - Lower reference: $(2)\;V_{ref2} = (1/3)V_{CC}$

2. **Two Open-Comparator Comparators** —
   - **Comparator 1 (Upper):** Compares Pin 6 (THR) against $(2/3)V_{CC}$. Output goes HIGH when $V_{THR} > (2/3)V_{CC}$.
   - **Comparator 2 (Lower):** Compares Pin 2 (TRIG) against $(1/3)V_{CC}$. Output goes HIGH when $V_{TRIG} < (1/3)V_{CC}$.

3. **SR Flip-Flop (Latch)** — Set-dominant storage element. The S input comes from the lower comparator, the R input from the upper comparator. The Q̄ output drives the discharge transistor, and the Q output drives the push-pull output stage.

4. **Discharge Transistor (Q1)** — An NPN transistor whose collector is connected to Pin 7. When Q̄ is HIGH, Q1 saturates and shorts Pin 7 to GND. When Q̄ is LOW, Q1 is OFF (open circuit).

5. **Output Driver** — A push-pull (totem-pole) stage capable of sourcing or sinking up to 200 mA, providing rail-to-rail (approximately) output swings.

### 2.2 Astable Multivibrator using 555 — Operational Analysis

In the **astable (free-running)** configuration, the 555 has **no stable state**. It continuously alternates between charging and discharging an external capacitor C, producing a rectangular wave at Pin 3 and a sawtooth (exponential) wave across C.

**External Components Required:** Two resistors (R_A, R_B) and one capacitor C, connected as:
- R_A: from V_CC to Pin 7
- R_B: from Pin 7 to Pin 6 (and also to Pin 2, which are tied together in astable mode)
- C: from Pin 6/Pin 2 to GND

**The Operating Cycle (Two Phases):**

**Phase 1 — CHARGING (Output HIGH):**
- Initially, capacitor C is discharged. Voltage across C is below $(1/3)V_{CC}$, so the lower comparator SETS the flip-flop.
- Q is HIGH → Output Pin 3 = HIGH.
- Q̄ is LOW → Discharge transistor Q1 is OFF (open).
- C charges through the **series combination of R_A and R_B** (current flows from V_CC → R_A → R_B → C → GND).
- Voltage across C rises exponentially toward V_CC with time constant $(R_A + R_B)C$.
- Charging continues **until** $V_C$ reaches $(2/3)V_{CC}$.

**Phase 2 — DISCHARGING (Output LOW):**
- The instant $V_C = (2/3)V_{CC}$, the upper comparator fires and RESETS the flip-flop.
- Q is LOW → Output Pin 3 = LOW.
- Q̄ is HIGH → Discharge transistor Q1 saturates (shorts Pin 7 to GND).
- C now discharges through **R_B only** (current flows from C → R_B → Pin 7 → GND).
- Voltage across C falls exponentially toward 0 V with time constant $R_B C$.
- Discharging continues **until** $V_C$ reaches $(1/3)V_{CC}$.
- At this instant, the lower comparator again SETS the flip-flop, and the cycle restarts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is R_B used in both phases?** During charging, current must go through both R_A and R_B because Q1 is OFF. During discharging, Q1 shorts Pin 7 to GND, bypassing R_A. This asymmetry is precisely **why the astable 555 has a duty cycle that can never reach exactly 50%** — the charging path has more resistance than the discharging path.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — 555 Circuits

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX NOTE:** The vertical bar `|` is forbidden inside markdown table cells. All absolute-value bars in the table are written using `\vert` or `\mid` as required by the engine protocol.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | KTU Board Frequency |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Charging Time (Astable) | $t_C = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + R_B) \cdot C$ | seconds | ★★★★★ |
| 2 | Discharging Time (Astable) | $t_D = 0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C$ | seconds | ★★★★★ |
| 3 | Total Period (Astable) | $T = t_C + t_D = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C$ | seconds | ★★★★★ |
| 4 | Frequency (Astable) | $f = \dfrac{1.45}{(R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C}$ | Hz | ★★★★★ |
| 5 | Duty Cycle (Astable) | $D = \dfrac{t_C}{T} \times 100\% = \dfrac{(R_A + R_B)}{(R_A + 2R_B)} \times 100\%$ | % | ★★★★★ |
| 6 | Pulse Width (Monostable) | $T = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C$ | seconds | ★★★★★ |
| 7 | Upper Threshold Voltage | $V_{THR} = (2/3) V_{CC}$ | volts | ★★★★ |
| 8 | Lower Trigger Voltage | $V_{TRIG} = (1/3) V_{CC}$ | volts | ★★★★ |
| 9 | Charging Capacitor Voltage | $V_C(t) = V_{CC}\left(1 - e^{-t/(R_{eq}C)}\right)$ | volts | ★★★ |
| 10 | Discharging Capacitor Voltage | $V_C(t) = V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t/(R_{eq}C)}$ | volts | ★★★ |
| 11 | Natural Constant 0.693 | $0.693 = \ln 2$ | dimensionless | ★★★ |
| 12 | 1.1 Coefficient | $1.1 = \ln 3$ | dimensionless | ★★★ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The 555 is mathematically exploiting the property that a capacitor charges/discharges exponentially, and the time taken to swing between **(1/3)V_CC** and **(2/3)V_CC** depends only on the natural logarithm of 2 (or 3 for monostable). This is why the 555 is so insensitive to supply voltage variations — both thresholds scale with V_CC, so the timing ratio is preserved.

### 2.4 Monostable Multivibrator using 555 — Operational Analysis

In the **monostable (one-shot)** configuration, the 555 has **one stable state** (output LOW) and one **quasi-stable state** (output HIGH). It requires an external negative-going trigger pulse to enter the quasi-stable state.

**External Components:**
- R: from V_CC to Pin 7 (also to Pin 6)
- C: from Pin 6 to GND
- Trigger: applied to Pin 2

**State 1 — STABLE STATE (Quiescent):**
- Output Pin 3 is LOW.
- Q̄ is HIGH → Discharge transistor Q1 is ON → Pin 7 is shorted to GND.
- Capacitor C is held at 0 V by Q1.
- The circuit waits indefinitely in this state. No timing occurs.

**State 2 — QUASI-STABLE (Triggered):**
- A **negative-going trigger pulse** arrives at Pin 2 with amplitude sufficient to pull it **below $(1/3)V_{CC}$**.
- The lower comparator SETS the flip-flop.
- Output Pin 3 goes HIGH.
- Q̄ goes LOW → Q1 turns OFF → Pin 7 becomes open.
- C now charges through R toward V_CC.
- Voltage across C rises exponentially.
- This state persists for a duration $T = 1.1 RC$, at the end of which $V_C = (2/3)V_{CC}$, the upper comparator fires, RESETS the flip-flop, and the circuit returns to State 1.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU 2024 Board Distinction:** In the **astable**, Pin 2 and Pin 6 are **tied together** (Pin 2 acts as both trigger and threshold). In the **monostable**, Pin 6 and Pin 7 are **tied together**, while Pin 2 receives an **external trigger** independently. Mixing these pin connections is the #1 mistake in KTU 2024 board answer sheets.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Astable Time Period

We begin with the fundamental charging equation of a capacitor in an RC series circuit, supplied by V_CC:

$$V_C(t) = V_{final} - (V_{final} - V_{initial}) \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 1 — Define the boundary conditions for the charging phase.**

The capacitor starts charging at the moment the lower comparator SETS the flip-flop, i.e., when $V_C$ has just dropped to $(1/3)V_{CC}$. The charging continues until $V_C$ reaches the upper threshold $(2/3)V_{CC}$. The "final" voltage the capacitor is charging toward is $V_{CC}$ (since Q1 is OFF and the full V_CC appears at the top of R_A).

So for the charging phase:
- $V_{initial} = (1/3)V_{CC}$
- $V_{final} = V_{CC}$
- $\tau_{charge} = (R_A + R_B) C$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the general charging equation.**

At the end of charging time $t = t_C$, the voltage across C equals $(2/3)V_{CC}$:

$$(2/3)V_{CC} = V_{CC} - \left[V_{CC} - (1/3)V_{CC}\right] \cdot e^{-t_C / [(R_A + R_B)C]}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the bracket.**

The expression in brackets is:

$$V_{CC} - (1/3)V_{CC} = (3/3)V_{CC} - (1/3)V_{CC} = (2/3)V_{CC}$$

So our equation becomes:

$$(2/3)V_{CC} = V_{CC} - (2/3)V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t_C / [(R_A + R_B)C]}$$

**Step 4 — Isolate the exponential term.**

Rearranging:

$$V_{CC} - (2/3)V_{CC} = (2/3)V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t_C / [(R_A + R_B)C]}$$

$$(1/3)V_{CC} = (2/3)V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t_C / [(R_A + R_B)C]}$$

**Step 5 — Cancel V_CC and solve for the exponent.**

$$1/2 = e^{-t_C / [(R_A + R_B)C]}$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln(1/2) = -t_C / [(R_A + R_B)C]$$

$$-\ln 2 = -t_C / [(R_A + R_B)C]$$

$$\ln 2 = t_C / [(R_A + R_B)C]$$

**Step 6 — Final expression for charging time.**

$$\boxed{t_C = \ln 2 \cdot (R_A + R_B) \cdot C = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + R_B) \cdot C}$$

**Step 7 — Apply identical logic to the discharging phase.**

During discharge, Q1 is ON, Pin 7 is shorted to GND, and C discharges through R_B alone toward 0 V. The capacitor starts at $(2/3)V_{CC}$ and discharges to $(1/3)V_{CC}$:

$$(1/3)V_{CC} = (2/3)V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t_D / (R_B C)}$$

$$1/2 = e^{-t_D / (R_B C)}$$

$$t_D = \ln 2 \cdot R_B \cdot C$$

**Step 8 — Final expression for discharging time.**

$$\boxed{t_D = 0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C}$$

**Step 9 — Total period and frequency.**

$$T = t_C + t_D = 0.693(R_A + R_B)C + 0.693 R_B C = 0.693(R_A + 2R_B)C$$

$$f = 1/T = \dfrac{1.45}{(R_A + 2R_B)C}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Monostable Pulse Width

In the monostable configuration, the capacitor charges from **0 V** (because Q1 is ON during the stable state) all the way to $(2/3)V_{CC}$ (which trips the upper comparator). So:

- $V_{initial} = 0$
- $V_{final} = V_{CC}$
- $V_{end} = (2/3)V_{CC}$

**Step 1 — Write the charging equation.**

$$(2/3)V_{CC} = V_{CC} - (V_{CC} - 0) \cdot e^{-T/(RC)}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify.**

$$(2/3)V_{CC} = V_{CC} - V_{CC} \cdot e^{-T/(RC)}$$

$$V_{CC} \cdot e^{-T/(RC)} = V_{CC} - (2/3)V_{CC} = (1/3)V_{CC}$$

**Step 3 — Cancel V_CC and take the logarithm.**

$$e^{-T/(RC)} = 1/3$$

$$-T/(RC) = \ln(1/3) = -\ln 3$$

**Step 4 — Final expression for monostable pulse width.**

$$\boxed{T = \ln 3 \cdot R \cdot C = 1.0986 \cdot R \cdot C \approx 1.1 RC}$$

### 3.3 Numerical Design Examples (Worked-Out)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example 1 — Astable Design:** Design a 555 astable multivibrator for **f = 1 kHz** with duty cycle **75%**. Assume C = 0.1 µF.

**Given:** $f = 1000$ Hz, $D = 0.75$, $C = 0.1 \times 10^{-6}$ F.

**Step 1 — Express the duty cycle in terms of R_A and R_B.**

$$D = \dfrac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} = 0.75$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$R_A + R_B = 0.75(R_A + 2R_B)$$

$$R_A + R_B = 0.75 R_A + 1.5 R_B$$

$$0.25 R_A = 0.5 R_B \Rightarrow R_A = 2 R_B$$

**Step 2 — Use the frequency formula to find the sum.**

$$f = \dfrac{1.45}{(R_A + 2R_B) C} = 1000$$

$$R_A + 2R_B = \dfrac{1.45}{1000 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{1.45}{10^{-4}} = 14500 \; \Omega = 14.5 \; k\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Solve the system of equations.**

From Step 1, substitute $R_A = 2R_B$:

$$2R_B + 2R_B = 14.5 \; k\Omega \Rightarrow 4R_B = 14.5 \; k\Omega \Rightarrow R_B = 3.625 \; k\Omega$$

Then:

$$R_A = 2 \times 3.625 = 7.25 \; k\Omega$$

**Final Answer:** $R_A = 7.25 \; k\Omega$, $R_B = 3.625 \; k\Omega$, $C = 0.1 \; \mu F$ → Astable at **1 kHz with 75% duty cycle**. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example 2 — Monostable Design:** Design a 555 monostable to produce a **10 ms** output pulse. Choose $C = 1 \; \mu F$ and find R.

**Given:** $T = 10 \times 10^{-3}$ s, $C = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ F.

$$T = 1.1 R C$$

$$10 \times 10^{-3} = 1.1 \times R \times 1 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$R = \dfrac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{1.1 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{10^{-2}}{1.1 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{10^4}{1.1} = 9090.9 \; \Omega$$

**Final Answer:** $R \approx 9.1 \; k\Omega$ (standard value), $C = 1 \; \mu F$ → Monostable pulse of **10 ms**. ✓

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Astable | Monostable |
|----------|---------|------------|
| Stable states | 0 | 1 (LOW) |
| External trigger required? | No | **Yes** (Pin 2) |
| Capacitor cycles between | $(1/3)V_{CC}$ and $(2/3)V_{CC}$ | **0 V** and $(2/3)V_{CC}$ |
| R_B path during discharge | Active | N/A (Q1 stays OFF only during pulse) |
| Period formula | $T = 0.693(R_A + 2R_B)C$ | $T = 1.1 R C$ |
| Pins 2 and 6 connected? | **Yes** (tied) | **No** (Pin 2 = external trigger) |
| Pin 7 use | Discharges C every cycle | Stays OFF during pulse, ON in stable state |
| Output waveform | Continuous square wave | Single pulse per trigger |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Internal Block Diagram of the 555 Timer IC

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VCCin[VCC Pin 8] --> DivTop[Top of Voltage Divider]
    GNDin[GND Pin 1] --> DivBot[Bottom of Voltage Divider]
    DivTop --> R1[Resistor 5k ohm]
    R1 --> N1[Node Vref 1 equals 2 by 3 VCC]
    N1 --> R2[Resistor 5k ohm]
    R2 --> N2[Node Vref 2 equals 1 by 3 VCC]
    N2 --> R3[Resistor 5k ohm]
    R3 --> DivBot

    N1 --> C1Pos[Upper Comparator Reference]
    N2 --> C2Pos[Lower Comparator Reference]

    THRin[Threshold Pin 6] --> C1Neg[Upper Comparator Inverting Input]
    C1Pos --> C1[Upper Comparator]
    C1Neg --> C1
    C1 --> SR_R[SR FlipFlop R Input]

    TRIGin[Trigger Pin 2] --> C2Neg[Lower Comparator Non Inverting Input]
    C2Pos --> C2[Lower Comparator]
    C2Neg --> C2
    C2 --> SR_S[SR FlipFlop S Input]

    SR_S --> FF[SR FlipFlop]
    SR_R --> FF
    FF --> Qbar[Qbar Output]
    FF --> Qout[Q Output]
    Qbar --> Q1[Discharge NPN Transistor]
    Q1 --> DISpin[Discharge Pin 7]
    Qout --> Driver[Push Pull Output Driver]
    Driver --> OUTpin[Output Pin 3]
    RSTin[Reset Pin 4] --> FF
    CVin[Control Voltage Pin 5] --> N1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** The two comparators are arranged so that:
> - The **upper comparator** (with its reference at $(2/3)V_{CC}$) drives the **RESET** input of the flip-flop.
> - The **lower comparator** (with its reference at $(1/3)V_{CC}$) drives the **SET** input.
> - Q̄ drives the discharge transistor, while Q drives the output buffer.

### 4.2 Astable Multivibrator — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VCCs[VCC Supply] --> RA[Resistor RA]
    RA --> NodeX[Node X Pin 7 and Pin 6 and Pin 2]
    NodeX --> RB[Resistor RB]
    RB --> NodeY[Node Y Pin 6 and Pin 2 to Capacitor]
    NodeY --> Cap[Timing Capacitor C]
    Cap --> GNDs[Ground]

    NodeX --> THRin[Pin 6 Threshold]
    NodeX --> TRIGin[Pin 2 Trigger]
    NodeX --> DISin[Pin 7 Discharge]

    THRin --> IC555[555 Timer Internal Comparators]
    TRIGin --> IC555
    IC555 --> DISdriver[Internal Discharge Transistor Pin 7]
    DISdriver --> NodeX
    IC555 --> OUTsig[Output Pin 3 Square Wave]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this:** Notice that the same node X (junction of R_A and R_B) connects to **three pins** of the 555: Pin 7 (discharge), Pin 6 (threshold), and Pin 2 (trigger). This is the visual fingerprint of the **astable** configuration.

### 4.3 Monostable Multivibrator — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VCCm[VCC Supply] --> Rm[Timing Resistor R]
    Rm --> NodeP[Node P Pin 7 and Pin 6]
    NodeP --> DISin[Pin 7 Discharge]
    NodeP --> THRin[Pin 6 Threshold]
    NodeP --> Cm[Timing Capacitor C]
    Cm --> GNDm[Ground]

    TRIGext[External Negative Trigger Pulse] --> TRIGpin[Pin 2 Trigger]
    TRIGpin --> IC555m[555 Timer Internal Comparators]
    IC555m --> OUTpulse[Output Pin 3 Single Pulse Width T equals 1.1 RC]
    IC555m --> DISdriverm[Internal Discharge Transistor]
    DISdriverm --> NodeP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this:** The monostable has **two distinct input paths** to the capacitor node: Pin 6 (threshold, for voltage sensing) and Pin 7 (discharge). Pin 2 (trigger) is **isolated** from the capacitor and receives the external trigger directly.

### 4.4 Timing Waveform Diagram (Astable)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Timeline1[One Full Astable Cycle]
        direction LR
        T0[T equals 0: Output goes HIGH C starts charging] --> T1[T equals tC: Vc reaches 2 by 3 VCC Output goes LOW]
        T1 --> T2[T equals tC plus tD: Vc reaches 1 by 3 VCC Output goes HIGH Cycle repeats]
    end

    subgraph WaveShape[Waveform Behavior]
        direction LR
        W1[Output Pin 3 HIGH for tC] --> W2[Output Pin 3 LOW for tD]
        W2 --> W1
    end
```

### 4.5 Functional State Machine — Monostable Operation

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Stable_LOW
    Stable_LOW: Stable State - Output LOW - C discharged - Q1 ON - Pin 7 shorted to GND
    Trigger_Arrives: External negative pulse on Pin 2 below 1 by 3 VCC
    Stable_LOW --> Trigger_Arrives : Trigger received
    Trigger_Arrives --> Quasi_HIGH : Lower comparator SETS flipflop
    Quasi_HIGH: Quasi stable State - Output HIGH - Q1 OFF - C charges through R
    Quasi_HIGH --> Stable_LOW : After T equals 1.1 RC Vc reaches 2 by 3 VCC and upper comparator RESETS flipflop
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **List the three types of multivibrators. Which type does not require an external trigger?**

**Model Answer:**
The three types of multivibrators are:
1. **Astable multivibrator** (free-running) — produces a continuous square wave without any external trigger.
2. **Monostable multivibrator** (one-shot) — requires an external trigger; produces one pulse of fixed width.
3. **Bistable multivibrator** (flip-flop) — requires an external trigger to toggle between two stable states.

**The astable multivibrator does not require an external trigger** because it has no stable state — it continuously oscillates between charging and discharging phases, governed entirely by its RC time constants.

*(Word limit: ~50 words; KTU board award: 3 marks = 1 mark for list + 1 mark for trigger condition + 1 mark for correct identification.)*

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **What is the function of the discharge transistor (Pin 7) in a 555 timer? How does its role differ between astable and monostable modes?**

**Model Answer:**
The discharge transistor (Pin 7) is an open-collector NPN transistor internal to the 555. Its function is to **short the timing capacitor to ground** when activated (saturated) and to **release the capacitor** for charging when deactivated (cut-off).

- **In astable mode:** The discharge transistor alternates between ON (during discharge phase, output LOW) and OFF (during charging phase, output HIGH) every half-cycle, producing continuous oscillation.
- **In monostable mode:** The discharge transistor remains **continuously ON** in the stable state (holding C at 0 V), and switches OFF **only for the duration of the output pulse** (≈ 1.1 RC) when a trigger arrives.

*(3 marks: 1 for function + 1 for astable behavior + 1 for monostable behavior.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper 2]
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a 555 timer connected in **astable multivibrator** mode. Derive the expressions for the time period and frequency of oscillation.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] An astable multivibrator using a 555 timer is designed with $R_A = 4.7 \; k\Omega$, $R_B = 1 \; k\Omega$, and $C = 0.01 \; \mu F$. Calculate (i) the charging time, (ii) the discharging time, (iii) the total time period, (iv) the frequency of oscillation, and (v) the duty cycle.

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a)

**Circuit Description (3 marks):**
The 555 astable multivibrator consists of:
- R_A connected between V_CC and Pin 7
- R_B connected between Pin 7 and the junction of Pin 6/Pin 2
- C connected between Pin 6/Pin 2 and GND
- Pin 4 (Reset) tied to V_CC; Pin 5 (Control Voltage) bypassed to GND via 0.01 µF capacitor; Pins 6 and 2 are tied together.

**Working Explanation (3 marks):**
Assume C is initially uncharged. The voltage at Pin 2 < (1/3)V_CC, so the lower comparator SETS the flip-flop, output goes HIGH, and Q̄ goes LOW, turning OFF the discharge transistor. C now charges through (R_A + R_B) toward V_CC. When V_C reaches (2/3)V_CC, the upper comparator RESETS the flip-flop, output goes LOW, and the discharge transistor turns ON. C now discharges through R_B alone (Pin 7 shorted to GND). When V_C falls to (1/3)V_CC, the lower comparator SETS the flip-flop again, and the cycle repeats continuously. The output at Pin 3 is a rectangular wave with period T = t_C + t_D.

**Derivation (1 mark — for the final boxed formula):**

The charging time is derived from the capacitor charging equation between (1/3)V_CC and (2/3)V_CC with time constant (R_A + R_B)C:

$$t_C = 0.693(R_A + R_B)C$$

The discharging time, with time constant R_B C:

$$t_D = 0.693 R_B C$$

$$\boxed{T = 0.693(R_A + 2R_B)C \quad ; \quad f = \frac{1.45}{(R_A + 2R_B)C}}$$

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b) — Numerical Computation

**Given:** $R_A = 4.7 \; k\Omega = 4700 \; \Omega$, $R_B = 1 \; k\Omega = 1000 \; \Omega$, $C = 0.01 \; \mu F = 10^{-8} \; F$.

**(i) Charging time (2 marks):**

$$t_C = 0.693 \times (R_A + R_B) \times C$$
$$t_C = 0.693 \times (4700 + 1000) \times 10^{-8}$$
$$t_C = 0.693 \times 5700 \times 10^{-8}$$
$$t_C = 3949.01 \times 10^{-8} \; s = 39.49 \; \mu s$$

**(ii) Discharging time (1 mark):**

$$t_D = 0.693 \times R_B \times C$$
$$t_D = 0.693 \times 1000 \times 10^{-8}$$
$$t_D = 6.93 \times 10^{-6} \; s = 6.93 \; \mu s$$

**(iii) Total time period (1 mark):**

$$T = t_C + t_D = 39.49 \; \mu s + 6.93 \; \mu s = 46.42 \; \mu s$$

**Verification using direct formula:**

$$T = 0.693 \times (R_A + 2R_B) \times C = 0.693 \times (4700 + 2000) \times 10^{-8}$$
$$T = 0.693 \times 6700 \times 10^{-8} = 46.43 \; \mu s \; ✓$$

**(iv) Frequency (1.5 marks):**

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{46.42 \times 10^{-6}} = 21.54 \; kHz$$

**Verification using direct formula:**

$$f = \frac{1.45}{(R_A + 2R_B)C} = \frac{1.45}{6700 \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{1.45}{6.7 \times 10^{-5}}$$
$$f = 2.164 \times 10^{4} \; Hz = 21.64 \; kHz \; ✓$$

**(v) Duty cycle (1.5 marks):**

$$D = \frac{t_C}{T} \times 100\% = \frac{39.49}{46.42} \times 100\% = 85.07\%$$

**Verification using direct formula:**

$$D = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} \times 100\% = \frac{5700}{6700} \times 100\% = 85.07\% \; ✓$$

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the working of a 555 timer as a **monostable multivibrator**. Derive the expression for the pulse width. Why is the duty cycle always less than 100% in a 555 monostable?
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Design a 555 monostable multivibrator to generate a pulse of **width 5 ms**. Also calculate the recovery time and maximum duty cycle if $C = 0.47 \; \mu F$.

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a)

**Circuit and Pin Connections (2 marks):**
In monostable mode:
- Timing resistor R is connected between V_CC and Pin 7 (which is also connected to Pin 6).
- Timing capacitor C is connected between Pin 6 and GND.
- Pin 2 receives an external negative-going trigger pulse.
- Pin 4 (Reset) is tied to V_CC; Pin 5 bypassed to GND.

**Working in Stable State (2 marks):**
In the stable state, the output is LOW. The discharge transistor (Q1) is saturated, so Pin 7 is shorted to GND. Hence, capacitor C is held at 0 V. The upper comparator sees $V_C = 0 < (2/3)V_{CC}$, so its output is LOW (no reset). The lower comparator sees Pin 2 above (1/3)V_{CC}$ (no trigger), so its output is also LOW. The flip-flop remains in the RESET state. The circuit waits indefinitely.

**Triggering Action (2 marks):**
When a **negative-going trigger pulse** pulls Pin 2 below (1/3)V_{CC}$, the lower comparator SETS the flip-flop. Output Pin 3 goes HIGH. The discharge transistor Q1 turns OFF, releasing Pin 7. Capacitor C now begins to charge through R toward V_{CC}.

**Pulse Termination and Derivation (1 mark):**

The voltage across C rises exponentially:

$$V_C(t) = V_{CC}\left(1 - e^{-t/(RC)}\right)$$

This continues until $V_C = (2/3)V_{CC}$, at which point the upper comparator RESETS the flip-flop. Output returns to LOW, Q1 turns ON, and C rapidly discharges through Q1 to 0 V. Solving for the time:

$$(2/3)V_{CC} = V_{CC}(1 - e^{-T/(RC)})$$
$$e^{-T/(RC)} = 1/3$$
$$T = \ln 3 \cdot R \cdot C \approx 1.1 RC$$

**Why Duty Cycle < 100% (the recovery time issue):**
In a 555 monostable, after the output pulse ends, the capacitor C must **discharge fully through Q1** before the next trigger can be accepted. This recovery time is approximately **$t_{recovery} \approx 3R_{ON} \cdot C$** (where $R_{ON}$ is the saturation resistance of Q1, typically ~10 Ω). This sets a minimum time between triggers, ensuring the duty cycle can never reach 100%.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b) — Design

**Given:** Required pulse width $T = 5 \; ms = 5 \times 10^{-3} \; s$, $C = 0.47 \; \mu F = 0.47 \times 10^{-6} \; F$.

**Step 1 — Find R (3 marks):**

$$T = 1.1 R C$$
$$5 \times 10^{-3} = 1.1 \times R \times 0.47 \times 10^{-6}$$
$$R = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.1 \times 0.47 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{5.17 \times 10^{-7}}$$
$$R = 9.67 \; k\Omega$$

**Use standard value:** $R \approx 10 \; k\Omega$ (gives slightly wider pulse, $T \approx 5.17$ ms). For exact 5 ms, use a $9.67 \; k\Omega$ precision resistor or fine-tune with a series trimmer.

**Step 2 — Calculate recovery time (2 marks):**

Taking $R_{ON} = 10 \; \Omega$ (typical 555 discharge transistor saturation resistance):

$$t_{recovery} \approx 3 \times R_{ON} \times C = 3 \times 10 \times 0.47 \times 10^{-6} = 14.1 \; \mu s$$

**Step 3 — Maximum duty cycle (2 marks):**

Maximum duty cycle is:

$$D_{max} = \frac{T}{T + t_{recovery}} \times 100\% = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-3} + 14.1 \times 10^{-6}} \times 100\%$$
$$D_{max} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{5.0141 \times 10^{-3}} \times 100\% \approx 99.72\%$$

This confirms that the duty cycle can approach but never reach 100%, due to the finite recovery time.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Pin connection mix-up** — In astable, pins 2 and 6 are TIED; in monostable, pin 2 is **separate** and pin 6 is tied to pin 7. Mixing these connections is the most common cause of 1–2 mark deductions across the entire answer sheet.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 for $R_B$** — When computing the total astable period, students frequently write $T = 0.693(R_A + R_B)C$ instead of $0.693(R_A + 2R_B)C$. The 2 appears because R_B conducts during **both** charging and discharging phases.
> 3. **Using 0.632 instead of 0.693** — Some students erroneously confuse the 63.2% time-constant mark with the 50% (one-half) threshold mark. The capacitor only needs to charge to **half** its way between thresholds, so the correct factor is $\ln 2 = 0.693$, not $1$.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert units** — KTU answer sheets lose easy marks when students mix $\mu F$, $nF$, $k\Omega$, and $M\Omega$ without consistent SI unit conversion. Always convert to **Farads** and **Ohms** before plugging in.
> 5. **The 0.01 µF bypass capacitor on Pin 5** — Drawing the circuit without this bypass is a 0.5 mark deduction in KTU valuation. It is mandatory to prevent noise injection into the upper comparator reference.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ A **multivibrator** is a two-stage regenerative switching circuit producing rectangular waves.
- ⭐ Three types: **Astable** (no stable state, free-running), **Monostable** (one stable state), **Bistable** (two stable states).
- ⭐ The **555 Timer** has 8 pins; the name derives from its three internal **5 kΩ** resistors forming a voltage divider.
- ⭐ Internal references: $(2/3)V_{CC}$ at upper comparator, $(1/3)V_{CC}$ at lower comparator (controllable via Pin 5).
- ⭐ **Astable — Pin Connections:** R_A from V_CC to Pin 7; R_B from Pin 7 to Pins 6 & 2 (tied); C from Pins 6/2 to GND.
- ⭐ **Monostable — Pin Connections:** R from V_CC to Pins 7 & 6 (tied); C from Pin 6 to GND; Pin 2 = external trigger.
- ⭐ **Astable formulas:** $t_C = 0.693(R_A + R_B)C$, $t_D = 0.693 R_B C$, $T = 0.693(R_A + 2R_B)C$, $f = 1.45/[(R_A + 2R_B)C]$.
- ⭐ **Duty cycle formula:** $D = (R_A + R_B)/(R_A + 2R_B) \times 100\%$ — **never reaches 100%** in 555 astable.
- ⭐ **Monostable pulse width:** $T = 1.1 R C$ (where 1.1 ≈ ln 3).
- ⭐ **Astable capacitor swings** between $(1/3)V_{CC}$ and $(2/3)V_{CC}$.
- ⭐ **Monostable capacitor swings** between **0 V** and $(2/3)V_{CC}$.
- ⭐ Output current capability: up to **200 mA** source or sink.
- ⭐ Supply voltage range: **+4.5 V to +16 V** (nominal +5 V or +15 V).
- ⭐ Maximum astable frequency: ~**500 kHz** (practical) or ~3 MHz (theoretical).
- ⭐ Pin 4 (Reset) is active-LOW; tied to V_CC in normal operation.
- ⭐ Pin 5 (Control Voltage) must be bypassed with **0.01 µF** to GND to prevent noise.
- ⭐ The discharge transistor (Pin 7) is an **open-collector NPN**, so external pull-up is not needed — internal R_A handles it.
- ⭐ The factor **0.693 = ln 2** comes from the capacitor charging to **half** its target range.
- ⭐ The factor **1.1 = ln 3** comes from the capacitor charging from **0** to **2/3** of V_CC.
- ⭐ Practical applications: **Astable** → clock generators, LED flashers, PWM, tone generators. **Monostable** → missing-pulse detectors, switch debouncers, delay timers, pulse stretchers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT402 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wave Generators - Triangular and Sawtooth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Triangular Wave Generator — Formal Definition

A **Triangular Wave Generator** is a non-sinusoidal oscillator circuit that produces a periodic output waveform whose amplitude varies linearly between two threshold levels with constant positive and negative slopes, resulting in a symmetric triangular shape. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, it is constructed by cascading a **Schmitt Trigger (Regenerative Comparator)** with a **Miller Integrator**, both implemented using operational amplifiers. The comparator generates a square wave which is then integrated to produce the triangular waveform.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCEBT402 — Module 3):** The triangular wave generator is a *function generator building block*. It is the standard first-stage output of every commercial function generator IC (e.g., **ICL8038, XR-2206, MAX038**).

### 1.2 Sawtooth Wave Generator — Formal Definition

A **Sawtooth Wave Generator** produces a periodic waveform that ramps up (or down) linearly with time, followed by a very rapid flyback (or rise) transition. It is essentially an asymmetrical triangular wave where the rising slope differs vastly from the falling slope. Mathematically, it can be generated by charging a capacitor with a **constant current source** and rapidly discharging it through a switch controlled by a comparator.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Bucket and Trip Switch":**
> - Imagine filling a water **bucket** at a steady rate (constant current charging a capacitor). The water level rises **linearly** with time — this is your **ramp**.
> - When the water hits a high-level sensor, a **trip switch** tips the bucket over (rapid discharge of the capacitor through a transistor switch). The water level snaps back to zero instantly.
> - The cycle repeats. The slow rise + sudden fall = **Sawtooth Wave**.
> - For a **Triangular Wave**, instead of tipping the bucket, you reverse the water flow direction at the high level and fill from the bottom, then reverse again at the low level — yielding **equal rise and fall times**.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bold Highlighted)

- The output amplitude is governed by the **comparator threshold voltage**: $\pm \beta V_{sat}$
- The slope of the triangular wave is determined by the **integrator RC time constant** and the comparator output level: $\frac{V_{sat}}{RC}$
- The frequency of oscillation is controlled by the ratio of the **reference voltage** to the **integrator RC product**: $f_0 = \frac{R_C}{4 R_1 R C}$ (for the op-amp based design)
- **Hysteresis window** of the Schmitt trigger: $V_{H} - V_{L} = 2 \beta V_{sat}$

### 1.5 Block Diagram of the Standard Function Generator

> [!NOTE]
> **Architecture:** A complete function generator = **Comparator (Schmitt)** + **Integrator**. The comparator output is a **Square wave**, the integrator output is a **Triangular wave**, and the Sawtooth is derived by replacing the integrator's input resistor with a **constant current source**.
