## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Triangular Wave Generator — Circuit Topology

The standard op-amp triangular wave generator consists of **two op-amp stages** in a closed loop:

- **Stage 1 — Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger (Comparator with Hysteresis):**
  - Compares the triangular output voltage $v_o(t)$ with **ground (0 V)**.
  - Output saturates at either $+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$.
  - The fraction $\beta$ of the output is fed back to the non-inverting input.

- **Stage 2 — Miller Integrator:**
  - Integrates the comparator square-wave output to generate a linearly rising/falling ramp.
  - The triangular output is fed back to the comparator input to control switching.

- **Closed-Loop Action:** The output of the integrator (triangular wave) is fed back to the comparator input. When it crosses the upper threshold, the comparator flips, reversing the integrator's input polarity, and the cycle repeats.

### 2.2 The Schmitt Trigger Threshold Equations

For a non-inverting Schmitt trigger with input $v_i$ at the inverting terminal, output $v_{sq}$ at the non-inverting feedback node, the input voltage at the non-inverting terminal is:

$$v_+ = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} v_{sq} = \beta v_{sq}$$

When $v_i = v_o$ (triangular wave connected to comparator input), switching occurs when $v_o = \beta v_{sq}$.

- **Upper Threshold (UTP):** $+V_{sat} \cdot \beta$
- **Lower Threshold (LTP):** $-V_{sat} \cdot \beta$

Therefore the **peak-to-peak triangular amplitude** is:

$$V_{opp} = 2 \beta V_{sat}$$

### 2.3 The Integrator Slope Equation

The output of the Miller integrator is given by:

$$v_o(t) = -\frac{1}{RC} \int v_{sq} \, dt$$

If $v_{sq} = +V_{sat}$ for a duration $T/2$, the change in $v_o$ is:

$$\Delta v_o = -\frac{V_{sat}}{RC} \cdot \frac{T}{2}$$

But this $\Delta v_o$ must equal the **peak-to-peak swing** of the triangular wave (from UTP to LTP):

$$2 \beta V_{sat} = \frac{V_{sat}}{RC} \cdot \frac{T}{2}$$

(absolute value, as the swing equals twice the threshold magnitude).

### 2.4 Frequency of Oscillation — Derivation

Rearranging the previous equation for the period $T$:

$$T = 4 R C \beta = 4 R C \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

Therefore the **frequency of the triangular wave** is:

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{R_1 + R_2}{4 R C R_1} = \frac{1}{4 R C} \left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter?** The frequency of the triangular wave can be **independently controlled** by adjusting $R$ (the integrator resistor) without affecting the amplitude — this is a critical design advantage over pure RC oscillators.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Feedback fraction $\beta$ | $\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Dimensionless | Set by resistor divider |
| Upper Threshold (UTP) | $V_{UTP} = +\beta V_{sat}$ | Volts (V) | Switching point (high) |
| Lower Threshold (LTP) | $V_{LTP} = -\beta V_{sat}$ | Volts (V) | Switching point (low) |
| Peak-to-Peak Amplitude | $V_{opp} = 2\beta V_{sat}$ | Volts (V) | Triangle peak-to-peak |
| Integrator Slope | $\dfrac{dv_o}{dt} = \mp \dfrac{V_{sat}}{RC}$ | V/s | Constant ramp slope |
| Period of Oscillation | $T = 4RC\beta$ | Seconds (s) | For symmetric triangle |
| Frequency of Oscillation | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{4RC\beta}$ | Hertz (Hz) | Independently tunable |
| Duty Cycle | $50\%$ | — | For symmetric triangular wave |
| Sawtooth Ramp Slope | $\dfrac{I}{C}$ | V/s | $I$ = constant current |
| Sawtooth Time Period | $T = \dfrac{2 \beta V_{sat} C}{I}$ | Seconds (s) | Time for one cycle |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Applications of Triangular/Sawtooth Waves:**
> - **Time-base generators** in CROs and digital oscilloscopes (sawtooth ramps deflect the electron beam).
> - **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** control signals in motor drives and SMPS.
> - **Sweep generators** in spectrum analyzers.
> - **Phase-locked loop (PLL)** voltage-controlled oscillators (VCOs).
> - **Audio synthesis** and music synthesizers (subtractive synthesis uses sawtooth for "string" and "brass" timbres).
> - **ADC testing** — triangular waves are ideal for ADC linearity measurement.
> - **Class D amplifier** modulation and **function generator ICs** (XR-2206, ICL8038).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Schmitt Trigger Output Levels and Threshold Voltages

**Given:** A non-inverting Schmitt trigger with resistors $R_1$ (input) and $R_2$ (feedback), powered by $\pm V_{sat}$.

**Step 1 — Write the node equation at the non-inverting input (v₊).**

The non-inverting terminal sees $v_{sq}$ through $R_2$ and the input signal $v_i$ through $R_1$. Using superposition or KCL at the $v_+$ node (assuming no current flows into the ideal op-amp input):

$$v_+ = \frac{R_1 \cdot v_{sq} + R_2 \cdot v_i}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 2 — Set the switching condition.**

The output flips when $v_- = v_+$. Since the inverting input is the triangular output $v_o$ in our topology (this is the closed-loop connection), the switching condition becomes:

$$v_o = v_+ = \frac{R_1 \cdot v_{sq} + R_2 \cdot v_o}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $v_{sq} = +V_{sat}$ for UTP calculation.**

$$v_{o,UTP} = \frac{R_1 \cdot V_{sat} + R_2 \cdot v_{o,UTP}}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 4 — Cross-multiply and isolate $v_{o,UTP}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{o,UTP} (R_1 + R_2) &= R_1 V_{sat} + R_2 v_{o,UTP} \\
v_{o,UTP} R_1 + v_{o,UTP} R_2 &= R_1 V_{sat} + R_2 v_{o,UTP} \\
v_{o,UTP} R_1 &= R_1 V_{sat} \\
v_{o,UTP} &= V_{sat}
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — this indicates the Schmitt trigger with the triangular wave directly as input and feedback through $R_2$ gives $V_{UTP} = V_{sat}$. We must clarify the topology: in the **standard KTU textbook configuration**, the triangular output $v_o$ is connected to the **inverting input** of the Schmitt trigger, while $R_1$ and $R_2$ form a feedback divider at the **non-inverting input** from the square wave output.

**Step 5 — Re-derive with the corrected topology.**

Let $v_-$ be connected to the triangular output $v_o$, and $v_+$ is the feedback node. Then:

$$v_+ = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} v_{sq} = \beta v_{sq}$$

The switching condition $v_- = v_+$ gives $v_o = \beta v_{sq}$.

- If $v_{sq} = +V_{sat}$, the triangle must reach $v_o = +\beta V_{sat}$ before the comparator flips → **UTP = $+\beta V_{sat}$**
- If $v_{sq} = -V_{sat}$, the triangle must reach $v_o = -\beta V_{sat}$ before the comparator flips → **LTP = $-\beta V_{sat}$**

**Step 6 — Compute the peak-to-peak amplitude.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{opp} &= V_{UTP} - V_{LTP} \\
V_{opp} &= (+\beta V_{sat}) - (-\beta V_{sat}) \\
V_{opp} &= 2 \beta V_{sat}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{V_{opp} = \frac{2 R_1 V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Period and Frequency of Triangular Wave

**Given:** The integrator has input $v_{sq}$ from the Schmitt trigger output, components $R$ and $C$.

**Step 1 — Write the integrator output equation.**

$$v_o(t) = -\frac{1}{RC} \int_0^t v_{sq}(\tau) \, d\tau + v_o(0)$$

**Step 2 — Consider one half-cycle where $v_{sq} = +V_{sat}$.**

The integrator ramps **downward** linearly:

$$v_o(t) = -\frac{V_{sat}}{RC} \cdot t + v_o(0)$$

**Step 3 — Set initial condition and final condition.**

- Initial condition at $t = 0$: $v_o(0) = +\beta V_{sat}$ (the wave just hit the UTP)
- Final condition at $t = T/2$: $v_o(T/2) = -\beta V_{sat}$ (it must reach the LTP)

**Step 4 — Substitute both conditions into the integrator equation.**

$$-\beta V_{sat} = -\frac{V_{sat}}{RC} \cdot \frac{T}{2} + (+\beta V_{sat})$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $T$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\beta V_{sat} - \beta V_{sat} &= -\frac{V_{sat} \cdot T}{2RC} \\
-2 \beta V_{sat} &= -\frac{V_{sat} \cdot T}{2RC} \\
2 \beta &= \frac{T}{2RC} \\
T &= 4 R C \beta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $\beta = R_1/(R_1 + R_2)$.**

$$T = 4 R C \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{4 R C R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 7 — Frequency.**

$$\boxed{f_0 = \frac{R_1 + R_2}{4 R C R_1} = \frac{1}{4 R C} \left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Sawtooth Wave Generator Period

**Given:** A sawtooth generator uses a constant current source $I$ charging a capacitor $C$, with a transistor switch triggered by a comparator at threshold $\pm \beta V_{sat}$.

**Step 1 — The voltage across the capacitor during charging.**

$$v_c(t) = \frac{1}{C} \int_0^t I \, d\tau = \frac{I \cdot t}{C}$$

(Linear ramp since $I$ is constant.)

**Step 2 — The capacitor charges from $-\beta V_{sat}$ to $+\beta V_{sat}$.**

The total voltage swing is $2 \beta V_{sat}$.

**Step 3 — Set the charging time equal to the period $T$ (for one ramp).**

$$2 \beta V_{sat} = \frac{I \cdot T}{C}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $T$.**

$$\boxed{T = \frac{2 \beta V_{sat} C}{I} = \frac{2 R_1 C V_{sat}}{I (R_1 + R_2)}}$$

**Step 5 — Frequency of sawtooth wave.**

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{I (R_1 + R_2)}{2 R_1 C V_{sat}}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Symmetric Triangle vs. Sawtooth Comparison

| Feature | Triangular Wave Generator | Sawtooth Wave Generator |
|---|---|---|
| Waveform shape | Symmetric — equal rise & fall time | Asymmetric — slow ramp + sudden flyback |
| Construction | Schmitt trigger + Miller integrator | Schmitt trigger + constant current source + transistor switch |
| Integrator element | Resistor $R$ in series with $C$ | Current source $I$ charging $C$ |
| Output slope | $\pm V_{sat}/(RC)$ | $I/C$ (positive) + instantaneous (negative flyback) |
| Frequency formula | $f_0 = \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{4 R C R_1}$ | $f_0 = \dfrac{I (R_1 + R_2)}{2 R_1 C V_{sat}}$ |
| Duty cycle | $50\%$ | Variable — depends on charging vs. discharge time |
| Amplitude control | Independent (set by $\beta$ and $V_{sat}$) | Same — set by Schmitt thresholds |
| Frequency control | By varying $R$ only | By varying $I$ (current source) |
| Primary application | Function generators, PWM, PLL | CRO time-base, sweep circuits |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Define a triangular wave generator. List any two applications of triangular wave generators.**

**Model Answer:**

A triangular wave generator is an electronic circuit that produces a periodic waveform with linearly rising and falling segments, having equal rise and fall times and a $50\%$ duty cycle. It is constructed using a Schmitt trigger (regenerative comparator) cascaded with a Miller integrator.

**Applications:**
1. Used in **function generators** to generate test signals.
2. Used in **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** circuits for motor speed control and SMPS.
3. Used as a **VCO** (Voltage Controlled Oscillator) input to PLLs.

*(Any two — 3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks applications)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] Differentiate between a triangular wave and a sawtooth wave.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Triangular Wave | Sawtooth Wave |
|---|---|---|
| Rise time | Equal to fall time | Much greater than fall time |
| Slope symmetry | Symmetric | Asymmetric |
| Flyback | Gradual (linear fall) | Sudden / rapid flyback |
| Generation | Integrator of square wave | Constant current charging + abrupt discharge |
| Duty cycle | $50\%$ (typical) | Variable, less than $50\%$ |
| Application | Function generators, PWM | CRO time-base, sweep generators |

*(3 marks: tabular comparison with key points)*

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **forget the negative sign** in the integrator output, leading to a sign error in the slope.
> - The **duty cycle** is assumed to be $50\%$, but only for the *symmetric* triangular wave. The sawtooth is **NOT** $50\%$.
> - In derivation problems, students must show the **switching condition $v_- = v_+$** explicitly; omitting this loses 2 marks.
> - The **feedback fraction $\beta = R_1/(R_1+R_2)$** must be clearly defined before the UTP/LTP equations; otherwise, the formula sheet substitution is treated as invalid.
> - Drawing the **circuit diagram** is mandatory — 2 to 3 marks are reserved for the schematic alone in KTU's marking scheme.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PCEBT402, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a triangular wave generator using op-amps. Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a triangular wave generator using op-amps to generate a $1 \text{ kHz}$ signal with an output amplitude of $\pm 5 \text{ V}$. Assume the op-amp saturation voltage is $\pm 13 \text{ V}$ and choose $C = 0.01 \mu F$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Circuit Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes)

**Working Principle:**

The circuit consists of two op-amp stages:

1. **Schmitt Trigger (A1):** A non-inverting comparator with positive feedback through resistor $R_2$ from output to non-inverting input, and input resistor $R_1$ connecting to the inverting input. It produces a square wave $V_{sq} = \pm V_{sat}$.

2. **Miller Integrator (A2):** An inverting integrator with input resistor $R$ and feedback capacitor $C$. It integrates the square wave to produce a triangular wave $V_o$.

**Closed-Loop Operation:**

- Assume initially $V_{sq} = +V_{sat}$. The integrator output ramps **downward** linearly.
- The triangular wave $V_o$ is fed back to the inverting input of A1.
- When $V_o$ falls to $V_{LTP} = -\beta V_{sat}$, A1 flips and $V_{sq}$ switches to $-V_{sat}$.
- Now the integrator ramps **upward** linearly.
- When $V_o$ rises to $V_{UTP} = +\beta V_{sat}$, A1 flips back, and the cycle repeats.

**Derivation of Frequency:**

The integrator output during one half-cycle (where $V_{sq} = +V_{sat}$):

$$V_o(t) = -\frac{V_{sat}}{RC} \cdot t + \beta V_{sat}$$

At $t = T/2$, $V_o$ must equal $-\beta V_{sat}$:

$$-\beta V_{sat} = -\frac{V_{sat} \cdot T}{2RC} + \beta V_{sat}$$

Solving:

$$T = 4 R C \beta = \frac{4 R C R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{R_1 + R_2}{4 R C R_1} = \frac{1}{4 R C} \left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)$$

**Amplitude:**

$$V_{opp} = 2 \beta V_{sat} = \frac{2 R_1 V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2}$$

*(7 marks: 2 marks for circuit + 2 marks for working explanation + 3 marks for derivation)*

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $f_0 = 1 \text{ kHz} \Rightarrow T = 1 \text{ ms}$
- $V_o = \pm 5 \text{ V} \Rightarrow V_{opp} = 10 \text{ V}$
- $V_{sat} = \pm 13 \text{ V}$
- $C = 0.01 \mu F = 10^{-8} \text{ F}$

**Step 1 — Find $\beta$ from the amplitude equation.**

$$V_{opp} = 2 \beta V_{sat}$$

$$10 = 2 \cdot \beta \cdot 13$$

$$\beta = \frac{10}{26} = \frac{5}{13} \approx 0.3846$$

**Step 2 — Choose $R_1$ and find $R_2$.**

Let $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$.

$$\beta = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = 0.3846$$

$$0.3846 (R_1 + R_2) = R_1$$

$$0.3846 \cdot 10\text{k} + 0.3846 \cdot R_2 = 10\text{k}$$

$$0.3846 \cdot R_2 = 10\text{k} (1 - 0.3846) = 6.154 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$R_2 = \frac{6.154}{0.3846} \approx 16 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Find $R$ from the period equation.**

$$T = 4 R C \beta$$

$$10^{-3} = 4 \cdot R \cdot 10^{-8} \cdot 0.3846$$

$$R = \frac{10^{-3}}{4 \cdot 10^{-8} \cdot 0.3846} = \frac{10^{-3}}{1.5384 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$R \approx 65 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Final Design Values:**
- $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$
- $R_2 = 16 \text{ k}\Omega$
- $R = 65 \text{ k}\Omega$
- $C = 0.01 \mu F$

*(7 marks: 2 marks for given data + 2 marks for $\beta$ + 3 marks for $R_1$, $R_2$, $R$ calculation)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PCEBT402, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the working of a sawtooth wave generator using an op-amp. Derive the expression for the time period of oscillation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sawtooth wave generator uses a constant current of $2 \text{ mA}$ to charge a capacitor of $0.1 \mu F$. The Schmitt trigger has $R_1 = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, and $V_{sat} = \pm 12 \text{ V}$. Calculate (i) the peak-to-peak amplitude of the sawtooth, and (ii) the frequency of oscillation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Circuit Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.4 of these notes)

**Working Principle:**

A sawtooth wave generator consists of:
1. A **Schmitt trigger (A1)** with a non-inverting comparator configuration.
2. A **constant current source** (typically using a BJT with emitter resistor) that linearly charges a capacitor $C$.
3. A **transistor switch Q1** whose base is driven by the Schmitt trigger output. When $V_{sq}$ goes high, Q1 saturates and **rapidly discharges** the capacitor (flyback).

**Operating Cycle:**

- **Charging Phase:** When the Schmitt output is $-V_{sat}$, the transistor Q1 is OFF, and the constant current source $I$ charges the capacitor linearly: $V_c(t) = V_c(0) + (I/C) \cdot t$.
- **Threshold Trigger:** When $V_c$ reaches $V_{UTP} = +\beta V_{sat}$, the comparator flips. $V_{sq}$ goes to $+V_{sat}$.
- **Discharge Phase:** Q1 turns ON, providing a low-impedance path for the capacitor to discharge almost instantaneously to a low value (e.g., $V_{LTP}$).
- **Cycle Repeats:** When $V_c$ falls below $V_{LTP} = -\beta V_{sat}$, the comparator flips back, and charging resumes.

**Derivation of Period:**

The voltage across the capacitor during charging (linear ramp):

$$V_c(t) = \frac{I \cdot t}{C}$$

Total swing required: $V_{UTP} - V_{LTP} = 2 \beta V_{sat}$.

Setting $V_c(T) = 2 \beta V_{sat}$:

$$T = \frac{2 \beta V_{sat} \cdot C}{I} = \frac{2 R_1 C V_{sat}}{I (R_1 + R_2)}$$

Frequency:

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{I (R_1 + R_2)}{2 R_1 C V_{sat}}$$

**Slope of the Ramp:**

$$\frac{dV_c}{dt} = \frac{I}{C}$$

*(7 marks: 2 marks for circuit + 2 marks for cycle explanation + 3 marks for derivation)*

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $I = 2 \text{ mA} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}$
- $C = 0.1 \mu F = 10^{-7} \text{ F}$
- $R_1 = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$
- $V_{sat} = \pm 12 \text{ V}$

**Step 1 — Compute $\beta$.**

$$\beta = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{5}{5 + 10} = \frac{5}{15} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$$

**Step 2 — Compute the peak-to-peak amplitude.**

$$V_{opp} = 2 \beta V_{sat} = 2 \times \frac{1}{3} \times 12 = 8 \text{ V}$$

So the sawtooth amplitude is $\pm 4 \text{ V}$.

**Step 3 — Compute the period.**

$$T = \frac{2 \beta V_{sat} \cdot C}{I} = \frac{8 \times 10^{-7}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.4 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the frequency.**

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{4 \times 10^{-4}} = 2500 \text{ Hz} = 2.5 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 5 — Verify the ramp slope.**

$$\text{Slope} = \frac{I}{C} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-7}} = 2 \times 10^{4} \text{ V/s}$$

*(7 marks: 2 marks for $\beta$ + 2 marks for amplitude + 2 marks for period/frequency + 1 mark for ramp slope verification)*

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] Triangular wave generator = **Schmitt trigger + Miller integrator** (closed loop).
- [ ] Square wave output of the comparator becomes the **triangular wave** at the integrator output.
- [ ] **Feedback fraction**: $\beta = R_1 / (R_1 + R_2)$ — set the **amplitude** of the triangle.
- [ ] **UTP = $+\beta V_{sat}$** and **LTP = $-\beta V_{sat}$** — these are the switching thresholds.
- [ ] **Peak-to-peak amplitude**: $V_{opp} = 2 \beta V_{sat}$.
- [ ] **Period**: $T = 4 R C \beta$; **Frequency**: $f_0 = (R_1 + R_2) / (4 R C R_1)$.
- [ ] **Frequency and amplitude are independently tunable** — frequency by $R$, amplitude by $\beta$.
- [ ] Sawtooth wave = **constant current source + capacitor + transistor switch** for rapid discharge.
- [ ] Sawtooth **ramp slope** = $I / C$ (constant, not $V_{sat}/RC$).
- [ ] Sawtooth **period**: $T = 2 \beta V_{sat} C / I$.
- [ ] Triangular wave has **$50\%$ duty cycle**; sawtooth has **asymmetric duty cycle**.
- [ ] Always **draw the circuit diagram first** in the exam — 2–3 marks reserved.
- [ ] **Op-amp saturation voltage $V_{sat}$** is typically $1$ to $2 \text{ V}$ less than the supply rail.
- [ ] **Square wave generator** and **triangular wave generator** share the same Schmitt trigger — only the second stage changes (integrator vs. simple comparator load).
- [ ] For IC function generators (e.g., **ICL8038**), the triangular wave is the master waveform; the square wave is derived from the comparator output directly.
- [ ] **Sawtooth applications**: CRO time-base, TV horizontal/vertical sweep, voltage-controlled oscillators, ADC ramp-compare converters.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RC Phase Shift and Wien Bridge Oscillators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT402 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** RC Phase Shift and Wien Bridge Oscillators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — RC Phase Shift Oscillator (RCPSO)

The **RC Phase Shift Oscillator** is a sinusoidal low-frequency oscillator that uses a combination of an inverting amplifier (CE BJT stage or op-amp) and a **three-section RC ladder network** to generate a sustained sine wave. Each RC section contributes a phase shift, and the three together provide exactly **180°**, which, combined with the amplifier's 180°, satisfies the **Barkhausen Criterion** for sustained oscillation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** RC Phase Shift Oscillator using **BJT (CE configuration)** and **Op-Amp (inverting mode)** — both are part of Module 3 deliverables.

## 1.2 Formal Definition — Wien Bridge Oscillator (WBO)

The **Wien Bridge Oscillator** is a low-to-audio frequency oscillator that uses a non-inverting amplifier and a frequency-selective **lead-lag network** composed of one series RC arm and one parallel RC arm. At the resonant frequency, the network provides **zero phase shift**, and the non-inverting amplifier supplies the required gain (with no phase inversion) to satisfy the Barkhausen Criterion.

> [!NOTE]
> The Wien Bridge is widely used in **audio signal generators (20 Hz – 20 kHz)** and is the canonical "audio oscillator" studied in KTU labs.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 1 — The "Push-Me-Pull-You" Swing (RCPSO):**
> Imagine three children sitting on a swing chain — child A pushes child B, B pushes C, and C pushes the original pusher. Each child delays the push by **60°** (a 1/6 cycle lag). Three such delays equal a **half-cycle (180°)**. To sustain the swing forever, the originator must also push back after another 180° delay. Total loop = 360° → oscillation. The amplifier plays the role of the original pusher (the "180° plus gain" element).

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 2 — The Tuned Tuning Fork (WBO):**
> A tuning fork vibrates only at its natural frequency, ignoring all others. The Wien Bridge's RC lead-lag network behaves identically: it "listens" for **one specific frequency** and forwards it perfectly (0° lag) while attenuating everything else. The op-amp then amplifies this preferred frequency back to the input, reinforcing it forever.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Barkhausen Loop Phase** | **0° (or 360°)** | Total phase around feedback loop |
| **Barkhausen Loop Gain** | **$\vert A \beta \vert \geq 1$** | Minimum loop gain for oscillation |
| **RCPSO Frequency Range** | **1 Hz – 100 kHz** | Low-frequency audio / sub-audio |
| **WBO Frequency Range** | **20 Hz – 20 kHz** | Audio band (human hearing) |
| **RCPSO Required Gain** | **$\vert A \vert = 29$** | For BJT CE / inverting op-amp |
| **WBO Required Gain** | **$\vert A \vert = 3$** | Non-inverting op-amp |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For oscillation to **start up**, the loop gain must **slightly exceed 1** (i.e., $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$). Amplitude-limiting non-linearities (e.g., diode clamps, thermistors, or bulb) bring it back to exactly unity for steady-state operation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Barkhausen Criterion (Foundational Principle)

Any sinusoidal oscillator must satisfy **both** conditions simultaneously:

1. **Loop Phase Condition:** Total phase shift around the closed loop = $0°$ or an integer multiple of $360°$.

$$\phi_A + \phi_\beta = 0° \text{ (or } n \times 360°)$$

2. **Loop Gain Condition:** Magnitude of the loop gain must be at least unity.

$$\vert A \beta \vert \geq 1$$

where $A$ is the amplifier voltage gain and $\beta$ is the feedback network transfer function.

---

## 2.2 RC Phase Shift Oscillator — Operational Logic

### Why three sections?
- Each RC high-pass section provides **less than 90°** of phase lag.
- Two sections can give at most **180°**, but with $|β| \to 0$ (no useful feedback).
- **Three identical sections** provide exactly **180°** at a finite, useful frequency where $\vert β \vert = 1/29$.

### How does it work?
- The amplifier (BJT in CE mode or op-amp inverting mode) provides **180°** phase inversion.
- The three-section RC ladder provides another **180°**.
- **Total loop phase = 360°** → Barkhausen satisfied.
- **Loop gain:** $A \times (1/29) = 1 \Rightarrow A = 29$.

### Frequency-of-Oscillation Logic (Step-by-Step Intuition)
- The three sections behave like three cascaded **first-order high-pass filters**.
- As frequency increases, each filter contributes **more lag** (approaching 90°).
- At a specific frequency $\omega_0$, the lag becomes **exactly 60° per section → 180° total**.
- This is the natural oscillation frequency.

---

## 2.3 Wien Bridge Oscillator — Operational Logic

### Why a lead-lag network?
- A single RC section gives **at most 90°** of phase shift — useful only for $\pm 90°$, not $0°$.
- A **series RC (Z₁)** combined with a **parallel RC (Z₂)** forms a **lead-lag network**:
  - Below resonance → capacitive (lag).
  - Above resonance → capacitive/inductive mix (lead).
  - **At resonance → purely resistive → 0° phase shift**.

### How does it work?
- The op-amp is wired as a **non-inverting amplifier** (gain $A = 1 + R_f/R_1$).
- The lead-lag network is the **positive feedback** element; a resistive divider is the **negative feedback** element.
- At $f_0$, the network is purely resistive, $\beta = 1/3$, so $A = 3$ satisfies the criterion.

### Frequency-of-Oscillation Logic
- At very low frequency → series capacitor dominates → network is **capacitive**.
- At very high frequency → parallel capacitor shorts out → network is **capacitive**.
- In between, there is a single frequency where the network is **purely resistive** — the resonance point.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### A. RC Phase Shift Oscillator (using BJT or Inverting Op-Amp)

| Quantity | Formula | Boundary / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency of Oscillation | $$f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$$ | Hz, where $R = R_1 = R_2 = R_3$, $C = C_1 = C_2 = C_3$ |
| Angular Frequency | $$\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{RC\sqrt{6}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{6}}{RC}$$ | rad/s |
| Feedback Factor (at $f_0$) | $$\beta = -\dfrac{1}{29}$$ | Dimensionless, phase = 180° |
| Magnitude of Feedback | $\vert \beta \vert = 1/29$ | — |
| Minimum Amplifier Gain | $\vert A \vert \geq 29$ | For BJT CE: $A_v \geq 29$; For op-amp: $R_f/R_{in} \geq 29$ |
| Total Loop Phase | $180° + 180° = 360°$ | Barkhausen satisfied |

### B. Wien Bridge Oscillator (using Non-Inverting Op-Amp)

| Quantity | Formula | Boundary / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency of Oscillation | $$f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$$ | Hz, where $R_1 = R_2 = R$, $C_1 = C_2 = C$ |
| Angular Frequency | $\omega_0 = 1/(RC)$ | rad/s |
| Feedback Factor (at $f_0$) | $\beta = 1/3$ | Dimensionless, phase = 0° |
| Minimum Amplifier Gain | $A = 1 + R_f/R_1 \geq 3$ | Op-amp non-inverting |
| Required Resistor Ratio | $R_f / R_1 = 2$ | i.e., $R_f = 2R_1$ |
| Total Loop Phase | $0° + 0° = 0°$ | Barkhausen satisfied |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison Insight:** The WBO is preferred over the RCPSO for **audio frequencies** because (i) it requires only $\beta = 1/3$ (much easier to realize), (ii) it uses only **two capacitors** instead of three, and (iii) it provides a **cleaner sinusoidal output** (less harmonic distortion).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Audio Test Equipment** | WBO is the heart of every audio signal generator (HP 200CD, etc.) |
| **Function Generators** | RCPSO for sub-audio (1 Hz – 1 kHz); WBO for audio band |
| **Phase-Locked Loops** | Both oscillators used as VCO references |
| **Musical Synthesis (Electronic)** | WBO forms the basic tone-generator stage |
| **Biomedical Instrumentation** | Low-frequency RCPSO used in ECG/EEG calibration |
| **Communications Test Sets** | Carrier-frequency references for IF alignment |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 RC Phase Shift Oscillator — Complete Derivation

### Circuit Setup
Consider the standard RCPSO using a BJT in Common-Emitter (CE) configuration:
- **Amplifier output** = $V_o$ (acts as source for the ladder).
- **First RC section:** $R_1$ from $V_o$ to node $a$; $C_1$ from node $a$ to ground.
- **Second RC section:** $R_2$ from node $a$ to node $b$; $C_2$ from node $b$ to ground.
- **Third RC section:** $R_3$ from node $b$ to node $c$ (feedback node); $C_3$ from node $c$ to ground.
- **Feedback voltage:** $V_f$ appears at node $c$ and feeds the amplifier input.

For simplification, set $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C_3 = C$.
Also assume the **amplifier input impedance is infinite** (no current drawn by input) and output impedance is negligible.

### Step 1 — KCL at Node $c$ (Feedback Node)

Current from $b$ to $c$ through $R_3$ = Current into $C_3$ to ground.

$$\frac{V_b - V_c}{R} = V_c \cdot j\omega C$$

Multiply through by $R$:

$$V_b - V_c = j\omega RC \cdot V_c$$

$$V_b = V_c(1 + j\alpha) \quad \text{where } \alpha = \omega RC \tag{1}$$

### Step 2 — KCL at Node $b$

Current from $a$ to $b$ through $R_2$ = Current from $b$ to $c$ + Current to ground through $C_2$.

$$\frac{V_a - V_b}{R} = \frac{V_b - V_c}{R} + V_b \cdot j\omega C$$

Substitute equation (1): $(V_b - V_c)/R = j\alpha V_c$.

$$\frac{V_a - V_b}{R} = j\alpha V_c + j\alpha V_b$$

Multiply by $R$:

$$V_a - V_b = j\alpha V_c + j\alpha V_b$$

$$V_a = V_b(1 + j\alpha) + j\alpha V_c$$

Substitute $V_b = V_c(1 + j\alpha)$ from (1):

$$V_a = V_c(1 + j\alpha)^2 + j\alpha V_c$$

$$V_a = V_c\left[(1 + j\alpha)^2 + j\alpha\right]$$

Expand $(1 + j\alpha)^2 = 1 + 2j\alpha - \alpha^2$:

$$V_a = V_c\left[1 + 2j\alpha - \alpha^2 + j\alpha\right]$$

$$V_a = V_c\left[(1 - \alpha^2) + 3j\alpha\right] \tag{2}$$

### Step 3 — KCL at Node $a$

Current from $V_o$ to $a$ through $R_1$ = Current to ground through $C_1$ + Current from $a$ to $b$ through $R_2$.

$$\frac{V_o - V_a}{R} = V_a \cdot j\omega C + \frac{V_a - V_b}{R}$$

Multiply by $R$:

$$V_o - V_a = j\alpha V_a + (V_a - V_b)$$

$$V_o = V_a + j\alpha V_a + V_a - V_b$$

$$V_o = V_a(2 + j\alpha) - V_b$$

Substitute (2) and (1):

$$V_o = V_c\left[(1 - \alpha^2) + 3j\alpha\right](2 + j\alpha) - V_c(1 + j\alpha)$$

### Step 4 — Expand the Product

Let $X = (1 - \alpha^2) + 3j\alpha$ and $Y = 2 + j\alpha$.

$$X \cdot Y = (1 - \alpha^2)(2) + (1 - \alpha^2)(j\alpha) + 3j\alpha(2) + 3j\alpha(j\alpha)$$

$$= 2(1 - \alpha^2) + j\alpha(1 - \alpha^2) + 6j\alpha - 3\alpha^2$$

$$= (2 - 2\alpha^2 - 3\alpha^2) + j\alpha(1 - \alpha^2 + 6)$$

$$= (2 - 5\alpha^2) + j\alpha(7 - \alpha^2)$$

So:

$$V_o = V_c\left[(2 - 5\alpha^2) + j\alpha(7 - \alpha^2)\right] - V_c(1 + j\alpha)$$

$$V_o = V_c\left[(2 - 5\alpha^2 - 1) + j\alpha(7 - \alpha^2 - 1)\right]$$

$$\boxed{V_o = V_c\left[(1 - 5\alpha^2) + j\alpha(6 - \alpha^2)\right]}$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Barkhausen Phase Condition

The feedback factor is:

$$\beta = \frac{V_c}{V_o} = \frac{1}{(1 - 5\alpha^2) + j\alpha(6 - \alpha^2)}$$

For oscillation, the **phase of $\beta$ must be 180°** (so it cancels the amplifier's 180°). This happens when the denominator is **purely real and negative**, i.e., its imaginary part is zero:

$$6 - \alpha^2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha^2 = 6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = \sqrt{6}$$

So $\omega_0 RC = \sqrt{6}$, giving:

$$\omega_0 = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{RC}$$

$$\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}}$$

### Step 6 — Apply the Barkhausen Gain Condition

Substitute $\alpha^2 = 6$ into the denominator:

$$1 - 5\alpha^2 = 1 - 5(6) = 1 - 30 = -29$$

Therefore:

$$\beta = \frac{1}{-29} = -\frac{1}{29}$$

The magnitude:

$$|\beta| = \frac{1}{29}$$

For $\vert A\beta \vert \geq 1$:

$$|A| \cdot \frac{1}{29} \geq 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{|A| \geq 29}$$

This is the required minimum gain of the inverting amplifier.

---

## 3.2 Wien Bridge Oscillator — Complete Derivation

### Circuit Setup
- **Series arm:** $Z_1 = R_1 + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_1}$ (resistor + capacitor in **series**).
- **Parallel arm:** $Z_2 = R_2 \parallel \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_2}$ (resistor + capacitor in **parallel**).
- Assume $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$.

### Step 1 — Compute Impedances

$$Z_1 = R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}$$

$$Z_2 = \frac{R \cdot \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}}{R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}} = \frac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Feedback Factor

The feedback network is a **voltage divider** between $Z_1$ and $Z_2$:

$$\beta = \frac{V_f}{V_o} = \frac{Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2}$$

Substitute:

$$\beta = \frac{\dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}}{\left(R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}\right) + \dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}}$$

### Step 3 — Common Denominator Manipulation

Multiply numerator and denominator by $j\omega C(1 + j\omega RC)$:

**Numerator:**

$$R \cdot j\omega C = j\omega RC$$

**Denominator:**

$$\left[R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}\right] \cdot j\omega C(1 + j\omega RC) + R \cdot j\omega C$$

Term-by-term:

$$R \cdot j\omega C (1 + j\omega RC) = j\omega RC + j^2 \omega^2 R^2 C^2 = j\omega RC - \omega^2 R^2 C^2$$

$$\frac{1}{j\omega C} \cdot j\omega C (1 + j\omega RC) = 1 + j\omega RC$$

$$R \cdot j\omega C = j\omega RC$$

**Sum:**

$$(j\omega RC - \omega^2 R^2 C^2) + (1 + j\omega RC) + (j\omega RC)$$

$$= (1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2) + 3j\omega RC$$

So:

$$\beta = \frac{j\omega RC}{(1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2) + 3j\omega RC}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Barkhausen Phase Condition

For oscillation with a **non-inverting** amplifier, the phase of $\beta$ must be **0°** at $f_0$. This requires the **denominator to be purely imaginary** (since the numerator $j\omega RC$ is already purely imaginary, the ratio becomes real only if the denominator's real part vanishes):

$$1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega^2 R^2 C^2 = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega RC = 1$$

$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC}$$

$$\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Barkhausen Gain Condition

Substitute $\omega RC = 1$ into the denominator:

$$(1 - 1) + 3j(1) = 3j$$

$$\beta = \frac{j(1)}{3j} = \frac{1}{3}$$

So:

$$|\beta| = \frac{1}{3}$$

For $\vert A\beta \vert \geq 1$:

$$|A| \geq 3$$

Since the non-inverting op-amp gain is $A = 1 + R_f / R_1$:

$$1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} \geq 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\frac{R_f}{R_1} \geq 2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, $R_f$ is often implemented using a **non-linear element** (a small incandescent bulb, thermistor, or back-to-back Zener diodes) so that the gain slightly exceeds 3 at startup (ensuring oscillation build-up) and self-adjusts to exactly 3 in steady state (ensuring low distortion).

---

## 3.3 Comparison Matrix — RCPSO vs WBO

| Parameter | RC Phase Shift Oscillator | Wien Bridge Oscillator |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedback Network** | 3 RC sections (high-pass) | 1 series RC + 1 parallel RC |
| **Number of Capacitors** | 3 | 2 |
| **Frequency Formula** | $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC\sqrt{6})$ | $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$ |
| **Feedback Factor $\vert β \vert$** | $1/29$ | $1/3$ |
| **Required Amplifier Gain** | $\geq 29$ | $\geq 3$ |
| **Amplifier Type** | Inverting (CE BJT or inverting op-amp) | Non-inverting op-amp |
| **Loop Phase** | $180° + 180° = 360°$ | $0° + 0° = 0°$ |
| **Typical Frequency Range** | 1 Hz – 100 kHz | 20 Hz – 20 kHz (audio) |
| **Output Distortion** | Higher (harder to stabilize) | Lower (easy amplitude control) |
| **KTU Lab Implementation** | Both BJT and Op-amp variants | Standard op-amp circuit |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture — Generalized Oscillator Feedback Loop

The following block diagram captures the canonical structure of every sinusoidal oscillator, applicable to both the RCPSO and the WBO.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Amplifier A with Phase Inversion or Non-Inversion] --> B[Output Node Vo]
    B --> C[Feedback Network Beta j omega]
    C --> D[Feedback Voltage Vf]
    D --> E[Comparator with Barkhausen Criteria]
    E --> F{Loop Phase equals 0 or 360 degrees}
    E --> G{Loop Gain magnitude greater than or equal 1}
    F --> H[Sustained Sinusoidal Oscillation]
    G --> H
    H -.Amplitude Limiting via Nonlinearity.-> A
```

## 4.2 RC Phase Shift Oscillator — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[BJT CE Amplifier Gain Av negative with 180 phase shift] --> B[Output Vo]
    B --> R1A[Resistor R Section 1] --> nodeA[Node a]
    nodeA --> C1A[Capacitor C to Ground Section 1]
    nodeA --> R2A[Resistor R Section 2] --> nodeB[Node b]
    nodeB --> C2A[Capacitor C to Ground Section 2]
    nodeB --> R3A[Resistor R Section 3] --> nodeC[Feedback Node]
    nodeC --> C3A[Capacitor C to Ground Section 3]
    nodeC --> FB[Feedback Vf to Amplifier Input]
```

**Signal Flow Legend:**
- Each **RC section** (R-series, C-to-ground) is a first-order low-pass network that contributes up to 90° lag.
- Three such sections produce a combined **180° phase lag** at $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC\sqrt{6})$.
- The BJT CE amplifier adds another **180°** (signal inversion).
- **Total loop phase = 360°** → oscillation.

## 4.3 Wien Bridge Oscillator — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Op-Amp Non-Inverting Gain A equals 1 plus Rf over R1] --> B[Output Vo]
    B --> Z1[Series Arm Z1 equals R plus 1 over j omega C] --> nodeX[Node X Lead-Lag Junction]
    nodeX --> Z2[Parallel Arm Z2 equals R parallel to 1 over j omega C] --> GNDA[Ground]
    nodeX --> FB[Feedback Vf equals Vo divided by 3 at f0]
    FB --> A
    A -.Negative Feedback Divider.-> RFB[Resistor R1] --> GNDB[Ground]
    A -.Negative Feedback Divider.-> RFF[Resistor Rf] --> A
```

**Signal Flow Legend:**
- The **lead-lag network** ($Z_1$ series + $Z_2$ parallel) acts as a band-pass filter centered at $f_0$.
- At $f_0$, the network is **purely resistive**, providing $\beta = 1/3$ with **0° phase shift**.
- The op-amp in non-inverting mode provides gain $A = 3$ with **0° phase shift**.
- **Total loop phase = 0°**, loop gain = $3 \times (1/3) = 1$ → Barkhausen satisfied.

## 4.4 Decision Flow — Which Oscillator to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Need a Sinusoidal Oscillator]) --> Q1{Operating Frequency Band?}
    Q1 -- Sub-audio 1 Hz to 1 kHz --> RCPSO[Use RC Phase Shift Oscillator]
    Q1 -- Audio 20 Hz to 20 kHz --> Q2{Available Amplifier Type?}
    Q2 -- BJT or Inverting Op-Amp --> RCPSO2[RCPSO Still Viable]
    Q2 -- Non-Inverting Op-Amp --> WBO[Use Wien Bridge Oscillator]
    Q1 -- Above 100 kHz --> LC[Use LC or Crystal Oscillator]
    RCPSO --> Note1[Requires Gain of 29 and 3 RC Sections]
    WBO --> Note2[Requires Gain of 3 and Only 2 Capacitors]
    Note1 --> End([Build the Circuit])
    Note2 --> End
    RCPSO2 --> End
    LC --> End
```

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation Tip:** The WBO is the **default choice** for KTU lab experiments in audio-frequency generation. The RCPSO is studied both as a BJT circuit (CE mode, $A_v \geq 29$) and as an op-amp circuit (inverting mode, $R_f/R_{in} = 29$).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State the Barkhausen Criterion for sustained oscillation. List the two conditions that any sinusoidal oscillator must satisfy.**

**Model Answer:**
The Barkhausen Criterion specifies the conditions for sustained sinusoidal oscillation in a feedback amplifier:

1. **Loop Phase Condition:** The total phase shift around the closed feedback loop must be zero (or an integer multiple of $360°$):
$$\phi_A + \phi_\beta = 0° \pmod{360°}$$

2. **Loop Gain Condition:** The magnitude of the loop gain must be at least unity:
$$|A\beta| \geq 1$$

Both conditions must hold simultaneously at the frequency of oscillation $f_0$.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Compare RC Phase Shift and Wien Bridge Oscillators on the basis of (i) feedback network, (ii) frequency formula, and (iii) amplifier gain required.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | RCPSO | WBO |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedback Network** | 3 RC sections (high-pass ladder) | 1 series RC + 1 parallel RC (lead-lag) |
| **Frequency Formula** | $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC\sqrt{6})$ | $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$ |
| **Amplifier Gain** | $\vert A \vert \geq 29$ (inverting) | $A \geq 3$ (non-inverting) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part B question below carries **14 marks** total, divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. KTU 2024 Scheme regulations mandate **internal choice** (either–or format) at the Part B level.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — RC Phase Shift Oscillator Focus

#### Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024] (Module 3)

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of an **RC Phase Shift Oscillator using a BJT in CE configuration**. Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation and the minimum gain required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design an RC Phase Shift Oscillator using an **op-amp** to generate a sine wave of frequency **2 kHz**. Choose $C = 10$ nF. Determine the values of all resistors and verify the Barkhausen Criterion. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q3(a):

**Circuit Operation:**
- The BJT in CE configuration acts as an **inverting amplifier** with gain $A_v$ providing 180° phase shift.
- The collector output drives a **three-section RC ladder network**.
- Each RC section (R-series, C-to-ground) is a **first-order low-pass** filter contributing phase lag.
- At $f_0$, each section provides **60° lag** (180° total), satisfying the Barkhausen phase condition with the amplifier's 180°.

**Derivation (concise form, full derivation in Section 3.1):**
- Apply KCL at the three RC nodes (a, b, c) with $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C_3 = C$.
- After algebraic simplification, the feedback factor is:

$$\beta = \frac{1}{(1 - 5\alpha^2) + j\alpha(6 - \alpha^2)}, \quad \alpha = \omega RC$$

- Phase condition: $6 - \alpha^2 = 0 \Rightarrow \alpha = \sqrt{6} \Rightarrow \omega_0 RC = \sqrt{6}$.

$$\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}}$$

- At this frequency, $\beta = -1/29$.
- Gain condition: $\vert A \vert \geq 29$.

**Minimum Gain of BJT CE Stage:**
The voltage gain $A_v$ of a CE amplifier is approximately $-R_C/R_E$ (with bypass capacitor) or $-g_m R_C$. To achieve $A_v = 29$, the designer must choose $R_C/R_E = 29$ or adjust $R_E$ accordingly.

---

#### Model Solution to Q3(b):

**Given:**
- $f_0 = 2$ kHz $= 2000$ Hz
- $C = 10$ nF $= 10 \times 10^{-9}$ F
- Op-amp based RCPSO (inverting configuration).

**Step 1 — Find $R$ from frequency formula:**

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_0 C \sqrt{6}}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 2000 \times 10 \times 10^{-9} \times 2.4495}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 2000 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.4495}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{3.078 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 3.25 \text{ k}\Omega}$$

(Choose standard value $R = 3.3$ kΩ.)

**Step 2 — Determine Op-Amp Resistor Values:**

For inverting op-amp: $A = -R_f/R_{in}$.

For $\vert A \vert = 29$:
$$\frac{R_f}{R_{in}} = 29$$

Choose $R_{in} = 10$ kΩ $\Rightarrow R_f = 290$ kΩ (use standard 270 kΩ + 20 kΩ in series, or 300 kΩ trim).

**Step 3 — Verify Barkhausen Criterion:**

- Loop gain: $|A\beta| = 29 \times (1/29) = 1$ ✓
- Loop phase: $180°$ (op-amp inversion) + $180°$ (RC ladder) = $360°$ ✓

Both conditions satisfied → sustained oscillation at 2 kHz.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Wien Bridge Oscillator Focus

#### Q4. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] (Module 3)

**(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a **Wien Bridge Oscillator using an op-amp** and explain its operation. Derive the expressions for the frequency of oscillation and the condition for sustained oscillation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Wien Bridge Oscillator is designed with $R = 10$ kΩ and $C = 1$ nF. Calculate (i) the frequency of oscillation, (ii) the feedback factor at resonance, and (iii) the required ratio of feedback resistors $R_f/R_1$. If the op-amp saturates at $\pm 13$ V, determine the peak-to-peak output swing if amplitude stabilization is achieved by a thermistor with $\beta_T = 3900$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q4(a):

**Circuit Operation:**
- The op-amp is wired in **non-inverting mode** with gain $A = 1 + R_f/R_1$.
- The positive feedback network is the **lead-lag network**: $Z_1 = R + 1/(j\omega C)$ in series, $Z_2 = R \parallel 1/(j\omega C)$ in parallel.
- The negative feedback network is the **resistive divider**: $R_1$ and $R_f$.

**Derivation (concise form, full derivation in Section 3.2):**
- $\beta = Z_2/(Z_1 + Z_2)$.
- After manipulation:

$$\beta = \frac{j\omega RC}{(1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2) + 3j\omega RC}$$

- Phase condition: real part of denominator = 0 → $\omega_0 RC = 1$.

$$\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$

- At this frequency, $\beta = 1/3$.
- Gain condition: $A \geq 3 \Rightarrow 1 + R_f/R_1 \geq 3 \Rightarrow R_f/R_1 \geq 2$.

**Amplitude Stabilization:** A **thermistor** (or incandescent bulb) replaces $R_f$ (or $R_1$). At startup, the thermistor is cool → resistance is high → gain > 3 → oscillation builds up. As amplitude grows, the thermistor heats up → resistance drops → gain settles to exactly 3, stabilizing the amplitude.

---

#### Model Solution to Q4(b):

**Given:** $R = 10$ kΩ, $C = 1$ nF $= 10^{-9}$ F.

**(i) Frequency of Oscillation:**

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{1}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{6.283 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\boxed{f_0 \approx 15.92 \text{ kHz}}$$

**(ii) Feedback Factor at Resonance:**

$$\beta = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$$

**(iii) Resistor Ratio for Sustained Oscillation:**

$$A = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\frac{R_f}{R_1} = 2}$$

**Amplitude Stabilization with Thermistor:**
Assume $R_1$ is fixed at 10 kΩ and $R_f$ is replaced by the thermistor $R_T$.

At steady state: $1 + R_T/R_1 = 3 \Rightarrow R_T = 2R_1 = 20$ kΩ.

The thermistor obeys: $R_T = R_0 \exp[\beta_T(1/T - 1/T_0)]$ where $T$ is in Kelvin.

For amplitude stabilization, as output grows, current through $R_T$ heats it, reducing $R_T$ until steady state at 20 kΩ. The peak-to-peak swing is limited by the op-amp saturation rails:

$$V_{out,pp} = 2 \times V_{sat} = 2 \times 13 = 26 \text{ V (peak-to-peak)}$$

In practice, due to non-linearity of the thermistor, the actual swing is **slightly below 26 V** (typically 24 V pp) before distortion becomes noticeable.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the two oscillators:** Students often write $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$ for the RCPSO — **this is the Wien Bridge formula**, not the RCPSO. The RCPSO has $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC\sqrt{6})$. Always write the square root explicitly.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to derive the feedback factor:** In Part B derivations, KTU examiners award **3 out of 7 marks** for the correct KCL setup and β-expression. Skipping these steps costs heavy marks.
>
> 3. **Using $\vert A \vert = 3$ for the RCPSO:** This is the Wien Bridge gain. For the RCPSO, it is **29**. Memorize both distinctly.
>
> 4. **Sign confusion in $\beta$:** For the RCPSO, $\beta = -1/29$ (the negative sign indicates 180° phase shift). Students sometimes write $+1/29$, losing the phase-context marks.
>
> 5. **Resistor selection in design problems:** When asked to "design" an oscillator, you MUST specify **standard E12/E24 values** (e.g., 10 kΩ, 4.7 kΩ, 3.3 kΩ) — not theoretical values like 3.25 kΩ.
>
> 6. **Phase shift confusion:** The three RC sections in the RCPSO are **low-pass** (R-series, C-to-ground), giving **phase lag**, not lead. The WBO's lead-lag network has **lag below $f_0$** and **lead above $f_0$**.
>
> 7. **Skipping the Barkhausen check:** Always end design problems with "Both Barkhausen conditions are satisfied: $\phi_{loop} = 360°, \vert A\beta \vert = 1$" to secure full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 3 (RCPSO & WBO):**

- [x] **Barkhausen Criterion** = Phase = $0°/360°$ AND Gain $\vert A\beta \vert \geq 1$.
- [x] **RCPSO** uses **3 identical RC sections** → each contributes 60° lag → total 180°.
- [x] **RCPSO frequency:** $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC\sqrt{6})$.
- [x] **RCPSO feedback:** $\beta = -1/29$ at $f_0$ (180° phase).
- [x] **RCPSO amplifier:** Inverting, $\vert A \vert \geq 29$.
- [x] **RCPSO with BJT CE:** Choose $R_C/R_E = 29$ for $A_v = 29$.
- [x] **RCPSO with Op-Amp:** $R_f/R_{in} = 29$.
- [x] **WBO** uses **1 series RC + 1 parallel RC** → lead-lag network.
- [x] **WBO frequency:** $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$.
- [x] **WBO feedback:** $\beta = 1/3$ at $f_0$ (0° phase).
- [x] **WBO amplifier:** Non-inverting, $A = 1 + R_f/R_1 \geq 3$.
- [x] **WBO resistor ratio:** $R_f = 2R_1$.
- [x] **Amplitude stabilization** uses thermistor, bulb, or back-to-back Zeners.
- [x] **WBO is preferred** for audio frequencies due to lower distortion and simpler design.
- [x] **RCPSO is preferred** for sub-audio and when BJT-based design is mandated.
- [x] **Always check both Barkhausen conditions** at the end of any derivation problem.
- [x] **Mention "startup gain > 1"** in viva to show understanding of oscillator build-up.
- [x] **Design problems require standard resistor values** — round off your theoretical answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IC Regulators - 723 - Block Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEBT402 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** IC Regulators - 723 - Block Diagram ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **IC 723** (also denoted as **LM723 / µA723**) is a monolithic, floating, multi-purpose **linear voltage regulator** fabricated on a single silicon chip. It belongs to the family of **adjustable positive/negative voltage regulators** and is functionally classified as a **Series Pass Type Linear Voltage Regulator IC** with built-in provisions for current limiting, remote shutdown, and thermal protection. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme – Module 4):** > The IC 723 is treated as a *classical generic regulator building block* because it is **not internally tied to a fixed ground** — it is a **floating regulator**. This means the *entire IC can float on top of the output voltage*, allowing it to regulate voltages up to its maximum input rating, irrespective of whether the output is positive or negative with respect to system ground. **Key datasheet ratings (must memorize for KTU exams):** | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Input–Output Differential Voltage | $V_{in}-V_{out}$ | **40 (max)** | V | | Continuous Output Current (internal) | $I_L$ | **150 (max)** | mA | | Reference Voltage | $V_{REF}$ | **7.15 (typ)** | V | | Line Regulation | $S_V$ | **0.01 %/V** | %/V | | Load Regulation | $R_{out}$ | **0.03 %** | % | | Reference Drift | $\Delta V_{REF}$ | **0.003** | %/°C | | Operating Temperature | $T_A$ | **0 to 70** | °C | | Package | — | **14-pin DIP / TO-100** | — | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Automatic Water-Tap Controller" Imagine a **water tank** mounted on the 5th floor. The water level (output voltage) must be kept constant regardless of how many flats draw water (load current changes) or how much pressure the pump supplies (input line variations). You hire a watchman holding a **measuring glass (reference = 7.15 V)** who constantly compares the tank's water level (feedback) with the mark on the glass. - If the tank level **falls** → watchman opens the inlet valve more → water flows in. - If the tank level **rises** → watchman closes the inlet valve → inflow reduces. This watchman is the **Error Amplifier**, the inlet valve is the **Series Pass Transistor**, the measuring glass is the **Zener Reference**, and a **safety clamp** that shuts the valve if too much current flows is the **Current Limit Circuit**. That, in essence, is how the 723 works — but with **voltage** instead of water. > [!NOTE] > **Why is it called "floating"?** > The IC's internal ground is *not* hardwired to the package ground pin. The user decides where the common terminal sits. This makes one part number usable for **positive regulators, negative regulators, switching pre-regulators, and floating boost configurations** — a unique advantage of the 723. ### 1.3 Standard Package & Pin Identification (14-Pin DIP) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU examiners **frequently** ask for a labelled pin diagram. Memorize the following table — pin numbers are *guaranteed* to appear as a 2-mark sub-question. | Pin No. | Pin Name | Function | | :---: | :---: | :--- | | 1 | NC | No internal connection | | 2 | CL (Current Limit) | Base of current-limit transistor; sense input | | 3 | CS (Current Sense) | Emitter of internal pass / one end of $R_{SC}$ | | 4 | IN$(-)$ | Inverting input of error amplifier | | 5 | IN$(+)$ | Non-inverting input of error amplifier | | 6 | $V_{REF}$ | Reference voltage output (≈ 7.15 V) | | 7 | $-V$ | Negative supply (for split-rail operation) | | 8 | NC | No internal connection | | 9 | $V_C$ | Frequency compensation (connect 100 pF to IN$(-)$) | | 10 | $V_{out}$ | Output of series pass transistor | | 11 | $V^+$ | Positive supply rail | | 12 | $V_{in}$ | Input to series pass transistor (collector) | | 13 | $V_C^{EXT}$ | Base drive for external boost transistor | | 14 | NC | No internal connection | ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Block-Level Functional Decomposition The IC 723 consists of **five functionally distinct sub-blocks** integrated on the same die. Each block has a single, sharply defined responsibility — a hallmark of the *modular analog design philosophy* of the late 1960s. 1. **Zener Reference Source ($V_{REF} \approx 7.15$ V)** A temperature-compensated buried-Zener (or Zener-plus-forward-biased-diode chain) provides a *stable, low-noise, low-drift* reference. The buried Zener (≈ 5.6 V) is series-summed with a forward-biased diode (≈ 1.55 V) to give the canonical **7.15 V** bandgap-like value. This reference is *ratiometric* — its absolute value matters less than its **stability with temperature and supply variation**. 2. **Error Amplifier (Differential / Operational Amplifier)** A high-gain (≈ 70 dB open-loop), differential-input amplifier. The **non-inverting input (Pin 5)** is tied to $V_{REF}$ (in high-voltage mode), and the **inverting input (Pin 4)** receives a feedback sample from the output. The error amplifier output *drives the base of the internal series pass transistor* such that the feedback voltage equals $V_{REF}$. A **100 pF compensation capacitor** is mandated on Pin 9 to prevent high-frequency oscillation. 3. **Series Pass Transistor (Internal NPN Darlington)** A composite NPN structure (Darlington for high current gain $\beta \approx 5000$+) acts as the *variable-resistance element* between $V_{in}$ and $V_{out}$. In its linear region, the transistor drops the *excess voltage* (=$V_{in} - V_{out}$) as heat. This element *is* the reason linear regulators are inherently inefficient. 4. **Current-Limit Sub-System** Comprises a dedicated **current-sense transistor** and an **external current-sense resistor $R_{SC}$** placed between Pins 3 and 2 (or Pin 10 and Pin 3). The voltage developed across $R_{SC}$ is monitored; when it exceeds the base–emitter cut-in voltage (≈ 0.65 V) of the current-limit transistor, the latter conducts and **steals base drive** away from the series pass, throttling the output current. 5. **Reference & Bias Network** Internal current sources and bias generators power all sub-blocks. The $-V$ pin (Pin 7) is used only when the regulator operates from a *split supply* (e.g., $\pm 15$ V) to provide negative rail bias to the differential pair of the error amplifier. In a single-supply positive regulator, Pin 7 is **grounded**. ### 2.2 Operating Principle — Closed-Loop Negative Feedback The 723 is a textbook **negative-feedback control system**. The error amplifier relentlessly enforces the condition: $$ V_{IN(+)} = V_{IN(-)} $$ If the load current increases → $V_{out}$ droops → feedback voltage at $V_{IN(-)}$ decreases → the differential input $(V_{IN(+)} - V_{IN(-)})$ becomes positive → the error amplifier output rises → more base drive is delivered to the series pass → its $V_{CE}$ decreases → $V_{out}$ is restored. The loop gain (≈ 3000) forces the steady-state error toward zero, yielding excellent regulation. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize the following table. It is the **only set of equations** you need for any numerical KTU question on the 723. | Configuration | Condition | Output Voltage Equation | Current Limit Equation | | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- | | High-Voltage Regulator | $V_{out} > V_{REF}$ | $V_{out} = V_{REF}\left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$ | $I_{L(\max)} = \dfrac{V_{sense}}{R_{SC}} = \dfrac{0.65}{R_{SC}}$ | | Low-Voltage Regulator | $V_{out} < V_{REF}$ | $V_{out} = V_{REF}\left(\dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}\right)$ | Same as above | | Positive Regulator (single supply) | $V_{out} \in [2, 37]$ V | Floating — reference common to $V_{out}$ | — | | Negative Regulator (using 723) | $V_{out} \in [-2, -37]$ V | Inverted topology | — | | External Boost Configuration | $I_L > 150$ mA | Add external NPN pass; $I_{L(\max)} \approx \beta_{ext} \cdot 150$ mA | $R_{SC}$ repositioned externally | | Line Regulation | — | $S_V = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} \times 100$ % | — | | Load Regulation | — | $R_{L\,reg} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{V_{out}} \times 100$ % | — | | Power Dissipation (Pass Transistor) | — | $P_D = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_L$ | — | **Legend for $R_1, R_2$ (high-voltage mode):** - $R_1$ = resistor connected *between $V_{out}$ and $V_{IN(-)}$ (Pin 4)* - $R_2$ = resistor connected *between $V_{IN(-)}$ (Pin 4) and Ground* > [!NOTE] > **Engineer's rule-of-thumb (mnemonic):** *In high-voltage mode, the non-inverting input is the reference — feedback comes to the inverting input — and the output is $V_{REF}$ scaled UP by the divider ratio. In low-voltage mode, the roles of $R_1$ and $R_2$ are conceptually swapped and the output is $V_{REF}$ scaled DOWN.* ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The 723 (and its modern descendants like LM317, LM350, LM338) is the workhorse of: - **Bench laboratory power supplies** (0–30 V variable, 0–3 A) — the 723 controls the loop, an external 2N3055 boosts current. - **Aircraft avionics** (historically) — 723's wide $V_{in}$ range handles 28 V bus transients. - **Post-regulation stage** in SMPS — the 723 cleans the residual ripple from a switching pre-regulator. - **Op-amp supply rails** in instrumentation — the 723 powers $\pm 15$ V rails for precision op-amps from a single transformer secondary using split-supply connection. ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Output Voltage — High-Voltage Configuration ($V_{out} > V_{REF}$) **Statement to prove:** $$ V_{out} = V_{REF}\left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right) $$ **Setup:** - Non-inverting input (Pin 5) is connected to the **$V_{REF}$ pin (Pin 6)**. Hence: $$ V_{IN(+)} = V_{REF} $$ - A resistor divider $R_1$ (top, between $V_{out}$ and Pin 4) and $R_2$ (bottom, between Pin 4 and Ground) feeds the inverting input (Pin 4). By the **voltage divider rule**: $$ V_{IN(-)} = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} $$ **Closed-loop constraint (virtual short of the error amplifier):** $$ V_{IN(+)} = V_{IN(-)} $$ **Substitution — Step 1:** $$ V_{REF} = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} $$ **Rearrangement — Step 2 (multiply both sides by $(R_1 + R_2)/R_2$):** $$ V_{out} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2} $$ **Simplification — Step 3 (split the fraction):** $$ V_{out} = V_{REF} \left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right) $$ **Q.E.D.** $\blacksquare$ **Numerical example (must appear in KTU paper):** - Target: $V_{out} = +12$ V - $V_{REF} = 7.15$ V (typical) - $R_2 = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$ (chosen for bias-current tolerance) - Required: $R_1$ $$ 12 = 7.15 \left(1 + \frac{R_1}{1000}\right) $$ $$ 1 + \frac{R_1}{1000} = \frac{12}{7.15} = 1.6783 $$ $$ \frac{R_1}{1000} = 0.6783 $$ $$ R_1 = 678.3\ \Omega \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{Use standard } 680\ \Omega\ (5\%\ \text{tolerance}) $$ ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Output Voltage — Low-Voltage Configuration ($V_{out} < V_{REF}$) **Statement to prove:** $$ V_{out} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} $$ **Setup:** - Inverting input (Pin 4) is tied to the **$V_{REF}$ pin (Pin 6)**. Hence: $$ V_{IN(-)} = V_{REF} $$ - A resistor divider $R_1$ (between $V_{REF}$ and Pin 5) and $R_2$ (between Pin 5 and Ground) feeds the non-inverting input (Pin 5). By voltage division: $$ V_{IN(+)} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} $$ **Closed-loop constraint:** $$ V_{IN(+)} = V_{IN(-)} $$ **Substitution — Step 1:** $$ V_{REF} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = V_{REF} $$ Wait — this would trivially give $R_1 = 0$. Let us carefully reconsider. In the low-voltage mode, the **feedback** comes from the divider *at the non-inverting input*, while the **inverting input** is grounded through a reference network. The corrected topology is: - Pin 4 (IN$(-)$) is connected to a divider that samples $V_{out}$. - Pin 5 (IN$(+)$) is connected directly to $V_{REF}$. Then by the same logic as Derivation 1: $$ V_{REF} = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} $$ $$ V_{out} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2} = V_{REF}\left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right) $$ > [!WARNING] > **Frequent student error in KTU exams:** Confusing the **low-voltage** and **high-voltage** mode *output equations*. The algebraic forms are **identical**; the distinction lies in the *resistor placement and which input pin is grounded/divider-fed*. Always draw the circuit before writing the equation. > [!IMPORTANT] > The classic textbook 723 low-voltage topology uses the *opposite* wiring: Pin 4 (IN$(-)$) is connected to a divider that samples $V_{out}$, and Pin 5 (IN$(+)$) receives a fraction of $V_{REF}$. The output equation then becomes $V_{out} = V_{REF} \cdot R_2/(R_1 + R_2)$. The key is the **closed-loop feedback** forces $V_{IN(+)} = V_{IN(-)}$, and you must apply it to whichever node the divider feeds. ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Current-Limit Threshold **Statement to prove:** $$ I_{L(\max)} = \frac{V_{sense}}{R_{SC}} \approx \frac{0.65\ \text{V}}{R_{SC}} $$ **Setup:** - The current-sense resistor $R_{SC}$ is placed in series with the load current path, between Pin 3 (CS) and Pin 2 (CL), or between Pin 10 ($V_{out}$) and the load. - The voltage developed across $R_{SC}$ is: $$ V_{sense} = I_L \cdot R_{SC} $$ - An internal NPN transistor monitors $V_{sense}$ between its **base** (Pin 2) and **emitter** (Pin 3). This transistor turns ON when: $$ V_{sense} \geq V_{BE(\text{on})} \approx 0.65\ \text{V} $$ **Threshold derivation — Step 1 (set the trigger condition):** $$ I_L \cdot R_{SC} = V_{BE(\text{on})} $$ **Solve for $R_{SC}$ — Step 2:** $$ R_{SC} = \frac{V_{BE(\text{on})}}{I_{L(\max)}} = \frac{0.65}{I_{L(\max)}} $$ **Alternatively — Step 3 (solve for the limit current):** $$ I_{L(\max)} = \frac{0.65\ \text{V}}{R_{SC}} $$ **Q.E.D.** $\blacksquare$ **Numerical example (KTU-style):** - Required: $I_{L(\max)} = 150$ mA - Calculate $R_{SC}$: $$ R_{SC} = \frac{0.65\ \text{V}}{0.150\ \text{A}} = 4.33\ \Omega $$ - Use a standard **4.3 Ω, ½ W** resistor. - Power dissipation in $R_{SC}$: $$ P_{R_{SC}} = I_{L(\max)}^2 \cdot R_{SC} = (0.150)^2 \cdot 4.33 = 97.4\ \text{mW} $$ - A ½ W resistor has a comfortable **5× safety margin**. Good design. ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Power Dissipation in the Series Pass Element **Statement to prove:** $$ P_D = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_L $$ **Derivation:** - The series pass transistor behaves as a **variable resistor** in the linear region. - Voltage across the pass element (collector to emitter) = $V_{CE} = V_{in} - V_{out}$. - Current through it = load current = $I_L$. - Power = $V \times I$: $$ P_D = V_{CE} \cdot I_L = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_L $$ **Q.E.D.** $\blacksquare$ **Numerical example:** - $V_{in} = 18$ V, $V_{out} = 12$ V, $I_L = 100$ mA - $P_D = (18 - 12) \times 0.100 = 0.6$ W > [!WARNING] > **Thermal design pitfall (KTU favourite trick question):** > The IC 723 DIP-14 package has a thermal resistance $\theta_{JA} \approx 80\ ^\circ$C/W. If the ambient is $50\ ^\circ$C and $P_D = 0.6$ W, the junction temperature is: > $T_J = 50 + (80 \times 0.6) = 98\ ^\circ$C — well within the 125 °C limit. But if $V_{in} = 24$ V and $I_L = 150$ mA, $P_D = 1.8$ W, then $T_J = 50 + 144 = 194\ ^\circ$C — **thermal shutdown** kicks in or the device is destroyed. This is why **heat sinking** is mandatory. ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Pinout Diagram (14-Pin DIP — Top View) ``` +-------------------+ NC 1 | | 14 NC CL 2 | | 13 V_C EXT (Base drive for ext. pass) CS 3 | LM 723 | 12 V_in (Collector of internal pass) IN(-) 4 | µA 723 | 11 V+ (Positive supply) IN(+) 5 | | 10 V_out (Emitter of internal pass) V_REF 6 | | 9 V_C (Compensation cap node) -V 7 | | 8 NC +-------------------+ DIP-14 / TO-100 ``` ### 4.2 Internal Block Diagram (Functional Architecture) The following Mermaid diagram renders the **internal block-level functional architecture** of the IC 723. Each block is shown with its interconnections — this is the *exact* block diagram KTU examiners expect you to redraw in the exam. ```mermaid graph TD node1["V+ Pin 11
Positive Supply"] node2["V_in Pin 12
Regulated DC Input"] node3["Internal Series Pass
NPN Darlington Transistor"] node4["V_out Pin 10
Regulated Output"] node5["Zener Reference Source
Vref 7.15 V Pin 6"] node6["Error Amplifier
Differential Op-Amp"] node7["Inverting Input Pin 4"] node8["Non Inverting Input Pin 5"] node9["Current Limit Transistor
Pin 2 Pin 3"] node10["External Sense Resistor
R_SC Between Pin 2 and Pin 3"] node11["External Feedback Divider
R1 from Vout to Pin 4"] node12["External Divider Resistor
R2 from Pin 4 to Ground"] node13["Compensation Capacitor
100 pF at Pin 9"] node14["External Load
R_L Connected to V_out"] node15["Negative Supply Pin 7
Grounded in Single Supply"] node16["External Boost Transistor
Driven from Pin 13"] node1 --> node3 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node4 node3 --> node16 node4 --> node14 node5 --> node8 node5 --> node6 node8 --> node6 node7 --> node6 node6 --> node3 node4 --> node11 node11 --> node7 node12 --> node7 node9 --> node3 node10 --> node9 node4 --> node10 node13 --> node7 node15 --> node6 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **forward (power) path**: $V_{in} \rightarrow$ Series Pass $\rightarrow V_{out} \rightarrow$ Load. - The **feedback (control) path**: $V_{out} \rightarrow R_1$–$R_2$ divider $\rightarrow$ Pin 4 $\rightarrow$ Error Amp $\rightarrow$ Series Pass base. - The **reference path**: $V_{REF} \rightarrow$ Pin 5 $\rightarrow$ Error Amp. - The **protection path**: $R_{SC}$ sense voltage $\rightarrow$ Current Limit transistor $\rightarrow$ Steals base drive from Series Pass. ### 4.3 High-Voltage Regulator — Complete Circuit Topology ``` V_in (+15 V to +40 V) | +----------------------------+ | | | [ Pin 11 V+ ] [ Pin 12 V_in ] | | | | v v | +-------------------+ | | LM 723 Internal | | | Series Pass | | +-------------------+ | | | v | [ Pin 10 V_out ] ----+------> +V_out (regulated) | | | | | | | +----+----+ | | | | | | [R1] [C_comp] | | | | | | +----+----+ | | | | | [ Pin 4 IN- ] | | | | | [R2] | | | | | | === === | GND GND | | [ Pin 6 V_REF ] -----> [ Pin 5 IN+ ] | | [ Pin 2 CL ] ---[R_SC]--- [ Pin 3 CS ] (current limit loop) | | [ Pin 13 ] ---> Base of external NPN (2N3055) for boost | | [ Pin 7 -V ] ---> GND (single-supply configuration) ``` **Output voltage equation:** $V_{out} = V_{REF} (1 + R_1 / R_2) = 7.15 (1 + R_1 / R_2)$ **Current limit:** $I_{L(\max)} = 0.65 / R_{SC}$ ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition) **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PCEBT402] **List any three salient features of the IC 723 voltage regulator.** **Model Answer (3 marks — KTU key pattern):** 1. The IC 723 is a **monolithic floating voltage regulator** with a wide input voltage range of up to **40 V** and a continuously adjustable output from **2 V to 37 V**. 2. It provides a **temperature-compensated internal reference** of approximately **7.15 V** with very low drift of **0.003 %/°C**. 3. It contains an **internal current-limit circuit** and supports **external pass transistor boosting** for load currents exceeding the internal **150 mA** limit. > [!TIP] > Three crisp points, each ≈ 1 mark. KTU examiners allot 1 mark per correct feature — do not write long paragraphs. --- **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024, PCEBT402] **What is meant by a "floating regulator"? Why is the IC 723 called so?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **floating regulator** is one in which the IC's internal reference and ground terminals are **not hardwired to a fixed chassis ground**. The user can *position the common terminal anywhere in the circuit* — including on top of a high-voltage rail. The IC 723 is called a floating regulator because the **$-V$ pin (Pin 7)** is brought out separately, allowing the user to connect it to either the system ground (single-supply positive regulator) or to a negative rail (split-supply or negative regulator). This **flexibility** lets one IC regulate any voltage from **2 V to 37 V** above (or below) the chosen common, making it suitable for **boost, buck, and inverting topologies** without modification. *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for floating concept + 1 mark for application flexibility.)* --- ### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, every 14-mark question offers an **internal choice**: answer either Question A or Question B. Each question is split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Provide *full derivations and labelled diagrams* in the answer booklet. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Voltage Regulator Design** **(a)** [7 marks] Draw and explain the **block diagram of IC 723**, identifying the function of each block. **Model Solution:** Refer to **Section 4.2** of these notes for the block diagram. The five internal blocks and their functions are: | Block | Function | | :--- | :--- | | Zener Reference Source | Generates a stable **7.15 V** temperature-compensated internal reference. | | Error Amplifier | Differential amplifier that compares the feedback sample with $V_{REF}$ and produces an error signal. | | Series Pass Transistor | NPN Darlington that acts as a variable resistance, dropping excess $V_{in} - V_{out}$. | | Current-Limit Sub-system | Monitors voltage across $R_{SC}$ and clamps output current at $I_{L(\max)} = 0.65 / R_{SC}$. | | Reference/Bias Network | Internal current sources and bias generators for all sub-blocks. | *(7 marks: 3 marks for labelled diagram + 2 marks for block identification + 2 marks for inter-block signal-flow explanation.)* **(b)** [7 marks] **Design a high-voltage regulator using IC 723 to provide +12 V output at 100 mA load current.** Calculate $R_1$, $R_2$, and $R_{SC}$. Also compute the power dissipation in the pass transistor if $V_{in} = 18$ V. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Choose topology.** Since $V_{out} = 12\ \text{V} > V_{REF} = 7.15\ \text{V}$, use the **high-voltage configuration**. **Step 2 — Pick $R_2$ for bias-current tolerance.** Use $R_2 = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$ (a value large enough to limit divider current yet small enough to swamp the error-amp input bias current of ≈ 1 µA). **Step 3 — Calculate $R_1$.** From the high-voltage equation: $$ V_{out} = V_{REF}\left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right) $$ $$ 12 = 7.15\left(1 + \frac{R_1}{1000}\right) $$ $$ 1 + \frac{R_1}{1000} = \frac{12}{7.15} = 1.6783 $$ $$ \frac{R_1}{1000} = 0.6783 \quad\Rightarrow\quad R_1 = 678.3\ \Omega $$ Use the nearest standard value: **$R_1 = 680\ \Omega$ (5% tolerance)**. *(1 mark)* **Step 4 — Calculate $R_{SC}$ for current limit.** Required: $I_{L(\max)} = 100$ mA (with safety margin, design for 120 mA): $$ R_{SC} = \frac{0.65}{0.120} = 5.42\ \Omega $$ Use the standard value **$R_{SC} = 5.6\ \Omega, \frac{1}{2}\ \text{W}$**. *(1 mark)* **Step 5 — Power dissipation in the pass transistor.** $$ P_D = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_L = (18 - 12) \times 0.100 = 0.6\ \text{W} $$ **Step 6 — Thermal check.** With $\theta_{JA} = 80\ ^\circ$C/W and $T_A = 50\ ^\circ$C (worst case): $$ T_J = T_A + P_D \cdot \theta_{JA} = 50 + 0.6 \times 80 = 98\ ^\circ\text{C} $$ This is **well below** the 125 °C limit. **No heat sink required** for this design. *(1 mark)* **Final component list (2 marks):** | Component | Value | Rating | | :---: | :---: | :---: | | $R_1$ | 680 Ω | ¼ W | | $R_2$ | 1 kΩ | ¼ W | | $R_{SC}$ | 5.6 Ω | ½ W | | $C_{comp}$ | 100 pF | Ceramic | | $C_{in}$ | 0.1 µF | Tantalum (at $V_{in}$) | | $C_{out}$ | 1 µF | Tantalum (at $V_{out}$) | | IC | LM723 / µA723 | DIP-14 | --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Current Limiting & Negative Regulator** **(a)** [7 marks] With a neat circuit diagram, explain the **operation of IC 723 as a high-voltage regulator** ($V_{out} > V_{REF}$). Derive the expression for $V_{out}$. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Circuit diagram.** Draw the high-voltage configuration (see **Section 4.3**). Key connections: - Pin 5 (IN+) $\rightarrow$ Pin 6 ($V_{REF}$). - Pin 4 (IN$-$) $\rightarrow$ junction of $R_1$–$R_2$ divider. - Pin 9 (compensation) $\rightarrow$ 100 pF to ground. - Pin 12 ($V_{in}$) $\rightarrow$ unregulated DC. - Pin 10 ($V_{out}$) $\rightarrow$ regulated output. - Pin 7 ($-V$) $\rightarrow$ ground (single-supply). *(2 marks for the labelled diagram.)* **Step 2 — Operating principle.** The error amplifier has a high open-loop gain (≈ 70 dB). Through negative feedback via the $R_1$–$R_2$ divider, the amplifier enforces $V_{IN(+)} = V_{IN(-)}$ in the steady state. Since $V_{IN(+)} = V_{REF}$, the inverting input is held at exactly $V_{REF}$. The voltage divider then determines $V_{out}$. **Step 3 — Derivation.** From the voltage divider: $$ V_{IN(-)} = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} $$ Setting $V_{IN(+)} = V_{IN(-)}$: $$ V_{REF} = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} $$ Solving for $V_{out}$: $$ V_{out} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2} = V_{REF}\left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right) $$ *(3 marks for derivation: 1 mark each for voltage divider, virtual short, and final simplification.)* **Step 4 — Numerical illustration.** With $V_{REF} = 7.15$ V, $R_1 = 680$ Ω, $R_2 = 1$ kΩ: $$ V_{out} = 7.15 \times (1 + 0.68) = 7.15 \times 1.68 = 12.01\ \text{V} \quad\checkmark $$ *(2 marks for verifying with a numerical example.)* --- **(b)** [7 marks] **Explain the current-limiting feature of IC 723 with a circuit diagram.** Calculate $R_{SC}$ to limit the output current to **150 mA**. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Need for current limiting.** A linear regulator's pass transistor can deliver *unlimited* current into a short circuit, causing catastrophic overheating. The current-limit sub-system prevents this by *folding back* the output current when the load draws more than a preset threshold. **Step 2 — Circuit.** The sense resistor $R_{SC}$ is placed in series with the load path: - One end of $R_{SC}$ $\rightarrow$ Pin 10 ($V_{out}$) of IC 723. - Other end of $R_{SC}$ $\rightarrow$ Pin 3 (CS, emitter of internal pass / sense node). - Pin 2 (CL) is the *base* of the current-limit NPN transistor; it senses the voltage at Pin 3 plus the drop across $R_{SC}$. A simpler equivalent: $R_{SC}$ is connected between Pin 2 and Pin 3, in series with the load current. *(2 marks for the labelled circuit.)* **Step 3 — Working.** Load current $I_L$ flows through $R_{SC}$, developing a voltage $V_{sense} = I_L \cdot R_{SC}$. This voltage appears between the base and emitter of the internal current-limit transistor. - **Normal operation** ($V_{sense} < 0.65$ V): The current-limit transistor is OFF. It does not affect the series pass base drive. - **Overload / short circuit** ($V_{sense} \geq 0.65$ V): The current-limit transistor turns ON and *shunts* base drive away from the series pass transistor. The output current is *clamped* at $I_{L(\max)}$. *(2 marks for the explanation.)* **Step 4 — Calculation.** $$ R_{SC} = \frac{V_{BE(\text{on})}}{I_{L(\max)}} = \frac{0.65\ \text{V}}{0.150\ \text{A}} = 4.33\ \Omega $$ Use a standard **4.3 Ω, ½ W** resistor. *(1 mark for derivation + 1 mark for numerical answer.)* **Step 5 — Power rating check.** $$ P_{R_{SC}} = I_{L(\max)}^2 \cdot R_{SC} = (0.150)^2 \times 4.33 = 97.4\ \text{mW} $$ A ½ W (500 mW) resistor has a 5× safety margin — *safe*. *(1 mark for design completeness.)* > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Deductions:** > 1. **Forgetting Pin 7 connection** in single-supply mode. Pin 7 *must* be grounded; if left floating, the error amplifier's negative rail is undefined and the output drifts wildly. **−1 mark.** > 2. **Using $V_{REF} = 5$ V** instead of the correct 7.15 V. Many students memorize 5 V (which is a *different* regulator like the LM7805). **−2 marks.** > 3. **Omitting the 100 pF compensation capacitor** at Pin 9.
