This capacitor is *mandatory* for stability; without it, the regulator oscillates at ≈ 1 MHz. **−1 mark.** > 4. **Not specifying resistor power ratings.** A 4.3 Ω sense resistor at 150 mA dissipates ≈ 100 mW; ¼ W is *barely* adequate, so ½ W is the *correct* choice. **−0.5 mark.** > 5. **Confusing $R_1$ and $R_2$ positions** in the high-voltage derivation. *Re-read the legend in Section 2.3.* **−1 to −2 marks.** --- ### 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > Read this section **5 minutes before the exam hall door closes.** - [ ] **IC 723 = floating, adjustable, linear regulator** with **$V_{REF} = 7.15$ V**. - [ ] **Output range: 2 V to 37 V**; **Input range: up to 40 V**; **Internal current: 150 mA** (boostable). - [ ] **Two operating modes**: High-voltage ($V_{out} > V_{REF}$, $V_{out} = V_{REF}(1 + R_1/R_2)$) and Low-voltage ($V_{out} < V_{REF}$). - [ ] **Five internal blocks**: Zener Reference, Error Amplifier, Series Pass, Current Limit, Bias Network. - [ ] **Current limit equation**: $I_{L(\max)} = 0.65 / R_{SC}$; always use a ½ W (or higher) resistor for $R_{SC}$. - [ ] **Pin 7 ($-V$):** *must* be grounded in single-supply positive regulator configuration. - [ ] **Pin 9 ($V_C$):** *always* connect a **100 pF** ceramic capacitor to ground (or to Pin 4) for compensation. - [ ] **Pin 13** drives the base of an *external* pass transistor (e.g., 2N3055) when $I_L > 150$ mA. - [ ] **Power dissipation**: $P_D = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_L$ — never exceed the IC's thermal limit ($T_J \leq 125$ °C). - [ ] **Heat sinking** is mandatory if $P_D > 0.5$ W in the DIP-14 package. - [ ] **Floating regulator advantage**: same IC works as positive, negative, boost, or buck regulator — just relocate the $-V$ pin. - [ ] **Line regulation ≈ 0.01 %/V**; **Load regulation ≈ 0.03 %** — these are *excellent* for a 1960s-era IC and are why the 723 is still in production today.

topics.title: Typical Low Voltage Regulator Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT402 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Typical Low Voltage Regulator Circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Typical Low Voltage Regulator Circuit** is an electronic circuit designed to deliver a constant, ripple-free, low DC output voltage (typically between **1.2 V and 15 V**) from an unregulated or fluctuating DC input source, while maintaining tight regulation against variations in **line voltage, load current, and temperature**. In the KTU 2024 scheme, low voltage regulators form the backbone of power management in portable, battery-operated, and embedded systems, and are implemented using **op-amp based series regulators, monolithic IC regulators (78xx/79xx families), three-terminal adjustable regulators (LM317/LM337), and Low Dropout (LDO) regulators**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):**
> A voltage regulator is a closed-loop feedback system that senses the output voltage, compares it with a precise internal reference, and dynamically adjusts the conduction of a pass element (BJT/MOSFET) so that the output remains stable at the desired level.

## 1.2 Block-Level Architecture of Any Voltage Regulator

Regardless of complexity, every linear low voltage regulator consists of **four fundamental sub-blocks**:

1. **Reference Voltage Source** — Provides a temperature-stable, noise-immune internal reference (bandgap, Zener, or buried Zener).
2. **Error Amplifier (EA)** — High-gain differential amplifier that compares the scaled feedback voltage with the reference and produces an error signal.
3. **Pass Element (Series/Shunt Device)** — A power BJT, Darlington pair, or power MOSFET that acts as a *variable resistor* dissipating the excess voltage as heat.
4. **Feedback Network (Sampling Divider)** — A precision resistor divider that scales down $V_{out}$ to be comparable with $V_{ref}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Water Tap" Model**
> Imagine the unregulated DC input as a high-pressure water pipe, and your sensitive electronic circuit (say, a microcontroller) as a delicate plant that needs exactly a slow trickle of water. The **pass transistor** is the *tap* (valve), the **error amplifier** is the *gardener* constantly watching the flow, and the **reference voltage** is the *target flow rate marked on a gauge*. The moment the pressure upstream (input voltage) rises, or the demand downstream (load current) changes, the gardener tightens or loosens the tap so the plant always gets the exact same trickle. The unused water is "burned" as heat by the tap — this is precisely why linear regulators are *inefficient* but *clean*.

## 1.3 Classification of Low Voltage Regulators

| Family | Operating Principle | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Series (Linear) Regulator** | Pass element in *series* with load | Most IC regulators (78xx, LM317) |
| **Shunt Regulator** | Pass element in *parallel* with load | Low-current references, Zener-based |
| **LDO (Low Dropout) Regulator** | PNP/MOSFET pass with very low $V_{DO}$ | Battery-powered, portable devices |
| **Switching Regulator** | Pass element switches ON/OFF | High efficiency (covered in adjacent modules) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The term **"Low Voltage"** in this context refers to the **output voltage level (≤ 15 V)**, not low dropout. The same regulator topology (LDO) is often used specifically when battery voltages approach the regulated output.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Op-Amp Based Series Voltage Regulator (Canonical Topology)

This is the **theoretical workhorse** of KTU examination questions. The op-amp replaces a discrete error amplifier and the BJT acts as the pass element.

### 2.1.1 Circuit Operation — Step-by-Step Logic

- The op-amp is powered from the unregulated input $V_{in}$.
- A **Zener diode** ($D_Z$) at the non-inverting terminal provides a stable reference voltage $V_{ref} = V_Z$.
- The output voltage $V_{out}$ is sampled by the resistor divider $R_1$–$R_2$ and fed back to the inverting terminal.
- The op-amp drives the base of the **NPN pass transistor** $Q_1$.
- The emitter of $Q_1$ delivers current to the load $R_L$.

**Closed-Loop Feedback Equation:**

$$V_{+} = V_Z \quad \text{(held constant by Zener)}$$

$$V_{-} = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \quad \text{(voltage divider feedback)}$$

By the **virtual short principle** of an ideal op-amp in negative feedback:

$$V_{-} = V_{+} \implies V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = V_Z$$

Rearranging:

$$V_{out} = V_Z \left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The student must remember that the op-amp must be able to swing its output up to $V_{BE} + V_{out}$, so its positive supply rail must be tied to $V_{in}$, not $V_{out}$.

## 2.2 The 78xx / 79xx Fixed Three-Terminal Regulators

- **78xx** (e.g., 7805, 7812) — Positive fixed output regulators; suffix denotes output voltage.
- **79xx** (e.g., 7905, 7912) — Negative fixed output regulators.

**Internal block diagram (conceptual):**
Bandgap Reference → Error Amplifier → Darlington Pass Transistor → $V_{out}$ pin, with internal **current limiting** and **thermal shutdown** (SOA protection).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Why does the 78xx require input and output capacitors?"*
> Answer: To prevent oscillation and to improve the **transient response** to sudden load changes. Typically $C_{in} = 0.33\,\mu F$ and $C_{out} = 0.1\,\mu F$ tantalum.

## 2.3 The LM317 Adjustable Three-Terminal Regulator

The LM317 is a **floating** regulator — the adjust pin senses voltage relative to $V_{out}$, allowing the output to be programmed from **1.25 V to 37 V**.

**Standard application circuit:**

- Pin 1 (ADJ) connects between $R_1$ and $R_2$.
- Pin 2 (OUT) connects to the load and to $R_1$.
- Pin 3 (IN) takes the unregulated input.

**Output voltage formula (the most-tested KTU equation):**

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) + I_{ADJ} \cdot R_2$$

where $V_{ref} = 1.25\,\text{V}$ (bandgap reference) and $I_{ADJ} \leq 100\,\mu\text{A}$ (often neglected in board exams if not mentioned).

**Programming examples:**
- $R_2 = 0 \implies V_{out} = 1.25\,\text{V}$ (minimum)
- $R_1 = 240\,\Omega,\, R_2 = 2.4\,\text{k}\Omega \implies V_{out} \approx 13.75\,\text{V}$

## 2.4 Low Dropout (LDO) Regulator Architecture

The **dropout voltage** $V_{DO}$ is the minimum difference $(V_{in} - V_{out})$ required to maintain regulation. Conventional 78xx uses an **NPN Darlington** with $V_{DO} \approx 2\text{–}3\,\text{V}$. An LDO uses a **PNP pass transistor** (or P-channel MOSFET), giving $V_{DO} \approx 100\text{–}500\,\text{mV}$.

**Key Advantage:** When powered from a **lithium-ion battery** (3.0 V–4.2 V) feeding a 2.8 V microcontroller, an LDO continues to regulate even when the battery sags to 3.1 V, whereas a 78xx would *drop out* of regulation.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the equations below — they account for 80% of the numerical marks in Module 4.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Symbols / Units | Remarks |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | **Op-amp series regulator output** | $V_{out} = V_Z \left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)$ | $V$ | $V_Z$ = Zener voltage |
| 2 | **LM317 output voltage** | $V_{out} = V_{ref}\left(1+\frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) + I_{ADJ}R_2$ | $V$ | $V_{ref}=1.25\,$V |
| 3 | **Line Regulation (LR)** | $LR = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} \times 100\%$ | $\%/\text{V}$ | Ideally $\to 0$ |
| 4 | **Line Regulation (normalized)** | $LR_n = \frac{\Delta V_{out}/V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} \times 100\%$ | $\%/\text{V}$ | KTU preferred form |
| 5 | **Load Regulation (LDR)** | $LDR = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\%$ | $\%/\text{mA}$ | $V_{NL}$=no-load, $V_{FL}$=full-load |
| 6 | **Dropout Voltage** | $V_{DO} = V_{in(min)} - V_{out}$ | $V$ | LDO: $V_{DO}<0.5\,$V |
| 7 | **Ripple Rejection (PSRR)** | $PSRR = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{ripple(in)}}{V_{ripple(out)}}\right)$ | dB | Higher is better |
| 8 | **Power Dissipation (Pass element)** | $P_D = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_L$ | W | Determines heatsink sizing |
| 9 | **Efficiency (linear)** | $\eta = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} \times 100\%$ | $\%$ | Maximum theoretical |
| 10 | **Current through $R_1$ (LM317)** | $I_{R_1} = \frac{V_{ref}}{R_1}$ | A | Used to size $R_1$ |
| 11 | **Thermal shutdown relation** | $T_J = P_D \cdot \theta_{JA} + T_A$ | $^\circ$C | $\theta_{JA}$=thermal resistance |
| 12 | **LM317 minimum load** | $I_{L(min)} = \frac{V_{ref}}{R_1}$ | A | Ensures regulation |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Low voltage regulators are the **unsung heroes** of every printed circuit board. They appear in:
- **USB chargers and power banks** (5 V LDO at the output stage).
- **Smartphone PMICs** (Power Management ICs) — contain dozens of LDOs at 0.8 V, 1.2 V, 1.8 V, 2.8 V, 3.3 V for SoC cores, RF, I/O, and memory rails.
- **Automotive ECUs** — wide-input LDOs (e.g., TPS7A47xx) survive 40 V load-dump transients.
- **IoT and Wearables** — quiescent current $I_Q < 1\,\mu\text{A}$ LDOs (e.g., TPS7A02) extend battery life.
- **Sensor signal conditioning** — ultra-low-noise LDOs (e.g., LT3042) power precision ADCs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Output Voltage of Op-Amp Based Series Regulator

**Given:** Ideal op-amp, NPN pass transistor $Q_1$, Zener reference $V_Z$, feedback divider $R_1$ (top, to $V_{out}$) and $R_2$ (bottom, to ground).

**Step 1 — Identify op-amp terminal voltages:**

$$V_{+} = V_Z$$

$$V_{-} = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the virtual short principle ($V_+ = V_-$) of an ideal op-amp with negative feedback:**

$$V_Z = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate $V_{out}$:**

$$V_{out} \cdot R_2 = V_Z (R_1 + R_2)$$

$$V_{out} \cdot R_2 = V_Z R_1 + V_Z R_2$$

**Step 4 — Group the $V_{out} R_2$ term to the right-hand side:**

$$V_Z R_1 = V_{out} R_2 - V_Z R_2 = R_2 (V_{out} - V_Z)$$

**Step 5 — Final expression:**

$$\boxed{\,V_{out} = V_Z \left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note the small inconsistency between this derivation and Section 2.1 (where we wrote $R_1$ as the lower resistor). The correct physical placement is: **$R_1$ is the upper resistor (between $V_{out}$ and $V_-$) and $R_2$ is the lower resistor (between $V_-$ and ground).** This is the convention KTU examiners expect.

## 3.2 Derivation: LM317 Output Voltage

**Given:** LM317 with $R_1$ between $V_{out}$ and ADJ pin, $R_2$ between ADJ pin and ground.

**Step 1 — Voltage across $R_1$:**

The LM317 internally maintains a constant $1.25\,\text{V}$ reference between OUT and ADJ pins. Hence:

$$V_{R_1} = V_{ref} = 1.25\,\text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Current through $R_1$ (Ohm's law):**

$$I_{R_1} = \frac{V_{ref}}{R_1} = \frac{1.25}{R_1}$$

**Step 3 — Current through $R_2$:**

Assuming $I_{ADJ} \approx 0$ (ideal case), the entire current $I_{R_1}$ flows through $R_2$:

$$I_{R_2} = I_{R_1} = \frac{1.25}{R_1}$$

**Step 4 — Voltage across $R_2$:**

$$V_{R_2} = I_{R_2} \cdot R_2 = \frac{1.25 \cdot R_2}{R_1}$$

**Step 5 — Apply KVL from $V_{out}$ to ground:**

$$V_{out} = V_{R_1} + V_{R_2} = 1.25 + \frac{1.25 \cdot R_2}{R_1}$$

**Step 6 — Factor out $V_{ref}$:**

$$V_{out} = 1.25 \left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Include $I_{ADJ}$ for the rigorous case:**

$$\boxed{\,V_{out} = V_{ref}\left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) + I_{ADJ} \cdot R_2\,}$$

with $V_{ref} = 1.25\,\text{V}$ and $I_{ADJ} \le 100\,\mu\text{A}$.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example: Design an LM317 for $V_{out} = 9\,\text{V}$

**Given:** $V_{ref} = 1.25\,\text{V}$, choose $R_1 = 240\,\Omega$, neglect $I_{ADJ}$.

**Step 1 — Required gain factor:**

$$1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{ref}} = \frac{9}{1.25} = 7.2$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $R_2$:**

$$\frac{R_2}{R_1} = 6.2 \implies R_2 = 6.2 \times 240 = 1488\,\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Standard value (5% E24 series):**

$$R_2 = 1.5\,\text{k}\Omega \quad \text{(recompute }V_{out}\text{)}$$

$$V_{out} = 1.25 \left(1 + \frac{1500}{240}\right) = 1.25 \times 7.25 = 9.06\,\text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Power dissipation in pass element** (assume $I_L = 500\,\text{mA}$, $V_{in} = 15\,\text{V}$):

$$P_D = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_L = (15 - 9.06) \times 0.5 = 2.97\,\text{W}$$

> [!WARNING]
> A 3 W dissipation in a TO-220 package *without a heatsink* will trigger thermal shutdown at $\approx 125\,^\circ\text{C}$ junction. The student must specify a heatsink rated for at least $5\,^\circ\text{C/W}$.

## 3.4 Derivation: Line Regulation of Op-Amp Regulator

**Closed-loop gain from $V_{in}$ disturbance to $V_{out}$:**

For a feedback factor $\beta = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ and op-amp open-loop gain $A$:

$$A_{CL} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta} \approx \frac{1}{\beta} \quad (\text{for } A\beta \gg 1)$$

The line regulation in the KTU normalized form becomes:

$$LR_n = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} \cdot \frac{1}{V_{out}} \times 100\% = \frac{1}{1 + A\beta} \cdot \frac{100\%}{V_{out}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This shows *why* op-amp regulators give excellent line regulation: as $A \to \infty$ (ideal op-amp), $LR_n \to 0$. A practical op-amp with $A = 10^5$ and $\beta = 0.1$ gives an effective line regulation improvement factor of $\approx 10^4$.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right Low Voltage Regulator

| Application Constraint | Recommended Topology | Justification |
|:--|:--|:--|
| Battery-powered, $V_{in}$ close to $V_{out}$ | **LDO (PNP / PMOS pass)** | Lowest dropout ($< 0.5\,$V) extends usable battery life |
| $V_{in}$ much higher than $V_{out}$ (e.g., 24 V → 5 V) | **78xx / NPN Darlington series** | Tolerates high headroom; cheaper |
| Output voltage must be user-adjustable | **LM317 (or LM337 for negative)** | Single IC, 3-pin simplicity, $1.25\text{–}37\,$V range |
| High current ($> 5\,\text{A}$) with low heat | **Switching buck regulator** | Not a linear regulator — covered in adjacent module |
| Ultra-low noise analog supply (ADC, DAC, PLL) | **LDO with $\mu$A-level PSRR** | Linear regulators have no switching noise |
| Cost-sensitive, fixed 5 V or 3.3 V | **78xx/79xx** | Cheapest, most rugged, well-documented |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**Define line regulation and load regulation of a voltage regulator. Why are these parameters important in low voltage regulator design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Line Regulation (LR):** It is the ability of the regulator to maintain a constant output voltage despite variations in the input (line) voltage. It is defined as:

$$LR = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} \times 100\%$$

Normalized form preferred:

$$LR_n = \frac{\Delta V_{out}/V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} \times 100\% \quad \text{(in \%/V)}$$

- **Load Regulation (LDR):** It is the ability of the regulator to maintain a constant output voltage despite variations in the load current. It is defined as:

$$LDR = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\%$$

- **Importance:** Both parameters quantify the *quality of regulation*. For low voltage regulators powering sensitive digital and analog ICs, *tight* line and load regulation (typically $< 0.1\%$) ensures the load sees a stable rail even when the source sags or the load steps. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest Paper]**
**What is a Low Dropout (LDO) regulator? How does it differ from a conventional 78xx series regulator in terms of the pass element and dropout voltage?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- An **LDO regulator** is a linear voltage regulator capable of maintaining regulation with a very small difference between input and output voltages (dropout voltage $V_{DO} \le 0.5\,\text{V}$).
- **Conventional 78xx** uses an **NPN Darlington** pass transistor — the two $V_{BE}$ drops give a dropout of $\approx 2\text{–}3\,\text{V}$.
- **LDO** uses a **PNP bipolar** or **P-channel MOSFET** as the pass element, which only needs to overcome a single $V_{CE(sat)}$ or $R_{DS(on)}$ drop, giving $V_{DO} \approx 100\text{–}500\,\text{mV}$.
- This makes LDOs ideal for **battery-powered applications** where the input voltage is only marginally above the desired output. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as **part (a) = 7 marks** and **part (b) = 7 marks**. Students must answer *either* the whole Question A *or* the whole Question B (Module 4 internal choice).

---

### ⭐ **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** ⭐

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4 Choice Set 1]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an **op-amp based low voltage series regulator**. Derive the expression for the regulated output voltage in terms of the Zener reference and feedback resistors. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Description (2 marks):**
The op-amp is powered from the unregulated input $V_{in}$. A Zener diode $D_Z$ (reverse biased through $R_S$) holds the non-inverting input at $V_Z$. The output of the op-amp drives the base of an NPN pass transistor $Q_1$, whose emitter supplies the load $R_L$. A resistor divider $R_1$ (upper) and $R_2$ (lower) samples the output and feeds the inverting input.

**Operation (3 marks):**
- If $V_{out}$ tends to *rise* (due to increased $V_{in}$ or decreased $I_L$), the voltage at the inverting input rises above $V_Z$. The op-amp output falls, reducing the base drive to $Q_1$, which in turn reduces the emitter current, restoring $V_{out}$.
- If $V_{out}$ tends to *fall*, the opposite action occurs — op-amp output rises, $Q_1$ conducts more, $V_{out}$ rises back. The op-amp continuously nullifies the error.

**Derivation (2 marks):**
Applying virtual short at the op-amp inputs and using the voltage divider at the inverting terminal:

$$V_Z = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

$$\boxed{\,V_{out} = V_Z \left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)\,}$$

The pass transistor $Q_1$ must satisfy $V_{in} \ge V_{out} + V_{CE(sat)}$ to remain in the active region (i.e., not in dropout).

---

**(b)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of the **LM317 adjustable voltage regulator**. Derive the expression for the output voltage and design the circuit for $V_{out} = 5\,\text{V}$ assuming $V_{ref} = 1.25\,\text{V}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit & Pin Description (2 marks):**
The LM317 has three pins — IN, OUT, ADJ. $R_1$ is connected between OUT and ADJ; $R_2$ is connected between ADJ and ground. A protective diode $D_1$ (from OUT to IN) and $D_2$ (from ADJ to IN) prevent reverse discharge of the output capacitor.

**Operation (2 marks):**
The LM317 internally maintains a $1.25\,\text{V}$ bandgap reference between OUT and ADJ. Any change in $V_{out}$ is sensed by the internal error amplifier, which adjusts the pass element to keep $V_{OUT} - V_{ADJ} = 1.25\,\text{V}$ constant. The output is therefore *programmed* by the external resistor ratio $R_2/R_1$.

**Derivation (2 marks):**
Current through $R_1$:

$$I_{R_1} = \frac{V_{ref}}{R_1} = \frac{1.25}{R_1}$$

Neglecting $I_{ADJ}$, the same current flows through $R_2$:

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} + I_{R_1} \cdot R_2 = 1.25\left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)$$

**Design for 5 V (1 mark):**
Choose $R_1 = 240\,\Omega$ (standard LM317 design guideline to keep $I_{L(min)} \ge 5\,\text{mA}$):

$$\frac{R_2}{R_1} = \frac{5}{1.25} - 1 = 3 \implies R_2 = 3 \times 240 = 720\,\Omega$$

Use standard value $R_2 = 750\,\Omega$ (recomputed $V_{out} = 5.16\,\text{V}$, acceptable).

---

### ⭐ **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** ⭐

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4 Choice Set 2]**

**(a)** Explain the working of **series and shunt voltage regulators** with neat diagrams. Compare their features in a tabular form. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Series Regulator (3 marks):**
The pass element (transistor or FET) is placed *in series* between the unregulated input and the load. A Zener reference and error amplifier sense $V_{out}$ and modulate the pass element's conduction. **Current through the series element equals the load current**. Excess voltage is dropped across the pass element and dissipated as heat. Efficiency is high (no shunt current path), making it the preferred topology for IC regulators (78xx, LM317).

**Shunt Regulator (2 marks):**
The pass element is placed *in parallel* with the load. The series resistor $R_S$ carries both the load current and the shunt current. The control circuit (typically a Zener) shunts excess current to ground when $V_{out}$ tries to rise. **Used for low-current, low-noise reference applications.**

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Feature | Series Regulator | Shunt Regulator |
|:--|:--|:--|
| Pass element position | In series with load | In parallel with load |
| Efficiency | High (no wasted shunt current) | Lower (constant current through $R_S$) |
| Load current range | Wide | Narrow (limited by $R_S$) |
| Short-circuit protection | Easy to add | Inherent (fuse blows) |
| Typical applications | 78xx, LM317, LDO | Zener references, op-amp biasing |
| Power dissipation | In pass element | Mostly in $R_S$ |

---

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **LDO regulator**. Mention its **advantages and disadvantages** over conventional linear regulators. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
The LDO consists of (i) a PNP or P-MOS pass element, (ii) a high-gain error amplifier, (iii) a bandgap reference ($\approx 1.2\,\text{V}$), (iv) a feedback resistor divider $R_1$–$R_2$, (v) soft-start circuitry, and (vi) optional current limit and thermal shutdown. The pass element is *between $V_{in}$ and $V_{out}$*, with the source/emitter at the higher potential.

**Working (2 marks):**
The error amplifier continuously compares the scaled feedback voltage $V_{out} \cdot \beta$ with the bandgap reference. Any error drives the gate/base of the P-MOS/PNP pass element to adjust its $R_{DS(on)}$ (or $V_{CE}$) so that the dropout across it compensates for the input variation. Because the P-channel device is "on" by default and modulated downward, the dropout can be as low as the $R_{DS(on)} \cdot I_L$ product — typically $< 500\,\text{mV}$ at full load.

**Advantages (2 marks):**
- Very low dropout voltage ($0.1\text{–}0.5\,\text{V}$) — extends battery life in portable devices.
- Simple external components (often only input/output capacitors).
- Low quiescent current ($\mu$A range) — suitable for always-on IoT rails.
- Excellent PSRR and low output noise — ideal for RF and audio.

**Disadvantages (1 mark):**
- Limited to low-to-medium currents ($\le 5\,\text{A}$).
- Efficiency still bounded by $V_{out}/V_{in}$ (e.g., 50% when $V_{in} = 2 \cdot V_{out}$).
- Requires careful PCB layout and output capacitor ESR selection for stability.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
>
> 1. **Sign convention trap:** In the op-amp regulator derivation, students often write $V_{out} = V_Z(1 + R_2/R_1)$ without clarifying *which resistor is upper and which is lower*. Examiners deduct 1 mark if the placement is ambiguous. **State explicitly: $R_1$ is between $V_{out}$ and $V_-$; $R_2$ is between $V_-$ and ground.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting $I_{ADJ}$ in LM317:** When the question specifies "with $I_{ADJ} = 100\,\mu\text{A}$", the rigorous formula $V_{out} = 1.25(1 + R_2/R_1) + I_{ADJ} R_2$ *must* be used. Dropping the $I_{ADJ}$ term loses 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Confusing dropout with headroom:** Dropout is the *minimum* $(V_{in} - V_{out})$ for regulation. Headroom is the *typical operating* $(V_{in} - V_{out})$. Examiners check this distinction.
>
> 4. **Omitting protection diodes on LM317:** A full-mark LM317 circuit must show the two protection diodes across IN–OUT and ADJ–IN. Forgetting them costs 0.5–1 mark.
>
> 5. **Writing "Zener" when it is a bandgap:** Modern IC regulators (LM317, LDO) use **bandgap references**, not Zener diodes. Examiners prefer the correct terminology.
>
> 6. **No heatsink calculation:** When the question gives $V_{in}$, $V_{out}$, and $I_L$, examiners *expect* a $P_D = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_L$ calculation. Skipping it costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master these 12 points before the exam.**

- ✅ A **voltage regulator** is a *closed-loop feedback system* — the four universal blocks are **Reference, Error Amplifier, Pass Element, Feedback Network**.
- ✅ **Series regulator** is preferred for IC implementation because it is more efficient than a shunt regulator.
- ✅ **Op-amp series regulator output:** $V_{out} = V_Z \left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$ where $R_1$ is the upper (feedback) resistor.
- ✅ **78xx** is a *fixed* positive regulator (7805 → 5 V, 7812 → 12 V); **79xx** is its *negative* counterpart.
- ✅ **LM317** is an *adjustable* positive regulator with $V_{out} = 1.25\left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) + I_{ADJ} R_2$ (with $V_{ref} = 1.25\,\text{V}$).
- ✅ **LM337** is the *negative* version of LM317; same formula but with negative output.
- ✅ **Dropout voltage** of conventional 78xx is $2\text{–}3\,\text{V}$ (NPN Darlington); **LDO** achieves $0.1\text{–}0.5\,\text{V}$ using PNP/PMOS pass.
- ✅ **LDO** is essential for **battery-powered, low-headroom** applications (Li-ion → 2.8 V MCUs).
- ✅ **Line regulation** tests $V_{out}$ stability against $\Delta V_{in}$; **load regulation** tests against $\Delta I_L$.
- ✅ **Power dissipation** $P_D = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_L$ dictates the heatsink rating; *always* check thermal shutdown.
- ✅ **Protection features** in modern IC regulators: current limit, thermal shutdown, SOA protection, reverse-battery (in some).
- ✅ **Efficiency of a linear regulator is bounded by $V_{out}/V_{in}$** — for large step-down ratios, use a **switching regulator** instead.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 78XX and 79XX IC Regulators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT402 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** 78XX and 79XX IC Regulators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **78XX and 79XX series** are monolithic three-terminal fixed voltage regulator ICs manufactured using bipolar linear technology. They are designed to deliver a constant, well-regulated DC output voltage from a variable (or noisy) unregulated DC input. The alphanumeric suffix **"XX"** denotes the magnitude of the nominal output voltage.

* The **78XX** family provides **positive** output voltages (e.g., 7805 → +5 V, 7812 → +12 V, 7815 → +15 V).
* The **79XX** family provides **negative** output voltages (e.g., 7905 → −5 V, 7912 → −12 V, 7915 → −15 V).

Internally, these ICs integrate a reference voltage source, a high-gain error amplifier, a series-pass transistor, and comprehensive protection circuitry (thermal shutdown, short-circuit current limiting, and safe-area compensation) into a single silicon die, typically packaged in a TO-220, TO-3, or TO-92 case.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Three-terminal fixed voltage regulators (78XX, 79XX) form the foundation for linear DC power supply design. Examiners frequently test the application circuits, ripple-rejection calculations, and dual-supply configurations built around these ICs.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Pressure Governor"

Imagine a municipal water pipeline delivering water to your house. The pressure arriving at your tap fluctuates wildly depending on neighbourhood demand, pumping-station load, and pipe height. You install a **mechanical pressure governor** at the inlet of your home. Regardless of whether the incoming pressure is too high, too low, or pulsating, the governor clamps the output to a precise, constant pressure that your geyser and washing machine can safely use.

The **78XX/79XX IC regulator is the electronic equivalent of this pressure governor.** The unregulated DC (rectified from mains) is the fluctuating "pressure" at the input pin; the regulator is the governor; and the constant DC voltage at the output pin is the steady "pressure" delivered to downstream analog/digital circuits such as op-amps, sensors, microcontrollers, and communication modules.

### 1.3 Key Physical and Electrical Constants

* **Maximum Input Voltage (V_IN max):** Typically **35 V** for the 78XX/79XX family (only **30 V** for the 7805/7905 to keep internal dissipation manageable).
* **Minimum Input–Output Differential (V_DROP):** Approximately **2 V to 3 V** (commonly stated as a headroom of 2.5 V at full load).
* **Output Current (I_OUT):** Up to **1 A** for the standard TO-220 package; up to **1.5 A** for the "K" suffix (e.g., 78M12 → 0.5 A; 78L12 → 0.1 A).
* **Line Regulation:** Typically **0.01 %/V** of V_OUT.
* **Load Regulation:** Typically **0.1 %** of V_OUT across the rated current range.
* **Ripple Rejection (PSRR):** Typically **60 dB to 80 dB** at 120 Hz (when input bypassing is provided).

> [!NOTE]
> **Pin Identification Tip (Always Verified Before Use):**
> 78XX (TO-220, looking at the front face): **Pin 1 = INPUT, Pin 2 = GND, Pin 3 = OUTPUT.**
> 79XX (TO-220, looking at the front face): **Pin 1 = GND, Pin 2 = INPUT, Pin 3 = OUTPUT.**
> The pinouts are mirror images — a classic board-burn mistake!
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Internal Architecture of a 78XX Regulator

A monolithic 78XX regulator is constructed from four primary functional blocks arranged in a closed negative-feedback loop:

1. **Reference Voltage Source (V_REF):** A temperature-compensated Zener-diode / bandgap reference producing a precise internal voltage (e.g., ≈ 5 V in the 7805, ≈ 12 V in the 7812).
2. **Error Amplifier (High-Gain Op-Amp):** Compares a fraction of the output voltage (V_OUT) with V_REF. The difference (error) is amplified to drive the base of the pass transistor.
3. **Series-Pass Transistor (Q_PASS):** A power Darlington pair that acts as a variable resistor, dropping the excess input voltage (V_IN − V_OUT) to maintain V_OUT constant.
4. **Protection Network:**
   * **Current Limiting (Short-Circuit Protection):** Senses load current via a small sense resistor and clamps it to ≈ **1.5 A** for the standard series.
   * **Thermal Shutdown:** Disables the pass transistor when the junction temperature exceeds ≈ **150 °C**, preventing catastrophic failure.
   * **Safe-Operating-Area (SOA) Protection:** Reduces the current limit as V_IN − V_OUT increases, preventing secondary breakdown of the pass transistor.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Block

* **Why a Darlington pass transistor?** — To achieve a very high current gain (β ≈ 1000), allowing the error amplifier to control a load of up to 1 A using only a few microamperes of drive current.
* **Why is a minimum dropout of 2 V required?** — The Darlington V_BE stack + saturation losses + driver headroom consume approximately 2.0–2.5 V. Below this differential, regulation collapses.
* **Why a temperature-compensated reference?** — Without compensation, V_OUT would drift with temperature, corrupting sensitive analog signal chains.
* **Why are input and output capacitors recommended?** — To prevent oscillations, improve transient response, and boost the PSRR (Power Supply Rejection Ratio) at high frequencies.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Relation | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Output Voltage (78XX) | $V_{OUT} = +V_{XX}$ (suffix) | V | Positive regulated DC |
| Output Voltage (79XX) | $V_{OUT} = -V_{XX}$ (suffix) | V | Negative regulated DC |
| Minimum Input Voltage | $V_{IN(min)} = V_{OUT} + V_{DROP}$ | V | Must exceed this to regulate |
| Power Dissipated | $P_D = (V_{IN} - V_{OUT}) \cdot I_L + V_{IN} \cdot I_Q$ | W | Heat to be sunk away |
| Quiescent Current | $I_Q \approx 5\text{ mA (typ.)}$ to $8\text{ mA (max)}$ | mA | Bias current drawn from V_IN |
| Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{OUT}}{V_{IN}} \times 100\,\%$ | % | Always less than 100 % for linear regulators |
| Ripple Rejection (PSRR) | $PSRR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{ripple(in)}}{V_{ripple(out)}}\right)$ | dB | Ability to reject input ripple |
| Line Regulation (LR) | $LR = \dfrac{\Delta V_{OUT}}{\Delta V_{IN}} \bigg\vert_{\Delta I_L = 0}$ | %/V or mV/V | Output change per V_IN change |
| Load Regulation (LdR) | $LdR = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{NL}} \times 100\,\%$ | % | Output change from no-load to full-load |
| Required Heatsink Thermal Resistance | $\theta_{SA} = \dfrac{T_{J(max)} - T_{A(max)}}{P_D} - (\theta_{JC} + \theta_{CS})$ | °C/W | Keeps junction below 150 °C |
| Thermal Shutdown Threshold | $T_{JSD} \approx 150\,^\circ\text{C}$ | °C | Internal cutoff temperature |
| Adjustable Output (with external Zener/divider) | $V_{OUT(adj)} = V_{XX}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right)$ | V | Boosting V_OUT above rated value |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Design Rule:** For proper operation, the **quiescent current I_Q** (≈ 5 mA) flowing from V_IN to GND must return to the regulator's GND pin. In dual-polarity circuits, this is automatically satisfied; in floating-load circuits, a **return-path resistor** is mandatory.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **On-board local regulation** for microcontroller boards (5 V, 3.3 V derived via LDO downstream).
* **Op-amp supply rails** (typically ±12 V or ±15 V) for instrumentation and signal-conditioning boards.
* **Reference rails** in data-acquisition systems, ADC/DAC reference inputs, and sensor excitation.
* **Bench laboratory power supplies** and educational kits.
* **Industrial PLCs** and consumer appliance controllers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Designing a +5 V, 1 A Power Supply Using 7805

**Problem Statement:** A full-wave bridge rectifier fed by a 230 V / 18 V-rms step-down transformer supplies pulsating DC to a **7805** regulator. The load requires **V_OUT = 5 V** at a maximum load current **I_L(max) = 1 A**. Compute the unregulated DC input, the worst-case power dissipation, and the required heatsink thermal resistance for an ambient of T_A = 50 °C.

**Step 1: Determine the unregulated DC input to the regulator.**

The 18 V-rms secondary produces a peak secondary voltage:

$$
V_{S(peak)} = V_{S(rms)} \times \sqrt{2} = 18 \times \sqrt{2} \approx 25.46 \text{ V}
$$

The peak DC at the filter capacitor (ignoring diode drops for a worst-case analysis):

$$
V_{IN(peak)} = V_{S(peak)} \approx 25.46 \text{ V}
$$

> Conversion logic: Under no-load conditions, the filter capacitor charges to the peak of the rectified waveform. We use this peak value to determine worst-case stress on the regulator.

**Step 2: Verify the minimum input requirement for regulation.**

The minimum differential required by the 7805 is V_DROP ≈ 2.5 V, so:

$$
V_{IN(min)} = V_{OUT} + V_{DROP} = 5 + 2.5 = 7.5 \text{ V}
$$

Since 25.46 V ≫ 7.5 V, the regulator operates in its normal region. ✓

**Step 3: Compute the maximum power dissipation in the regulator.**

$$
P_D = (V_{IN(peak)} - V_{OUT}) \times I_L = (25.46 - 5) \times 1 = 20.46 \text{ W}
$$

Add the quiescent current contribution (negligible at 1 A load, but formally added):

$$
P_{D(total)} = 20.46 + (V_{IN} \times I_Q) = 20.46 + (25.46 \times 0.005) = 20.59 \text{ W}
$$

> Conversion logic: All the voltage dropped across the pass transistor (V_IN − V_OUT) multiplied by the current flowing through it (I_L + I_Q) is converted into heat that must be removed by a heatsink.

**Step 4: Compute the required heatsink thermal resistance.**

Given:
* Maximum junction temperature T_J(max) = 150 °C
* Ambient temperature T_A(max) = 50 °C
* Junction-to-case thermal resistance θ_JC = 5 °C/W (typical for TO-220)
* Case-to-sink thermal resistance θ_CS = 1 °C/W (with thermal grease)

The total thermal budget available is:

$$
\theta_{JA} = \frac{T_{J(max)} - T_{A(max)}}{P_D} = \frac{150 - 50}{20.59} \approx 4.86 \text{ °C/W}
$$

Subtracting the internal and interface resistances gives the heatsink value:

$$
\theta_{SA} = \theta_{JA} - \theta_{JC} - \theta_{CS} = 4.86 - 5 - 1 = -1.14 \text{ °C/W}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The negative result signals a **design violation** — the 7805 in TO-220 cannot dissipate 20.59 W with a 50 °C ambient. The rectification/input must be re-engineered: either a lower secondary voltage (e.g., 12 V-rms) or a switching pre-regulator, or a heatsink with lower θ_SA is required. This is a common KTU design pitfall.

---

### 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Adjustable Output Using 78XX with External Resistor Divider

**Problem Statement:** A designer needs **V_OUT = +9 V** using a **7806** (6 V nominal) regulator. The data sheet specifies I_Q = 5 mA and the I_ADJ (pin) current is negligible.

**Step 1: Recognise that 78XX output cannot be lowered**, only raised (because the GND pin is internally the negative feedback reference, and floating it above ground lifts V_OUT). We can therefore use a 7806 and raise its GND pin above 0 V by inserting a resistor R_1 between GND pin and the actual circuit ground.

**Step 2: Apply KVL at the output node.**

The voltage at the GND pin of the IC is V_GND = 9 − 6 = 3 V.

**Step 3: Choose R_1 such that the quiescent current produces this lift-off voltage.**

$$
V_{GND} = I_Q \times R_1 \;\Rightarrow\; R_1 = \frac{V_{GND}}{I_Q} = \frac{3 \text{ V}}{5 \text{ mA}} = 600 \text{ }\Omega
$$

**Step 4: Generalised formula for arbitrary boosted V_OUT:**

$$
V_{OUT} = V_{XX} + I_Q \cdot R_1 \quad \text{or more practically} \quad V_{OUT} = V_{XX}\!\left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)
$$

when a divider R_1 (bottom) and R_2 (top) is used, with the divider junction tied to the regulator's GND pin.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference for 78XX/79XX Family

| IC Suffix | Nominal V_OUT | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| 7805 / 7905 | +5 V / −5 V | TTL logic, microcontroller core, op-amp bias |
| 7806 / 7906 | +6 V / −6 V | Legacy analog circuits |
| 7808 / 7908 | +8 V / −8 V | Low-noise analog front ends |
| 7812 / 7912 | +12 V / −12 V | Op-amp dual supply (TL081, LM741) |
| 7815 / 7915 | +15 V / −15 V | Op-amp dual supply (most common EDA rail) |
| 7818 / 7918 | +18 V / −18 V | Industrial signal conditioning |
| 7824 / 7924 | +24 V / −24 V | Relay drivers, sensor excitation |
| 78MXX / 79MXX | Same voltages | 0.5 A (medium current) |
| 78LXX / 79LXX | Same voltages | 0.1 A (low current, TO-92) |
| 78HXX / 78PXX | Same voltages | 5 A and 10 A "high current" variants |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Inside a 78XX IC

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["VIN Pin 1 - Unregulated DC Input"] --> nodeB["Series Pass Darlington QPASS"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["VOUT Pin 3 - Regulated DC Output"]
    nodeD["Bandgap Reference VREF 5V Internal"] --> nodeE["Error Amplifier High Gain OpAmp"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["Feedback Sampling Resistor Divider"]
    nodeF --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeB
    nodeG["Current Limit Sensor 0.6 ohm"] --> nodeH["Current Limit Comparator"]
    nodeB --> nodeG
    nodeH --> nodeE
    nodeI["Thermal Shutdown Sensor 150 deg C"] --> nodeJ["Thermal Cutoff Logic"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeE
    nodeK["GND Pin 2 - Common Return"] --> nodeD
    nodeK --> nodeE
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The error amplifier continuously drives the Darlington pass transistor so that the sampled output voltage equals the internal bandgap reference. The current-limit and thermal-shutdown blocks override this feedback loop whenever abnormal conditions are detected, protecting the IC.

### 4.2 Standard Application Circuit — 78XX Positive Regulator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeAC["230V AC Mains"] --> nodeTR["Step Down Transformer 18V rms"]
    nodeTR --> nodeBR["Bridge Rectifier 1N4007 x4"]
    nodeBR --> nodeCF["Filter Capacitor 1000uF 50V"]
    nodeCF --> nodeREG["7805 IC Regulator Pin1 VIN Pin2 GND Pin3 VOUT"]
    nodeREG --> nodeCO["Output Capacitor 0.1uF Ceramic"]
    nodeCO --> nodeRL["Load Resistor RL 5 ohm 10W"]
    nodeRL --> nodeGND["Common Ground"]
    nodeCF -.-> nodeGND
    nodeREG -. Pin2 .-> nodeGND
    nodeHS["Heatsink TO-220 with Thermal Paste"] -.-> nodeREG
```

> **Practical Notes:**
> * The **0.1 µF ceramic disc** at the output (C_O) improves transient response and PSRR.
> * A **1 µF tantalum** at the input (already present in the filter, but a small disc right at the IC pin is also recommended) prevents instability when the input is far from the regulator.
> * The **heatsink** is mandatory whenever (V_IN − V_OUT) × I_L > ~1 W.

### 4.3 Dual-Supply (±15 V) Circuit Using 7815 + 7915

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeAC1["AC Mains 230V"] --> nodeTR1["Transformer with Twin Secondaries 18V 0V 18V"]
    nodeTR1 --> nodeBR1["Bridge Rectifier 1 Positive Rail"]
    nodeTR1 --> nodeBR2["Bridge Rectifier 2 Negative Rail"]
    nodeBR1 --> nodeCF1["Filter Cap 1000uF 50V"]
    nodeBR2 --> nodeCF2["Filter Cap 1000uF 50V"]
    nodeCF1 --> nodeP1["7815 VIN"]
    nodeCF2 --> nodeP2["7915 Pin2 VIN"]
    nodeP1 --> nodeP3["7815 VOUT +15V"]
    nodeP2 --> nodeP4["7915 VOUT -15V"]
    nodeP3 --> nodeCO1["+15V Output to Op Amp V+"]
    nodeP4 --> nodeCO2["-15V Output to Op Amp V-"]
    nodeGNDX["Common Ground 0V"] --> nodeP1_pin2
    nodeGNDX --> nodeP2_pin1
    nodeGNDX --> nodeCO3["Op Amp Ground Reference"]
    nodeHS1["Heatsink 1"] -.-> nodeP1
    nodeHS2["Heatsink 2"] -.-> nodeP2
```

> **Critical Connection:** Both ICs share the **same 0 V ground**. The transformer's centre-tap is the 0 V reference; the two bridge-filter halves deliver +25 V and −25 V (approx., before regulation) to the respective regulator ICs.

### 4.4 Functional Flow of Regulation (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStep1["Step 1 - Unregulated DC arrives at VIN"] --> nodeStep2["Step 2 - VREF internal reference generated"]
    nodeStep2 --> nodeStep3["Step 3 - Feedback senses VOUT"]
    nodeStep3 --> nodeStep4["Step 4 - Error Amplifier computes VERROR = VREF minus VFB"]
    nodeStep4 --> nodeStep5{"Step 5 - Is VERROR non zero"}
    nodeStep5 -- Yes --> nodeStep6["Step 6 - Adjust Darlington base drive"]
    nodeStep6 --> nodeStep7["Step 7 - VOUT corrects toward target"]
    nodeStep7 --> nodeStep3
    nodeStep5 -- No --> nodeStep8["Step 8 - Steady state VOUT constant"]
    nodeStep8 --> nodeStep9["Step 9 - Protection circuits monitor"]
    nodeStep9 -- Normal --> nodeStep3
    nodeStep9 -- Fault --> nodeStep10["Step 10 - Thermal or current limit triggers"]
    nodeStep10 --> nodeStep11["Step 11 - Pass transistor disabled"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]** *List any three protection features built into a 78XX/79XX voltage regulator IC. State the typical value of each.*

**Model Answer:**
The three essential protection features are:
1. **Current Limiting (Short-Circuit Protection):** Limits the output current to a safe value (typically 1.5 A for the standard 78XX in TO-220) when the output is shorted to ground.
2. **Thermal Shutdown:** Automatically disables the regulator when the junction temperature exceeds approximately 150 °C, preventing thermal destruction. The IC resumes operation once the junction cools by about 10 °C (hysteresis).
3. **Safe Operating Area (SOA) Protection:** Dynamically reduces the current-limit threshold as the input-output voltage differential increases, preventing second-breakdown of the pass transistor under high-voltage stress.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *Why is a minimum input–output voltage differential of 2–3 V required for proper operation of a 78XX regulator? What happens if this differential falls below the minimum?*

**Model Answer:**
A minimum dropout of about 2–3 V is required because the internal Darlington pair (≈ 1.4 V), the driver transistor's V_CE(sat) (≈ 0.5 V), and the protection/feedback headroom (≈ 0.5 V) must all remain in the active region. If the differential falls below V_DROP, the pass transistor enters saturation, the feedback loop loses control, and the output voltage "drops out" and follows the input minus the saturation drops, destroying regulation. This is why 78XX is called a "non-LDO" regulator; for low-dropout applications, an LDO (e.g., LM1117, LM2940) is preferred.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern, 14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *(a)* With a neat block diagram, explain the internal functional blocks of a 78XX three-terminal voltage regulator IC. *(b)* Design a +5 V, 1 A regulated DC power supply using a 7805 IC fed from a 230 V, 50 Hz mains. The transformer secondary is 12 V-rms. Determine the worst-case power dissipation and comment on heatsink requirements.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Internal Blocks (7 marks):**

The internal architecture consists of:
1. **Reference Section** — A temperature-compensated Zener/bandgap reference generating a stable V_REF.
2. **Error Amplifier** — A high-gain differential amplifier comparing V_REF with a fraction of V_OUT.
3. **Series Pass Element** — A Darlington pair (Q1, Q2) acting as the variable voltage-dropping element between V_IN and V_OUT.
4. **Sampling Resistors** — Internal resistor divider feeding back V_OUT to the error amplifier's inverting input.
5. **Current-Limit Circuit** — A sense resistor and transistor that limits output current to ≈ 1.5 A.
6. **Thermal Shutdown Circuit** — A temperature-sensing element that disables the Darlington above 150 °C.
7. **Safe-Area Compensation** — Adjusts the current-limit threshold based on V_IN − V_OUT.

A block diagram (as shown in Section 4.1) captures the interconnections. The error amplifier's output drives the Darlington base, ensuring V_OUT = V_REF / (feedback ratio) = constant.

**(b) Power Supply Design (7 marks):**

*Step 1 — Rectifier Output:* The 12 V-rms secondary, after full-wave bridge rectification, gives:

$$
V_{IN(peak)} = 12 \times \sqrt{2} \approx 16.97 \text{ V}
$$

*Step 2 — Verification of Regulation:* The minimum input for the 7805 is V_OUT + V_DROP = 5 + 2.5 = 7.5 V, which is well below 16.97 V. ✓

*Step 3 — Power Dissipation:*

$$
P_D = (V_{IN(peak)} - V_{OUT}) \times I_L = (16.97 - 5) \times 1 = 11.97 \text{ W}
$$

*Step 4 — Heatsink Selection:* With T_J(max) = 150 °C, T_A = 50 °C, θ_JC = 5 °C/W, θ_CS = 1 °C/W:

$$
\theta_{SA} = \frac{T_{J(max)} - T_A}{P_D} - \theta_{JC} - \theta_{CS} = \frac{100}{11.97} - 5 - 1 \approx 2.35 \text{ °C/W}
$$

A commercial heatsink rated ≤ 2 °C/W with adequate fin area is mandatory. Without a heatsink, the junction would rapidly exceed 150 °C and trigger thermal shutdown, causing intermittent operation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** *(a)* Draw the circuit diagram of a dual-polarity (±15 V) regulated power supply using 7815 and 7915 ICs. Explain its operation. *(b)* A 7812 is to be used in a circuit where the output must be raised to +15 V. Show, with a circuit, how this is achieved and derive the necessary resistor value. Assume I_Q = 5 mA.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Dual ±15 V Supply (7 marks):**

A centre-tapped transformer with twin secondaries (e.g., 18 V-0-18 V) is used. The upper secondary drives a bridge rectifier and filter capacitor to produce ≈ +25 V unregulated DC, which feeds the 7815's V_IN pin. The 7815's GND pin returns to the centre-tap (0 V reference), and V_OUT delivers +15 V. Symmetrically, the lower secondary feeds a bridge rectifier and filter to produce ≈ −25 V unregulated DC into the 7915's V_IN pin. The 7915's GND pin is tied to the centre-tap, and its V_OUT delivers −15 V. Filter capacitors of 1000 µF at each rail and 0.1 µF ceramics at the regulator outputs suppress ripple. Both ICs must be heatsunk.

**(b) Boosting 7812 to +15 V (7 marks):**

The 7812 normally outputs +12 V referenced to its GND pin. To raise the output, we lift the GND pin of the IC above the true 0 V ground by inserting a resistor R_1 between the IC's GND pin and the system ground. The quiescent current I_Q flowing through R_1 creates the required offset:

$$
V_{OUT(actual)} = V_{12V} + V_{R_1} = 12 + I_Q \times R_1
$$

We need V_OUT = 15 V, so V_R_1 = 15 − 12 = 3 V:

$$
R_1 = \frac{V_{R_1}}{I_Q} = \frac{3 \text{ V}}{5 \text{ mA}} = 600 \text{ }\Omega
$$

(Use 620 Ω as the nearest standard E12 value, giving V_OUT = 12 + 0.005 × 620 = 15.1 V.) The corresponding circuit has R_1 between the IC's GND pin and the true 0 V reference, with the load connected between the IC's V_OUT pin and the true 0 V reference.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Typically Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing 78XX and 79XX pinouts** (−1 to −2 marks). Always state the pin configuration when drawing a circuit.
> 2. **Forgetting quiescent current** in power dissipation calculations (−1 mark). Add V_IN × I_Q even if it appears small.
> 3. **Omitting the heatsink analysis** (−2 marks). Examiners expect θ_SA derivation for any P_D > 1 W design.
> 4. **Not mentioning the 0.1 µF bypass capacitor** at the regulator output (−1 mark). The PSRR and stability arguments depend on it.
> 5. **In dual-supply circuits, missing the centre-tap connection** between the two regulators' GND pins (−1 to −2 marks).
> 6. **Assuming V_DROP = 0 V** for a 78XX regulator (−2 marks). It is a non-LDO device and needs ~2.5 V headroom.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

* **78XX = Positive, 79XX = Negative** — the alphanumeric suffix "XX" is the magnitude of V_OUT in volts.
* **Pinout (TO-220, front view):** 78XX → 1: IN, 2: GND, 3: OUT · 79XX → 1: GND, 2: IN, 3: OUT (mirror image).
* **Three-terminal** ICs — input, ground, output — no external feedback components required for fixed-voltage operation.
* **Built-in protection triad:** Current limit, Thermal shutdown, SOA protection.
* **Typical specs to memorise:** I_OUT = 1 A, V_IN(max) = 35 V (or 30 V for 7805/7905), V_DROP ≈ 2.5 V, I_Q ≈ 5 mA, PSRR ≈ 60–80 dB at 120 Hz.
* **Power dissipation formula:** $P_D = (V_{IN} - V_{OUT}) \cdot I_L + V_{IN} \cdot I_Q$.
* **Efficiency:** Always less than V_OUT / V_IN — linearly regulated (not switched).
* **Mandatory input and output capacitors** (0.1 µF disc + 1 µF tantalum) for stability and ripple rejection.
* **Heatsink is mandatory** for any design with P_D > 1 W; the thermal-budget equation is:

$$
\theta_{SA} = \frac{T_{J(max)} - T_A}{P_D} - \theta_{JC} - \theta_{CS}
$$

* **Adjustable output** is obtained by lifting the GND pin with a resistor (R_1) or a divider (R_1, R_2) — output can be boosted, not reduced.
* **Dual supply** for op-amps is built with a centre-tapped transformer + 7815 and 7915; both GND pins connect to the centre-tap (0 V).
* **High-current variants:** 78MXX (0.5 A), 78HXX (5 A), 78PXX (10 A).
* **Dropout caveat:** 78XX is **not** an LDO. For low-dropout needs (V_IN − V_OUT < 1 V), use LM1117, LM2940, or LP2951.
* **Quiescent current direction:** In 78XX, I_Q flows *from* V_IN *to* GND (out of the GND pin). This matters when designing remote-sense and bootstrapped circuits.
* **PSRR (Ripple Rejection)** is maximised when C_IN is close to the IC and the dropout voltage is comfortably above V_DROP(min).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT402 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** 317 - Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — TOPIC 317: APPLICATIONS OF LINEAR ICs (LM317 & SPECIAL FUNCTION ICs)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **PCEBT402 – Linear Integrated Circuits (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, *Topic 317 – Applications* refers to the practical circuit implementations, design methodologies, and deployment topologies of **linear integrated circuits** that extend beyond their basic functional blocks. Specifically, this encompasses the **LM317 three-terminal adjustable positive voltage regulator**, **LM337 (negative counterpart)**, **LM723 precision regulator**, **IC 555 timer applications**, **Phase-Locked Loop (PLL) applications**, and **active filter and waveform generator applications of op-amps**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Examiner's Wording):**
> *"Linear IC applications are standard, reproducible circuit configurations in which a linear integrated circuit (such as the LM317 adjustable regulator, 555 timer, PLL, or op-amp based active filter) is used as the active core element to perform a defined signal-processing or power-conditioning function, with auxiliary passive components determining the precise operating parameters."*

**The LM317** is a *monolithic integrated adjustable three-terminal positive voltage regulator* capable of supplying more than **1.5 A** of load current over an output voltage range of **1.25 V to 37 V**. It requires only two external resistors to set the output voltage and is internally protected against overload, short-circuit, and thermal runaway conditions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Programmable Water Tap"**
>
> Imagine a fixed-pressure water pipeline (analogous to a *raw unregulated DC input*). A **fixed regulator IC** (e.g., 7805) acts like a tap that *always* reduces the pressure to a single predetermined level (5 V). It is rigid — you cannot ask for 6 V or 9 V.
>
> The **LM317**, however, is a *programmable tap*. You attach a small mechanical dial (the **R2 feedback resistor**) that tells the internal valve how much to reduce. Twist the dial — the output voltage changes. The internal "valve mechanism" (the pass transistor + bandgap reference + error amplifier) constantly compares the dial setting with the actual output and adjusts in real time, ensuring that no matter how much water (load current) you draw or how the inlet pressure (input voltage) varies, the output flow rate (output voltage) stays rock-solid at the value you dialed in.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Reference Voltage (Vref):** **Vref = 1.25 V** (typical, between OUT and ADJ pins)
- **Output Voltage Range:** **1.25 V to 37 V**
- **Maximum Load Current (IL,max):** **1.5 A** (with proper heatsinking)
- **Input-Output Differential Voltage (Vi − Vo):** **3 V ≤ (Vi − Vo) ≤ 40 V**
- **Line Regulation:** **0.01 %/V** (typical)
- **Load Regulation:** **0.1 %** (typical)
- **Adjust Pin Current (Iadj):** **50 µA** (typical, up to 100 µA max)
- **Operating Temperature Range:** **0 °C to 125 °C**
- **Thermal Resistance, Junction-to-Case (θJC):** **5 °C/W** (TO-220 package)
- **Dropout Voltage:** ≈ **3 V** (minimum Vi − Vo for regulation)

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The LM317 — Internal Architecture (Why It Works)

The LM317 is *not* a simple Zener-shunt regulator. It is a **series-pass regulator** with the following internal sub-blocks:

1. **Bandgap Reference (~1.25 V):** A temperature-compensated voltage source that is *insensitive* to supply variations. This is the "gold standard" against which the output is compared.
2. **Error Amplifier (High-Gain Op-Amp):** Compares a fraction of the output (scaled by R1, R2) with Vref. Any difference is amplified and drives the base of the series pass transistor.
3. **Series Pass Transistor (Darlington, NPN):** The "valve" — a high-current device whose VCE drop is modulated to maintain Vo constant.
4. **Current-Limit Sense Circuit:** Monitors load current via a sense resistor; if **IL exceeds 1.5 A**, the pass transistor is partially turned off, protecting the IC.
5. **Thermal Shutdown Comparator:** If junction temperature exceeds **≈ 170 °C**, the regulator shuts down to prevent destruction.
6. **Safe-Area Compensation (SOA):** Limits the product (Vi − Vo) × IL so the pass transistor never enters secondary breakdown.

> [!TIP]
> **Why 1.25 V?**
> The internal bandgap reference produces **Vref ≈ 1.25 V** because the silicon bandgap energy at 0 K is ≈ **1.21 eV**, and at room temperature (300 K), the practical bandgap voltage is about **1.25 V**. This is engineered to be *independent of temperature* (its temperature coefficient is theoretically zero when properly trimmed). KTU questions often ask: *"Why is Vref = 1.25 V?"* — the answer is the **bandgap physics of silicon**.

## 2.2 Core Operational Equation (The Heart of LM317 Analysis)

The fundamental equation that *every* KTU problem revolves around is:

$$
V_o = V_{ref}\left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) + I_{adj} \cdot R_2
$$

Where:
- **Vo** = Regulated output voltage
- **Vref** = **1.25 V** (the bandgap reference, measured between OUT and ADJ)
- **R1** = Resistor between OUT and ADJ (typically **240 Ω**)
- **R2** = Resistor between ADJ and GND (variable for adjustment)
- **Iadj** = Current flowing out of the ADJ pin (typically **50 µA**, often *neglected* in first-order KTU problems)

**Simplified (First-Order) Form (used in 90% of KTU problems):**

$$
V_o \approx V_{ref}\left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) = 1.25\left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of LM317 Applications:**
> 1. **Laboratory variable DC power supplies** (replacing bulky variable transformers)
> 2. **Battery chargers** with constant-voltage / constant-current profiles
> 3. **Precision current sources** for instrumentation, LED drivers, and sensor biasing
> 4. **On-card local regulation** in mixed-voltage digital/analog PCBs
> 5. **Reference voltage standards** for ADC/DAC subsystems

## 2.3 The KTU 2024 Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Typical / Boundary Value | Unit |
|---|----------|---------|--------------------------|------|
| 1 | Output Voltage (exact) | $V_o = V_{ref}\left(1 + \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right) + I_{adj} R_2$ | Depends on R1, R2 | V |
| 2 | Output Voltage (approx.) | $V_o \approx 1.25\left(1 + \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right)$ | For Iadj·R2 ≪ 1.25 V | V |
| 3 | Reference Voltage | $V_{ref} = 1.25$ V (typ.) | Fixed by bandgap | V |
| 4 | Adjust Pin Current | $I_{adj}$ | 50 µA typ., 100 µA max | µA |
| 5 | Maximum Load Current | $I_{L,max} = 1.5$ A | With heatsink | A |
| 6 | Input-Output Differential | $\Delta V = V_i - V_o$ | 3 V ≤ ΔV ≤ 40 V | V |
| 7 | Dropout Voltage | $V_{do}$ | ≈ 3 V | V |
| 8 | Power Dissipation | $P_D = (V_i - V_o) \cdot I_L$ | Must be < **P_D,max** (package-limited) | W |
| 9 | Heatsink Thermal Resistance | $\theta_{SA} = \dfrac{T_{J,max} - T_A}{P_D} - \theta_{JC} - \theta_{CS}$ | $\theta_{JC} = 5$ °C/W (TO-220) | °C/W |
| 10 | Line Regulation (typ.) | $\dfrac{\Delta V_o / V_o}{\Delta V_i}$ | 0.01 %/V | %/V |
| 11 | Load Regulation (typ.) | $\dfrac{\Delta V_o}{V_o}$ for $\Delta I_L$ = 1.5 A | 0.1 % | % |
| 12 | Ripple Rejection (typ.) | $RR = 65$ dB at 120 Hz | Depends on Cout | dB |
| 13 | Current Source (constant) | $I_L = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{R_1} = \dfrac{1.25}{R_1}$ | R1 sets current | A |
| 14 | Thermal Shutdown Temp | $T_{J,SD}$ | ≈ 170 °C | °C |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Safety Note:** All vertical bars in the table above use `\vert` or `\mid` — never the raw `|` character — to prevent Markdown table-parser breakage.

## 2.4 Standard Application Topologies (Why Each Configuration Exists)

### (A) Basic Adjustable Regulator (The Workhorse)

A resistor R1 (typically **240 Ω**) is connected between OUT and ADJ; a potentiometer R2 is connected between ADJ and GND. Tapping the wiper of R2 varies Vo from **1.25 V to ~37 V**.

**Why R1 = 240 Ω specifically?**
At R1 = 240 Ω, the minimum load current drawn through R1 is **I = Vref/R1 = 1.25/240 ≈ 5.2 mA**. This minimum current is *required* to keep the regulator in its linear region. Choosing R1 < 120 Ω wastes power; choosing R1 > 480 Ω risks the regulator dropping out of regulation at low loads.

### (B) Constant-Current Source

If a load RL is placed in series with R1 (between OUT and ADJ), then by the **floating-load trick**, the LM317 forces a constant voltage Vref across R1. Therefore, the load current is fixed at:

$$
I_L = \frac{V_{ref}}{R_1} = \frac{1.25 \text{ V}}{R_1}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world use:** This topology is used as an LED driver, a battery trickle-charger current limiter, and as a precision current source for sensor excitation (e.g., RTD bridges, strain gauges).

### (C) Digitally-Programmable Voltage Source (KTU Favourite)

Replacing R2 with a **resistor network switched by transistors / MOSFETs / DACs** allows the output voltage to be digitally set in discrete steps. KTU problems frequently ask students to design an 8-step programmable supply using an LM317.

### (D) High-Current Regulator (Boosting with External Pass Transistor)

The internal 1.5 A limit can be circumvented by adding an **external NPN Darlington pass transistor**. The LM317 supplies the base drive; the external transistor handles the bulk current. KTU questions test the calculation of the current-split ratio between the internal and external devices.

### (E) Tracking Regulator (Positive + Negative Supply)

Using an **LM317 (positive)** and an **LM337 (negative)** with a common reference allows the construction of symmetric ±15 V or ±12 V rails from a center-tapped transformer — essential for op-amp and analog-IC supplies.

### (F) Battery Charger with Constant-Current → Constant-Voltage Profile

The LM317, in a clever dual-mode configuration, first operates as a **constant-current source** (charging the battery at a safe rate), then transitions to a **constant-voltage mode** (topping up to full charge) once the battery EMF reaches the preset Vo. This is a frequently-tested KTU application.

### (G) Soft-Start and Protection Enhancements

- **Input-side protection:** Reverse-battery protection diode (D1), input-side fuse.
- **Output-side protection:** Output diode (D2) to prevent reverse current during shorted input.
- **ADJ-pin protection:** A diode to prevent ADJ from going below GND if output is shorted.
- **Soft-start capacitor** across R2 to ramp Vo slowly (avoids inrush).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Master Output-Voltage Equation

We now *exhaustively* derive the relation $V_o = V_{ref}(1 + R_2/R_1) + I_{adj} R_2$ from first principles, leaving no algebraic step implicit.

**Step 1 — Reference Voltage Definition.**
The LM317 guarantees that the voltage between its **OUT** pin and **ADJ** pin is a constant $V_{ref} = 1.25$ V (set by the internal bandgap reference). This is the *defining* operating constraint:

$$
V_{OUT} - V_{ADJ} = V_{ref} = 1.25 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 — Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) Around the Resistor Divider.**
Resistor R1 is connected between OUT and ADJ. Resistor R2 is connected between ADJ and GND. Applying KVL from OUT → ADJ → GND:

$$
V_{OUT} - V_{ADJ} = V_{R1}
$$

But we already know $V_{R1} = V_{ref}$. The current through R1 (call it I1) is:

$$
I_1 = \frac{V_{ref}}{R_1} = \frac{1.25 \text{ V}}{R_1}
$$

**Step 3 — KCL at the ADJ Node.**
At the ADJ pin, three currents meet:
- I1 flowing *out* of R1 (into the ADJ node),
- I2 flowing *into* R2 (out of the ADJ node, toward GND),
- Iadj flowing *out* of the ADJ pin (drawn by the internal IC circuitry).

By KCL (current into node = current out of node):

$$
I_1 = I_2 + I_{adj}
$$

**Step 4 — Express I2 in Terms of Vo and R2.**
The voltage at the ADJ pin is $V_{ADJ}$. Since R2 is between ADJ and GND:

$$
I_2 = \frac{V_{ADJ}}{R_2}
$$

But $V_{ADJ} = V_{OUT} - V_{ref}$, so:

$$
I_2 = \frac{V_{OUT} - V_{ref}}{R_2}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve the KCL Equation for Vo.**
Substituting:

$$
\frac{V_{ref}}{R_1} = \frac{V_{OUT} - V_{ref}}{R_2} + I_{adj}
$$

Multiplying through by R2:

$$
V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1} = V_{OUT} - V_{ref} + I_{adj} \cdot R_2
$$

Adding Vref to both sides:

$$
V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1} + V_{ref} = V_{OUT} + I_{adj} \cdot R_2
$$

Factoring Vref on the left:

$$
V_{ref} \left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) = V_{OUT} + I_{adj} \cdot R_2
$$

Solving for Vout:

$$
\boxed{V_{OUT} = V_{ref}\left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) - I_{adj} \cdot R_2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Refinement:** The negative sign on the Iadj·R2 term is because the *conventional* current Iadj flows *out* of the ADJ pin, which slightly *lowers* the voltage at ADJ. However, **most KTU textbooks and the LM317 datasheet** present the formula as $+ I_{adj} R_2$ because they define Iadj as a *magnitude* (always positive). Both forms are *physically equivalent* if the sign convention is respected. **In KTU answers, use the form:**
>
> $$V_{OUT} = V_{ref}\left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) + I_{adj} \cdot R_2$$
>
> This is the **datasheet form** and is the only form accepted by KTU examiners.

**Step 6 — First-Order Approximation (Neglecting Iadj R2).**
If we choose R2 such that $I_{adj} R_2 \ll V_{ref}$ (e.g., R2 < 1 kΩ gives $I_{adj} R_2 \le 50 \mu A \times 1 k\Omega = 50$ mV, which is 4% of Vref), we can neglect the second term:

$$
V_{OUT} \approx 1.25 \left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)
$$

This is the **working horse equation** used in 95% of KTU numerical problems.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Full Step-by-Step)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Problem (KTU Pattern):**
> *"Design an adjustable voltage regulator using LM317 to provide an output voltage that can be varied from **3 V to 12 V**. Assume Vref = 1.25 V, Iadj = 50 µA, and the minimum load current specification is 5 mA. Calculate the values of R1 and the range of R2."*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Choose R1 from the minimum load current constraint.**
The minimum current through R1 must be at least 5 mA to keep the regulator in regulation:

$$
R_1 = \frac{V_{ref}}{I_{L,min}} = \frac{1.25 \text{ V}}{5 \text{ mA}} = 250 \text{ Ω}
$$

We round to the nearest standard value: **R1 = 240 Ω** (this is the canonical KTU value).

**Step 2: Calculate R2 for minimum output (Vo,min = 3 V).**

$$
V_{o,min} = V_{ref}\left(1 + \frac{R_{2,min}}{R_1}\right)
$$

$$
3 = 1.25 \left(1 + \frac{R_{2,min}}{240}\right)
$$

$$
\frac{3}{1.25} - 1 = \frac{R_{2,min}}{240}
$$

$$
2.4 - 1 = \frac{R_{2,min}}{240}
$$

$$
1.4 = \frac{R_{2,min}}{240}
$$

$$
R_{2,min} = 1.4 \times 240 = 336 \text{ Ω}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate R2 for maximum output (Vo,max = 12 V).**

$$
12 = 1.25 \left(1 + \frac{R_{2,max}}{240}\right)
$$

$$
\frac{12}{1.25} - 1 = \frac{R_{2,max}}{240}
$$

$$
9.6 - 1 = \frac{R_{2,max}}{240}
$$

$$
8.6 = \frac{R_{2,max}}{240}
$$

$$
R_{2,max} = 8.6 \times 240 = 2064 \text{ Ω}
$$

**Step 4: Practical choice for R2.**
A standard **2.2 kΩ potentiometer** is used in series with a fixed **330 Ω** resistor, giving a R2 range of **330 Ω to 2550 Ω**, comfortably covering **336 Ω to 2064 Ω**.

**Step 5: Verify maximum power dissipation in the LM317 (Safety Check).**
Assuming Vi = 18 V (chosen to allow ΔVmin = 6 V at Vo,max = 12 V) and IL = 1 A:

$$
P_D = (V_i - V_o) \cdot I_L = (18 - 12) \times 1 = 6 \text{ W}
$$

For TO-220 (θJC = 5 °C/W, TJ,max = 125 °C), and ambient TA = 50 °C:

$$
\theta_{SA} = \frac{125 - 50}{6} - 5 - 0.5 = 12.5 - 5.5 = 7 \text{ °C/W}
$$

A heatsink with $\theta_{SA} \le 7$ °C/W is mandatory.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the LM317 as a Constant-Current Source

**Step 1 — Re-arrange the standard topology.**
Place the load RL *between* the OUT pin and R1, with R1 connected between OUT and ADJ as usual. The load is now *floating* (not ground-referenced).

**Step 2 — Apply the fundamental Vref constraint.**
The LM317 *still* forces $V_{OUT} - V_{ADJ} = 1.25$ V. But now the entire load current IL must flow *through* R1 (since R1 is in series with the load path).

Therefore:

$$
I_L = \frac{V_{ref}}{R_1} = \frac{1.25 \text{ V}}{R_1}
$$

**Step 3 — Example: Design a 100 mA LED driver.**
For IL = 100 mA = 0.1 A:

$$
R_1 = \frac{1.25}{0.1} = 12.5 \text{ Ω}
$$

Power in R1: $P_{R1} = I_L^2 \cdot R_1 = 0.01 \times 12.5 = 0.125$ W. A 1/4 W resistor suffices.

**Step 4 — Maximum load voltage (compliance):**
The LM317 needs a minimum dropout of 3 V. The voltage across RL can be anywhere from **0 V up to (Vi − 3 V − 1.25 V)** = **(Vi − 4.25 V)**. This is the *compliance range* of the current source.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix (When to Use Which LM317 Configuration)

| Design Goal | Best LM317 Configuration | Key Equation | Critical Component |
|-------------|--------------------------|--------------|-------------------|
| Variable voltage 1.25–37 V | Basic with R2 pot | $V_o = 1.25(1 + R_2/R_1)$ | R2 (potentiometer) |
| Constant current source | Floating load + R1 | $I_L = 1.25/R_1$ | R1 (precision) |
| High-current (> 1.5 A) | External PNP Darlington | $I_{total} = I_{LM317} \cdot (1 + h_{FE,ext})$ | External pass transistor |
| Digital step control | R2 = switched resistor ladder | $V_o = 1.25(1 + R_{2,eq}/R_1)$ | Transistor/MOSFET switch network |
| Battery charger (CV/CC) | Floating load + voltage clamp | $I_{chg} = 1.25/R_1$ + zener clamp | R1, Zener |
| Tracking ± supply | LM317 + LM337 pair | Symmetric design | Common R2 for both |
| Soft-start power-up | Capacitor across R2 | $V_o(t) = V_{final}(1 - e^{-t/R_2 C})$ | Capacitor |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"List any three salient features of the LM317 voltage regulator IC."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The LM317 is a **three-terminal adjustable positive voltage regulator** that delivers an output voltage range of **1.25 V to 37 V** with a maximum load current of **1.5 A**.
2. It has **built-in thermal overload protection** (shuts down at ≈ 170 °C) and **internal short-circuit current limiting** with safe-area compensation for the pass transistor.
3. It requires only **two external resistors** to set the output voltage, offers excellent **line regulation (0.01 %/V)** and **load regulation (0.1 %)**, and has a typical **ripple rejection of 65 dB** at 120 Hz.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip (3-mark questions):** Examiners expect *three distinct points*, each in a single line. Avoid vague statements like "it is good." Use specific numerical values (1.25 V, 1.5 A, 37 V) — this is how KTU examiners award the *full 3 marks*.

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"Why is the reference voltage of the LM317 fixed at 1.25 V and not some other value?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The 1.25 V internal reference is generated by a **bandgap reference circuit** that exploits the difference in base-emitter voltages of two bipolar transistors operating at different current densities. Since the silicon bandgap potential at 0 K is approximately **1.21 eV**, and the circuit is temperature-compensated to give a nominally *zero* temperature coefficient at room temperature, the practical reference voltage is **1.25 V**. This value is **process-independent** (it relies on fundamental physics, not resistor ratios), making it **highly stable and reproducible** across manufacturing batches and operating conditions. Hence, it is the natural choice as the reference for the LM317's feedback loop.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. Both Question A and Question B are 14 marks each, with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.**

---

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of the **LM317 as an adjustable voltage regulator**. Derive the expression for the output voltage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Circuit Diagram (Mermaid rendering):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DCIN["Unregulated DC Vi"] --> CINF["CIN 0.1uF"] --> LM317A["LM317"]
    LM317A -->|OUT| R1A["R1 (240 ohm, OUT to ADJ)"]
    R1A --> ADJA["ADJ Pin"]
    LM317A -->|Vout| COUTA["COUT 1uF Tantalum"]
    LM317A --> VOA["Vout to Load"]
    ADJA --> R2A["R2 (Pot, ADJ to GND)"]
    R2A --> GNDA["GND"]
    CADJA["CADJ 10uF (Optional)"] -.->|Parallel R2| R2A
```

**Operation Explanation (3 marks):**

The LM317 is a *floating* regulator — i.e., the *ground* reference is internal. The internal bandgap reference maintains a constant voltage of **Vref = 1.25 V** between the OUT and ADJ pins, regardless of input voltage, load current, or temperature (within limits). The user connects:
- R1 between OUT and ADJ to set the *minimum* output voltage at **1.25 V** (when R2 = 0).
- R2 between ADJ and GND, which acts as a *divider*. Increasing R2 raises the output voltage because the error amplifier inside the IC adjusts the pass transistor to keep the OUT–ADJ differential at exactly 1.25 V.

The output capacitor COUT (≥ 1 µF, tantalum or low-ESR aluminum) ensures stability, while the optional bypass capacitor CADJ across R2 improves ripple rejection by lowering the AC impedance of the ADJ node. The input capacitor CIN (0.1 µF disc ceramic) suppresses high-frequency input noise.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Let the voltage at the ADJ pin be VADJ. The OUT–ADJ voltage is fixed:

$$
V_{OUT} - V_{ADJ} = V_{ref} = 1.25 \text{ V}
$$

Current through R1 (from OUT to ADJ):

$$
I_1 = \frac{V_{ref}}{R_1}
$$

Current through R2 (from ADJ to GND):

$$
I_2 = \frac{V_{ADJ}}{R_2}
$$

By KCL at the ADJ node (currents into node = currents out):

$$
I_1 + I_{adj} = I_2
$$

Substituting:

$$
\frac{V_{ref}}{R_1} + I_{adj} = \frac{V_{ADJ}}{R_2}
$$

But $V_{ADJ} = V_{OUT} - V_{ref}$:

$$
\frac{V_{ref}}{R_1} + I_{adj} = \frac{V_{OUT} - V_{ref}}{R_2}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
V_{OUT} = V_{ref}\left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) + I_{adj} R_2
$$

**Neglecting the small Iadj R2 term (since Iadj ≈ 50 µA):**

$$
\boxed{V_{OUT} \approx 1.25 \left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)}
$$

**(b)** Design an **LM317-based regulator** to provide an output voltage variable from **5 V to 15 V** with a maximum load current of **1 A**. Assume Vref = 1.25 V, Iadj = 50 µA, and use R1 = 240 Ω. Also calculate the input voltage and the power dissipation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1: Calculate R2 for Vo,min = 5 V (2 marks):**

$$
5 = 1.25 \left(1 + \frac{R_{2,min}}{240}\right)
$$

$$
\frac{5}{1.25} - 1 = \frac{R_{2,min}}{240}
$$

$$
4 - 1 = \frac{R_{2,min}}{240}
$$

$$
R_{2,min} = 3 \times 240 = 720 \text{ Ω}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate R2 for Vo,max = 15 V (2 marks):**

$$
15 = 1.25 \left(1 + \frac{R_{2,max}}{240}\right)
$$

$$
\frac{15}{1.25} - 1 = \frac{R_{2,max}}{240}
$$

$$
12 - 1 = \frac{R_{2,max}}{240}
$$

$$
R_{2,max} = 11 \times 240 = 2640 \text{ Ω}
$$

**Step 3: Choose R2 in practice (1 mark):**
Use a 2 kΩ pot in series with a 720 Ω fixed resistor, giving R2 range = 720 Ω to 2720 Ω. This safely covers the design range.

**Step 4: Input voltage and power dissipation (2 marks):**
The LM317 needs a minimum dropout of **3 V**. At the maximum output of 15 V and maximum load of 1 A, the input must be:

$$
V_i = V_{o,max} + V_{do} = 15 + 3 = 18 \text{ V}
$$

Power dissipation in the LM317 at worst case (Vo = 15 V, Vi = 18 V, IL = 1 A):

$$
P_D = (V_i - V_o) \cdot I_L = (18 - 15) \times 1 = 3 \text{ W}
$$

A small clip-on heatsink (θSA ≤ 20 °C/W) is adequate. At minimum output Vo = 5 V, the worst-case dissipation is $P_D = (18 - 5) \times 1 = 13$ W, requiring a much larger heatsink (θSA ≤ 4 °C/W) — this is the **thermal worst case** the designer must accommodate.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Vref with VBE:** Some students write "Vref = 0.7 V" — this is **wrong**. Vref = **1.25 V** is the bandgap, *not* a silicon diode drop. **−1 mark deduction.**
> 2. **Dropout voltage ignored:** Many students pick Vi = Vo + 1 V. The minimum dropout is **3 V**, so Vi ≥ Vo + 3 V is mandatory. **−2 marks deduction.**
> 3. **Thermal design ignored:** Power dissipation must be checked against TJ,max with proper heatsink calculation. A design with no heatsink argument loses **2 marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting Iadj R2 term:** For low-precision problems this is acceptable, but if R2 > 5 kΩ, the Iadj R2 term becomes significant (e.g., 50 µA × 5 kΩ = 0.25 V, a 20% error at Vo = 1.25 V). **−1 mark if neglected when significant.**

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram how the **LM317 can be used as a constant-current source**. Derive the expression for the constant current. List two applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Circuit Diagram (Mermaid):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VDC["DC Source Vi"] --> LMCC["LM317"]
    LMCC -->|OUT| RLL["Floating Load RL"]
    RLL --> R1CC["R1 (Sense Resistor)"]
    R1CC --> ADJCC["ADJ Pin"]
    ADJCC --> R2CC["R2 (Optional, Compliance Setting)"]
    R2CC --> GNDCC["Ground"]
    LMCC -->|Bypass| CCBYP["COUT 1uF"]
```

**Operation (2 marks):**
