The LM317 forces a constant **Vref = 1.25 V** between its OUT and ADJ pins. In the constant-current configuration, the load RL and the sense resistor R1 are placed *in series* between OUT and ADJ. Since the voltage across R1 is *pinned* at 1.25 V by the IC, the current through R1 — which equals the load current IL — is *fixed* regardless of the load resistance, the input voltage, or temperature.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

The OUT–ADJ voltage is fixed at Vref:

$$
V_{OUT} - V_{ADJ} = V_{ref}
$$

But in this configuration, $V_{OUT} - V_{ADJ}$ is the sum of the load voltage (VRL) and the voltage across R1 (VR1):

$$
V_{RL} + V_{R1} = V_{ref}
$$

Since $V_{R1} = I_L \cdot R_1$ and $V_{RL} = I_L \cdot R_L$:

$$
I_L R_L + I_L R_1 = V_{ref}
$$

$$
I_L (R_L + R_1) = V_{ref}
$$

$$
\boxed{I_L = \frac{V_{ref}}{R_1} = \frac{1.25 \text{ V}}{R_1}}
$$

Note that **IL is independent of RL** — the defining property of a current source. IL depends only on Vref (a stable bandgap voltage) and R1 (a precise external resistor), giving excellent current accuracy.

**Two Applications (2 marks):**
1. **LED driver** — drives a string of LEDs at a constant current (e.g., IL = 20 mA for a typical indicator LED), preventing thermal runaway and ensuring uniform brightness.
2. **Battery charger (constant-current mode)** — charges a rechargeable battery at a controlled rate (e.g., C/10) for the bulk-charge phase, before transitioning to constant-voltage topping.

**(b)** Design an **LM317-based digital step-voltage regulator** that can produce **four discrete output voltages: 3 V, 5 V, 9 V, and 12 V** by switching different resistors into the ADJ-to-GND path using **MOSFET switches controlled by a 2-bit digital code** (D1 D0). Show the resistor calculations and the switching logic. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1: Fix R1 = 240 Ω (1 mark):**
The standard value R1 = 240 Ω (selected so that I1 = 5.2 mA minimum) is used.

**Step 2: Compute R2 values for each output (3 marks):**

Using $V_o = 1.25(1 + R_2/R_1)$, solve for R2:

$$
R_2 = R_1 \left(\frac{V_o}{1.25} - 1\right) = 240 \left(\frac{V_o}{1.25} - 1\right)
$$

| D1 D0 | Vo (V) | R2 (Ω) | Closest Standard |
|-------|--------|--------|------------------|
| 0 0 | 3 | 240 × (3/1.25 − 1) = 240 × 1.4 = **336** | 330 Ω + 10 Ω trim, or 330 Ω |
| 0 1 | 5 | 240 × (5/1.25 − 1) = 240 × 3 = **720** | 720 Ω |
| 1 0 | 9 | 240 × (9/1.25 − 1) = 240 × 6.2 = **1488** | 1.5 kΩ |
| 1 1 | 12 | 240 × (12/1.25 − 1) = 240 × 8.6 = **2064** | 2.0 kΩ + 68 Ω |

**Step 3: Switching topology (2 marks):**
Each R2 is placed in series with an N-channel MOSFET (e.g., BS170). The MOSFET gates are driven by the decoded 2-bit code via a 2-to-4 decoder (e.g., 74LS139). Only one MOSFET is ON at a time, inserting only its corresponding R2 into the ADJ-to-GND path. The other MOSFETs are OFF, disconnecting their R2.

```
D1 D0 → Decoder → Selected MOSFET ON → Corresponding R2 active
```

**Step 4: Accuracy check (1 mark):**
Using the closest standard values 330 Ω, 720 Ω, 1.5 kΩ, 2.07 kΩ (2 kΩ + 68 Ω), the resulting output voltages are:
- 330 Ω → Vo ≈ 2.97 V
- 720 Ω → Vo ≈ 5.00 V
- 1500 Ω → Vo ≈ 9.06 V
- 2070 Ω → Vo ≈ 12.03 V

All within **±3%** of the design targets, which is acceptable for most digital and analog loads.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 KTU Quick-Revision Checklist (Topic 317 — Applications)**
>
> ☐ The **LM317** is a *3-terminal, adjustable, positive* voltage regulator with **Vref = 1.25 V** (bandgap), **1.25 V ≤ Vo ≤ 37 V**, **IL,max = 1.5 A**, and a **dropout of 3 V**.
>
> ☐ The **master equation** is $V_o = V_{ref}(1 + R_2/R_1) + I_{adj} R_2 \approx 1.25(1 + R_2/R_1)$. **Always use R1 = 240 Ω as the default** unless told otherwise.
>
> ☐ The **constant-current** trick: place the load *floating* in series with R1. Then $I_L = 1.25 / R_1$ — independent of load resistance.
>
> ☐ **Digital programmability** is achieved by switching different R2 values using a resistor network + MOSFET/decoder/digital logic.
>
> ☐ **High-current boost** uses an external NPN Darlington with the LM317 driving the base; the LM317 supplies the *reference*, not the *bulk* current.
>
> ☐ **Tracking ± supplies** are built with **LM317 (positive)** + **LM337 (negative)** sharing a common R2 potentiometer.
>
> ☐ **Protection diodes** are mandatory in KTU designs: D1 across input–output (reverse polarity), D2 across output–ADJ (discharge path for Cout).
>
> ☐ **Heatsink calculation** is essential: $P_D = (V_i - V_o) I_L$; the *worst case* for thermal design is **minimum Vo** (since ΔV is largest), not maximum Vo.
>
> ☐ **Ripple rejection** improves dramatically (up to **80 dB**) when a **bypass capacitor CADJ (10 µF)** is added across R2.
>
> ☐ The LM317 must always have a load current **≥ 5 mA** (hence R1 ≤ 240 Ω) to maintain regulation — this is a *frequently-asked* KTU question.
>
> ☐ **Bandgap reference** = 1.25 V comes from silicon physics, *not* from a Zener — Zeners are noisy and temperature-dependent; bandgaps are not.
>
> ☐ The **IC 555** (covered in parallel in this module) operates in **astable** (oscillator, f = 1.44/((RA+2RB)C)), **monostable** (one-shot, T = 1.1 RC), and **bistable** (flip-flop) modes.
>
> ☐ The **PLL (Phase-Locked Loop)** has three blocks: **Phase Detector**, **VCO** (Voltage-Controlled Oscillator), and **Loop Filter** (low-pass). Applications: FM demodulation, frequency synthesis, motor-speed control, FSK demodulation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Timers - 555 - Functional Block Diagram - Astable and Monostable Multivibrators Using 555
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT402 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Timers - 555 - Functional Block Diagram - Astable and Monostable Multivibrators Using 555

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of the 555 Timer

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
The **IC 555 Timer** is a highly stable, monolithic timing integrated circuit originally designed by **Signetics Corporation in 1972**, capable of producing accurate time delays or oscillations. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCEBT402 - Linear Integrated Circuits)**, the 555 is classified as a precision analog building block that functions as a one-shot (monostable), astable oscillator, or Schmitt trigger. It operates from a single supply voltage ranging typically from **+5 V to +15 V** and can source or sink up to **200 mA** of load current, making it an interface between digital and analog domains.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 555 timer belongs to the family of *Linear Integrated Circuits* where the silicon die contains both analog comparators and a digital flip-flop. This hybrid nature is what makes it the most ubiquitous timer IC ever manufactured, with billions of units produced annually.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy
Imagine a **mechanical water bucket with two float switches**:
- The **upper float switch (Threshold comparator)** shuts off the water inlet the moment the bucket fills up to the high-water mark (⅔ Vcc).
- The **lower float switch (Trigger comparator)** opens the drain valve the moment water drops below the low-water mark (⅓ Vcc).
- A **latch (SR Flip-Flop)** remembers whether water is currently being filled or drained.
- A **discharge pipe (Transistor Q1)** connects to the bottom to empty the bucket rapidly when needed.

Every time a **resistor-capacitor (RC) network** acts as the "bucket" filling/emptying with charge, the two floats toggle the latch, and the latch controls the discharge pipe. This creates either a **single timed event (Monostable)** or a **continuous oscillation (Astable)** — exactly what the 555 does electronically with electrons instead of water.

### 1.3 Key Technical Highlights
- **Supply Voltage (Vcc):** **+4.5 V to +16 V** (Standard operation uses **+5 V**).
- **Threshold Voltages:** Internally fixed at **⅓ Vcc (Trigger level)** and **⅔ Vcc (Threshold level)**.
- **Output Current:** Up to **±200 mA**, allowing direct drive of relays, LEDs, and loudspeakers.
- **Frequency Range:** Operable from **< 0.1 Hz up to ~500 kHz** (CMOS variants reach **3 MHz**).
- **Temperature Stability:** Typically **50 ppm/°C** (parts per million per degree Celsius).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition for KTU Board Exams:** *"The 555 Timer is an 8-pin monolithic integrated circuit containing two comparators, one SR flip-flop, a discharge transistor, and a resistive voltage divider. It is used to generate precise timing pulses in monostable and astable modes of operation."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pin Configuration of the 8-Pin 555 Timer
The standard **8-pin DIP (Dual In-line Package)** pin-out is as follows:

| Pin No. | Pin Name | Function |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **1** | **GND** | Ground reference (0 V). The negative terminal of the supply. |
| **2** | **TRIGGER** | A negative-going pulse (< ⅓ Vcc) on this pin sets the internal flip-flop, driving the output HIGH and turning OFF the discharge transistor. |
| **3** | **OUTPUT** | The load-driving output pin. It swings between **~0 V (LOW)** and **~Vcc − 1.7 V (HIGH)**. |
| **4** | **RESET** | Active-LOW asynchronous reset. A logic **'0'** here forces the output LOW and turns ON the discharge transistor. Connected to Vcc when not used. |
| **5** | **CONTROL VOLTAGE** | A direct access point to the upper comparator's reference (⅔ Vcc). Used for modulation; bypassed to ground with **0.01 µF** capacitor if unused. |
| **6** | **THRESHOLD** | A positive-going pulse (> ⅔ Vcc) on this pin resets the internal flip-flop, driving the output LOW and turning ON the discharge transistor. |
| **7** | **DISCHARGE** | An open-collector transistor (Q1) that shorts the external timing capacitor to ground when the flip-flop is RESET. |
| **8** | **Vcc** | Positive supply voltage (**+5 V to +15 V**). |

### 2.2 Internal Functional Block Diagram — Component-Level Analysis
The 555 is composed of **five major sub-blocks**:

**A) Resistive Voltage Divider (3 × 5 kΩ)**
Three equal **5 kΩ resistors** form a precision voltage divider. This is the origin of the name *"555"*. The taps create reference voltages of:
- **V_ref1 = ⅓ Vcc** (at the non-inverting input of Trigger comparator)
- **V_ref2 = ⅔ Vcc** (at the inverting input of Threshold comparator)

**B) Two Open-Loop Comparators**
- **Comparator 1 (Upper):** Inverting input is Pin 6 (THRESHOLD). Non-inverting input is **⅔ Vcc**. Output goes HIGH when Pin 6 < ⅔ Vcc.
- **Comparator 2 (Lower):** Inverting input is Pin 2 (TRIGGER). Non-inverting input is **⅓ Vcc**. Output goes HIGH when Pin 2 < ⅓ Vcc.

**C) SR Flip-Flop (Latch)**
A level-triggered Set-Reset latch. The S-input comes from Comparator 2 (Trigger), and the R-input comes from Comparator 1 (Threshold).
- **S = 1** → Q = 1 (Output HIGH)
- **R = 1** → Q = 0 (Output LOW)
- **Q-bar** controls the discharge transistor.

**D) Discharge Transistor (Q1)**
An **NPN transistor** with its collector tied to Pin 7 and emitter to GND. It is **ON** when Q-bar = 1 (output LOW) and **OFF** when Q-bar = 0 (output HIGH). This provides a low-impedance path to discharge the timing capacitor.

**E) Output Buffer (Push-Pull Stage)**
A totem-pole complementary pair of transistors capable of sourcing or sinking **200 mA**, providing TTL-compatible logic levels at Pin 3.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the name "555"?** The original bipolar 555 timer used three **5 kΩ** resistors in its internal voltage divider. This naming convention is purely historical and is preserved even in modern CMOS variants where the resistors are no longer 5 kΩ.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Parameter | Monostable Mode | Astable Mode |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Timing Capacitor Charging Path** | Vcc → R → C | Vcc → R_A → C and Vcc → R_A → R_B → C |
| **Timing Capacitor Discharging Path** | Pin 7 (Discharge) → C → GND | Pin 7 → R_B → C → GND |
| **Charging Time Constant** | τ_c = R·C | τ_1 = (R_A + R_B)·C |
| **Discharging Time Constant** | τ_d = R·C | τ_2 = R_B·C |
| **Time to Reach ⅔ Vcc from 0** | T = R·C·ln(3) | T_high = (R_A + R_B)·C·ln(2) |
| **Time to Reach ⅓ Vcc from ⅔ Vcc** | Not applicable | T_low = R_B·C·ln(2) |
| **Pulse Width (T)** | **T = 1.1 · R · C** | **T = T_high + T_low = 0.693·(R_A + 2·R_B)·C** |
| **Frequency (f)** | Not periodic | **f = 1.45 / [(R_A + 2·R_B)·C]** |
| **Duty Cycle (D)** | Not applicable | **D = (R_A + R_B) / (R_A + 2·R_B) × 100%** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The constant **ln(2) ≈ 0.693** arises because the capacitor charges from ⅓ Vcc to ⅔ Vcc (a ratio of 2:1) and the constant **ln(3) ≈ 1.0986 ≈ 1.1** arises because the capacitor charges from 0 V to ⅔ Vcc, where the exponential reaches ⅔ of its final value Vcc. These derivations are shown explicitly in SECTION 3.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
The 555 timer is one of the most versatile ICs in electronics. It is used as:
- **Astable:** LED flashers, pulse generators, square-wave clock generators, PWM controllers, tone generators in toys, and simple inverters.
- **Monostable:** Debounce circuits, missing-pulse detectors, touch switches, timer-based relays, sequential timers, and analog frequency meters.
- **Bistable (Schmitt Trigger):** Logic-level converters, signal conditioners, and zero-crossing detectors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Monostable Pulse Width (T = 1.1·R·C)

**Circuit Setup:** In monostable mode, the 555 has **one stable state (LOW)**. A negative trigger pulse at Pin 2 forces the output HIGH for a fixed duration, after which it automatically returns LOW.

**Initial Condition (t = 0⁻):** The circuit is in its stable state.
- The output is **LOW**.
- The internal flip-flop is in RESET state.
- The discharge transistor Q1 is **ON**, so Pin 7 is shorted to GND.
- The voltage across the timing capacitor C is **V_C(0) = 0 V**.

**Trigger Event (t = 0⁺):** A negative pulse at Pin 2 drops below **⅓ Vcc**.
- Comparator 2 output goes HIGH, setting the SR flip-flop.
- **Output goes HIGH** at Pin 3.
- The discharge transistor Q1 is turned **OFF**, removing the short from Pin 7.
- The capacitor C now begins charging through resistor R from Vcc.

**Charging Phase (t > 0):** Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the RC loop:

$$
V_{cc} = V_R(t) + V_C(t)
$$

$$
V_{cc} = R \cdot i(t) + V_C(t)
$$

Using the capacitor current-voltage relation $i(t) = C \cdot \frac{dV_C}{dt}$:

$$
V_{cc} = R \cdot C \cdot \frac{dV_C}{dt} + V_C(t)
$$

This is a first-order linear differential equation. Rearranging and applying the integrating factor $e^{t/RC}$:

$$
\frac{dV_C}{dt} + \frac{V_C}{RC} = \frac{V_{cc}}{RC}
$$

The general solution is:

$$
V_C(t) = V_{cc} + A \cdot e^{-t/RC}
$$

Applying the initial condition $V_C(0) = 0$ V:

$$
0 = V_{cc} + A \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = -V_{cc}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
V_C(t) = V_{cc} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)
$$

**Reset Condition:** The capacitor charges until $V_C(t) = \frac{2}{3} V_{cc}$ (the threshold level), at which point the upper comparator toggles the flip-flop, returning the output to LOW. Setting $V_C(t) = \frac{2}{3} V_{cc}$:

$$
\frac{2}{3} V_{cc} = V_{cc} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-T/RC}\right)
$$

Dividing both sides by $V_{cc}$:

$$
\frac{2}{3} = 1 - e^{-T/RC}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
e^{-T/RC} = 1 - \frac{2}{3} = \frac{1}{3}
$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
-\frac{T}{RC} = \ln\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) = -\ln(3)
$$

$$
\frac{T}{RC} = \ln(3)
$$

**Final Result for Monostable Pulse Width:**

$$
T = RC \cdot \ln(3) \approx 1.0986 \cdot RC
$$

$$
\boxed{T \approx 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Astable Frequency and Duty Cycle (f = 1.45 / [(R_A + 2R_B)·C])

**Circuit Setup:** In astable mode, the 555 oscillates continuously with **no stable state**. The output alternates between HIGH and LOW indefinitely.

**Charging Phase (Output HIGH):** The capacitor charges from $\frac{1}{3} V_{cc}$ to $\frac{2}{3} V_{cc}$ through resistors $R_A$ and $R_B$ in series (because the discharge transistor is OFF and Pin 7 is floating).

**Discharging Phase (Output LOW):** The capacitor discharges from $\frac{2}{3} V_{cc}$ to $\frac{1}{3} V_{cc}$ through resistor $R_B$ only (because the discharge transistor is ON and shorts Pin 7 to GND, bypassing $R_A$).

**Step A — Charging Duration (T_high):**
The charging equation uses the initial value $V_C(0) = \frac{1}{3} V_{cc}$ and final value $V_C(\infty) = V_{cc}$:

$$
V_C(t) = V_{cc} - \left(V_{cc} - \frac{1}{3}V_{cc}\right) \cdot e^{-t/(R_A+R_B)C}
$$

$$
V_C(t) = V_{cc} - \frac{2}{3}V_{cc} \cdot e^{-t/(R_A+R_B)C}
$$

Setting $V_C(T_{high}) = \frac{2}{3} V_{cc}$:

$$
\frac{2}{3} V_{cc} = V_{cc} - \frac{2}{3} V_{cc} \cdot e^{-T_{high}/(R_A+R_B)C}
$$

$$
\frac{2}{3} V_{cc} \cdot e^{-T_{high}/(R_A+R_B)C} = V_{cc} - \frac{2}{3} V_{cc} = \frac{1}{3} V_{cc}
$$

$$
e^{-T_{high}/(R_A+R_B)C} = \frac{1}{2}
$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$
-\frac{T_{high}}{(R_A+R_B)C} = \ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -\ln(2)
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{high} = (R_A + R_B) \cdot C \cdot \ln(2) \approx 0.693 \cdot (R_A + R_B) \cdot C}
$$

**Step B — Discharging Duration (T_low):**
The discharging equation uses initial value $V_C(0) = \frac{2}{3} V_{cc}$ and final value $V_C(\infty) = 0$:

$$
V_C(t) = \frac{2}{3} V_{cc} \cdot e^{-t/R_B C}
$$

Setting $V_C(T_{low}) = \frac{1}{3} V_{cc}$:

$$
\frac{1}{3} V_{cc} = \frac{2}{3} V_{cc} \cdot e^{-T_{low}/R_B C}
$$

$$
e^{-T_{low}/R_B C} = \frac{1}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{low} = R_B \cdot C \cdot \ln(2) \approx 0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C}
$$

**Step C — Total Time Period (T):**

$$
T = T_{high} + T_{low} = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + R_B) \cdot C + 0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C
$$

$$
T = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C
$$

**Step D — Frequency of Oscillation (f):**

$$
\boxed{f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1.45}{(R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C}}
$$

**Step E — Duty Cycle (D):**
The duty cycle is the ratio of HIGH time to total period, expressed as a percentage:

$$
D = \frac{T_{high}}{T} \times 100\% = \frac{0.693 \cdot (R_A + R_B) \cdot C}{0.693 \cdot (R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{D = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} \times 100\%}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Observation:** In a standard astable 555 circuit, the duty cycle **can never be 50% or less** because $T_{low}$ depends only on $R_B$, while $T_{high}$ depends on $R_A + R_B$. This means $T_{high} > T_{low}$ always, giving a duty cycle strictly greater than **50%**. To achieve a duty cycle below 50%, a **diode is added in parallel with $R_B$** to provide a separate charging path, which is a frequently asked question in KTU examinations.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Internal Block Diagram of the 555 Timer (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    VCC1[Vcc Pin 8] --> R1A[Resistor 5k]
    R1A --> R1B[Resistor 5k]
    R1B --> R1C[Resistor 5k]
    R1C --> GND1[GND Pin 1]
    R1A -.->|2/3 Vcc| CMP1[Comparator 1 Upper]
    R1B -.->|1/3 Vcc| CMP2[Comparator 2 Lower]
    PIN6[Pin 6 THRESHOLD] --> CMP1
    PIN2[Pin 2 TRIGGER] --> CMP2
    CMP1 -->|Reset| FLIPFLOP[SR Flip Flop]
    CMP2 -->|Set| FLIPFLOP
    FLIPFLOP --> Q[Q Output]
    FLIPFLOP --> QB[Q Bar Output]
    Q --> BUFFER[Output Buffer]
    BUFFER --> PIN3[Pin 3 OUTPUT]
    QB --> Q1[Discharge Transistor NPN]
    Q1 --> PIN7[Pin 7 DISCHARGE]
    PIN4[Pin 4 RESET] --> FLIPFLOP
    PIN5[Pin 5 CONTROL VOLTAGE] --> CMP1
```

### 4.2 Monostable Multivibrator Circuit using 555 (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TB
    VCCNODE[Vcc +5V to +15V] --> RADJ[R Timing Resistor]
    VCCNODE --> PIN8A[Pin 8 Vcc]
    VCCNODE --> PIN4A[Pin 4 RESET tied HIGH]
    RADJ --> NODE1[Node A]
    NODE1 --> PIN7A[Pin 7 DISCHARGE]
    NODE1 --> CAPA[Capacitor C]
    CAPA --> GNDA[GND]
    PIN2A[Pin 2 TRIGGER] --> NODE1
    PIN6A[Pin 6 THRESHOLD] --> NODE1
    PIN5A[Pin 5 CONTROL] --> BYPASS[0.01uF Bypass Cap]
    BYPASS --> GNDA
    PIN3A[Pin 3 OUTPUT] --> LOAD[Load LED or Relay]
    LOAD --> GNDA
    PULSE[Negative Trigger Pulse Input] --> PIN2A
```

**Operation Summary:**
- The **R and C** values determine the pulse width: $T = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C$.
- The **trigger pulse** at Pin 2 must go **below ⅓ Vcc** and be **narrower than T** to function correctly.
- The output at Pin 3 goes HIGH for the duration T, then automatically returns LOW.

### 4.3 Astable Multivibrator Circuit using 555 (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TB
    VCCASTB[Vcc +5V to +15V] --> PIN8B[Pin 8 Vcc]
    VCCASTB --> PIN4B[Pin 4 RESET]
    VCCASTB --> RAA[Resistor RA]
    RAA --> NODEB[Node B]
    NODEB --> RBB[Resistor RB]
    RBB --> PIN7B[Pin 7 DISCHARGE]
    NODEB --> PINA2A[Pin 2 TRIGGER]
    NODEB --> PIN6B[Pin 6 THRESHOLD]
    PIN7B --> RBB
    PIN7B -.->|Discharge Path| RBB
    RBB --> NODEB
    NODEB --> CAPB[Capacitor C]
    CAPB --> GNDB[GND]
    PIN5B[Pin 5 CONTROL] --> BYPASB[0.01uF Bypass]
    BYPASB --> GNDB
    PIN3B[Pin 3 OUTPUT] --> OUTSIG[Square Wave Output]
    GNDB --> GNDREF[Ground Reference]
```

**Operation Summary:**
- The capacitor C charges from Vcc through **R_A and R_B in series** during the HIGH phase.
- The capacitor C discharges through **R_B only** (via Pin 7) during the LOW phase.
- The output is a continuous square wave with frequency $f = \frac{1.45}{(R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C}$.

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Stage: Vcc and GND] --> B[Reference Generator: Three 5k Resistors]
    B --> C[Threshold Reference 2/3 Vcc]
    B --> D[Trigger Reference 1/3 Vcc]
    C --> E[Comparator 1: Threshold Detection]
    D --> F[Comparator 2: Trigger Detection]
    E --> G[SR Flip Flop Latch]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Output Buffer Stage]
    H --> I[Pin 3 Output Load Driver]
    G --> J[Q Bar Signal]
    J --> K[Discharge Transistor Control]
    K --> L[Pin 7 External RC Timing Network]
    L --> E
    L --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters. All labels use plain uppercase text without markdown formatting. The internal feedback path from Pin 7 back to the comparators (Pins 2 and 6) represents the external RC timing network that creates the time-dependent voltage waveform.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*List the functional blocks of the 555 timer IC and state the function of the control voltage pin.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The internal functional blocks of the 555 timer are: (1) A resistive voltage divider consisting of three **5 kΩ resistors**, (2) Two open-loop comparators, (3) An SR flip-flop (latch), (4) A discharge transistor (NPN), and (5) An output buffer stage (push-pull).
The **Control Voltage pin (Pin 5)** provides external access to the upper comparator's reference voltage of **⅔ Vcc**, allowing frequency modulation, pulse-width modulation, or external threshold control. When not in use, it must be bypassed to ground with a **0.01 µF capacitor** to suppress noise.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Why is the 555 timer IC named "555"? Mention any two applications of the 555 in astable mode.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The 555 timer derives its name from the internal voltage divider network, which consists of three equal resistors of **5 kΩ** each, generating reference voltages of ⅓ Vcc and ⅔ Vcc. The bipolar design was introduced by **Signetics in 1972**.
Two applications in astable mode: (1) **Square wave and pulse generator** for digital clock circuits, and (2) **Tone generator** for alarms, sirens, and electronic musical instruments. (Other valid applications: LED flasher, PWM controller, voltage-controlled oscillator.)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A: Monostable Multivibrator using 555

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **monostable multivibrator using IC 555**. Derive the expression for its pulse width. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design a monostable multivibrator using 555 timer to produce a pulse width of **10 ms**. Choose C = 1 µF and explain the trigger pulse requirements. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Operation and Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Circuit Description:** In the monostable configuration, Pin 8 (Vcc) and Pin 4 (RESET) are tied to Vcc. Pin 1 is grounded. A resistor **R** is connected between Vcc and Pin 7 (discharge). A capacitor **C** is connected between Pin 7 and GND. Pins 2 and 6 are shorted together and connected to the junction of R and C. Pin 5 is bypassed with **0.01 µF** to GND.

**Operation States:**
- **Stable State:** Output is LOW. The discharge transistor Q1 is ON, so capacitor C is shorted to GND. Voltage at Pin 6 is **0 V** (less than ⅔ Vcc), so Comparator 1 output is LOW.
- **Triggering:** A negative pulse at Pin 2 drops below **⅓ Vcc**. Comparator 2 output goes HIGH, setting the SR flip-flop. Q = 1, so output goes HIGH. Discharge transistor Q1 turns OFF.
- **Quasi-stable State:** Capacitor C charges through R from Vcc. The voltage across C rises exponentially.
- **Reset:** When V_C reaches **⅔ Vcc**, Comparator 1 output goes HIGH, resetting the flip-flop. Output returns LOW, Q1 turns ON, and C discharges rapidly to 0 V. The circuit is back in its stable state.

**Derivation of Pulse Width:**
Starting from $V_C(t) = V_{cc} \cdot (1 - e^{-t/RC})$ with $V_C(0) = 0$:

Setting $V_C(T) = \frac{2}{3} V_{cc}$:

$$
\frac{2}{3} = 1 - e^{-T/RC} \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^{-T/RC} = \frac{1}{3}
$$

$$
\boxed{T = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C}
$$

**Part (b) — Design Calculation (7 Marks):**

Given: Required pulse width T = 10 ms = $10 \times 10^{-3}$ s, and C = 1 µF = $1 \times 10^{-6}$ F.

Using $T = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C$:

$$
R = \frac{T}{1.1 \cdot C} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{1.1 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
R = \frac{10^{-2}}{1.1 \times 10^{-6}} = 9.09 \times 10^{3} \, \Omega
$$

$$
\boxed{R \approx 9.09 \, \text{k}\Omega}
$$

A standard **9.1 kΩ** resistor should be used.

**Trigger Pulse Requirements:**
- **Amplitude:** The trigger pulse amplitude at Pin 2 must fall **below ⅓ Vcc = 1.67 V** (for Vcc = 5 V) to activate the trigger comparator.
- **Pulse Width:** The trigger pulse must be **narrower than the output pulse width T** to avoid re-triggering. A width of **less than 10 µs** is typically recommended.
- **Edge:** A **sharp negative-going edge** is required. The pulse should not exceed ⅓ Vcc for longer than 100 ns to avoid timing inaccuracies.

---

#### Question B: Astable Multivibrator using 555

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of an **astable multivibrator using IC 555**. Derive the expression for its frequency of oscillation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An astable 555 circuit uses R_A = 1 kΩ, R_B = 10 kΩ, and C = 0.1 µF. Calculate the **frequency of oscillation, time period, and duty cycle**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Operation and Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Circuit Description:** In astable mode, R_A is connected between Vcc and Pin 7. R_B is connected between Pin 7 and the junction of Pins 2 and 6. Capacitor C is connected between the junction and GND.

**Charging Phase:** Initially assume the output is HIGH, so Q1 is OFF. Capacitor C charges through R_A and R_B in series from Vcc. The voltage rises exponentially from **⅓ Vcc** toward Vcc.

**Threshold Trigger:** When V_C reaches **⅔ Vcc**, the upper comparator resets the flip-flop. Output goes LOW, Q1 turns ON.

**Discharging Phase:** Capacitor C now discharges through R_B and Q1 to GND. The voltage falls exponentially from ⅔ Vcc toward 0.

**Trigger Reactivation:** When V_C falls to **⅓ Vcc**, the lower comparator sets the flip-flop. Output goes HIGH, Q1 turns OFF, and the cycle repeats.

**Derivation of Frequency:**
From the charging and discharging analysis:

$$
T_{high} = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + R_B) \cdot C
$$

$$
T_{low} = 0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C
$$

$$
T = T_{high} + T_{low} = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C
$$

$$
\boxed{f = \frac{1.45}{(R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C}}
$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 Marks):**

Given: R_A = 1 kΩ = $10^3$ Ω, R_B = 10 kΩ = $10^4$ Ω, C = 0.1 µF = $10^{-7}$ F.

**Step 1: Calculate T_high:**

$$
T_{high} = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + R_B) \cdot C = 0.693 \cdot (1000 + 10000) \cdot 10^{-7}
$$

$$
T_{high} = 0.693 \cdot 11000 \cdot 10^{-7} = 0.693 \cdot 1.1 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{high} = 7.623 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{s} = 762.3 \, \mu\text{s}}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate T_low:**

$$
T_{low} = 0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C = 0.693 \cdot 10000 \cdot 10^{-7} = 6.93 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{low} = 6.93 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{s} = 693 \, \mu\text{s}}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Time Period T:**

$$
T = T_{high} + T_{low} = 7.623 \times 10^{-4} + 6.93 \times 10^{-4} = 14.553 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{T = 1.4553 \, \text{ms}}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Frequency f:**

$$
f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{1.4553 \times 10^{-3}} = 687.1 \, \text{Hz}
$$

$$
\boxed{f \approx 687 \, \text{Hz}}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate Duty Cycle D:**

$$
D = \frac{T_{high}}{T} \times 100\% = \frac{7.623 \times 10^{-4}}{14.553 \times 10^{-4}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
D = \frac{7.623}{14.553} \times 100\% = 0.5238 \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{D = 52.38\%}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing R_A and R_B:** Many students incorrectly assign R_A to the discharge path. Remember: R_B is between Pin 7 and the capacitor; R_A is between Vcc and Pin 7. The discharge current flows only through **R_B**, not R_A.
> 2. **Forgetting the 0.693 factor:** The constant **ln(2) ≈ 0.693** is non-negotiable. Skipping it leads to a calculation error of nearly 30%.
> 3. **Assuming 50% duty cycle:** The default astable 555 circuit **cannot** produce 50% duty cycle. A diode across R_B is required for that.
> 4. **Wrong unit conversions:** µF must be converted to **F** ($10^{-6}$), kΩ to **Ω** ($10^3$), and ms to **s** ($10^{-3}$) before calculation. Many students lose 1–2 marks for unit errors.
> 5. **Misinterpreting Pin 4 (RESET):** Pin 4 must always be tied to Vcc. Leaving it floating causes erratic operation and is a frequent board-exam trap.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **555 timer** is an 8-pin IC with internal components: voltage divider, two comparators, SR flip-flop, discharge transistor, and output buffer.
- ✅ The internal divider creates **⅓ Vcc** (Trigger reference) and **⅔ Vcc** (Threshold reference).
- ✅ The name "555" comes from the three **5 kΩ** internal resistors (bipolar version).
- ✅ Pin 7 (Discharge) is an open-collector transistor used to discharge the timing capacitor.
- ✅ **Monostable Pulse Width:** $\boxed{T = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C}$
- ✅ **Astable Frequency:** $\boxed{f = 1.45 / [(R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C]}$
- ✅ **Astable Duty Cycle:** $\boxed{D = (R_A + R_B) / (R_A + 2R_B) \times 100\%}$
- ✅ In astable mode, **T_high > T_low** always, so duty cycle is always **greater than 50%** in the standard configuration.
- ✅ The control voltage pin (Pin 5) must be **bypassed to GND with 0.01 µF** if unused, to prevent noise injection.
- ✅ Pin 4 (RESET) is **active-LOW** and must be tied to **Vcc** for normal operation.
- ✅ The output stage can **source or sink up to 200 mA**, enabling direct relay/LED drive.
- ✅ The 555 is fundamentally an analog IC, but its **digital output** makes it the most popular hybrid building block in KTU syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: VCO - 566
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT402 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** VCO - 566

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

The **VCO 566** is a monolithic, second-generation analog integrated circuit (Signetics NE/SE 566) that functions as a **Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO)**. It generates simultaneously a **square wave** and a **triangle wave** whose frequency is a linear function of an externally applied DC control voltage ($V_C$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO):** An oscillator whose output frequency is directly proportional to an externally applied control/input voltage. In KTU terminology, it is a *frequency-to-voltage transducer* in reverse — a *voltage-to-frequency converter*.

### Key Specifications
| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Frequency Range | **1 Hz to 1 MHz** |
| Sweep Range (typical) | **10 : 1** |
| Frequency Modulation Distortion | < 0.2 % |
| Power Supply ($V_{CC}$) | ±6 V to ±12 V (dual) or 12 V to 24 V (single) |
| Control Voltage Range | **0.75 $V_{CC}$ ≤ $V_C$ ≤ $V_{CC}$** |
| Output Types | Triangle wave + Square wave (TTL compatible) |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Water Bucket Tipping" Model

Imagine a **bucket being filled by a tap whose flow rate is controlled by a knob** (the control voltage $V_C$).

1. The **tap flow** is the *charging current* — turn the knob, and the fill rate changes.
2. The bucket has a **trigger level** — when the water hits the upper mark, it tips over and starts emptying.
3. When it empties to the lower mark, it tips back to fill again.
4. The **time between tips** is the period $T$ of oscillation; **faster flow = more tips per second = higher frequency**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 566 is essentially an **electronic "tipping bucket"** — a capacitor is filled by a voltage-controlled current source, and a Schmitt trigger circuit decides when to "tip" the charging direction.

## 1.3 Pin Configuration (8-Pin DIP)

> [!NOTE]
> The NE566 has the industry-standard 8-pin mini-DIP outline.

| Pin No. | Symbol | Function |
|:---:|:---:|---|
| 1 | GND | Ground / Negative Supply |
| 2 | NC | No internal connection (used as ground in some layouts) |
| 3 | SQ OUT | **Square Wave** output |
| 4 | TRI OUT | **Triangle Wave** output |
| 5 | $R_1$ | External timing resistor (sets charging current) |
| 6 | $C_1$ | External timing capacitor |
| 7 | $V_C$ | **Modulating / Control Voltage** input |
| 8 | $V^+$ | Positive supply ($+V_{CC}$) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Block Architecture

The 566 is constructed from **four primary sub-blocks**:

1. **Current Source** — biased by the external $R_1$, produces a current $I_C$ proportional to $(V_{CC} - V_C)$.
2. **Current Sinks (two)** — alternate to **charge and discharge** $C_1$ symmetrically.
3. **Schmitt Trigger** — senses the capacitor voltage and switches the direction of current flow at the two threshold points ($V_{CC}/2 \pm$ offset).
4. **Output Buffers** — provide the buffered square wave (from the Schmitt trigger) and the triangle wave (directly from $C_1$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Schmitt trigger decides **when** the current source switches from charging to discharging $C_1$. Because the current $I_C$ is the *same magnitude* for both charge and discharge, the waveform is **symmetric** — equal charge and discharge times.

## 2.2 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step

- The control voltage $V_C$ is applied at pin 7. It modulates the current $I_C$ sourced by the internal transistor.
- $I_C$ is given by the classic transconductance relation:

$$I_C = \frac{V_{CC} - V_C}{R_1}$$

- This current alternately **charges** $C_1$ to the upper threshold and **discharges** it to the lower threshold.
- The Schmitt trigger thresholds are symmetric about $V_{CC}/2$, so the total voltage swing on $C_1$ is split equally: the capacitor traverses $\Delta V = V_{CC}/2$ on each half-cycle (using the standard simplified model).
- During charging: $C_1$ accumulates charge $Q = I_C \cdot t_1$, where $t_1$ is the charging time.
- By symmetry, $t_1 = t_2 = T/2$, where $T$ is the full period.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Master these **three core relations** — they cover ~90 % of KTU problems on the 566.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $I_C = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_C}{R_1}$ | Charging / discharging current | A |
| 2 | $f_O = \dfrac{2 \, (V_{CC} - V_C)}{R_1 \, C_1 \, V_{CC}}$ | **Output frequency** | Hz |
| 3 | $f_O = \dfrac{2}{R_1 C_1}\left(1 - \dfrac{V_C}{V_{CC}}\right)$ | Frequency — alternate form | Hz |
| 4 | $V_C$ range: $0.75\,V_{CC}$ to $V_{CC}$ | Valid control range | V |
| 5 | $T = \dfrac{1}{f_O} = \dfrac{R_1 C_1 V_{CC}}{2(V_{CC} - V_C)}$ | Time period | s |
| 6 | $f_{O,\max}$ at $V_C = 0.75 V_{CC}$ | $\dfrac{0.5}{R_1 C_1}$ | Hz |
| 7 | $f_{O,\min}$ at $V_C = V_{CC}$ | $\approx 0$ | Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sweep Range** is the ratio of the maximum to the minimum usable frequency. For the 566, this is typically **10 : 1** (i.e., the minimum frequency is one-tenth of the maximum).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The 566 is a workhorse in:
- **FSK (Frequency Shift Keying)** transmitters for low-speed digital comms
- **FM signal generation** (the 566 itself is essentially a voltage-to-frequency converter — a direct FM modulator)
- **Function generators** (square + triangle simultaneously)
- **Tone generation** for telephony, alarms, and test equipment
- **PLL (Phase Locked Loop)** blocks as the VCO element
- **Tachometers** and **voltage-to-frequency converters** in ADC subsystems
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Output Frequency $f_O$

### Step 1 — Charging Current

The external timing resistor $R_1$ is connected between $V_{CC}$ (pin 8) and pin 5. Internally, pin 5 is the emitter of a PNP transistor whose base is held at $V_C$. Hence the voltage across $R_1$ is $(V_{CC} - V_C)$, and the resulting charging current is:

$$I_C = \frac{V_{CC} - V_C}{R_1}$$

### Step 2 — Capacitor Voltage Swing per Half-Cycle

The Schmitt trigger inside the 566 has its switching thresholds arranged symmetrically about $V_{CC}/2$, separated by $V_{CC}/2$ (a simplified, KTU-standard idealisation). Therefore, the capacitor $C_1$ must charge through a voltage of $\Delta V = V_{CC}/2$ during one half-cycle.

### Step 3 — Time for One Half-Cycle

Using the fundamental capacitor relation $I = C \, (dV/dt)$, and with constant current $I_C$:

$$\Delta V = \frac{I_C \, t_1}{C_1}$$

Solving for the charging time $t_1$:

$$t_1 = \frac{C_1 \, \Delta V}{I_C} = \frac{C_1 \cdot \dfrac{V_{CC}}{2}}{\dfrac{V_{CC} - V_C}{R_1}} = \frac{C_1 \, V_{CC} \, R_1}{2 (V_{CC} - V_C)}$$

### Step 4 — Full Period (Symmetric Charge/Discharge)

Because the **same magnitude** $I_C$ discharges $C_1$ through an identical voltage swing, $t_2 = t_1$, and:

$$T = t_1 + t_2 = 2 t_1 = \frac{C_1 \, V_{CC} \, R_1}{V_{CC} - V_C}$$

### Step 5 — Output Frequency

Taking the reciprocal:

$$f_O = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_C}{R_1 \, C_1 \, V_{CC}} \times \frac{1}{1} \cdot \frac{1}{1}$$

But we factor the **2** from the half-cycle sum to give the canonical KTU form:

$$\boxed{\,f_O = \frac{2 \,(V_{CC} - V_C)}{R_1 \, C_1 \, V_{CC}} = \frac{2}{R_1 C_1}\!\left(1 - \frac{V_C}{V_{CC}}\right)\,}$$

### Step 6 — Limiting Cases (Sanity Check)

- **At** $V_C = 0.75 \, V_{CC}$: $f_O = \dfrac{2(1-0.75)}{R_1 C_1} = \dfrac{0.5}{R_1 C_1}$ — **maximum** output frequency.
- **At** $V_C = V_{CC}$: $f_O = 0$ — the oscillator ceases; the output latches at DC. 
- **At** $V_C = V_{CC}/2$: $f_O = \dfrac{1}{R_1 C_1}$ — useful **centre-frequency** design point.

> [!NOTE]
> The 2:1 ratio between $f_{O,\max}$ and $f_{O,\text{centre}}$ illustrates why the **sweep range** is defined as $f_{\max}/f_{\min}$ (often quoted as 10:1, limited by component tolerances and the usable $V_C$ range).

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** A 566 VCO is wired with $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C_1 = 1 \text{ nF}$, and $V_{CC} = 12 \text{ V}$. The control voltage is $V_C = 9 \text{ V}$. Determine the output frequency.

**Solution:**

$$f_O = \frac{2 \, (V_{CC} - V_C)}{R_1 \, C_1 \, V_{CC}}$$

$$f_O = \frac{2 \times (12 - 9)}{10^4 \times 10^{-9} \times 12}$$

$$f_O = \frac{2 \times 3}{1.2 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{6}{1.2 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$f_O = 5 \times 10^{4} = 50 \text{ kHz}$$

**Maximum attainable frequency** (at $V_C = 0.75 \times 12 = 9 \text{ V}$ in this case — the limit):

$$f_{O,\max} = \frac{0.5}{R_1 C_1} = \frac{0.5}{10^4 \times 10^{-9}} = 50 \text{ kHz}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When $V_C = 0.75 V_{CC}$ exactly, the design is at the *edge* of the valid control range. Reduce $V_C$ slightly (e.g., to 0.8 $V_{CC}$) to leave a safety margin against temperature drift.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid — Functional Block Diagram of VCO 566

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vcInput[Control Voltage Vc - Pin 7] -->|biases PNP base| csource
    vcc[V plus - Pin 8] -->|via R1 - Pin 5| csource
    csource[Current Source I_C equal to VCC minus Vc over R1] -->|charges| capnode
    capnode[External Capacitor C1 - Pin 6] -->|discharges via| csink1[Current Sink 1]
    capnode -->|charges via| csink2[Current Sink 2]
    capnode -->|Vc1 triangle voltage| schmitt[Schmitt Trigger]
    schmitt -->|switching control| csource
    schmitt -->|buffered square wave| sqout[Square Wave Out - Pin 3]
    capnode -->|direct triangle wave| triout[Triangle Wave Out - Pin 4]
    gnd[GND - Pin 1] -->|reference| schmitt
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the Mermaid model, the Schmitt trigger **closes the feedback loop** by commanding the current source/sink pair to flip direction each time the capacitor voltage crosses a threshold.

## 4.2 Mermaid — Equivalent Charging Circuit (Single Half-Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Positive Supply VCC] -->|through R1| B[Current Source Ic]
    B -->|Ic into node X| C[Capacitor C1]
    C -->|voltage Vc1 triangle| D[Schmitt Trigger Sensing]
    D -->|when Vc1 hits upper threshold| E[Reverse Current Direction]
    E --> B
    D -->|output replica| F[Square Wave Pin 3]
    C -->|direct tap| G[Triangle Wave Pin 4]
```

## 4.3 Waveform Relationship

| Time Event | Capacitor Voltage $V_{C1}$ | Square-Wave Output (Pin 3) |
|---|---|---|
| Start of charge | $V_{CC}/2$ (low threshold) | LOW |
| Charging half-cycle | rises linearly to upper threshold | LOW |
| Schmitt trips | $V_{CC}$ (upper threshold) | flips to HIGH |
| Discharging half-cycle | falls linearly back to lower threshold | HIGH |
| Schmitt trips | $V_{CC}/2$ (low threshold) | flips to LOW |
| Cycle repeats | … | … |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The square wave is **in phase with the discharge half-cycle** of the triangle — this is a frequent KTU short-answer topic.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List the salient features of VCO IC 566.**

**Model Answer:**
- Operates over a wide frequency range: **1 Hz to 1 MHz**.
- Generates **two simultaneous outputs** — a square wave (pin 3) and a triangle wave (pin 4).
- **Sweep range** of **10 : 1** is typical.
- The output frequency is a **linear function** of the control voltage $V_C$ at pin 7.
- Operates from a **single or dual supply** (typically ±6 V to ±12 V).
- Produces **TTL-compatible** square wave output.
- Low frequency modulation distortion (< 0.2 %).
- Applications: **FSK generation, FM modulators, signal generators, tone generators**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is the function of pin 5, pin 6, and pin 7 in IC 566?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Pin 5** — External timing resistor $R_1$ is connected here. It sets the magnitude of the internal charging current $I_C = (V_{CC} - V_C)/R_1$.
- **Pin 6** — External timing capacitor $C_1$ is connected here. Together with $R_1$, it sets the output frequency.
- **Pin 7** — **Control / Modulating voltage** $V_C$ input. The applied DC voltage at this pin linearly modulates the output frequency.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

#### **(a) [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of VCO using IC 566. Derive the expression for the output frequency.**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram Explanation (4 marks):**

The IC 566 consists of four major sub-blocks:

1. **Current Source / Current Sinks** — Controlled by the external $R_1$ and the modulating voltage $V_C$, an internal current $I_C = (V_{CC} - V_C)/R_1$ is generated. This current alternately **charges and discharges** the external timing capacitor $C_1$ connected at pin 6.
2. **Schmitt Trigger** — It senses the voltage across $C_1$ and flips state when the voltage reaches the **upper threshold** ($V_{CC}$) or the **lower threshold** ($V_{CC}/2$). Its output is the **square wave** at pin 3.
3. **Output Buffers** — A high-input-impedance buffer presents the triangle waveform from $C_1$ to **pin 4** without loading the timing capacitor.
4. **Bias Network** — Generates internal reference voltages for the Schmitt trigger.

**Operation (2 marks):**
- During the first half-cycle, the Schmitt trigger directs the current source to **charge** $C_1$ from $V_{CC}/2$ towards $V_{CC}$.
- Once the upper threshold is reached, the trigger flips, redirecting the current to **discharge** $C_1$ back to $V_{CC}/2$.
- The cycle repeats, producing periodic triangle and square waves.

**Derivation (1 mark for final result):**

The charging time is $t_1 = \dfrac{C_1 V_{CC} R_1}{2(V_{CC} - V_C)}$ and by symmetry $t_2 = t_1$. Therefore:

$$f_O = \frac{1}{2t_1} = \frac{2(V_{CC} - V_C)}{R_1 C_1 V_{CC}}$$

#### **(b) [7 Marks] For an SE/NE 566 VCO with $R_1 = 6.8 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C_1 = 1.2 \text{ nF}$ operating from $V_{CC} = 12 \text{ V}$, find (i) the free-running frequency at $V_C = V_{CC}/2$, (ii) the maximum output frequency, and (iii) the sweep range.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Free-running frequency at $V_C = V_{CC}/2 = 6$ V:**

$$f_O = \frac{2(V_{CC} - V_C)}{R_1 C_1 V_{CC}} = \frac{2 \times (12 - 6)}{6.8 \times 10^3 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-9} \times 12}$$

$$f_O = \frac{12}{6.8 \times 1.2 \times 12 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{12}{97.92 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$f_O \approx 122.55 \text{ kHz}$$

**(ii) Maximum output frequency at $V_C = 0.75 V_{CC} = 9$ V:**

$$f_{O,\max} = \frac{2(12 - 9)}{6.8 \times 10^3 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-9} \times 12} = \frac{6}{97.92 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$f_{O,\max} \approx 61.27 \text{ kHz}$$

> Note: For higher $V_{CC}/R$ designs, the maximum is also expressed as $f_{O,\max} = \dfrac{0.5}{R_1 C_1} = \dfrac{0.5}{6.8 \times 10^3 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 61.27$ kHz.

**(iii) Sweep Range:**

The minimum usable frequency corresponds to $V_C = V_{CC}$ (theoretical limit $f_O \to 0$). Practical sweep range is quoted as **10 : 1**, i.e., the minimum is roughly one-tenth of the maximum. So:

$$\text{Sweep range} = \frac{f_{O,\max}}{f_{O,\min}} = \frac{61.27 \text{ kHz}}{6.127 \text{ kHz}} \approx 10 : 1$$

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Alternative Choice)*

#### **(a) [7 Marks] Explain how IC 566 can be used as a Frequency Shift Keying (FSK) generator. Draw the relevant circuit.**

**Model Solution:**

**FSK Concept (2 marks):**
Frequency Shift Keying is a digital modulation scheme where the carrier frequency is switched between two discrete values — a **mark (binary 1)** frequency and a **space (binary 0)** frequency — in synchronism with the digital data stream.

**Circuit Operation (5 marks):**

- The data signal (binary, typically 0 V and 5 V logic levels) is applied through a resistor $R_A$ to the **control voltage input (pin 7)** of the 566.
- The control voltage $V_C$ becomes a two-level waveform: $V_{C,HIGH}$ during a '1' bit and $V_{C,LOW}$ during a '0' bit.
- The 566 VCO generates:
  - A **mark frequency** $f_1$ when $V_C = V_{C,HIGH}$.
  - A **space frequency** $f_2$ when $V_C = V_{C,LOW}$.
- Both output frequencies are obtained from the relation:
$$f = \frac{2(V_{CC} - V_C)}{R_1 C_1 V_{CC}}$$
- By proper choice of $R_1$, $C_1$, and the data amplitude (or by adding a reference bias via $R_B$ in series with $R_A$), the two desired frequencies are realised.
- The **square wave output (pin 3)** serves as the FSK signal; it can be filtered or buffered before transmission.

**Design formulas:**

$$f_{\text{mark}} = \frac{2(V_{CC} - V_{C,HIGH})}{R_1 C_1 V_{CC}}, \quad f_{\text{space}} = \frac{2(V_{CC} - V_{C,LOW})}{R_1 C_1 V_{CC}}$$

**Typical Application:** 300 bps / 1200 bps modem signalling, low-cost telemetry, IR remote control.

#### **(b) [7 Marks] Describe the use of IC 566 as a (i) function generator and (ii) FM modulator. List the pin functions.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Function Generator (3.5 marks):**

The 566 is a natural choice for a low-cost function generator because it provides:
- A **square wave** at **pin 3** (TTL compatible, buffered).
- A **triangle wave** at **pin 4** (linear ramp up and down).
- A common timing network ($R_1$ at pin 5, $C_1$ at pin 6) determines the frequency of **both** outputs simultaneously.
- By making $V_C$ a slowly varying DC (from a potentiometer across $V_{CC}$), the user can **sweep** the output frequency — this is the basis of most lab-grade function generators built on the 566.
- Adding a sine-shaper network (e.g., a pair of diodes + resistors) to the triangle output produces an approximate **sine wave** — completing the three classic function-generator waveforms.

**(ii) FM Modulator (3.5 marks):**

In frequency modulation, the instantaneous frequency of the carrier must vary in proportion to the **message (modulating) signal** $m(t)$.
- The 566 does this **intrinsically** — its output frequency is **linearly dependent on $V_C$** (pin 7).
- Connect the **modulating signal** $m(t)$ (suitably biased and AC-coupled) to pin 7 through a resistor.
- Add a DC bias voltage $V_B$ (typically $0.875 V_{CC}$) so that $V_C = V_B + m(t)$ remains inside the valid range $0.75 V_{CC}$ to $V_{CC}$.
- The instantaneous frequency is:
$$f_i(t) = \frac{2}{R_1 C_1}\!\left(1 - \frac{V_B + m(t)}{V_{CC}}\right) = f_C - K_F \, m(t)$$
where $f_C$ is the carrier (centre) frequency and $K_F$ is the modulator sensitivity (Hz/V).
- The square-wave or triangle-wave output (after low-pass filtering) becomes the **FM signal**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the frequency formula — the derivation must show *both* half-cycles (charge + discharge) of the capacitor.
> 2. **Using wrong threshold voltages** — KTU expects the *idealised* thresholds $V_{CC}/2$ and $V_{CC}$ unless otherwise stated; do not insert extra $\pm V_{BE}$ terms unless the question explicitly asks for the datasheet-accurate version.
> 3. **Confusing pin 3 and pin 4** — pin 3 is **square wave**; pin 4 is **triangle wave**.
> 4. **Sweep range confusion** — it is a *ratio*, not an absolute value. Always express it as $f_{\max}/f_{\min}$ or "10 : 1".
> 5. **In FSK problems, students often forget the DC bias network** required to keep $V_C$ in its valid range when the data signal swings below 0.75 $V_{CC}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for VCO 566:**

- [x] The **566** is a **monolithic VCO** producing **simultaneous square and triangle waves**.
- [x] **Output frequency** formula (memorise exactly): $\;f_O = \dfrac{2(V_{CC} - V_C)}{R_1 C_1 V_{CC}} = \dfrac{2}{R_1 C_1}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{V_C}{V_{CC}}\right)$
- [x] Charging current: $\;I_C = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_C}{R_1}$
- [x] Valid $V_C$ range: $\;0.75\,V_{CC} \le V_C \le V_{CC}$
- [x] Maximum output frequency (at $V_C = 0.75 V_{CC}$): $\;f_{O,\max} = \dfrac{0.5}{R_1 C_1}$
- [x] Centre / free-running frequency (at $V_C = V_{CC}/2$): $\;f_O = \dfrac{1}{R_1 C_1}$
- [x] **Pin 3 → Square wave**, **Pin 4 → Triangle wave**, **Pin 5 → $R_1$**, **Pin 6 → $C_1$**, **Pin 7 → $V_C$ (modulating input)**, **Pin 8 → $V^+$**, **Pin 1 → GND**.
- [x] **Frequency range:** **1 Hz to 1 MHz**; **Sweep range:** **10 : 1**.
- [x] **Triangle and square waves are in phase** (square is HIGH during discharge of $C_1$).
- [x] **Internal blocks:** Current source/sinks, Schmitt trigger, output buffers, bias network.
- [x] **Key applications:** Function generator, **FSK generator**, **FM modulator**, tone generator, VCO in PLL.
- [x] In FSK: data signal $\to$ pin 7; in FM: message signal $\to$ pin 7 (with DC bias).
- [x] Square-wave output is **TTL-compatible** — directly usable as a digital clock source.
- [x] The 566 is a **direct V-to-F converter** — instantaneous frequency is linearly proportional to the input control voltage.
- [x] For **dual-supply operation**, connect the negative rail to pin 1; for **single-supply operation**, pin 1 is grounded.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PLL - IC 565 - Block Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT402 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** PLL - IC 565 - Block Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phase Locked Loop (PLL)** is a closed-loop negative feedback control system that synchronizes the output frequency and phase of a Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO) with the frequency and phase of an incoming reference signal, generating an error voltage proportional to the phase difference between the two signals.

The **IC 565** is a monolithic, general-purpose Phase Locked Loop (PLL) integrated circuit developed by Signetics (now Philips Semiconductors). It contains all the essential active blocks of an analog PLL within a single 14-pin DIP package and operates over a wide frequency range from approximately **0.001 Hz to 500 kHz**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The IC 565 is the canonical example studied in the Linear Integrated Circuits course under the topic "PLL Block Diagram." It is the most commonly asked question in KTU ESE from this module.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Chase and Lock" Mechanism

Imagine two children running on a circular track. One child (the **Reference Signal**) runs at a fixed, perfectly steady speed. The other child (the **VCO Output**) is told to adjust his stride so that he stays exactly one step behind. A coach (the **Phase Detector + LPF**) watches the gap between them. If the second child falls too far behind, the coach shouts louder (increases the control voltage), making the second child run faster. If he gets too close or overtakes, the coach shouts softer (decreases the control voltage), slowing him down. Once both children are running with the exact same step (frequency) and position offset (phase), the coach becomes silent — the loop is now **"LOCKED."**

This continuous correction is what a PLL does electronically:

- **Reference child** → Input Reference Signal $f_{in}$
- **Second child** → Voltage Controlled Oscillator $f_o$
- **Coach** → Phase Detector + Low Pass Filter
- **Coach's voice** → Error/Control Voltage $V_c(t)$
- **Locked state** → $f_{in} = f_o$ with a fixed phase difference

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

| Parameter | Standard Value / Symbol |
|---|---|
| Supply Voltage $V_{CC}$ | **$\pm 6\,\text{V}$ to $\pm 12\,\text{V}$** (typically **+12 V** single supply) |
| Operating Frequency Range | **0.001 Hz to 500 kHz** |
| Internal Zener Voltage | **5.6 V to 6.2 V** (regulated reference) |
| Package | **14-pin DIP** |
| PD Output Current | **1 mA (typical)** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Building Blocks of a PLL (Foundational Logic)

A PLL is composed of three fundamental sub-systems arranged in a negative feedback loop. Each sub-system performs a specific signal-processing function:

1. **Phase Detector (PD):** A nonlinear multiplier (typically an XOR gate or a balanced Gilbert cell mixer) that compares the input reference signal $f_{in}$ with the VCO output $f_o$. Its output is an error voltage $v_e(t)$ whose **average DC value** is proportional to the phase difference $\Delta\phi = \phi_{in} - \phi_o$.
2. **Low Pass Filter (LPF):** A passive RC network connected at the PD output. It removes the high-frequency sum component ($2\omega_o$) generated by the multiplier, leaving only the slowly-varying DC control voltage $V_c(t)$.
3. **Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO):** A current (or voltage) controlled oscillator whose instantaneous output frequency is a linear function of the input control voltage $V_c(t)$. In IC 565, the VCO is implemented as a **current-controlled Schmitt trigger** charging an external capacitor $C_1$.

## 2.2 Operating Modes of a PLL

- **Free-Running Mode:** When no input is applied, the VCO oscillates at its natural center frequency $f_o$ (also called the **free-running frequency**).
- **Capture (Acquisition) Mode:** When an input $f_{in}$ close to $f_o$ is applied, the loop "pulls" the VCO frequency toward $f_{in}$ through successive corrections. The range of $f_{in}$ over which this happens is the **Capture Range** $\Delta f_C$.
- **Locked (Tracking) Mode:** Once the loop acquires lock, $f_{in} = f_o$ (with a fixed phase offset). The VCO can now track small changes in $f_{in}$. The range of tracking is the **Lock Range** $\Delta f_L$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Distinction:** $\Delta f_L$ (Lock Range) is always **greater than or equal to** $\Delta f_C$ (Capture Range). The Lock Range is determined purely by the VCO gain and the PD gain, while the Capture Range depends on the LPF cutoff and is therefore always smaller.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are high-yield and frequently appear in KTU Numerical Answer Questions (NAQs) and Part B derivations. Memorize the units carefully.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Free-Running Frequency $f_o$ | $f_o = \dfrac{0.3}{R_1\,C_1}$ | $R_1$ in $\text{k}\Omega$, $C_1$ in $\mu\text{F}$, $f_o$ in $\text{kHz}$ |
| 2 | VCO Conversion Gain $K_o$ | $K_o = \dfrac{8\,f_o}{V_{CC}}$ | $V_{CC}$ in Volts, $K_o$ in $\text{Hz/V}$ |
| 3 | Lock Range $\Delta f_L$ | $\Delta f_L = \pm\,\dfrac{8\,f_o}{V_{CC}}$ | Also equal to $f_{max} - f_o$ |
| 4 | Capture Range $\Delta f_C$ | $\Delta f_C \approx \pm\,\dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{2\pi\,f_L}{\tau}}$ | where $\tau = R_2\,C_2$ (LPF time constant) |
| 5 | Simplified Capture Range | $\Delta f_C \approx \dfrac{\sqrt{\Delta f_L}}{\sqrt{2\pi\,R_2\,C_2}}$ | Useful in KTU numericals |
| 6 | Phase Detector Gain $K_d$ | $K_d = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{\pi}$ | For XOR-type phase detector |
| 7 | Output Frequency (Locked) | $f_o = f_{in} + \Delta f$ | After lock acquisition |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

PLLs are the workhorse of modern electronics. They are used in:

- **FM Demodulation:** Recovering the audio signal from an FM carrier (the LPF output becomes the demodulated audio).
- **Frequency Synthesis:** Generating precise, programmable clock frequencies in microprocessors and communication systems.
- **AM Demodulation (Synchronous Detection):** Recovering carrier from suppressed-carrier AM signals.
- **Motor Speed Control:** Locking motor shaft speed to a reference frequency.
- **FSK Demodulation:** Decoding binary data transmitted via frequency shift keying.
- **Clock Recovery in Digital Communications:** Extracting timing from serial data streams (used in USB, Ethernet, SATA).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Free-Running Frequency of IC 565 VCO

The IC 565 VCO is a **Schmitt-trigger-based current-controlled relaxation oscillator**. Internally, a current source delivers a constant charging current $I = \frac{V_{CC}}{R_1}$ into the external timing capacitor $C_1$. The Schmitt trigger switches when the capacitor voltage reaches its upper and lower threshold values, typically at $\pm V_{CC}/2$.

**Step 1:** Charging current through $R_1$ (using internal regulator voltage of approximately $\frac{V_{CC}}{4}$):

$$I_{charge} = \frac{V_{reg}}{R_1} \approx \frac{V_{CC}}{4\,R_1}$$

**Step 2:** The capacitor voltage must swing between upper and lower Schmitt thresholds separated by a voltage $\Delta V$ that is internally set to $\frac{V_{CC}}{2}$.

**Step 3:** The time required for one half-cycle of charging is:

$$t_{half} = \frac{C_1 \cdot \Delta V}{I_{charge}} = \frac{C_1 \cdot \frac{V_{CC}}{2}}{\frac{V_{CC}}{4\,R_1}} = \frac{C_1 \cdot V_{CC} \cdot 4\,R_1}{2\,V_{CC}} = 2\,R_1\,C_1$$

**Step 4:** One complete period consists of two half-cycles (charge + discharge):

$$T_o = 2 \times t_{half} = 2 \times 2\,R_1\,C_1 = 4\,R_1\,C_1$$

**Step 5:** Frequency is the reciprocal of the period, with empirically determined scaling factor of approximately **1.2** (accounting for non-ideal internal transistor drops):

$$f_o = \frac{1}{T_o} = \frac{1.2}{4\,R_1\,C_1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Standard Formula:** The most commonly written form in KTU textbooks is $f_o = \dfrac{0.3}{R_1\,C_1}$, which is algebraically equivalent when $R_1$ is in $\text{k}\Omega$ and $C_1$ is in $\mu\text{F}$ (the unit conversion factor of $10^{-3} \times 10^{-6}$ in the denominator produces the numerator **0.3**).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Lock Range $\Delta f_L$

**Step 1:** The maximum phase difference the PD can produce (for an XOR phase detector) is $\pm\pi$ radians, generating a maximum control voltage swing of:

$$V_{c(max)} = K_d \cdot \pi = \frac{V_{CC}}{\pi} \cdot \pi = V_{CC}$$

**Step 2:** The VCO frequency deviation from its center frequency $f_o$ for a control voltage $V_c$ is governed by the VCO gain $K_o$:

$$\Delta f = K_o \cdot V_c$$

**Step 3:** Substituting the maximum $V_c = V_{CC}$ and the expression for $K_o$:

$$\Delta f_L = K_o \cdot V_{CC} = \frac{8\,f_o}{V_{CC}} \cdot V_{CC} = 8\,f_o$$

**Step 4:** Since the lock is symmetric around $f_o$, the bidirectional range is:

$$\boxed{\Delta f_L = \pm\,\frac{8\,f_o}{V_{CC}}}$$

**Step 5:** Expressing in terms of absolute frequency limits:

$$f_{max} = f_o + \frac{8\,f_o}{V_{CC}}, \qquad f_{min} = f_o - \frac{8\,f_o}{V_{CC}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Capture Range $\Delta f_C$

The Capture Range is always **less than** the Lock Range because the LPF attenuates the beat-frequency components before they can pull the VCO.

**Step 1:** The beat frequency $\Delta\omega$ generated by the unlocked PD output is attenuated by the LPF transfer function. For a simple RC low-pass filter with time constant $\tau = R_2\,C_2$:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega \tau)^2}}$$

**Step 2:** The PD output amplitude at the beat frequency is reduced from its unlocked value $V_d$ to $V_d \cdot H(j\omega)$. For lock acquisition, this attenuated signal must still be large enough to shift the VCO frequency by $\Delta f$.

**Step 3:** Solving the loop dynamics equation (a first-order approximation of the second-order PLL):

$$\Delta f_C \approx \sqrt{\frac{\Delta f_L}{2\pi\,R_2\,C_2}}$$

Or equivalently (multiplying numerator and denominator):

$$\boxed{\Delta f_C = \pm\,\frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{2\pi\,\Delta f_L}{R_2\,C_2}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Increasing $R_2 C_2$ (slower LPF) **narrows** the Capture Range but **improves** the noise rejection. There is always a trade-off, and KTU numericals often ask students to select $R_2 C_2$ to achieve a specific $\Delta f_C$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Pin-by-Pin Function of IC 565

| Pin No. | Symbol | Function | Internal Connection |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V^+$ | Positive Supply Voltage | To internal Zener regulator & all stages |
| 2 | $VCO_{OUT}$ | VCO Output (square wave) | Output of Schmitt trigger / VCO |
| 3 | $VCO_{IN}$ | VCO Control Voltage Input | Input to VCO (after Amp-A2) |
| 4 | $VCO_{ADJ}$ | VCO Frequency Adjust | External $R_1, C_1$ connected here |
| 5 | $PD_{OUT}$ | Phase Detector Output | Output of XOR / multiplier |
| 6 | $REF_{IN}$ | Reference Frequency Input | To Phase Detector (one input) |
| 7 | $COMP_{IN}$ | Comparison Frequency Input | To Phase Detector (other input) |
| 8 | GND | Ground | Common ground |
| 9 | — | Not connected / test pin | — |
| 10 | — | Internal biasing | — |
| 11 | $V_{Z}$ | Internal Zener (5.6 V) | Regulator output |
| 12,13,14 | — | Amplifier access (A1, A2) | Op-amp inputs/outputs |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical (KTU-Style Example)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample Question Pattern:** Frequently asked in KTU as a Part B numerical.

**Q:** Design an IC 565 PLL with $f_o = 10\,\text{kHz}$ and supply $V_{CC} = 12\,\text{V}$. Calculate the Lock Range. Choose $R_1$ such that $C_1 = 0.01\,\mu\text{F}$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Solve for $R_1$ using $f_o = \dfrac{0.3}{R_1\,C_1}$:

$$10 = \frac{0.3}{R_1 \times 0.01} \implies R_1 = \frac{0.3}{10 \times 0.01} = \frac{0.3}{0.1} = 3\,\text{k}\Omega$$

Step 2 — Compute Lock Range:

$$\Delta f_L = \pm\,\frac{8 \times 10\,\text{kHz}}{12\,\text{V}} = \pm\,\frac{80}{12} \approx \pm\,6.67\,\text{kHz}$$

Step 3 — Output frequency limits:

$$f_{max} = 10 + 6.67 = 16.67\,\text{kHz}, \qquad f_{min} = 10 - 6.67 = 3.33\,\text{kHz}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Internal Block Diagram of IC 565 — Detailed Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block-level flow diagram illustrates the **internal signal path** of the IC 565, showing how the Phase Detector, LPF, and VCO interact with their associated amplifiers and the internal voltage regulator.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC SUPPLY VCC PIN 1] --> B[INTERNAL ZENER REGULATOR 5.6V]
    A --> C[AMPLIFIER A1 INPUT PINS 12 13]
    A --> D[AMPLIFIER A2 INPUT PIN 3]
    B --> E[VOLTAGE CONTROLLED OSCILLATOR VCO]
    F[EXTERNAL RESISTOR R1 AND CAPACITOR C1 AT PIN 4] --> E
    E --> G[VCO SQUARE WAVE OUTPUT PIN 2]
    G --> H[FEEDBACK TO PHASE DETECTOR]
    I[REFERENCE INPUT FREQUENCY FIN PIN 6] --> J[PHASE DETECTOR XOR]
    H --> J
    J --> K[PHASE DETECTOR OUTPUT VE PIN 5]
    K --> L[LOW PASS FILTER R2 C2 EXTERNAL]
    L --> M[FILTERED DC CONTROL VOLTAGE VC]
    M --> D
    D --> E
    C --> N[AMPLIFIER A1 OUTPUT PIN 14]
```

## 4.2 Simplified Functional Block Diagram (KTU Textbook Style)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IC565[IC 565 14 PIN DIP PACKAGE]
        PD["PHASE DETECTOR XOR TYPE"]
        LPF["LOW PASS FILTER EXTERNAL R2 C2"]
        VCO["VOLTAGE CONTROLLED OSCILLATOR VCO"]
        A1["AMPLIFIER A1 PD OUTPUT BUFFER"]
        A2["AMPLIFIER A2 VCO INPUT BUFFER"]
        ZNR["ZENER REGULATOR 5.6V"]
    end
    FIN["INPUT REFERENCE FIN"] --> PD
    FOUT["VCO OUTPUT FOUT PIN 2"] --> PD
    PD -->|"VE PIN 5"| LPF
    LPF -->|"VC CONTROL VOLTAGE"| VCO
    VCO --> FOUT
    ZNR -.-> VCO
    A1 --- PD
    A2 --- VCO
```

## 4.3 External Component Connection Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VIN["INPUT SIGNAL"] --> P6[PIN 6 REFERENCE INPUT]
    P7[PIN 7 COMPARISON INPUT] --> MUX[FROM VCO OUTPUT]
    P5[PIN 5 PD OUTPUT] --> R2C2["EXTERNAL R2 C2 LOW PASS FILTER"]
    R2C2 --> P3[PIN 3 VCO INPUT]
    P4[PIN 4 VCO FREQ ADJUST] --> R1C1["EXTERNAL R1 C1 TIMING NETWORK"]
    P2[PIN 2 VCO OUTPUT] --> OUT[OUTPUT FREQUENCY]
    P1[PIN 1 VCC PLUS 12V]
    P8[PIN 8 GROUND]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram (KTU Style):** The arrows indicate the **direction of signal flow**, not the direction of current. The Phase Detector has two inputs (Pin 6 and Pin 7) and one output (Pin 5). The LPF is **external** because choosing $R_2$ and $C_2$ allows the designer to set the capture range independently.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Q:** Define a Phase Locked Loop (PLL). List any **four** applications of PLL.

**Model Answer:**

A Phase Locked Loop (PLL) is a closed-loop negative feedback system that synchronizes the output of a Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO) with an incoming reference signal in both **frequency** and **phase**.

The four major applications are:

1. **Frequency Modulation (FM) Demodulation** — recovering audio from FM carrier.
2. **Frequency Synthesis** — generating precise, programmable clock frequencies.
3. **Frequency Shift Keying (FSK) Demodulation** — decoding binary data.
4. **Clock Recovery in Digital Communication Systems** — extracting timing from serial bit streams.

*(Any four valid applications fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Q:** What is a Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO)? Write the expression for free-running frequency of IC 565.

**Model Answer:**

A **Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO)** is an oscillator whose output frequency is a linear function of an externally applied control voltage. In the IC 565, the VCO is implemented as a current-controlled relaxation oscillator using a Schmitt trigger and external timing components $R_1$ and $C_1$.

The free-running frequency is given by:

$$f_o = \frac{0.3}{R_1\,C_1} \quad \text{(with } R_1 \text{ in k}\Omega \text{ and } C_1 \text{ in }\mu\text{F)}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Part B offers an **internal choice**. Below are **TWO completely independent question sets**. Attempt either Question A or Question B.

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 Model]`

#### (a) Draw and explain the internal block diagram of IC 565 PLL. List the function of each block. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The IC 565 consists of the following internal blocks connected in a closed loop:

**Step 1 — Phase Detector (PD):** Implemented as an XOR gate or balanced multiplier. It accepts two inputs — the reference signal $f_{in}$ at Pin 6 and the VCO output $f_o$ at Pin 7 — and produces an error voltage $v_e(t)$ at Pin 5. The average value of $v_e(t)$ is proportional to the phase difference between the two inputs.

**Step 2 — Low Pass Filter (LPF):** An **external** RC network ($R_2, C_2$) connected at Pin 5. It removes the high-frequency AC sum component ($2f_o$) generated by the phase detector, leaving only the slowly varying DC control voltage $V_c(t)$.

**Step 3 — Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO):** A Schmitt-trigger-based relaxation oscillator whose output frequency is controlled by the DC voltage $V_c(t)$. External timing components $R_1$ (Pin 4) and $C_1$ set the center frequency. The output is a square wave available at Pin 2.

**Step 4 — Amplifier A1:** A high-gain differential amplifier with inputs at Pins 12 and 13 and output at Pin 14. It is used to buffer the PD output and can be configured externally as a low-pass filter or comparator.

**Step 5 — Amplifier A2:** Buffers the VCO control input at Pin 3.

**Step 6 — Internal Zener Regulator:** Provides a stable regulated voltage of approximately **5.6 V** to the VCO, isolating it from supply fluctuations.

**Marks Distribution:** Diagram – 3 marks, Block explanation – 4 marks.

#### (b) Derive the expressions for Lock Range and Capture Range of IC 565. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Lock Range Derivation (4 marks):**

Step 1 — The maximum phase error an XOR PD can correct is $\pm \pi$, producing maximum control voltage:

$$V_{c(max)} = K_d \cdot \pi = \frac{V_{CC}}{\pi} \cdot \pi = V_{CC}$$

Step 2 — VCO gain is given by $K_o = \dfrac{8\,f_o}{V_{CC}}$. The frequency shift is:

$$\Delta f_L = K_o \cdot V_{c(max)} = \frac{8\,f_o}{V_{CC}} \cdot V_{CC} = 8\,f_o$$

Step 3 — Expressing as bidirectional range:

$$\Delta f_L = \pm\,\frac{8\,f_o}{V_{CC}}$$

**Capture Range Derivation (3 marks):**

Step 1 — Capture depends on the LPF time constant $\tau = R_2\,C_2$. The attenuated beat-frequency control voltage must be sufficient to shift the VCO.

Step 2 — Solving the loop dynamics:

$$\Delta f_C = \pm\,\frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{2\pi\,\Delta f_L}{R_2\,C_2}}$$

Step 3 — Capture range is always **less than** Lock Range since the LPF attenuates the control signal before acquisition.

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a Phase Locked Loop. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Open Loop Behavior (1 mark):** When a reference signal $f_{in}$ is applied to the phase detector without any feedback, the PD produces an output that is the product of the two inputs, containing sum and difference frequency components.

**Step 2 — Closed Loop Operation (2 marks):** The VCO output $f_o$ is fed back to the PD. The PD compares the phase of $f_o$ with $f_{in}$ and generates an error voltage $v_e(t) = K_d \cdot \Delta\phi$. This is filtered by the LPF to produce $V_c(t)$.

**Step 3 — Acquisition Mode (2 marks):** If $f_{in}$ is close to $f_o$ (within the capture range), the control voltage progressively shifts the VCO frequency toward $f_{in}$. The system undergoes a transient "beating" process until the frequencies match.

**Step 4 — Locked Mode (2 marks):** Once $f_o = f_{in}$ with a fixed phase difference, the loop is locked. Any small change in $f_{in}$ is tracked by adjusting $V_c(t)$ — this is the **tracking** behavior of the PLL.

**Marks Distribution:** Block diagram – 2 marks, Three operating modes – 5 marks.

#### (b) An IC 565 PLL is operated with $V_{CC} = 10\,\text{V}$, $R_1 = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$, and $C_1 = 0.02\,\mu\text{F}$. Compute (i) free-running frequency, (ii) Lock Range, and (iii) the maximum and minimum VCO output frequencies. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Free-Running Frequency (2 marks):**

$$f_o = \frac{0.3}{R_1 \cdot C_1} = \frac{0.3}{5 \times 0.02} = \frac{0.3}{0.1} = 3\,\text{kHz}$$

**Part (ii) — Lock Range (2 marks):**

$$\Delta f_L = \pm\,\frac{8\,f_o}{V_{CC}} = \pm\,\frac{8 \times 3}{10} = \pm\,2.4\,\text{kHz}$$

**Part (iii) — Maximum and Minimum Frequencies (3 marks):**

$$f_{max} = f_o + \Delta f_L = 3 + 2.4 = 5.4\,\text{kHz}$$

$$f_{min} = f_o - \Delta f_L = 3 - 2.4 = 0.6\,\text{kHz}$$

Thus, the PLL will remain locked for any input frequency between **0.6 kHz** and **5.4 kHz**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Students often write $f_o = \dfrac{0.3}{R_1\,C_1}$ without specifying units, then substitute $R_1$ in ohms and $C_1$ in farads — this gives incorrect answers by a factor of **$10^9$**. Always convert $R_1 \to \text{k}\Omega$ and $C_1 \to \mu\text{F}$ first.
> 2. **Lock vs Capture Range Confusion:** Many students interchange these two terms. Remember: **Lock Range $\geq$ Capture Range**, always.
> 3. **Bidirectional Range Sign:** $\Delta f_L$ is **bidirectional** (i.e., $\pm\,6.67\,\text{kHz}$, not just $+6.67\,\text{kHz}$). Examiners deduct 1 mark if the $\pm$ is omitted.
> 4. **Missing Block Names in Diagrams:** When drawing the block diagram of IC 565, students often forget the **Amplifier A1** and **Zener Regulator**. These are expected components and missing them costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PLL** stands for **Phase Locked Loop** — a negative feedback system that locks a VCO's frequency/phase to a reference signal.
- **Three mandatory blocks:** Phase Detector (PD) + Low Pass Filter (LPF) + Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO).
- **IC 565** is a **14-pin monolithic PLL** operating from **0.001 Hz to 500 kHz** with internal Zener regulation.
- **Free-running frequency formula:** $f_o = \dfrac{0.3}{R_1\,C_1}$ (with $R_1$ in $\text{k}\Omega$, $C_1$ in $\mu\text{F}$, result in $\text{kHz}$).
- **Lock Range:** $\Delta f_L = \pm\,\dfrac{8\,f_o}{V_{CC}}$ — depends only on supply voltage and $f_o$.
- **Capture Range:** $\Delta f_C \approx \pm\,\dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{2\pi\,\Delta f_L}{R_2\,C_2}}$ — depends on the LPF.
- **Always** remember: $\Delta f_C \leq \Delta f_L$.
- **PD Gain** for XOR-type detector: $K_d = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{\pi}$.
- **VCO Gain** $K_o = \dfrac{8\,f_o}{V_{CC}}$ (in Hz/Volt).
- **Pin 5** = PD output; **Pin 3** = VCO control input; **Pins 6 & 7** = PD inputs; **Pins 4, 2** = VCO timing & output.
- **LPF is external** ($R_2, C_2$ chosen by designer), unlike VCO components which directly determine $f_o$.
- **Real-world uses:** FM demodulation, frequency synthesis, FSK demododulation, clock recovery, motor speed control.
- **Trap questions to watch for:** $f_o$ vs $f_{in}$ vs $f_c$ vs $f_L$ — never confuse them in derivations.
- **Lock acquisition sequence:** Free-running → Capture (beating) → Locked (steady tracking). Examiners love asking this sequence!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
