<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The 555 Timer IC — The "Swiss Army Knife" of Analog ICs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> The **IC 555 Timer** is a highly stable, monolithic timing circuit capable of producing accurate time delays or oscillation. It can be operated in three fundamental modes: **Monostable** (one-shot), **Astable** (free-running oscillator), and **Bistable** (flip-flop) modes. It is a hybrid analog–digital IC that combines a pair of comparators, a flip-flop, a discharge transistor, and an output driver stage on a single silicon chip.

### Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **kitchen egg-timer** with a buzzer:
- You twist the dial to set a duration (like setting a resistor–capacitor pair, the **RC time constant**).
- When you start it, the buzzer *waits* for a fixed interval, then **rings loudly** for an instant, and then *goes silent* and waits to be triggered again.

The 555 Timer is essentially that egg-timer — but instead of buzzing, it flips its **OUTPUT PIN** from **0 V (LOW)** to **+V_CC (HIGH)** and back, after a precisely controlled time interval set by external **R** and **C** components.

In **astable mode**, the timer *never stops oscillating* — like a child's swing that keeps moving back and forth forever until you cut the power.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard 555 IC Naming Convention:**
> - **NE 555** (Signetics/Philips, Bipolar Junction Transistor based)
> - **SE 555** (Improved electrical specifications)
> - **LM 555** (Texas Instruments / National Semiconductor)
> - **TLC 555** (CMOS variant — much lower power consumption, rail-to-rail swing)
> - **LMC 555** (CMOS precision variant)

## 1.2 Physical Constants and Standard Operating Limits

The following are the **manufacturer-rated absolute maximum values** that every KTU examiner expects you to know by heart:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Supply Voltage (Bipolar 555) | $V_{CC}$ | **+4.5 V to +16 V** |
| Supply Voltage (CMOS TLC/LMC 555) | $V_{DD}$ | **+2 V to +18 V** |
| Maximum Output Current (sink/source) | $I_{OUT}$ | **200 mA** |
| Operating Temperature Range | $T_A$ | **0 °C to +70 °C** |
| Power Dissipation (Plastic DIP-8) | $P_D$ | **600 mW** |
| Threshold Voltage (Pin 6) | $V_{TH}$ | **2/3 × V_CC** |
| Trigger Voltage (Pin 2) | $V_{TR}$ | **1/3 × V_CC** |
| Control Voltage (Pin 5) | $V_{CON}$ | **2/3 × V_CC** |
| Reset Pin Active Level (Pin 4) | $\overline{MR}$ | **≤ 0.4 V (Active LOW)** |

## 1.3 Pin Configuration — The 8-Pin DIP Package

The IC 555 is conventionally housed in an **8-pin Dual In-line Package (DIP-8)**. The pinout is the most frequently asked diagram in KTU examinations, so memorization is non-negotiable.

| Pin # | Pin Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | **GND** | Ground reference (0 V) — the negative supply rail |
| **2** | **TRIGGER** | Active-LOW input; starts the timing cycle when $V_{pin2} \le 1/3\,V_{CC}$ |
| **3** | **OUTPUT** | Provides the timed output waveform; capable of sourcing/sinking **200 mA** |
| **4** | **RESET** | Active-LOW master reset; forces output LOW when pulled below **0.4 V** |
| **5** | **CONTROL VOLTAGE** | External access to the internal **2/3 V_CC** divider; modulates threshold |
| **6** | **THRESHOLD** | Active-HIGH input; ends the timing cycle when $V_{pin6} \ge 2/3\,V_{CC}$ |
| **7** | **DISCHARGE** | Open-collector transistor; used to discharge the timing capacitor |
| **8** | **V_CC (+V_S)** | Positive supply voltage (typically **+5 V to +15 V**) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mental Anchor for Pins 2, 6, and 7:**
> - **Pin 2 (TRIGGER)** = *Lower comparator* fires when voltage **falls below 1/3 V_CC**
> - **Pin 6 (THRESHOLD)** = *Upper comparator* fires when voltage **rises above 2/3 V_CC**
> - **Pin 7 (DISCHARGE)** = The *switch* (transistor) that **drains the capacitor** when ON
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Block Diagram Architecture — A Layered Functional Walkthrough

The 555 IC is best understood by peeling away its silicon layers. The complete internal schematic contains **five functional building blocks**:

1. **A Resistive Voltage Divider** — a string of three equal **5 kΩ** resistors connected between $V_{CC}$ and GND.
   > This is precisely where the IC gets its name: the "**555**" — three 5 kΩ resistors in series.
2. **Two Precision Comparators** — Comparators 1 and 2 (built from op-amps with differential input stages).
3. **A Set–Reset (SR) Flip-Flop** — the digital memory heart of the IC.
4. **A Discharge Transistor (Q1)** — an NPN BJT whose collector is brought out to Pin 7.
5. **A Totem-Pole Output Stage** — a push-pull driver capable of delivering high current.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a resistive divider of exactly 5 kΩ?**
> Because the three identical resistors form a passive, temperature-stable, supply-independent voltage reference network. Since the resistors are **monolithic** (fabricated on the same silicon die), they **track each other** with temperature, giving the internal reference voltages a near-zero temperature coefficient — a critical property for timing accuracy.

## 2.2 Functional Operation of Each Internal Stage

### Stage 1 — The Resistive Divider
With three 5 kΩ resistors between $V_{CC}$ and GND, the internal nodes settle at:
- **Upper node** (junction of R2 and R3) = $\frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$ → fed to the **inverting input** of Comparator 1.
- **Lower node** (junction of R1 and R2) = $\frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$ → fed to the **non-inverting input** of Comparator 2.

### Stage 2 — The Two Comparators
- **Comparator 1** (Upper Comparator):
  - Non-inverting (+) input = **Pin 6 (THRESHOLD)**
  - Inverting (−) input = $\frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$
  - **Output = HIGH** when $V_{pin6} > \frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$
- **Comparator 2** (Lower Comparator):
  - Non-inverting (+) input = $\frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$
  - Inverting (−) input = **Pin 2 (TRIGGER)**
  - **Output = HIGH** when $V_{pin2} < \frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$

### Stage 3 — The SR Flip-Flop (Latching Memory)
- **S input** ← driven by Comparator 2 (lower comparator)
- **R input** ← driven by Comparator 1 (upper comparator)
- **Q output** ← drives the OUTPUT pin (Pin 3) directly
- **Q̄ output** ← drives the BASE of the discharge transistor Q1

### Stage 4 — The Discharge Transistor
- When the flip-flop's Q̄ is HIGH, transistor Q1 is **saturated ON** → **Pin 7 is shorted to GND** → external timing capacitor *discharges rapidly*.
- When Q̄ is LOW, Q1 is **cut-off OFF** → **Pin 7 floats** → the timing capacitor *charges* through the external resistor.

### Stage 5 — The Output Buffer (Totem-Pole Stage)
- A push-pull pair of transistors that can both **source** current (drive a load HIGH) and **sink** current (pull a load LOW).
- This is what makes the 555 capable of directly driving **relays, LEDs, and small speakers** up to **200 mA**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Memorize These!

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is THE cheat sheet you must memorize for KTU exams. Every single formula here has appeared (or will appear) in KTU End-Semester Examinations.**

| Mode / Application | Key Formula | Parameter Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Monostable Pulse Width** | $T = 1.1\,R\,C$ | $R$ in Ω, $C$ in F, $T$ in seconds |
| **Astable Frequency** | $f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B)\,C}$ | $R_A, R_B$ in Ω, $C$ in F, $f$ in Hz |
| **Astable Time Period** | $T = 0.693\,(R_A + 2R_B)\,C$ | Total period of oscillation |
| **Astable HIGH Time (t_H)** | $t_H = 0.693\,(R_A + R_B)\,C$ | Charging duration |
| **Astable LOW Time (t_L)** | $t_L = 0.693\,R_B\,C$ | Discharging duration |
| **Duty Cycle (Astable)** | $D = \dfrac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} \times 100\%$ | Always $> 50\%$ |
| **Schmitt Trigger Upper Threshold** | $V_{UT} = \dfrac{2}{3}\,V_{CC}$ | Upper trip point |
| **Schmitt Trigger Lower Threshold** | $V_{LT} = \dfrac{1}{3}\,V_{CC}$ | Lower trip point |
| **Hysteresis Voltage (Schmitt)** | $V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = \dfrac{1}{3}\,V_{CC}$ | Noise immunity window |
| **Charging Equation (Universal)** | $V_C(t) = V_{\infty} - (V_{\infty} - V_0)\,e^{-t/RC}$ | Capacitor voltage transient |
| **Discharging Equation (Universal)** | $V_C(t) = V_0\,e^{-t/RC}$ | Decay toward 0 V |
| **LM317 Output Voltage** | $V_{OUT} = 1.25\left(1 + \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right)$ | Adjustable regulator output |
| **LM317 Reference Voltage** | $V_{REF} = 1.25\,\text{V}$ | Internal bandgap reference |
| **78XX Output Voltage** | $V_{OUT} = +V_{\text{XX}}$ (fixed) | e.g., 7805 → +5 V, 7812 → +12 V |
| **79XX Output Voltage** | $V_{OUT} = -V_{\text{XX}}$ (fixed) | e.g., 7905 → −5 V, 7915 → −15 V |
| **PLL Lock Range** | $f_L = \pm f_{osc} \cdot \dfrac{V_{CC}}{2 \cdot V_{CO\,max}}$ | Frequency tracking range |
| **PLL Capture Range** | $f_C = \pm \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{2\pi f_L}{\tau}}$ | Acquisition range |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The 555 timer is not merely a textbook curiosity. It is one of the **best-selling ICs of all time**, with billions of units shipped annually. In modern engineering practice, you will encounter it in:

- **Automotive electronics:** turn-signal flashers, windshield-wiper interval timers
- **Consumer appliances:** washing-machine cycle timers, microwave-oven buzzers
- **Industrial control:** missing-pulse detectors, sequential timing chains
- **Hobby electronics:** robotics, model trains, LED blinkers
- **Test & measurement:** function generators (with op-amp summers), pulse-width-modulation motor drivers
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION — Monostable Multivibrator (One-Shot)

### Step 1 — Circuit Topology and Initial Steady State

In **monostable mode**, the 555 is configured as follows:
- **External Resistor R** is connected between $V_{CC}$ and Pin 7.
- **External Capacitor C** is connected between Pin 7 (also tied to Pin 6) and GND.
- **Pin 2 (TRIGGER)** receives an externally applied negative-going pulse.

In the **quiescent stable state** (before triggering):
- $V_{pin6}$ sits at $V_{CC}$ (capacitor is fully charged).
- $V_{pin6} > \frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$ → Comparator 1 output = HIGH → **R = 1** on flip-flop.
- The flip-flop is **RESET** → **Q = 0** → Pin 3 OUTPUT = LOW.
- $\overline{Q} = 1$ → Discharge transistor Q1 is **ON** → Pin 7 is shorted to GND → Capacitor C is held at **0 V**.

This is the *single* stable state of the circuit — hence the name **mono-stable**.

### Step 2 — Application of the Trigger Pulse

At $t = t_0$, an external negative-going pulse drops $V_{pin2}$ below $\frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$.

This causes:
- Comparator 2 output = HIGH → **S = 1** on flip-flop.
- The flip-flop is **SET** → **Q = 1** → Pin 3 OUTPUT goes **HIGH**.
- $\overline{Q} = 0$ → Discharge transistor Q1 is **OFF** → Pin 7 floats.
- The capacitor now **begins charging** through resistor R from $V_{CC}$.

### Step 3 — Capacitor Charging Toward $V_{CC}$

The capacitor charges according to the universal first-order RC charging equation:

$$
V_C(t) = V_{\infty} - (V_{\infty} - V_0)\,e^{-t/RC}
$$

For our case: $V_{\infty} = V_{CC}$ (final value as $t \to \infty$), $V_0 = 0$ (initial value at $t = t_0$). Substituting:

$$
V_C(t) = V_{CC} - (V_{CC} - 0)\,e^{-(t-t_0)/RC} = V_{CC}\left(1 - e^{-(t-t_0)/RC}\right)
$$

### Step 4 — Determining When the Timing Cycle Terminates

The cycle **automatically ends** the instant the capacitor voltage reaches the upper threshold:

$$
V_{TH} = \frac{2}{3} V_{CC}
$$

Setting $V_C(t) = \frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$ and solving for the elapsed time $T = t - t_0$:

$$
\frac{2}{3} V_{CC} = V_{CC}\left(1 - e^{-T/RC}\right)
$$

Divide both sides by $V_{CC}$:

$$
\frac{2}{3} = 1 - e^{-T/RC}
$$

Rearrange to isolate the exponential term:

$$
e^{-T/RC} = 1 - \frac{2}{3} = \frac{1}{3}
$$

Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
-\frac{T}{RC} = \ln\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) = -\ln(3)
$$

Multiply by $-RC$:

$$
T = RC \cdot \ln(3)
$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Evaluation of $\ln(3)$

The natural logarithm of 3 is an irrational number, but its decimal expansion is well known:

$$
\ln(3) = 1.0986 \approx 1.1
$$

Therefore:

$$
T = 1.1\,R\,C
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Derived Result — KTU Examination Ready:**
> $$\boxed{T = 1.1\,R\,C}$$
> This is the **monostable pulse width** of the 555 timer. It is the most important formula in LIC Module 4.

### Step 6 — Post-Timing Behavior (Return to Stable State)

At $t = t_0 + T$, the threshold comparator fires:
- Comparator 1 output = HIGH → flip-flop is **RESET** → Q = 0 → Pin 3 OUTPUT goes **LOW**.
- $\overline{Q} = 1$ → Discharge transistor Q1 is **ON** → Pin 7 is shorted to GND.
- Capacitor C **rapidly discharges** through the saturated transistor (which has a very low ON resistance, typically $\approx 10\,\Omega$).
- The circuit **returns** to its original stable state, ready for the next trigger pulse.

## 3.2 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION — Astable Multivibrator (Free-Running Oscillator)

### Step 1 — Circuit Topology

In **astable mode**, the 555 has no stable state — it oscillates continuously:
- **$R_A$** is connected between $V_{CC}$ and Pin 7.
- **$R_B$** is connected between Pin 7 and Pin 2 (which is also tied to Pin 6).
- **C** is connected between Pin 2/Pin 6 and GND.

The capacitor C charges through **$R_A$ + $R_B$** (path: $V_{CC} \to R_A \to R_B \to C$) and discharges through **$R_B$ only** (path: $C \to R_B \to \text{Pin 7} \to \text{GND}$, when discharge transistor is ON).

### Step 2 — Derivation of the Charging Time $t_H$

The capacitor charges from $\frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$ (lower threshold) toward $V_{CC}$ through the series combination $(R_A + R_B)$.

Using the general charging equation:

$$
V_C(t) = V_{\infty} - (V_{\infty} - V_0)\,e^{-t/\tau}
$$

Here: $V_{\infty} = V_{CC}$, $V_0 = \frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$, $\tau = (R_A + R_B) C$.

At the end of the charging phase, $V_C(t_H) = \frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$:

$$
\frac{2}{3} V_{CC} = V_{CC} - \left(V_{CC} - \frac{1}{3} V_{CC}\right)e^{-t_H/[(R_A + R_B)C]}
$$

Simplify the bracket term:

$$
V_{CC} - \frac{1}{3} V_{CC} = \frac{2}{3} V_{CC}
$$

So:

$$
\frac{2}{3} V_{CC} = V_{CC} - \frac{2}{3} V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t_H/[(R_A + R_B)C]}
$$

Rearrange:

$$
\frac{2}{3} V_{CC} e^{-t_H/[(R_A + R_B)C]} = V_{CC} - \frac{2}{3} V_{CC} = \frac{1}{3} V_{CC}
$$

Divide by $V_{CC}$ and multiply by $\frac{3}{2}$:

$$
e^{-t_H/[(R_A + R_B)C]} = \frac{1/3}{2/3} = \frac{1}{2}
$$

Take the natural logarithm:

$$
-\frac{t_H}{(R_A + R_B)C} = \ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -\ln(2) = -0.693
$$

Therefore:

$$
t_H = 0.693\,(R_A + R_B)\,C
$$

### Step 3 — Derivation of the Discharging Time $t_L$

The capacitor discharges from $\frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$ toward **0 V** (GND), but the discharge path is through **$R_B$ only** (the discharge transistor Q1 acts as a near-ideal short to ground when ON).

Using the universal discharging equation:

$$
V_C(t) = V_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau}
$$

Here: $V_0 = \frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$, $\tau = R_B C$, and the target is $V_C(t_L) = \frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$:

$$
\frac{1}{3} V_{CC} = \frac{2}{3} V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t_L/(R_B C)}
$$

Divide both sides by $\frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$:

$$
e^{-t_L/(R_B C)} = \frac{1/3}{2/3} = \frac{1}{2}
$$

Take the natural logarithm:

$$
-\frac{t_L}{R_B C} = -\ln(2)
$$

Therefore:

$$
t_L = 0.693\,R_B\,C
$$

### Step 4 — Total Period and Frequency

The total period of one complete cycle is the sum of the charging and discharging intervals:

$$
T = t_H + t_L = 0.693\,(R_A + R_B)\,C + 0.693\,R_B\,C
$$

$$
T = 0.693\,(R_A + 2R_B)\,C
$$

The frequency is the reciprocal of the period:

$$
f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{0.693\,(R_A + 2R_B)\,C}
$$

Using the identity $\frac{1}{0.693} \approx 1.443 \approx 1.44$:

$$
\boxed{f = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B)\,C}}
$$

### Step 5 — Duty Cycle Derivation

The duty cycle is defined as the fraction of the period for which the output is HIGH:

$$
D = \frac{t_H}{T} \times 100\% = \frac{0.693\,(R_A + R_B)\,C}{0.693\,(R_A + 2R_B)\,C} \times 100\%
$$

The $0.693$ and $C$ cancel, leaving:

$$
\boxed{D = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} \times 100\%}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Observation for KTU Examinations:**
> In the standard astable configuration, the duty cycle is **ALWAYS greater than 50%** and can approach but **never reach 100%**. The minimum possible duty cycle is 50% (when $R_A \to 0$). To achieve duty cycles **below 50%**, a **modified astable configuration** with a diode in parallel with $R_B$ must be used.

## 3.3 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION — LM317 Adjustable Voltage Regulator Output

The **LM317** is a three-terminal adjustable positive voltage regulator. The internal reference voltage between the OUTPUT pin and the ADJUST pin is:

$$
V_{REF} = V_{OUT} - V_{ADJ} = 1.25\,\text{V}
$$

A fixed resistor $R_1$ is connected between the OUTPUT pin and the ADJUST pin. A potentiometer $R_2$ is connected between the ADJUST pin and GND.

The current flowing through $R_1$ is:

$$
I_{R_1} = \frac{V_{REF}}{R_1} = \frac{1.25}{R_1}
$$

In normal operation, the ADJUST pin draws a very small constant current $I_{ADJ} \approx 50\,\mu\text{A}$ (often neglected in textbook problems). Assuming $I_{ADJ} \approx 0$:

$$
I_{R_2} = I_{R_1} = \frac{1.25}{R_1}
$$

The output voltage is the sum of the voltage across $R_1$ and the voltage across $R_2$:

$$
V_{OUT} = V_{REF} + I_{R_2} \cdot R_2 = 1.25 + \frac{1.25 \cdot R_2}{R_1}
$$

Factoring out 1.25:

$$
\boxed{V_{OUT} = 1.25\left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)}
$$

If $I_{ADJ}$ is **not** neglected, the more rigorous form is:

$$
V_{OUT} = 1.25\left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) + I_{ADJ} \cdot R_2
$$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Internal Block Diagram of IC 555 (Mermaid Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VCC1["VCC Pin 8"] --> DIVIDER["Resistive Divider Three 5k Resistors"]
    GND1["GND Pin 1"] --> DIVIDER
    DIVIDER --> REF1["2 by 3 VCC Reference"]
    DIVIDER --> REF2["1 by 3 VCC Reference"]
    REF1 --> CMP1["Comparator 1 Upper Threshold"]
    REF2 --> CMP2["Comparator 2 Lower Threshold"]
    PIN6["Pin 6 THRESHOLD Input"] --> CMP1
    PIN2["Pin 2 TRIGGER Input"] --> CMP2
    CMP1 --> FLIPFLOP["SR Flip Flop Latch"]
    CMP2 --> FLIPFLOP
    PIN4["Pin 4 RESET Input"] --> FLIPFLOP
    FLIPFLOP --> OUTBUF["Totem Pole Output Buffer"]
    OUTBUF --> PIN3["Pin 3 OUTPUT"]
    FLIPFLOP --> DISCH["Discharge Transistor Q1 NPN"]
    DISCH --> PIN7["Pin 7 DISCHARGE"]
    PIN5["Pin 5 CONTROL VOLTAGE"] -. Modulates REF1 .-> REF1
```

## 4.2 Monostable Multivibrator Circuit Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VCCX["VCC Supply"] --> REXT["External Resistor R"]
    REXT --> P7P6["Pins 7 and 6 tied together"]
    P7P6 --> CEXT["External Capacitor C"]
    CEXT --> GNDX["GND"]
    P7P6 -. Monitored by .-> CMPU["Upper Comparator"]
    PIN2X["Pin 2 Trigger Input Pulse"] -. Triggers .-> CMPL["Lower Comparator"]
    CMPU --> FFX["SR Flip Flop"]
    CMPL --> FFX
    FFX --> OUTX["Pin 3 OUTPUT Pulse of width T = 1.1RC"]
    FFX --> QX["Discharge Transistor"]
    QX -. Drains .-> P7P6
```

## 4.3 Astable Multivibrator Circuit Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VCCY["VCC Supply"] --> RAY["Resistor RA"]
    RAY --> NODEC["Node A Pins 7 6 2 tied"]
    NODEC --> RBY["Resistor RB"]
    RBY --> NODEC2["Node B Pins 6 and 2"]
    NODEC2 --> CEY["Timing Capacitor C"]
    CEY --> GNDY["GND"]
    NODEC -. Charge path RA plus RB .-> NODEC2
    NODEC2 -. Discharge path RB only .-> CEY
    FFL["SR Flip Flop"] --> OUTY["Pin 3 OUTPUT Continuous Square Wave"]
    FFL --> QY["Discharge Transistor"]
    QY -. Shunts Node A to GND when ON .-> NODEC
```

## 4.4 Functional State Machine — Astable Mode Timing Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> CHARGING
    CHARGING --> DISCHARGING : Capacitor reaches 2 by 3 VCC
    DISCHARGING --> CHARGING : Capacitor reaches 1 by 3 VCC
    note right of CHARGING : Output Pin 3 is HIGH\nCharge via RA plus RB\nDuration tH = 0.693 times RA plus RB times C
    note right of DISCHARGING : Output Pin 3 is LOW\nDischarge via RB only\nDuration tL = 0.693 times RB times C
```

## 4.5 LM317 Adjustable Regulator — Application Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VIN["Unregulated DC Input Vin"] --> LM317["LM317 Adjustable Regulator IC"]
    LM317 --> VOUT["Regulated Output Vout = 1.25 times 1 plus R2 by R1"]
    LM317 --> RADJ["R1 Fixed Resistor"]
    RADJ --> ADJ["ADJ Pin Pin 1"]
    ADJ --> RPOT["R2 Potentiometer"]
    RPOT --> GNDL["GND"]
    CD1["Input Capacitor Cin 0.1uF"] -. Filter .-> VIN
    CD2["Output Capacitor Cout 1uF Tantalum"] -. Improves transient response .-> VOUT
    DPROT["Protection Diode D1 D2"] -. Prevents reverse discharge .-> LM317
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define monostable multivibrator using IC 555. Derive an expression for the output pulse width.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A monostable multivibrator using IC 555 has only **one stable state**. In the absence of a trigger, the output remains at **LOW (0 V)**. When an external **negative-going trigger pulse** of magnitude less than $\frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$ is applied to Pin 2, the output goes **HIGH** and remains HIGH for a duration $T$ determined by the external RC network, after which it automatically returns to LOW.

The pulse width derivation begins with the capacitor charging from 0 V toward $V_{CC}$ through resistor R. Setting the capacitor voltage equal to the upper threshold $\frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$:

$$
\frac{2}{3} V_{CC} = V_{CC}\left(1 - e^{-T/RC}\right)
$$

Solving:

$$
T = RC \cdot \ln(3) = 1.1\,R\,C
$$

**Exam Tip:** Always state the **boundary conditions** for the threshold voltages ($\frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$ and $\frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$) in your answer — this is a guaranteed 1-mark item.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any three applications of IC 555 timer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Astable Multivibrator:** Used as a square-wave generator (clock pulses, tone generation, LED flashers).
2. **Monostable Multivibrator:** Used for missing-pulse detection, bounce-free switches, and precise time-delay generation.
3. **Schmitt Trigger:** When Pin 2 and Pin 6 are tied together and the input is applied directly, the 555 functions as an inverting Schmitt trigger with **hysteresis voltage $V_H = \frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$**.
4. **(Bonus)** Pulse-Width Modulator (PWM), Linear Ramp Generator, Frequency Divider.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of IC 555 connected as an **astable multivibrator**. Derive expressions for the frequency of oscillation and the duty cycle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design an astable multivibrator using IC 555 to generate a **1 kHz square wave** with a **60% duty cycle** from a **+12 V** supply. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Circuit Description (2 marks):**
In the astable configuration:
- $R_A$ is connected between $V_{CC}$ and Pin 7.
- $R_B$ is connected between Pin 7 and the junction of Pins 2 and 6.
- C is connected between Pins 2/6 and GND.
- Pin 4 (RESET) is tied to $V_{CC}$ to disable the reset function.
- Pin 5 is bypassed to GND via a 0.01 μF capacitor to filter noise on the control voltage.

**Operation — Charging Phase (2 marks):**
- Initially, the discharge transistor is OFF. The capacitor charges through the series combination $R_A + R_B$ toward $V_{CC}$.
- When $V_C$ reaches $\frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$, the upper comparator triggers the flip-flop to RESET, making the output LOW and turning ON the discharge transistor.

**Operation — Discharging Phase (1 mark):**
- Capacitor C now discharges from $\frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$ to $\frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$ through $R_B$ only.
- When $V_C$ falls to $\frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$, the lower comparator SETs the flip-flop, making the output HIGH and turning OFF the discharge transistor. The cycle repeats indefinitely.

**Derivations (2 marks):**
The charging and discharging time intervals are:
$$
t_H = 0.693\,(R_A + R_B)\,C, \qquad t_L = 0.693\,R_B\,C
$$

Total period:
$$
T = t_H + t_L = 0.693\,(R_A + 2R_B)\,C
$$

Frequency:
$$
f = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B)\,C}
$$

Duty cycle:
$$
D = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} \times 100\%
$$

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Given:** $f = 1\,\text{kHz}$, $D = 60\%$, $V_{CC} = 12\,\text{V}$.

**Step 1 — Find the Total Period:**
$$
T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1000} = 1\,\text{ms} = 10^{-3}\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Express $t_H$ and $t_L$ in terms of $T$ and $D$:**
$$
t_H = D \cdot T = 0.60 \times 1\,\text{ms} = 0.6\,\text{ms}
$$
$$
t_L = T - t_H = 1\,\text{ms} - 0.6\,\text{ms} = 0.4\,\text{ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Choose a Convenient Capacitor Value:**
Let $C = 0.1\,\mu\text{F} = 10^{-7}\,\text{F}$ (a standard E12 value).

**Step 4 — Solve for $R_B$ from the $t_L$ equation:**
$$
t_L = 0.693\,R_B\,C \implies R_B = \frac{t_L}{0.693 \cdot C} = \frac{0.4 \times 10^{-3}}{0.693 \times 10^{-7}}
$$
$$
R_B = \frac{4 \times 10^{-4}}{6.93 \times 10^{-8}} = 5772\,\Omega \approx 5.6\,\text{k}\Omega \text{ (nearest E12 standard value)}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $R_A$ from the $t_H$ equation:**
$$
t_H = 0.693\,(R_A + R_B)\,C \implies R_A + R_B = \frac{t_H}{0.693 \cdot C} = \frac{0.6 \times 10^{-3}}{0.693 \times 10^{-7}} = 8658\,\Omega
$$
$$
R_A = 8658 - 5772 = 2886\,\Omega \approx 2.7\,\text{k}\Omega \text{ (nearest E12 standard value)}
$$

**Verification:**
$$
T_{actual} = 0.693 \times (2.7k + 2 \times 5.6k) \times 0.1\mu = 0.693 \times 13.9k \times 0.1\mu = 0.963\,\text{ms}
$$
$$
f_{actual} = \frac{1}{0.963\,\text{ms}} \approx 1.04\,\text{kHz} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Final Design:** $R_A = 2.7\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_B = 5.6\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Draw the internal block diagram of IC 555 and explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of IC 555 as a **monostable multivibrator**. Derive the expression for the output pulse width and explain its use as a **missing-pulse detector**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to SECTION 4.1.

**Functional Explanation (5 marks):**

1. **Resistive Voltage Divider:** Three equal 5 kΩ resistors in series between $V_{CC}$ and GND. Produces reference voltages of $\frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$ and $\frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$ for the two comparators.

2. **Comparator 1 (Upper):** Compares the THRESHOLD voltage (Pin 6) with $\frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$. Output goes HIGH when $V_{pin6} > \frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$.

3. **Comparator 2 (Lower):** Compares the TRIGGER voltage (Pin 2) with $\frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$. Output goes HIGH when $V_{pin2} < \frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$.

4. **SR Flip-Flop:** Stores the latched state. SET input ← Comparator 2; RESET input ← Comparator 1. Its Q output drives the output buffer and its $\overline{Q}$ output drives the discharge transistor.

5. **Discharge Transistor (Q1):** An NPN BJT whose collector is brought to Pin 7. When $\overline{Q} = 1$, Q1 is ON and shorts Pin 7 to GND (rapidly discharging any external capacitor). When $\overline{Q} = 0$, Q1 is OFF and Pin 7 floats.

6. **Output Buffer (Totem-Pole):** A push-pull amplifier that delivers up to **200 mA** to drive external loads such as LEDs, relays, and small speakers.

7. **RESET Input (Pin 4):** Asynchronous master reset; an active-LOW signal forces the output LOW regardless of all other inputs.

8. **CONTROL VOLTAGE (Pin 5):** External access to the $\frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$ reference. Applying a voltage here directly modulates the threshold voltage, which is the basis for **voltage-controlled oscillation (VCO)**.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Circuit Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to SECTION 4.2.

**Operation (2 marks):**
- In stable state, the discharge transistor is ON, so the capacitor is held at 0 V. The output is LOW.
- A negative trigger pulse ($< \frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$) applied to Pin 2 SETs the flip-flop, making the output HIGH and turning OFF the discharge transistor.
- The capacitor charges through R toward $V_{CC}$. When $V_C$ reaches $\frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$, the upper comparator RESETs the flip-flop, output goes LOW, and the capacitor rapidly discharges through Q1.
- The output pulse width is $T = 1.1\,R\,C$.

**Missing-Pulse Detector Application (3 marks):**
In a missing-pulse detector:
- A train of **negative-going trigger pulses** is fed to Pin 2.
- The time constant $T = 1.1\,R\,C$ is chosen to be **slightly longer** than the normal inter-pulse period $T_{in}$.
- **Normal operation:** Each new pulse arrives before the timing capacitor has finished charging to $\frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$. The flip-flop is continually re-triggered, and the output stays HIGH.
- **Missing pulse case:** If one pulse in the train is absent, the capacitor continues to charge and eventually reaches $\frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$, resetting the flip-flop and pulling the output LOW. This LOW state serves as a **visual or audible alarm** indicating the missing pulse.
- **Applications:** Heart-rate monitors, conveyor-belt jam detection, and watchdog timers in embedded systems.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the constant 1.1 in the monostable formula.** Many students write $T = RC$ — this is **WRONG**. The correct expression is $T = 1.1\,RC$, and you **must** show the derivation of $\ln(3) = 1.0986$ to get full credit.
> 2. **Confusing charging vs. discharging paths in astable mode.** A very common error is writing $t_L = 0.693(R_A + R_B)C$ instead of the correct $t_L = 0.693\,R_B\,C$. The discharge path **excludes** $R_A$ because the discharge current enters Pin 7, bypassing $R_A$ and going directly to GND through the saturated transistor.
> 3. **Writing duty cycle as 50% always.** This is only true when $R_A \to 0$. For non-zero $R_A$, the duty cycle is always greater than 50%. Examiners specifically look for the **general formula** $D = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} \times 100\%$.
> 4. **Forgetting the control voltage pin bypass capacitor.** A 0.01 μF capacitor between Pin 5 and GND is **mandatory** in practical designs to prevent noise from modulating the threshold. Not mentioning this loses a mark on design problems.
> 5. **Mixing up 78XX and 79XX series.** Remember: **78XX is positive** (e.g., 7805 → +5 V), **79XX is negative** (e.g., 7905 → −5 V). The 79XX series has the same pinout as 78XX **except the GND and Vout pins are swapped** — confusion here is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 6. **LM317 I_ADJ neglect.** In standard KTU problems, you can neglect the 50 μA ADJ pin current, but in **precision design** questions (5-mark design problems), you must add the $I_{ADJ} \cdot R_2$ correction term.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section and read it the night before the exam — it covers 90% of the markable content.**

- [x] **Three Modes of 555:** Monostable (one stable state), Astable (no stable state), Bistable (two stable states).
- [x] **Monostable Pulse Width:** $T = 1.1\,R\,C$ — derived from $\ln(3) = 1.0986$.
- [x] **Astable Frequency:** $f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B)\,C}$ — remember the constant **1.44**, not 1.1.
- [x] **Astable Duty Cycle:** $D = \dfrac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} \times 100\%$ — **always** > 50%.
- [x] **Threshold Voltages:** Upper = $\frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$, Lower = $\frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$, Hysteresis = $\frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$.
- [x] **Internal Resistor Value:** 5 kΩ (hence the name "555"). Three in series give the reference voltages.
- [x] **Discharge Path:** Only through $R_B$ in astable mode — $R_A$ is bypassed.
- [x] **Output Current Capability:** Up to **200 mA** (sink and source) — can directly drive relays and LEDs.
- [x] **Output Pin = Pin 3**, Discharge = Pin 7, Trigger = Pin 2, Threshold = Pin 6, Reset = Pin 4, Control Voltage = Pin 5, GND = Pin 1, V_CC = Pin 8.
- [x] **555 as Schmitt Trigger:** Tie Pins 2 and 6 together; output is inverting; $V_H = \frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$.
- [x] **555 as VCO:** Apply a modulating voltage at Pin 5; the output frequency becomes a function of the control voltage.
- [x] **Missing-Pulse Detector:** Monostable mode with $T_{pulse} = 1.1RC$ slightly greater than the expected inter-pulse period.
- [x] **LM317 Output:** $V_{OUT} = 1.25\left(1 + \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right)$ — the constant **1.25 V** is the internal bandgap reference.
- [x] **78XX Series:** Fixed positive regulators (7805, 7809, 7812, 7815, 7824).
- [x] **79XX Series:** Fixed negative regulators (7905, 7912, 7915, 7924).
- [x] **Pinout Difference:** In 79XX, **Pin 2 = GND** and **Pin 3 = Output** (swapped relative to 78XX).
- [x] **Line Regulation:** Measure of how well the regulator rejects changes in input voltage; ideally as small as possible (typically 0.01% / V).
- [x] **Load Regulation:** Measure of how well the regulator maintains constant output under varying load current; ideally as small as possible (typically 0.1%).
- [x] **PLL Applications:** FM demodulation, frequency synthesis, motor speed control, FSK demodulation, AM demodulation (squared-loop detector), tone decoding.
